lessons.title: What’s the difference between a TradingView script’s filename and display name?
lessons.clean_text: # What’s the difference between a TradingView script’s filename and display name? ## Different filename and display name ## Set filename to display name ### Find scripts in code editor ## Summary ### References A script setting we always configure is its name. But that isn’t the only name a script uses. In fact, TradingView scripts have two types of names: Now here’s the thing. The name we set with title (and shorttitle) is independent from the filename we use to save the script in our Personal Library [1] . When we open the the ‘Indicators & Strategies’ window to find a particular script to add to the chart, we use the filename under which we saved the script. And not its display name. This can be confusing. Because the name we’re most familiar with is the display name we see in TradingView’s user interface. But that isn’t the name we find the script with. Let’s see how this works in practice. Let’s say we make a strategy script and use the strategy() function to configure the script like so: Here the title setting gives the script a name of ‘My Bollinger Strategy’. And the shorttitle setting gives the script an alternative, shortened name of ‘MBS’. Now let’s add this strategy to the chart. So we open the ‘Indicators & Strategies’ window and type in ‘My Bollinger Strategy’: That’s weird, the strategy isn’t there. Perhaps when we search for ‘MBS’? This has no luck either; the script doesn’t show. TradingView cannot find the script. What’s going on? If we open the script’s source code in TradingView’s code editor, we see that it’s filename isn’t ‘My Bollinger Strategy’ or ‘MBS’. Instead it’s ’example script’: TradingView’s ‘Indicators & Strategies’ window works with filenames, not the title name or the shorttitle abbreviated name. So we cannot find a script with one of those names. Instead we look for the script’s filename. So we search for ’example script’ instead: Now we do find the script! Finding a script based on its filename is something we only do with our personal scripts. When we publish a script on TradingView, then other users find that script with its title or shorttitle name . So other people don’t use the script’s filename to find our published script, only we do. It’s a good practice to save scripts with a filename that matches the script’s display name. That makes it easy to find scripts in the future. When the filename is already taken, use one that’s highly similar to the script’s display name. When we change the script’s display name by updating the title setting and/or the shorttitle setting, it’s a smart move to also update the script’s filename in TradingView’s code editor. This 10-second task saves a lot of time and frustration later. Perhaps today, when we make the script, it’s hard to imagine how we ever forget the script’s filename. But because its display name shows throughout TradingView’s user interface, over time we see that name as the script’s name. Then when we want to add the script to the chart again, we’ll struggle to think of its filename. If you can’t find a recent script with the ‘Indicators & Strategies’ window, try the ‘Open’ button in TradingView’s code editor (called the Pine Editor). That brings up a window with all your scripts. This list can be sorted by modification date (and in other ways) to find the script in that way. ```pine_script //@version=5 strategy(title="My Bollinger Strategy", shorttitle="MBS", overlay=true) ``` ```pine_script [2] ``` - A display name. This name shows throughout TradingView’s user interface as the script’s name. We configure this name with the title setting and the shorttitle setting. - A filename. This name we use to save the script’s source code file in TradingView’s code editor. - Every TradingView script has two names: a filename and display name. - To add a script to the chart, we use its filename. But it’s the display name that shows throughout TradingView’s user interface. - To make it easy to find the script in the future, we best set the script’s filename to its display name. 1. TradingView Docs (n.d. a). Declaration statements. Retrieved on November 10, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/language/Declaration_statements.html 1. TradingView Docs (n.d. b). Publishing scripts. Retrieved on November 10, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/writing/Publishing.html

lessons.title: Find out if a Pine Script string is uppercase text: here’s how
lessons.clean_text:
# Find out if a Pine Script string is uppercase text: here’s how

## Custom function

## Function examples

## Quick example

## Function features

## Example script

## Summary

### References

Pine Script doesn’t have a function that tests if a string is in uppercase text [see 1] . But this information is definitely useful when we compare strings.

Thankfully, we can make our own function with a bit of code. Let’s see how.

This function tells if a string is in uppercase text:

This StrIsUpper() custom function has one parameter: source. Of this string we want to know if it’s in uppercase.

We do two things inside the function. First we convert source to uppercase with the str.upper() function. The uppercased text we store in the sourceUpper variable. We’ll use this variable with the comparisons we do next.

We evaluate two things to see if the source parameter is uppercase text. First we look if that parameter in uppercase is different than source in lowercase. So we test if sourceUpper is unequal to (!=) the outcome of the str.lower() function.

That check is a small trick to see if the string has alphabetical characters. If source has no letters, but only numbers and symbols for instance, then the string’s uppercase and lowercase version are the same. And since a string without letters isn’t uppercase text, we want to exclude this option.

The second evaluation tests if source equals (==) the same source value in uppercase (which we stored in the sourceUpper variable). If this is the case, the source string is in uppercase.

The and operator combines the two evaluations. This gives a true outcome when both evaluations are true. If one or both are false, however, their combination is false too. This result of and is what the function returns.

In plain English, the string the function analyses should contain one (or more) letters. That is what the first evaluation checks. And the string has to be in uppercase – the second evaluation. These two features combine mean the string is uppercase text.

Some examples of the StrIsUpper() function and its result are:

Say we have a piece of text and want to know it’s uppercase. To code that in Pine Script we do:

We pass the StrIsUpper() function the "NAS100" literal string. The function’s outcome we store in the symbolUpper variable. Since the tested text only has uppercase letters, we can tell the variable holds a true value.

There are a couple of things we can do with the function’s outcome. One possibility is to colour the chart’s background:

We call the bgcolor() function to paint the background. Inside the function the conditional operator (?:) evaluates the symbolUpper variable. When the variable is true, the operator returns red (color.red) with 95% transparency set by the color.new() function. That colour is then used by bgcolor() for the background. In case the variable is false, the operator returns the na value. That turns off the coloured background.

Since we already know the variable is true, we can predict the outcome: the chart background becomes red.

Let’s explore how the StrIsUpper() function works in a script. The indicator below checks if the instrument’s description is in uppercase. We show the result of that evaluation with a text label on the chart.

The code of the indicator is:

First we call the indicator() function. This names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then we insert the StrIsUpper() function with copy/paste.

Next we test if the instrument description is all uppercase text:

We call the StrIsUpper() function and give it the syminfo.description variable. That variable returns a string with the description of the chart’s instrument.

When all letters in that string are uppercase, StrIsUpper() returns true (and it returns false otherwise). We store this true/false outcome in the upperDesc variable.

Then we display the result with a text label on the chart:

This if statement evaluates the barstate.islast variable. On each of the chart’s last bars, this variable returns true. On those bars, the if statement calls the label.new() function to make a new label.

That label appears at the bar’s high (bar_index, high). Its colour is black (color.black) with white (color.white) letters.

For the label’s text the + operator combines several string pieces together. First is a literal string and then the syminfo.description variable. Then another literal text and finally the outcome of the StrIsUpper() function, which we put in the upperDesc variable earlier.

However, that variable is a true/false value. And the label’s text requires a string. To fix this incompatibility, we convert the true/false variable to text with the str.tostring() function.

On the chart, the label shows the instrument’s description. It also tells if that string is in uppercase. For the GBP/USD chart below, StrIsUpper() returns true as the description only has uppercase letters:

```pine_script
// StrIsUpper() returns 'true' if all letters in the provided string // are in uppercase. Returns 'false' with one or more lowercase // characters and when the string is empty, 'na', or has no letters. StrIsUpper(string source) => sourceUpper = str.upper(source) sourceUpper != str.lower(source) and source == sourceUpper
```

```pine_script
// Check if the 'NAS100' symbol has letters that are only in uppercase symbolUpper = StrIsUpper("NAS100")
```

```pine_script
// When the symbol's cased letters are all uppercase, make the // background into red bgcolor(symbolUpper ? color.new(color.red, 95) : na)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="String is in uppercase", overlay=true) // StrIsUpper() returns 'true' if all letters in the provided string // are in uppercase. Returns 'false' with one or more lowercase // characters and when the string is empty, 'na', or has no letters. StrIsUpper(string source) => sourceUpper = str.upper(source) sourceUpper != str.lower(source) and source == sourceUpper // Test if the instrument's description is in all uppercase upperDesc = StrIsUpper(syminfo.description) // On the chart's last bar, tell if the description is in uppercase if barstate.islast label.new(bar_index, high, color=color.black, textcolor=color.white, text="Instrument description:\n" + syminfo.description + "\n\nEntirely in uppercase? " + str.tostring(upperDesc))
```

```pine_script
// Test if the instrument's description is in all uppercase upperDesc = StrIsUpper(syminfo.description)
```

```pine_script
// On the chart's last bar, tell if the description is in uppercase if barstate.islast label.new(bar_index, high, color=color.black, textcolor=color.white, text="Instrument description:\n" + syminfo.description + "\n\nEntirely in uppercase? " + str.tostring(upperDesc))
```

| Code example | Result |
| --- | --- |
| StrIsUpper("USDJPY") | true. All letters in the string are in uppercase. |
| StrIsUpper("UsdJpy") | false. Not all alphabetical characters are in uppercase, so the string isn’t all uppercase text. |
| StrIsUpper(",USD@JPY;?") | true. All letters in this string are in uppercase. The non-letter characters are irrelevant; the function only looks at alphabetical characters. And since those characters are in uppercase, the result is true. |
| StrIsUpper("16.32") | false. There are no letters in the string and thus also no uppercase characters. |
| StrIsUpper("(◠‿◠)") | false. With no letters, the string isn’t uppercase text. |

- When StrIsUpper() returns false, we can’t know for certain the string is lowercase. It’s also possible that the string is empty or only has numbers or symbols.
- When we give StrIsUpper() the na value or an empty string (""), the function always returns false. Those values are never uppercase text.

- The custom StrIsUpper() function tells if all letters in a string are uppercase.
- The function has one argument: the string (piece of text) to analyse.
- When all alphabetical characters of that string are in uppercase, the function returns true.
- With one or more lowercase letters, or no letters at all, the function’s outcome is false.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on September 12, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/

lessons.title: Tip: TradingView backtest results are different when calculating on every tick
lessons.clean_text:
# Tip: TradingView backtest results are different when calculating on every tick

## Configuring a TradingView strategy’s calculation frequency

## Example strategy: trading extreme prices with every real-time tick

## Example charts: the TradingView strategy’s real-time performance

## Strategy performance: historical backtesting of the TradingView strategy

## Insights: comparing real-time with historical strategy performance

## Summary

### References

The standard behaviour of a TradingView strategy is to calculate on the close of each price bar. But our trading script can also process every real-time price update. With that setting, however, the strategy’s backtest performance becomes irrelevant. Let’s see why that’s the case.

Specifying the settings of a TradingView strategy programmatically is done with the strategy() function, which has to be present in every strategy’s code (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). And its title argument, which specifies the strategy’s name, always needs to be set too (TradingView, n.d.).

Another argument of strategy() is calc_on_every_tick. This argument defaults to false, and in that case the strategy calculates on the close of every bar during backtesting and real-time trading (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). But when we set calc_on_every_tick to true, then the strategy calculates on every real-time price update (TradingView, n.d.).

That feature allows for much greater speed: instead of ‘waiting’ till the bar closes, the strategy can perform its calculations as many times as there are price updates in a bar. This makes the time between subsequent calculations much shorter, especially on higher time frames. However, these intra-bar calculations are not done on historical data (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). On those historical bars, the strategy calculates only at the bar’s close (meaning, calculating just once per bar) – regardless of whether calc_on_every_tick is enabled or disabled.

This has an important consequence: with calc_on_every_tick enabled, the backtest results of nearly all strategies are unusable and completely irrelevant. Because TradingView calculates those strategies differently on historical bars compared to real-time data, it often seems we have two different strategies when we compare the historical and real-time performance of a strategy. In fact, with calc_on_every_tick=true only results collected when the strategy runs on real-time data give any indication of how the strategy can perform during live trading.

The difference that we typically see when comparing historical to real-time performance of those strategies is much quicker scaling into the maximum position size set by the pyramiding setting. Furthermore, on real-time data an order condition can be true inside the bar, only to be invalidated when the bar closes. That means the strategy would have traded that bar in real time, but won’t trade the same bar during the historical backtest (because there wasn’t an order signal when the bar closed).

For a better understanding of how backtest results of a strategy that calculates with every real-time tick cannot be compared with real-time performance, let’s first create an example strategy. After that we’ll see how it behaves on real-time data and then perform a historical backtest on those same bars to see the difference.

The example strategy below trades whenever the highest high or lowest low is penetrated. That is, when the bar’s high is above the highest high of the preceding 15 bars, we submit an enter long market order. Likewise, with a bar’s low less than the lowest low of the previous 15 bars, we initiate a short position.

Since there are no other exits, we’re always in the market with this strategy. We furthermore allow up to 7 entries in the same direction and have the strategy calculate on every real-time tick.

A quick view of the strategy’s behaviour on real-time data (first chart) and historical data (second chart) is given below. After discussing the code we’ll take a closer look at the difference between real-time behaviour and historical performance.

First we configure the strategy’s settings with strategy(). With this function’s title argument we name the strategy and, by setting the overlay argument to true, have the strategy display on the price chart (TradingView, n.d.). With calc_on_every_tick=true the strategy processes every real-time tick, and with the pyramiding argument we allow up to 7 entries in the same direction.

When we enable calc_on_every_tick, the strategy evaluates real-time price bars as if they were already closed. This means built-in variables like close return the current, latest price of the real-time bar. That ‘closing’ price of the last bar of the chart is then updated with every tick. Since TradingView doesn’t know whether the recent tick is the last of the bar or if more price updates will follow, each new price update ’extends’ the price bar’s close.

Next we create two input options:

User-configurable inputs are added with specialised Pine Script functions. These functions also return the input’s current value (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). Here we store those values in a variable with the assignment operator (=). That way we can access the input’s value later on by referring to the variable.

Both input options are numerical integer inputs, which accept whole numbers only. To make those we call the input.int() function. We provide that function with two arguments. The first is the input’s default, the other, title, sets the name that TradingView displays before the input in the script settings (TradingView, n.d.).

We name the first input “Highest Length” and track its value in the highestLen variable. The value of the “Lowest Length” input is assigned to the lowestLen variable.

Both input variables are used when calculating the highest high and lowest low:

We set the highestHigh variable to the value returned by the ta.highest() function. This function returns the highest value for a recent number of bars, and requires two arguments: a series of values and an integer with the amount of bars to calculate on (TradingView, n.d.). Here we set those arguments to the bar’s high prices (high) and the highestLen input variable, which we gave a default value of 15.

But when ta.highest() calculates on high prices, it also uses the price of the current bar. That would be problematic for us since we go long when the bar’s high is greater than the highest high. And that situation cannot happen when the highest high includes the current bar’s high.

We solve that by placing the history referencing operator ([]) with a value of 1 just behind the ta.highest() function call. This makes the function not return the highest high value for the current bar, but the function’s value on the previous bar (see Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). That previous bar’s highest high value is what we store in the highestHigh variable for use later on.

We calculate the lowest low value in much the same way. For this we use the ta.lowest() function, which can work on two arguments: a series of values to process and an integer that sets the number of bars to calculate on (TradingView, n.d.). We set those arguments to the bar’s low prices (low) and lowestLen, the input variable that we gave a standard value of 15 earlier. Then with the help of the history referencing operator we assign the 15-bar lowest low to the lowestLow variable.

Then we make a time filter for the strategy’s trades:

The purpose of this timeFilter true/false variable is to have the strategy only trade during a short period of time. That way the strategy doesn’t have to run on an extended period of time to collect its real-time results, and that makes it easier for us to compare those results with the historical backtest report. With this filter, our example strategy should only trade between 8:30 and 12:00 hour on July 15, 2016.

To translate that time window into code, we set the value of timeFilter to 4 true/false expressions joined together with the and logical operator. This operator returns true whenever the value on its left and the value on its right are both true too; now when one or both values are false, then and returns false too (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). This means each expression has to be true before the value of timeFilter is true too.

The first expression evaluates if the year variable, which returns the bar’s year in the exchange’s time zone (TradingView, n.d.), equals (==) 2016. With the second expression we check if month (the bar’s month in exchange time zone; TradingView, n.d.) is equal to (==) 7. And the third expression processes dayofmonth to see whether the current bar’s date in exchange time zone is 15 (TradingView, n.d.). Combined, these three expressions check if the bar’s date is July 15, 2016.

The last true/false expression that affects the value of timeFilter evaluates whether the bar’s time is within a certain time window (8:30 till 12:00 in the morning). For this we use time(), a function that either returns the current bar’s time when it falls inside the specified range, or a “not a number” (NaN) value when the bar is outside that range (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). This means time() returns some numerical value when the bar is inside the range, and a non-numerical value when it isn’t.

The time() function requires two arguments: the bar’s resolution and a string with the time range that the bar should fall in (TradingView, n.d.). We set these arguments to timeframe.period, a built-in variable that returns the chart’s current resolution (TradingView, n.d.), and "830-1200" to specify the 8:30-12:00 time range. Now when time() returns a value that’s greater than (>) 0, we know that the current bar is inside the specified session.

So these 4 true/false expressions combined mean that the timeFilter variable holds true whenever the current bar falls inside the 8:30-12:00 session on July 15, 2016.

Next we plot the strategy’s values for visual confirmation:

The plot() function displays the values of its first argument as a solid line by default on the chart (TradingView, n.d.). Here we use that function twice. First we plot the 15-bar highest high (highestHigh) values on the chart in the green (color.green) basic TradingView colour. And with the second plot() statement we display the 15-bar lowest low (lowestLow) values as a red (color.red) line.

To help identify which price bars falls inside our time range and which ones don’t, we also colour the chart’s background from top to bottom. We do that with the bgcolor() function (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). Since we only want to colour the background of bars inside our time range (and not the background of bars outside the range), we need to colour the chart’s background conditionally.

For that we use the conditional operator (?:) that works on three values. The first is a true/false expression that, when true, makes the operator return its second value. When that expression is false, then the operator returns its third and last value (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). This makes the conditional operator work like an if/else statement: ‘if this is true, then return A; else return B’.

In our case, the argument of the bgcolor() function is set to the value returned by the conditional operator. And that makes our background change its colour conditionally. The expression that’s evaluated by the conditional operator here is the timeFilter variable. Now when the current price bar falls inside our time range, that variable holds true and the conditional operator returns the orange (color.orange) basic TradingView colour. We make this colour highly transparent (90%) with the color.new() function.

When timeFilter is false, the conditional operator returns na. That built-in variable returns a “not a number” value (TradingView, n.d.) which, when used as a colour, gives a transparent effect (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). This way we either colour the chart’s background when the bar is inside the time range, or apply no colour when the bar is outside that range.

We end our example by submitting the strategy’s orders:

To submit orders conditionally, we use two if statements. Both evaluate two true/false expressions that are combined with the and logical operator. This means both expressions have to be true before TradingView sees their combined result as true too (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.).

The first if statement checks if the current bar falls inside our specified time range (timeFilter) and whether there’s a price breakout to the upside. In our example we defined that latter as the situation in which the bar’s high (high) is greater than (>) the highest high of the preceding 15 bars (highestHigh). Now when both situations occur, we submit an order with the strategy.entry() function.

That function enters into a position with a market order by default and, when there’s already an open position in the other direction, reverses that open position (TradingView, n.d.). We use strategy.entry() with two arguments here. The first specifies the order identifier, and this name appears on the chart and in the ‘Strategy Tester’ window. The second argument, when set to a value of strategy.long, makes the function submit an enter long order while strategy.short has strategy.entry() submit an enter short order (TradingView, n.d.). We set these two arguments to “Enter Long” and strategy.long here to submit an enter long market order.

The next if statement and subsequent strategy.entry() function call are much the same. Here we evaluate whether the bar is in the specified time range (timeFilter) and whether the bar’s low is less than the lowest low of the previous 15 bars (low < lowestLow). When this is the case, we submit a short order (strategy.short) named “Enter Short” with strategy.entry().

This ends our discussion of the programming example. Now let’s see how the strategy behaves in real-time and when backtested on historical data.

Our above example strategy creates the following input options in the script’s settings:

The behaviour of the strategy example in real-time is the following. Here it just began its trading period (8:30 till 12:00) with an enter short order:

Not long after that several long trades were executed:

As the chart continues to create new price bars, we see that multiple long orders executed on the bar after the initial enter long order. And the short position also consists out of multiple orders:

Next another short signal happens:

And that position is closed later on by an enter long trade:

This process continues until there are so many trades on the chart that TradingView doesn’t show their order names anymore:

A little while later, when there are less orders in the current chart view, the order names come back:

These multiple orders for a new higher high or lower low continue for a while:

And the trading strategy stops trading at the end of the time range (12:00 hour):

Now let’s look at the strategy’s performance: how did the strategy fare when calculating on every real-time tick? In the ‘Overview’ tab of the ‘Strategy Tester’ window we see that the strategy generated 91 trades:

The script achieved a net profit of -0.03 pound with a profit factor of -0.259:

Its behaviour of multiple trades per bar is visible in the ‘List of Trades’ tab too. The first 4 trades, for instance, all entered and exited on the same price bar (shown by the identical ‘Date/Time’ values):

Now let’s see how the strategy performs during a historical backtest on the same time period.

When we apply the example strategy to the chart after the time range of 8:30 till 12:00 on July 15, 2016 ends, we see the script submitting its trades shortly after 8:30:

This immediately shows the difference between real-time data and historical calculations: on historical data, the strategy submits one order per bar whereas in real-time it submitting multiple orders per bar.

Since we set the calc_on_every_tick argument to true, the strategy processes every real-time price update. But that strategy setting doesn’t affect how the script calculates on historical bars, and those historical calculations are only performed at the close of each price bar (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.).

For our strategy, one consequence is that it takes a lot longer before the strategy reaches its maximum position size of the pyramiding setting on those historical price bars. Actually, it might not even reach that limit of 20 entries in the same direction at all. That’s because we need 20 price bars with an order signal on historical data. But with real-time data, we only need 20 price updates during a price bar on which the order condition was true.

Another difference is that, on historical data, orders fill on the bar after the one on which a new highest high or lowest low was reached. For instance:

There’s also another reason why some of the trades happen on different bars than they did in real-time. The maximum number of entries in the same direction is reached much sooner in real-time – and then on any subsequent price bar on which the order condition is also true, the trade isn’t submitted. But with historical data, where the strategy only submits one order per bar, much more price bars can be traded.

So even though we didn’t change any of the strategy’s manual settings or its code, the trades look remarkably different compared to their real-time behaviour:

So it’s not a big surprise that, when look at the ‘Strategy Tester’ window, the strategy performs different now on historical bars than it did with real-time data. For instance, in the ‘Overview’ tab we see 46 trades (instead of 91) and a profit factor of -0.197 (instead of -0.259):

While this difference is modest, we did only trade one morning session. And backtesting that part of only one day caused our number of trades and profit factor to be roughly 50% different than the strategy would achieve in real-time.

In the ‘Performance Summary’ tab we see a net profit that’s one-third better (or, better said, less worse) than when the strategy traded on real-time data:

And there’s a difference in the ‘List of Trades’ tab too. When the strategy executed on real-time data, its first 4 orders all opened and closed at the same bar time. That happened since multiple orders were executed per bar. But on historical data, the strategy only calculates once per bar (and thus our strategy can submit an order once). And so now we see that each entry order fills on a different bar:

Now let’s see which insights we can draw from this comparison between the strategy’s real-time performance and its backtest results.

A strategy that calculates with every real-time tick performs differently in real-time than during backtesting. Several things to keep in mind with this are:

So while calculating a strategy with every real-time tick is helpful because it makes the strategy respond quicker in real time, this feature cannot be backtested currently. This means the backtest performance of a strategy that uses the calc_on_every_tick argument is irrelevant and the only indication of how the script would perform is to have it run on real-time data.

At the moment there isn’t a way to save or export the data from the ‘Strategy Tester’. So when we evaluate the performance of a strategy in real time, we’ll manually need to record its performance because the next time the chart or script loads, the data in the ‘Strategy Tester’ window is erased and recalculated.

We configure the settings of a TradingView strategy with strategy(), a function that has to be added to every strategy script. One of this function’s arguments is calc_on_every_tick. When this optional argument is true, the strategy calculates with every real-time price update. That makes it possible for the script to respond quicker to price action, and that way orders (or other actions) can be performed sooner. The default behaviour of TradingView strategies is to calculate on every bar’s close, and we get that behaviour by omitting the calc_on_every_tick argument from strategy() or by setting this argument to false. Now when a strategy calculates with every price update, it only does that on real-time data; when backtesting on historical bars the strategy still calculates on bar close. This means the performance of the strategy in real-time differs remarkably from its backtest performance. Because of that the only way to currently test a strategy that uses calc_on_every_tick=true is to have it run on real-time data.

Pine Script Language Tutorial (n.d.). Retrieved on February 24, 2016, from https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sCfC873xJEMV7MGzt1L70JTStTE9kcG2q-LDuWWkBeY/

TradingView (n.d.). Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on July 15, 2016, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Trading with every tick", overlay=true, calc_on_every_tick=true, pyramiding=7) // Inputs highestLen = input.int(15, title="Highest Length") lowestLen = input.int(15, title="Lowest Length") // Compute values highestHigh = ta.highest(high, highestLen)[1] lowestLow = ta.lowest(low, lowestLen)[1] timeFilter = year == 2016 and month == 7 and dayofmonth == 15 and time(timeframe.period, "830-1200") > 0 // Plot values plot(highestHigh, color=color.green) plot(lowestLow, color=color.red) bgcolor(timeFilter ? color.new(color.orange, 90) : na) // Submit orders if timeFilter and high > highestHigh strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if timeFilter and low < lowestLow strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

```pine_script
// Inputs highestLen = input.int(15, title="Highest Length") lowestLen = input.int(15, title="Lowest Length")
```

```pine_script
// Compute values highestHigh = ta.highest(high, highestLen)[1] lowestLow = ta.lowest(low, lowestLen)[1]
```

```pine_script
timeFilter = year == 2016 and month == 7 and dayofmonth == 15 and time(timeframe.period, "830-1200") > 0
```

```pine_script
// Plot values plot(highestHigh, color=color.green) plot(lowestLow, color=color.red) bgcolor(timeFilter ? color.new(color.orange, 90) : na)
```

```pine_script
// Submit orders if timeFilter and high > highestHigh strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if timeFilter and low < lowestLow strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

- In real time, subsequent orders execute at prices closely to each other. This happens because, when the strategy calculates with every price update, it has multiple opportunities to submit orders during that same bar. But on historical data the strategy calculates on bar close (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). And so there’s more time between orders then (namely, the duration of the price bar). The effect of orders close to each other in real-time versus more spaced in time during backtesting is more pronounced when the instrument has a high volatility or trends strongly, or when we trade a high time frame (like an hourly chart or higher).
- During a backtest, orders fill at places where an order wouldn’t fill during real-time trading. This happens because a strategy with calc_on_every_tick enabled can submit a bunch of orders quickly after each other in real time, and so any subsequent entry signals won’t be traded due to the position sizing limit. On historical bars the strategy won’t reach its pyramiding limit that quickly, and so can trade more different bars. One consequence is that during strong, profitable trends the strategy performs better in real-time than it did during backtesting. That’s because, with filling multiple orders per bar, our script can open a big position sooner at the beginning of the trend while during a backtest it’s likely still filling orders when the trend is already long underway.
- In real-time trading orders can fill at places where there wouldn’t be a trading signal during backtesting. This happens because during an intra-bar calculation an order condition can be true (like a close above a moving average). However, that order condition can be invalid when the bar closes. And so in real time the strategy would submit an order, but not during backtesting since the order condition wasn’t there when the bar closed.
- The more volatile the market, the bigger the difference between backtest and real-time performance. When there aren’t a lot of price updates happening with each price bar, our strategy has less calculation opportunities and there’s also less room for submitting a lot of orders quickly after each other. And when the market moves sideways, then it doesn’t matter a whole lot how quickly an order fills since the entry prices will be more or less the same (as opposed to when the market trends strongly or fluctuates wildly).
- The difference in real-time results and backtest performance is much less when the strategy doesn’t submit additional orders, when it only submits orders on bar close, or when the strategy’s pyramiding setting is turned off. This means our script’s characteristics (its code logic) and its settings strongly influence the extend to which there’s a big difference between real-time and backtest performance when calculating with every real-time price update.

lessons.title: Change the border size of a box with TradingView Pine Script code
lessons.clean_text:
# Change the border size of a box with TradingView Pine Script code

## Quick example

## Ways to use box.set_border_width()

## Example script

## Features of box.set_border_width()

## Summary

### References

After we make a box drawing, we change the width of that box’s border with the box.set_border_width() function [1] [2] . That new size applies to each of the box’s four borders.

The function’s default pattern is [1] :

To modify a box’s border size, we need a box drawing first. To make one we call the box.new() function:

Here we save the identifier that box.new() returns in a variable. We can then use that identifier with the box.set_border_width() function. For example:

This modifies the myBox box drawing. We give that box a border thickness of 3 pixels.

On the chart such a box looks like:

By the way, keyword arguments aren’t needed with the box.set_border_width() function. My examples just have them to make the code clearer. But you can omit them for less typing. This works without problem:

There are three ways to use the box.set_border_width() function:

In practice, we usually combine box.set_border_width() with a true/false condition. That modifies the box’s border size when a certain situation occurs, but leaves the border otherwise unchanged.

If our goal is to set the border size in every situation, so without a filter, then we can also define that size when we make the box with the box.new() function. That requires less typing and gives clearer code.

The code below shows how box.set_border_width() looks in a complete indicator. This script plots two moving averages. When they cross, we draw a box to highlight a range of possible values. We then also remove the border of the old box.

The indicator’s code is:

First we configure the indicator’s properties with the indicator() function. That gives the script a name and makes it appear on top of the chart’s instrument.

Then we calculate two Exponential Moving Averages with the ta.ema() function. We store the 20-bar average in the fastEMA variable. The slowEMA variable holds the value of the 80-bar EMA.

We plot both averages with the plot() function. The fast one shows in orange (color.orange), while the slower one appears in teal (color.teal). Since we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes a regular line plot for both.

Then we make the crossBox variable. This box variable persists its value from one bar to the next (var). We make it outside the if statement so we can properly reference its historical values.

Next we make the box and change its border width:

This if statement evaluates the ta.cross() function. That sees if the fast average crossed over or under the slow one. When that happens, the if code executes.

That code makes a box with the box.new() function. It’s length is between the current bar (bar_index) and 20 bars from now (bar_index + 20). The box’s height is 10% above the high (high * 1.1) and the same percentage below the low (low * 0.9).

For the background we apply transparency to the color.teal colour with the color.new() function. The initial border size is 3 pixels and that border shows in navy blue (color.navy).

After we make a new box, we also retire the old box. To do so we remove that box’s border. So we call the box.set_border_width() function. The first argument is crossBox[1], which returns the identifier of the previous box. And the width argument set to zero (0) removes the border.

On the price chart this script makes several boxes. Only the most recent one has a blue border. The ‘outdated’ are without a border:

```pine_script
box.set_border_width(id, width)
```

```pine_script
// Draw a box from the high 50 bars ago and // the low just 3 bars prior to the current bar myBox = box.new(left=bar_index[50], top=high[50], right=bar_index[3], bottom=low[3], bgcolor=na)
```

```pine_script
// Change the box's border width to 3 pixels box.set_border_width(id=myBox, width=3)
```

```pine_script
// Change the box's border width to 3 pixels box.set_border_width(myBox, 3)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Example of box border width", overlay=true) // Calculate two Exponential Moving Averages fastEMA = ta.ema(close, 20) slowEMA = ta.ema(close, 80) // Plot both moving averages as a regular line plot plot(series=fastEMA, color=color.orange, title="Fast EMA") plot(series=slowEMA, color=color.teal, title="Slow EMA") // Create the box drawing variable var box crossBox = na // When the moving averages cross, make new box if ta.cross(fastEMA, slowEMA) crossBox := box.new(left=bar_index, top=high * 1.1, right=bar_index + 20, bottom=low * 0.9, bgcolor=color.new(color.teal, 90), border_width=3, border_color=color.navy) // Disable border on the previous, 'outdated' box box.set_border_width(id=crossBox[1], width=0)
```

```pine_script
// When the moving averages cross, make new box if ta.cross(fastEMA, slowEMA) crossBox := box.new(left=bar_index, top=high * 1.1, right=bar_index + 20, bottom=low * 0.9, bgcolor=color.new(color.teal, 90), border_width=3, border_color=color.navy) // Disable border on the previous, 'outdated' box box.set_border_width(id=crossBox[1], width=0)
```

- id is the identifier of the box we like to change.This ID is returned by the box.new() function when we make the box.With that identifier we tell Pine Script which box to modify. Else Pine Script doesn’t know which box to change.
- This ID is returned by the box.new() function when we make the box.
- With that identifier we tell Pine Script which box to modify. Else Pine Script doesn’t know which box to change.
- width is the new size of the box’s four borders, in pixels.The default size for a box’s border is 1. This is also the smallest width possible.To turn off a box’s border, set its width to zero (0).
- The default size for a box’s border is 1. This is also the smallest width possible.
- To turn off a box’s border, set its width to zero (0).

- This ID is returned by the box.new() function when we make the box.
- With that identifier we tell Pine Script which box to modify. Else Pine Script doesn’t know which box to change.

- The default size for a box’s border is 1. This is also the smallest width possible.
- To turn off a box’s border, set its width to zero (0).

- Change the border thickness of a box created earlier.
- Disable the border of a box made earlier. We do that by setting its border width to zero (0).
- Enable a box’s border again by giving its width a value of 1 or higher.

- box.set_border_width() sets the width for all four box borders [1] . The function cannot give a particular border a certain width.
- When the box’s border colour is the same as its background colour, the box.set_border_width() function has no visual effect. In that case we have to give the box a different border colour.
- If we set a box’s border colour to na, the border is disabled. In that situation box.set_border_width() neither changes how the box looks. To fix that, give the box’s border a colour.
- Pine Script cannot retrieve a box’s width. To know that a box has a certain width, we call box.set_border_width() with that particular border size.

- The box.set_border_width() function changes the border size of a box.
- The default border size is 1 and higher values make the border thicker.
- When we set the border size to zero (0), the box doesn’t have a border anymore.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on September 13, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Drawings. Retrieved on September 13, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v4/essential/Drawings.html

lessons.title: See if TradingView uses hourly time frame with Pine Script
lessons.clean_text:
# See if TradingView uses hourly time frame with Pine Script

## Custom function

### Function characteristics

## Function examples

## Example script

## Summary

At this time, Pine Script lacks a variable that tells if code runs on a hourly time frame. But that information helps in several situations.

We may, for instance, have an intra-day strategy that doesn’t work well on low time frames. Or want to use different parameters on hourly intra-day charts than on minute time frames. Or simply have calculations that depend on a certain number of hours per bar.

So let’s write custom Pine Script code that finds hourly time frames.

To see if a script uses an hourly time frame, we combine two time frame variables:

For easy code reuse, this custom function combines the two variables to identify hourly time frames:

The IsHourlyTF() function returns true for time frames of 1 hour and up. That includes the 3 hour and 12 hour time frames, but also fractional hour time frames or 1:30 (90 minutes) and 2:20 (140 minutes). On minute time frames as well as day and higher resolutions, the function returns false.

The function’s code first checks timeframe.isminutes. This variable returns true on time frames made with minute data. On second, day, week, and month time frames it returns false.

Next we check timeframe.multiplier. This one returns the time frame’s period. With hourly charts made from minute data, that period got to be greater than or equal to (>=) 60.

We combine both expressions with the and operator. This returns true when the chart uses minute data and has a period of 60 or more. If both things aren’t so, the custom function returns false instead.

The IsHourlyTF() function has a couple features worth knowing:

With the IsHourlyTF() function copied to our script, there are several ways to use it. Let’s take a look.

To test if the script runs on an hourly chart, we evaluate if the function returns true. When it does, we can for instance make a text label:

Another option is to test if the chart is not an hourly time frame. To do that, we place the not operator before the function. For example:

Besides if statements, the function also works with switch statements and conditional operators. In fact, we can use it anywhere Pine Script can handle a true/false value.

Say we want to give hourly time frames a particular background colour. So we call the bgcolor() function. To pick between orange and blue, we have the conditional operator (?:) evaluate the IsHourlyTF() function like so:

Let’s see a full script use the IsHourlyTF() function. The indicator below draws a text label that says if the chart uses an hourly time frame (or not). When we change the chart’s time frame, the label changes with it.

The indicator’s code is:

We first call the indicator() function. That gives the script a name. And overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then we include the IsHourlyTF() function we talked about above.

Next an if statement evaluates barstate.islastconfirmedhistory. This variable is true on the chart’s last historical bar. This check makes the if statement’s code only run once, and not on every bar. That code does two things.

First an if/else statement determines the label’s text. The condition we use is the IsHourlyTF() function. When that one returns true, the text says the chart uses an hourly time frame. Otherwise, the else code tell the time frame is something else. We put the selected text in the labelText variable for use later.

The label.new() function then makes the label itself. This drawing shows two bars to the right (bar_index + 2) and at the bar’s midpoint price (hl2). Its style points to the lower left (label.style_label_lower_left). The label’s background is #D0F0C0, the hexadecimal colour for tea green. For the text we use the labelText variable, which shows in a large font (size.large).

Let’s see what the script does on the chart. On an hourly chart, the indicator identifies that time frame as such:

If we change the chart to a time frame above 1 hour, the indicator tells us as much:

```pine_script
// IsHourlyTF() returns 'true' if the script runs on data with an // hourly time frame (>= 60 minutes and below 1 day). Returns 'false' // otherwise. // Note: works on all chart types, including price-based ones. IsHourlyTF() => timeframe.isminutes and timeframe.multiplier >= 60
```

```pine_script
// Test if the script runs on an hourly time frame if IsHourlyTF() label.new(bar_index, high, text="This is an hourly chart!")
```

```pine_script
// Verify that the code doesn't run on an hourly-based time frame if not IsHourlyTF() label.new(bar_index, close, text="This chart doesn't use hourly data")
```

```pine_script
// Make the background of hourly charts orange. Use blue for other time frames. bgcolor(IsHourlyTF() ? color.new(color.orange, 90) : color.new(color.blue, 85))
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Hourly time frame", overlay=true) // IsHourlyTF() returns 'true' if the script runs on data with an // hourly time frame (>= 60 minutes and below 1 day). Returns 'false' // otherwise. // Note: works on all chart types, including price-based ones. IsHourlyTF() => timeframe.isminutes and timeframe.multiplier >= 60 // On the last historical bar, show if the chart uses an // hourly time frame or not if barstate.islastconfirmedhistory labelText = if IsHourlyTF() "This chart uses an\nhourly time frame!" else "This chart's is below 1 hour\nor 1 day and higher" label.new(bar_index + 2, hl2, style=label.style_label_lower_left, color=#D0F0C0, size=size.large, text=labelText)
```

- The timeframe.isminutes variable to identify minute data. TradingView makes hourly charts from this kind of data. So if a chart doesn’t use minute data, it cannot be an hourly time frame.
- And the timeframe.multiplier variable needs to be 60 or higher. Since hourly charts are made from minutes, their period has to be at least 60.

- The function also works on price-based charts. While those charts don’t have bars with a fixed time interval, TradingView still builds those charts from time-based data. And that data is what the timeframe.isminutes and timeframe.multiplier variables look at.So for Heikin Ashi, Renko, Line Break, Kagi, and Point & Figure charts, the function returns true or false based on the chart’s underlying data.On Range charts the function always returns false. TradingView makes those charts from 1-minute data, no matter how many ranges we configure. So they can’t use hourly data.
- So for Heikin Ashi, Renko, Line Break, Kagi, and Point & Figure charts, the function returns true or false based on the chart’s underlying data.
- On Range charts the function always returns false. TradingView makes those charts from 1-minute data, no matter how many ranges we configure. So they can’t use hourly data.
- IsHourlyTF() tells if the script runs on an hourly chart. But we can also pass this function to the request.security() function. When we do, we find out if that background data function calculates with hourly data.

- So for Heikin Ashi, Renko, Line Break, Kagi, and Point & Figure charts, the function returns true or false based on the chart’s underlying data.
- On Range charts the function always returns false. TradingView makes those charts from 1-minute data, no matter how many ranges we configure. So they can’t use hourly data.

- With two Pine Script variables we see if the script uses an hourly time frame.
- The timeframe.isminutes variable says if the time frame uses minute data. This is needed since TradingView makes hourly charts from minute data.
- And the timeframe.multiplier variable has to be 60 or higher. That’s how we differentiate between minute time frames and hourly ones.

lessons.title: How to get an open order’s entry time in TradingView Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to get an open order’s entry time in TradingView Pine Script?

## Default pattern

## Quick examples

## Reports bar time, not order time

## Features of strategy.opentrades.entry_time()

## Example strategy

## Summary

### References

When a Pine Script strategy has an open position, we get the entry time of a certain open order with the strategy.opentrades.entry_time() function [1] . This tells at which time and date a particular order opened.

Knowing an order’s entry time helps in several situations, such as:

The function has this standard pattern [1] :

The strategy.opentrades.entry_time() function returns one of two things:

There are several different ways to use the strategy.opentrades.entry_time() function.

To retrieve the entry time of the position’s initial order (that is, the oldest entry from all open orders), we use strategy.opentrades.entry_time() with an order number of zero:

And to get the time of the most recent entry order (meaning, the last opened order), we use strategy.opentrades.entry_time() with the strategy.opentrades - 1 expression as its order number:

Another option is to work with all open orders. To do so we loop over all open orders.

Say we want to know the average entry time. To find that out, we have a for loop go over all open orders. Inside the loop, we add each order’s strategy.opentrades.entry_time() value to a variable. When the loop is over, that variable holds the sum of all entry times. Then we divide with the number of open orders to get the average entry time.

This is how those steps look in Pine Script code:

An important strategy.opentrades.entry_time() feature: it doesn’t return the order’s exact entry time. What it does instead is return the time of the bar on which the order filled.

It depends whether that Pine Script approximation is a problem:

Pine Script generally overestimates how long an order is open. Say an order filled somewhere inside the bar. But strategy.opentrades.entry_time() only returns the bar’s opening time. That time, however, happened much earlier than when the order actually filled.

Let’s see a strategy use the strategy.opentrades.entry_time() function. The script below trades highest high and lowest low breakouts from the long and short side.

When the strategy has an open position, every 10 bars we calculate how many minutes passed since the position’s first entry. This tells how long ago the position opened.

The strategy’s full code is:

We first call the strategy() function. That names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then the ta.highest() function calculates the 20-bar highest high. The ta.lowest() function computes the lowest low over the same period.

Pine Script’s plot() function shows those extreme prices on the chart. The highest high appears in green (color.green) and the lows use red (color.red).

Next the strategy generates orders with two if statements. The first sees if the current bar’s high is above (>) the highest high. When it is, we call the strategy.entry() function to open a long trade (strategy.long).

The other if statement tests if the bar’s low (low) is beneath (<) the lowest low. When that’s so, strategy.entry() opens a short trade (strategy.short).

Then we report how long the current position is open:

This if statement checks two things. First it checks if the strategy has an open position. That’s the case when the strategy.position_size variable has a value different than (!=) zero (0). Next the modulus operator (%) test if the current bar number (bar_index) is a multiple of 10. (This happens once every ten bars, so our code doesn’t make a label on each bar.)

When both (and) things are true, code inside the if statement runs. This first calculates how many minutes passed since the first entry order. To get that value, we subtract the order’s entry time (strategy.opentrades.entry_time()) from the bar’s open time (time).

That returns a milliseconds amount. To make this value more practical we convert it to minutes. So we divide with how many milliseconds a minute has (60 * 1000).

Next the label.new() function makes a text label. This label shows on the current bar (bar_index, high). Its background is black (color.black) with white (color.white) letters. The label text tells the minutes since entry. Since those minutes are a number, while the label text requires a string, we convert it to text with the str.tostring() function.

On the chart, every 10 bars the strategy report the number of minutes since the initial entry. Here’s how that looks:

```pine_script
strategy.opentrades.entry_time(trade_num)
```

```pine_script
// Retrieve entry time of initial entry in open position firstEntryTime = strategy.opentrades.entry_time(0)
```

```pine_script
// Get entry time of last (most recent) trade entry lastEntryTime = strategy.opentrades.entry_time(strategy.opentrades - 1)
```

```pine_script
// Sum all entry times from the strategy's currently open trades entryTimeSum = 0 for tradeNumber = 0 to strategy.opentrades - 1 entryTimeSum += strategy.opentrades.entry_time(tradeNumber) // Calculate the average entry time (in milliseconds since January 1, 1970) avgEntryTime = entryTimeSum / strategy.opentrades
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Open trade entry time", overlay=true) // Calculate and plot the highest high and lowest low highestHigh = ta.highest(high, 20)[1] lowestLow = ta.lowest(low, 20)[1] plot(highestHigh, color=color.green, title="Highest High") plot(lowestLow, color=color.red, title="Lowest Low") // Generate the breakout trades if high > highestHigh strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long, stop=close) if low < lowestLow strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short) // With an open position, show the minutes since initial entry every 10 bars if strategy.position_size != 0 and bar_index % 10 == 0 minutesSinceEntry = (time - strategy.opentrades.entry_time(0)) / (60 * 1000) label.new(bar_index, high, color=color.black, textcolor=color.white, text=str.tostring(minutesSinceEntry) + " minutes\nsince entry")
```

```pine_script
// With an open position, show the minutes since initial entry every 10 bars if strategy.position_size != 0 and bar_index % 10 == 0 minutesSinceEntry = (time - strategy.opentrades.entry_time(0)) / (60 * 1000) label.new(bar_index, high, color=color.black, textcolor=color.white, text=str.tostring(minutesSinceEntry) + " minutes\nsince entry")
```

- See how much time ago the position’s first entry order happened. Or measure how much time passed since the most recent entry.
- Calculate the amount of time between successive open entry orders.
- Compute the profit, drawdown, and run up for all open orders entered more than a certain number of hours ago.

- trade_num is the open order number. This integer tells Pine Script from which open order to return the entry time.This trade number is zero-based. The order opened first has number 0, the second open order 1, the third 2, and so on.With strategy.opentrades - 1 we get the strategy’s current last order number. We use this count to loop over open orders and to get the most recent open order.
- This trade number is zero-based. The order opened first has number 0, the second open order 1, the third 2, and so on.
- With strategy.opentrades - 1 we get the strategy’s current last order number. We use this count to loop over open orders and to get the most recent open order.

- This trade number is zero-based. The order opened first has number 0, the second open order 1, the third 2, and so on.
- With strategy.opentrades - 1 we get the strategy’s current last order number. We use this count to loop over open orders and to get the most recent open order.

- A time value that represents the date and time of day that the order opened. Pine Script reports times as the number of milliseconds since 00:00:00 UTC on January 1, 1970.
- Or otherwise the na value. The function returns this value when:The order number is incorrect. A faulty number is below zero (0) or greater than the current number of open orders (that is, higher than the value of strategy.opentrades - 1).There are no open trades at the moment.
- The order number is incorrect. A faulty number is below zero (0) or greater than the current number of open orders (that is, higher than the value of strategy.opentrades - 1).
- There are no open trades at the moment.

- The order number is incorrect. A faulty number is below zero (0) or greater than the current number of open orders (that is, higher than the value of strategy.opentrades - 1).
- There are no open trades at the moment.

- When the order filled at the bar’s open, strategy.opentrades.entry_time() gives an accurate measure of the entry time. (Since TradingView’s bar timestamps are based on the bar’s open time.)
- If the order filled somewhere inside the bar, strategy.opentrades.entry_time() doesn’t return the actual entry time. Instead we get an estimate, which is less precise on high time frames (because bars take a long time on such a time frame).

- strategy.opentrades.entry_time() returns time as a number of milliseconds. That’s usually not a format we easily work with. To make this time amount more practical, we convert it to seconds, minutes, hours, or days.
- The time that strategy.opentrades.entry_time() returns is in the UTC time zone. This is in contrast with Pine Script’s variables and functions for working with time, which almost all return data in the instrument’s time zone.
- When several entry orders happened during the same bar, those orders all have the same strategy.opentrades.entry_time() value. This even happens when orders filled at different points in the bar (and thus cannot have the same time).
- strategy.opentrades.entry_time() is available since Pine Script version 5 [see 1] . This function isn’t in version 4 and earlier [2] . When our script code begins with the //@version=4 comment (or with a lower version number), then we can’t use this function.
- We cannot use strategy.opentrades.entry_time() in indicators. When we try, Pine Script generates the ‘cannot use strategy functions (strategy.opentrades.entry_time) in indicator script’ error.

- The strategy.opentrades.entry_time() function returns the entry time of a particular open order.
- An order number tells the function from which order to return the time. The first open order uses 0, the last (most recent) order strategy.opentrades - 1.
- strategy.opentrades.entry_time() returns time as a milliseconds count since a certain point in the UTC time zone.
- The function doesn’t return the order’s exact entry time. Instead it makes an estimate with the bar’s open time.

1. TradingView (n.d. a). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on May 10, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView (n.d. b). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on May 10, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/

lessons.title: How to create a transparent colour in TradingView?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to create a transparent colour in TradingView?

## Solution: generate transparent colours with color.new()

## Example: transparent colours based on the day of week

## Discussion: examples and using color.new()

### Exploring transparent colours generated by color.new()

### All TradingView functions that use transparent colours

### References

Situation: You want to know how to create a transparent colour from any standard TradingView colour or hexadecimal colour value.

We generate transparent colours in TradingView with the color.new() function (TradingView Wiki, 2016). This function has the following syntax (TradingView, n.d.):

The color.new() function works with two arguments. The first is a standard colour or hexadecimal colour value. The second is an integer with the amount of transparency we want. That value ranges from 0 (no transparency for a solid colour) to 100 for a fully transparent, invisible colour.

Currently, the second argument of the color.new() function has to be a literal integer (like 20, 45, or 70). This means we cannot use color.new() with a computed variable or an input variable. And so for now we specify the transparency by typing it directly into the code of our indicator or strategy.

Based on the kind of colour, there are two ways to use color.new():

Approach 1. A straightforward way to use color.new() is with one of the 17 different standard TradingView colours. For instance, this makes the teal standard colour 80% transparent:

Approach 2. The color.new() function can also change the transparency of the millions of hexadecimal colour values. For example, this returns the coral colour (#FF7F50) with 20% transparency:

The example indicator below plots a 12-bar RSI (Relative Strength Index) in red against a teal chart background.

With the color.new() function those colours get a transparency of 40% or 85% based on the day of week. That is, on Monday and Friday we use a low transparency to make the indicator’s plot more pronounced with solid colours. On the other days, when we aren’t that interested in the RSI values, we use a much higher transparency.

When we add this example indicator to a 30-minute Dow Jones Industrial Average chart, it looks like:

To get an idea of the transparency effect created by the color.new() function, let’s look at different transparencies for the color.red, color.green, and color.blue standard TradingView colours. After that we discuss the functions that can use transparent colours.

First off, the different shades of red that the color.new() function generates look like:

And the color.green standard colour with different transparencies looks like:

And the shades of the color.blue basic colour created by color.new() appear as:

To show the transparent colour generated by color.new(), we’ll need to use a function that applies that colour to the chart. In our indicator and strategy scripts, we can use color.new() with the following functions (TradingView, n.d.):

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on June 10, 2017, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

TradingView Wiki (2016, December 27). Transparency in pine scripts. Retrieved on June 10, 2017, from https://www.tradingview.com/wiki/Transparency_in_pine_scripts

```pine_script
color.new(color, transp)
```

```pine_script
color.new(teal, 80)
```

```pine_script
color.new(#FF7F50, 20)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Recipe - transparent colours", overlay=false) monFri = dayofweek == dayofweek.monday or dayofweek == dayofweek.friday // Determine the different plot colours bgColour = monFri ? color.new(color.teal, 40) : color.new(color.teal, 85) plotColour = monFri ? color.new(color.red, 40) : color.new(color.red, 85) // Plot the RSI and colour the background plot(ta.rsi(close, 12), color=plotColour, linewidth=2) bgcolor(bgColour)
```

| TradingView code | Example colour value |
| --- | --- |
| color.new(red, 0) (no transparency) |  |
| color.new(red, 10) |  |
| color.new(red, 20) |  |
| color.new(red, 30) |  |
| color.new(red, 40) |  |
| color.new(red, 50) |  |
| color.new(red, 60) |  |
| color.new(red, 70) |  |
| color.new(red, 80) |  |
| color.new(red, 90) |  |
| color.new(red, 100) (full transparency) |  |

| TradingView code | Example colour value |
| --- | --- |
| color.new(green, 0) (no transparency) |  |
| color.new(green, 10) |  |
| color.new(green, 20) |  |
| color.new(green, 30) |  |
| color.new(green, 40) |  |
| color.new(green, 50) |  |
| color.new(green, 60) |  |
| color.new(green, 70) |  |
| color.new(green, 80) |  |
| color.new(green, 90) |  |
| color.new(green, 100) (full transparency) |  |

| TradingView code | Example colour value |
| --- | --- |
| color.new(blue, 0) (no transparency) |  |
| color.new(blue, 10) |  |
| color.new(blue, 20) |  |
| color.new(blue, 30) |  |
| color.new(blue, 40) |  |
| color.new(blue, 50) |  |
| color.new(blue, 60) |  |
| color.new(blue, 70) |  |
| color.new(blue, 80) |  |
| color.new(blue, 90) |  |
| color.new(blue, 100) (full transparency) |  |

| Function | Description |
| --- | --- |
| barcolor() | Sets the colour of the price bars from the chart’s instrument. |
| bgcolor() | Sets the background of price bars or the background of the indicator’s plot background. |
| fill() | Fills the background between two plots or two horizontal lines with a certain colour. |
| hline() | Draws a horizontal line at a fixed price level on the chart in a certain colour. |
| plot() | Plots a series of data on the chart in a specified colour. |
| plotarrow() | Draws an up or down arrow on the chart. |
| plotbar() | Plots an OHLC bar on the chart. |
| plotcandle() | Plots a candlestick on the chart. |
| plotchar() | Displays a specified character on the price chart. |
| plotshape() | Displays a visual shape (like flag, cross, or arrow) on the price chart. |

lessons.title: How to add an element to the start of a Pine Script array?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to add an element to the start of a Pine Script array?

## Quick example

## Step-by-step array.unshift() example

## Ways to use array.unshift()

## Example script

## Features of array.unshift()

## Summary

### References

After we make an array, we use the array.unshift() function to add a new element to the start of the array [1] [2] .

Each time we execute this function, Pine Script increases the array’s size with 1 and adds a new element at the start of the array.

The function has this default pattern [1] :

To perform its task, array.unshift() needs to know which array to modify and what value to add.

When we made a floating-point array earlier and stored its identifier in the myArray variable, then we add fractional values to the array’s start like so:

Just so you know, the id and value keyword arguments are optional. My examples use them to clarify what the code does. But you’re not required to use them. This code works just as good:

So how does the array.unshift() function work in practice? Let’s look at a step-by-step example.

Before we can add a new element to the start of an array, we make an array first:

Here the array.new_int() function makes an integer array. We put the array identifier in the myArray variable for access later.

Since we didn’t specify an initial array size, the array starts empty:

Let’s change that by adding three values:

Here we call Pine Script’s array.push() function three times to populate the array. That function adds a new value to the array’s end.

Because we call that function with values of 1, 2, and 3, the array’s content is:

Now let’s add a new element to the array’s start:

Here the array.unshift() function adds the value of 4 to the start of the myArray array. Since there are already elements in the array, those existing elements move one spot to the right.

That means the new value appears in front of the original numbers:

Let’s add another element with array.unshift() and see what happens:

This time array.unshift() adds the value of 5 to the myArray array.

That operation changes the array to:

Now 5 is the first element in the array. The elements already in the array are moved one step to the right.

That also changed the index of those existing elements. Before this step, the 4 element had an index of 0. Now with 5 added to the start of the array, 4 moved to index 1. If our script still thinks it can find 4 at index 0, it’s in for a surprise!

Let’s add yet another element with array.unshift():

This time we call array.unshift() with a value of 6. Pine Script then adds that value to the start of the array:

Note how the array elements appear in reverse order. First array.unshift() added 4, then 5, and finally 6. Yet their array order is 6, 5, 4.

That’s because each time we execute array.unshift(), Pine Script adds the new element in front of all other elements.

With the array.unshift() function we increase the array’s size and add a new element to the start of the array. That’s something we’ll use in several scripts.

But other than that, there aren’t other situations where we use the array.unshift() function.

Let’s see how the array.unshift() function works in a complete script. The indicator below collects, on each bar, the range of 3 recent bars in an array.

We add those ranges with the array.unshift() function. On the chart’s last bar, we output the array as well as its average.

The script’s code is:

First we configure the indicator with the indicator() function. That names the script and places it on top of the chart’s instrument.

Then we make the array:

Here the array.new_float() function makes a new floating-point (fractional values) array. Since we don’t set an initial size, we get an empty array. The array’s identifier we store in the barRanges variable for later.

Note that this array isn’t persistent (which requires the var keyword). As a result, the array is created again on each bar (and its content doesn’t carry over from one bar to the next).

Then we fill the array:

To add array values we use the array.unshift() function. Each time we call this function with two values. The first is the barRanges variable, which holds the array identifier. The other is Pine Script’s ta.tr variable, which returns the true range of the bar.

Here we use that variable to get the current bar’s range, as well as the ranges of the previous bar (ta.tr[1]) and two bars ago (ta.tr[2]).

Since array.unshift() adds an element to the start of the array each time, it adds those elements in the opposite order that the array.unshift() statements appear. In the code, we first added ta.tr[2], then ta.tr[1], and finally ta.tr. But the array’s content has the values like so: [ ta.tr, ta.tr[1], ta.tr[2] ].

Next we show the array on the chart:

This if statement checks barstate.islast. That variable is true on each of the chart’s last price bars. When the script processes one of those bars, the if statement’s code runs.

That code makes a new text label with the label.new() function. We place this label on the current bar’s high (bar_index, high). It points left (label.style_label_left) and has a black (color.black) background. Its text appears in white (color.white) with a large font (size.large).

The label’s text combines literal strings with two str.tostring() function calls. That function converts values to text, which makes those values compatible with the label’s other text pieces. The first of those calls displays the content of the barRanges array.

The second call shows the average range. We get that value from the array with the array.avg() function. Here we also use the "0.00" format string. That has str.tostring() convert the average to a number with two decimal digits.

When we run the script on the chart, it draws a label with the range of the 3 most recent bars. The first element is the current bar’s range, the second element the previous bar, and the last array element is the range from 2 bars ago. The indicator also shows the average range for those bars:

```pine_script
array.unshift(id, value)
```

```pine_script
// Add 1.5, 2.5, and 3.5 to the array whose // identifier is stored in the 'myArray' variable array.unshift(id=myArray, value=1.5) array.unshift(id=myArray, value=2.5) array.unshift(id=myArray, value=3.5)
```

```pine_script
// Add 1.5, 2.5, and 3.5 to 'myArray' array array.unshift(myArray, 1.5) // now without the 'id=' and 'value=' part array.unshift(myArray, 2.5) array.unshift(myArray, 3.5)
```

```pine_script
// Create an array that can hold integers myArray = array.new_int()
```

```pine_script
[ ]
```

```pine_script
// Add three new values to the array array.push(id=myArray, value=1) array.push(id=myArray, value=2) array.push(id=myArray, value=3)
```

```pine_script
[1, 2, 3]
```

```pine_script
// Add a value to the start of the array array.unshift(id=myArray, value=4)
```

```pine_script
[4, 1, 2, 3]
```

```pine_script
// Add another value to the array's beginning array.unshift(id=myArray, value=5)
```

```pine_script
[5, 4, 1, 2, 3]
```

```pine_script
// And put yet another value before all other array elements array.unshift(id=myArray, value=6)
```

```pine_script
[6, 5, 4, 1, 2, 3]
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Basic example of 'array.unshift()'", overlay=true) // Make an array that can hold floating-point values barRanges = array.new_float() // Add the range of recent bars to the array, in // reverse order (last added element gets position 0) array.unshift(id=barRanges, value=ta.tr[2]) array.unshift(id=barRanges, value=ta.tr[1]) array.unshift(id=barRanges, value=ta.tr) // Display array for verification if barstate.islast label.new(x=bar_index, y=high, style=label.style_label_left, color=color.black, textcolor=color.white, size=size.large, text="Last 3 bar ranges:\n(new to old)\n\n" + str.tostring(barRanges) + "\n\nAverage:\n" + str.tostring(array.avg(id=barRanges), "0.00"))
```

```pine_script
// Make an array that can hold floating-point values barRanges = array.new_float()
```

```pine_script
// Add the range of recent bars to the array, in // reverse order (last added element gets position 0) array.unshift(id=barRanges, value=ta.tr[2]) array.unshift(id=barRanges, value=ta.tr[1]) array.unshift(id=barRanges, value=ta.tr)
```

```pine_script
// Display array for verification if barstate.islast label.new(x=bar_index, y=high, style=label.style_label_left, color=color.black, textcolor=color.white, size=size.large, text="Last 3 bar ranges:\n(new to old)\n\n" + str.tostring(barRanges) + "\n\nAverage:\n" + str.tostring(array.avg(id=barRanges), "0.00"))
```
