- id is the identifier of the array to add a new element.This ID value is returned by the function that made the array, like one of the array.new_*() functions.That identifier tells Pine Script which array we want to modify. Without the ID, Pine Script doesn’t understand what array to change.
- This ID value is returned by the function that made the array, like one of the array.new_*() functions.
- That identifier tells Pine Script which array we want to modify. Without the ID, Pine Script doesn’t understand what array to change.
- value is the new element to add to the array’s start.This can be a bool, color, float, int, or string value, but also a box, label, line, table value.The type of element we add has to match the array’s type. If we made an integer array, then we can only add whole numbers to the array. We cannot mix types; an integer array cannot get a colour or table value, for instance.
- This can be a bool, color, float, int, or string value, but also a box, label, line, table value.
- The type of element we add has to match the array’s type. If we made an integer array, then we can only add whole numbers to the array. We cannot mix types; an integer array cannot get a colour or table value, for instance.

- This ID value is returned by the function that made the array, like one of the array.new_*() functions.
- That identifier tells Pine Script which array we want to modify. Without the ID, Pine Script doesn’t understand what array to change.

- This can be a bool, color, float, int, or string value, but also a box, label, line, table value.
- The type of element we add has to match the array’s type. If we made an integer array, then we can only add whole numbers to the array. We cannot mix types; an integer array cannot get a colour or table value, for instance.

- array.unshift() adds a new array element at index zero (0). Any existing elements in the array are shifted to the right. That increases their index with one. If your code depends on certain elements having a specific index, then array.unshift() will impact that code.
- Each time our script calls array.unshift(), Pine Script automatically increases the array size with 1, shifts the existing elements to the right by one, and add the new element to the start of the array [2] . This all happens automatically; we don’t have to worry about the array’s size and capacity.
- There is, however, a maximum array size of 100,000 elements. When an array is at that size, and we call array.unshift() to add one more element, TradingView generates the ‘array is too long’ error.
- Since array.unshift() adds a new element to the start of the array, elements are added in reverse order. Meaning, the element we add last will be the array’s first element. To add elements in chronological order we use the array.push() function.

- The array.unshift() function adds a new element to the start of an existing array.
- The type of element we add has to match the array’s type. To a Boolean array we can only add true/false values. And likewise we cannot add a line to a colour array.
- When array.unshift() adds a new element, the existing elements move one spot to the right. This affects code that relies on certain elements being at a certain index.
- array.unshift() automatically makes the array bigger for the new element, up to a maximum of 100,000 elements.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on October 7, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Arrays. Retrieved on October 7, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/language/Arrays.html

lessons.title: How to highlight TradingView alert setups with text and shapes?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to highlight TradingView alert setups with text and shapes?

## Show alerts on the TradingView chart with shapes and text

## 3 steps that make shapes and text highlight alerts

### Step 1: Create a Boolean true/false variable with the alert setup

### Step 2: Make a shape with text based on that true/false variable

### Step 3: Use the true/false variable with alertcondition()

## Example: highlight TradingView alerts with shapes and text

## Other options that highlight TradingView alerts

## Summary

### References

Alert conditions we code with alertcondition() don’t show on the chart. That makes it hard to see if the script’s code identifies alert setups successfully. But we can highlight alerts ourselves. Let’s see how we use TradingView’s shapes and text for that.

With TradingView’s alertcondition() function we can code alert conditions in our script. Then when our script runs on the chart we activate those alert conditions when we enable and configure alerts by hand. From that point on whenever the alert setup happens we get an alert pop-up, email, and/or SMS message.

But alertcondition() has one noteworthy limitation: this function does not show alerts on the chart [1] [2] . That’s a shame for several reasons. It makes it hard to validate our code and see if the alert happens when we expect it to. We cannot see how changed script settings affect alert frequency. And we don’t know when and where our alert would have triggered.

But there’s also good news: with a small bit of extra code we can highlight alert setups ourselves. This way we do get a visual highlight that shows us when and where the alert condition happened. One way to make those highlights is to draw shapes and text on the chart. Let’s see how we do that.

To highlight an alert on the chart with a shape and text we take three actions:

First we make a Boolean variable that holds true when our alert setup occurs. And that is false when the setup didn’t happen during the current bar calculation. When we store an alert setup in a variable, we can be sure that both the plotted shape and alert condition happen at the same time.

The code for this step can look like:

Next we use plotshape(), a function that draws a shape and optionally text on the chart [1] . To make that shape appear on the same bar as the alert setup, we set the function’s first argument to the Boolean variable from step 1. With the text argument of plotshape() we specify which text goes along the shape.

An example of this step is:

Then we code the actual alert condition in our script. We do that with the alertcondition() function. To make that alert trigger at the same time as our plotted shape, we use the Boolean variable that holds the alert setup with alertcondition().

That basically looks like:

There’s a difference between real-time and historical alert conditions. In real-time, our indicator processes every price update in the last bar of the chart. But on historical bars the script only calculates once per bar, on bar close.

That difference gives us more real-time alerts than historical bars suggest. Whether this makes your historical alert highlights inaccurate depends on the manual alert settings. If the alert is set to fire intra-bar, then it will fire more often on real-time data than historical data suggests. But if the alert only fires on bar close, historical and real-time alert setups match.

Now let’s see how we apply the above three steps in a complete indicator.

The indicator below generates two breakout alerts. The first happens when the prices cross above the 20-bar highest close. The other alert is a volume alert, and triggers when volume gets above the 10-bar highest volume. We plot shapes with text on the chart to see when and where these alerts could trigger.

Here’s how the example indicator looks on the chart:

This is the code of the full indicator:

Let’s see what the code does. First we define indicator properties with the indicator() function:

Next we make variables that hold our alert setups:

We define two Boolean variables here. The first, priceBreakout, holds true whenever the close is above the 20-bar highest close. To code that we check if the bar’s closing price (close) is greater than (>) the highest close. We compute that latter with the ta.highest() function, which we run here on close prices for 20 bars.

The second true/false variable, volumeBreakout, is true when volume is above the 10-bar volume high. For this we compare whether volume is greater than ta.highest(volume, 10)[1]. That latter piece of code gets us the 10-bar highest volume reading.

Notice how we use the history referencing operator ([]) with a value of 1 (so [1]) with both ta.highest() functions. We do that to get the highest close and volume of the previous bars, without including the current bar’s data. This way we can, for instance, check if the close is above the highest close. That wouldn’t be possible if the highest close already contained the current bar’s data.

Then we highlight the indicator’s alert setups:

We annotate the alert setups with plotshape(). Whether that function plots shapes and their text depends on its first argument. When that argument is true, a shape appears; if we set it to a false value, nothing gets plotted.

Here with the first plotshape() function call we show a shape for price breakouts (priceBreakout). The shape we use is a green-coloured flag (shape.flag). With the text argument set to "Price\nbreakout" we show ‘Price breakout’ on the chart. The \n piece in that string is a newline character. That way we get an Enter between both words.

The other plotshape() statement shows orange-coloured diamonds (shape.diamond) when volume is above the 10-bar high (volumeBreakout). The blue text we show with these shapes is ‘Volume breakout’, and both the shape and text appear below the bar (location.belowbar).

Now that we got the shapes coded we create the alerts with the alertcondition() function:

We make two alert conditions based on the Boolean variables we made earlier. The first alertcondition() statement creates an alert for when priceBreakout is true. When the closing price is indeed higher than the 20-bar highest close, we trigger an alert message that says so.

The other alertcondition() function call makes an alert for when volume is above the 10-bar high. For that we use the volumeBreakout Boolean variable. Note that, before these alert conditions actually trigger alerts, we first got to enable and configure the script’s alerts.

Plotting shapes and text on the chart isn’t the only way to highlight TradingView alerts. Here are other options we have:

The alerts we highlight with the above approaches also have another benefit: with them we get an ‘output function’ in our code. TradingView requires that all indicators plot or colour something. But alertcondition() does neither. And so a script that only creates alerts triggers TradingView’s ‘script must have at least one output function call’ error. We prevent that error if we plot or colour something.

The alert conditions we code with the alertcondition() function don’t show on the chart. That makes it hard to test alert setups. And it doesn’t give us an idea of how often an alert fires.

However, we can annotate alert setups ourselves with shapes and text. That has three required steps. First we make a Boolean variable that holds our alert setup. Then we use that variable with TradingView’s plotshape() function. That function can plot a shape (like a flag, arrow, label, or diamond) as well as text on the chart. Next we use that same true/false variable with the alertcondition() function. Thanks to that variable both the shape and alert happen at the same time.

Note that there might be a difference between real-time and historical alert setups. That happens because on historical data a script calculates once per bar, on bar close. But in real-time each price update gets processed. Whether this makes your historical alert highlights underestimate the alert frequency depends on your manual alert settings.

```pine_script
emaCross = ta.crossover(ta.ema(close, 10), ta.ema(close, 30))
```

```pine_script
plotshape(emaCross, style=shape.flag, text="EMA cross")
```

```pine_script
alertcondition(condition=emaCross, message="EMA crossover!")
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Highlighting alerts with shapes and text", overlay=true) // Determine alert conditions priceBreakout = close > ta.highest(close, 20)[1] volumeBreakout = volume > ta.highest(volume, 10)[1] // Plot shapes on the chart plotshape(priceBreakout, style=shape.flag, color=color.green, text="Price\nbreakout") plotshape(volumeBreakout, style=shape.diamond, color=color.orange, location=location.belowbar, text="Volume\nbreakout", textcolor=color.blue) // Create alert conditions alertcondition(condition=priceBreakout, title="Price breakout", message="Closing price is above 20-bar highest close") alertcondition(condition=volumeBreakout, title="Volume breakout", message="Volume above the 10-bar highest volume")
```

```pine_script
indicator(title="Highlighting alerts with shapes and text", overlay=true)
```

```pine_script
// Determine alert conditions priceBreakout = close > ta.highest(close, 20)[1] volumeBreakout = volume > ta.highest(volume, 10)[1]
```

```pine_script
// Plot shapes on the chart plotshape(priceBreakout, style=shape.flag, color=color.green, text="Price\nbreakout") plotshape(volumeBreakout, style=shape.diamond, color=color.orange, location=location.belowbar, text="Volume\nbreakout", textcolor=color.blue)
```

```pine_script
// Create alert conditions alertcondition(condition=priceBreakout, title="Price breakout", message="Closing price is above 20-bar highest close") alertcondition(condition=volumeBreakout, title="Volume breakout", message="Volume above the 10-bar highest volume")
```

- We can colour the chart background for alert setups.
- Or we just colour price bars for alerts.
- A more subtle way is to plot a text character for alert conditions.
- Another option is to highlight alert setups with plotted shapes.
- Instead of plotting shapes and text, as we did in this article, we can also show text on the chart for alert setups.
- Or we simply highlight alerts with up and down arrows.
- And with TradingView’s plot() function we can plot alert trigger levels on the chart.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on August 17, 2018, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/
1. TradingView Wiki (2018, June 29). Alerts in pine. Retrieved on August 8, 2018, from https://www.tradingview.com/wiki/Alerts_in_pine

lessons.title: How to limit a number to a range of values in Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to limit a number to a range of values in Pine Script?

## Set a minimum value

### Custom function

### Quick examples

### Example script

## Set a maximum value

### Custom function

### Quick examples

### Example script

## Set a range (minimum to maximum)

### Custom function

### Quick examples

### Example script

## Learn more

TradingView indicators and strategies analyse charts by computing all kinds of numerical values. But we don’t always want to use any possible value for a number. Sometimes a range that limits the number works best.

There are three ways to limit the range of a Pine Script number:

Let’s see how we code these limits.

When we give a number a minimum value, we implement a lower bound on its values. The number will then always be at, or above, a certain level. It won’t be able to have values less than the minimum.

This custom ApplyMin() function applies a minimum value to a given number:

The ApplyMin() function has two arguments. value specifies the number to which we want to add the minimum range. And minimum sets what the lowest acceptable value should be.

Inside the function, the math.max() function returns the highest value of either the given value or else its defined minimum. This makes the function return a new number that’s never less than the specified minimum.

Let’s see how we use the function. Say we want to calculate the change in close prices, but not get negative values. In that case we call ApplyMin() like so:

If we need to calculate with the strategy’s net profit, but not use a value below 250, we use ApplyMin() in this way:

We can also use another variable or expression as the minimum.

Let’s assume that we calculate an order size with the Average True Range (ATR). But we don’t want to use an ATR less than 15 ticks. In that case we call ApplyMin() like so:

The indicator below shows how we can use the ApplyMin() function in a script. The code calculates the 1-bar volume change. The custom function makes that volume change never go below zero. When we plot those values, we can easily recognise bars with high changes in volume.

The script’s code is:

First we configure the indicator with Pine Script’s indicator() function. That names the script and formats the plot values as volume numbers, with ‘K’ and ‘M’ to signal thousands and millions.

Next we include the ApplyMin() function we discussed above.

Then we make the volumeChange variable and limit its value with the ApplyMin() function. We give that function two values. The first is the minimum (0). The other the 1-bar volume change. To get that value we subtract the previous bar’s volume (volume[1]) from the current bar’s volume (volume).

The plot() function then shows the volume change on the chart. We make that volumeChange variable appear as a columns plot (plot.style_columns).

When this script runs on the chart, its plot only shows bars with positive volume changes:

When we give a number a maximum value, we add an upper bound on its values. That makes the number always at, or below, some level. Its values cannot be more than the given maximum anymore.

This ApplyMax() custom function applies a maximum value to a given number:

There are two arguments with this ApplyMax() function. value specifies the number to which we want to add the maximum range. And maximum sets what the highest acceptable value should be.

Inside the function, the math.min() function returns the smallest value of either the given value or else its defined maximum. This makes the function return a new number that’s never more than the specified maximum.

Let’s see how we can use the ApplyMax() function. If we’re interested in the 1-bar close change, but without positive values, we use the function like this:

Or let’s say set a cross level for the momentum oscillator with an input, but not use a crossover level higher than 5. In that situation we use ApplyMax() in this way:

When we calculate a position size, we may not want to trade more than 500 shares. To either trade 1.5% of equity or not more than that limit, we call ApplyMax() like so:

The maximum value doesn’t need to be a literal value. We can also calculate the maximum or use a variable.

Say our stop distance should be the distance between the previous and current low, but not be more than 20 ticks. To make such a maximum we use ApplyMax() like this:

Let’s explore how a script can use the ApplyMax() function. The indicator below calculates the 1-bar volume change. With ApplyMax() the script applies a maximum value of 0. When the plot shows those values on the chart, it brings attention to bars where the volume change is negative.

The script’s entire code is:

First we call the indicator() function to configure the indicator. That gives the script a name. And makes the plot formatting appear as volume, with ‘K’ for thousands and ‘M’ for millions.

Then we include the ApplyMax() function we discussed above.

Next we calculate the one-bar volume change. We limit that value with ApplyMax(). So, inside that function, we subtract the last bar’s volume (volume[1]) from the current bar’s volume (volume). With 0 we tell ApplyMax() to not return a value above zero. We put that returned value in the volumeChange variable.

Then the plot() function shows that variable on the chart. We make the 1-bar volume difference show with a columns plot (plot.style_columns).

When the script runs on the chart, none of the volume changes get above the maximum of 0:

When we give a number a range, we add an upper and lower bound on its values. The number will then always be at, or above, the minimum, but never more than its maximum.

This custom ApplyRange() function applies a minimum and maximum to a given number:

This function has three arguments. value is the number we want to apply a range too. minimum and maximum define what its lowest and highest acceptable value should be.

The function’s code places the math.max() function inside the math.min() function. Let’s look at math.max() first. Here that function returns the highest value of either the given number or else its minimum. This ensures the number isn’t below the minimum.

Pine Script then uses the result of math.max() with the math.min() function. That latter returns the lowest of either the given maximum or else what math.max() returned. This way we don’t get a value higher than the maximum.

Because of the two filters, the ApplyRange() can return three possible values: the number unchanged, the minimum, or the maximum. Regardless of which value the function gives us, the number is now range bound between the minimum and maximum. And, as a result, we removed outliers from that number.

Let’s explore some examples. Say our script calculates the Average True Range (perhaps to size orders). But we don’t want an ATR that’s too high or too low. To use a minimum of 5 and a maximum of 25, we call the ApplyRange() function like so:

Or assume we calculate a position size with 1% of strategy equity. But we want to trade at least 10 shares, and definitely not more than 2,500. To get that range of values we use ApplyRange() like this:

It’s also possible to set the minimum and maximum with variables or calculate these limits.

Say we want to use the highest high and lowest low to set a profit target distance. But we don’t want a target less then 20 ticks or more than 100 ticks away. To get a target distance in that range we do:

Let’s see how a script uses the ApplyRange() function. The indicator below calculates the 1-bar volume change. To not skew the chart with outliers, the script limits the range of volume differences to a minimum of -10,000 and a maximum of 10,000.

The script’s code is:

First we configure the indicator with the indicator() function. That names the script. The format.volume value formats plot numbers as volume, with ‘K’ and ‘M’ suffixes for thousands and millions.

Then we copy/paste the custom ApplyRange() function we discussed above.

Next we make the volumeChange variable and limit its value with the ApplyRange() function. We give that function three arguments. The first is the single bar volume change, for which we subtract the previous bar’s volume (volume[1]) from the current bar’s volume (volume). The second and third arguments are the minimum of -10000 and maximum of 10000.

Then we plot that variable with the plot() function. This makes the 1-bar volume difference show as a columns plot (plot.style_columns).

When we add the script to the chart, its volume changes are neatly limited to the -10k to 10k range:

```pine_script
// ApplyMin() applies the given minimum to the range // of the specified value. Returns value unchanged // when it's above the minimum. ApplyMin(value, minimum) => math.max(minimum, value)
```

```pine_script
// Compute the 1-bar close change, with a minimum value of 0 closeChange = ApplyMin(0, close - close[1])
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the strategy's net profit, with a minimum of 250 netProfit = ApplyMin(250, strategy.netprofit)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the 14-bar Average True Range (for position sizing), // but use a minimum of 15 ticks minimumAtr = ApplyMin(15 * syminfo.mintick, ta.atr(14))
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Minimum value example", format=format.volume) // ApplyMin() applies the given minimum to the range // of the specified value. Returns value unchanged // when it's above the minimum. ApplyMin(value, minimum) => math.max(minimum, value) // Calculate the 1-bar difference in volume, with a minimum // of 0 (so we don't plot negative volume changes) volumeChange = ApplyMin(0, volume - volume[1]) // Make a columns plot with the volume change values plot(series=volumeChange, style=plot.style_columns, title="Volume change")
```

```pine_script
// ApplyMax() applies the given maximum to the range // of the specified value. Returns value unchanged // when it's below the maximum. ApplyMax(value, maximum) => math.min(maximum, value)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the 1-bar close change, with a maximum of 0 // (which, when plotted, only shows the negative values) closeChange = ApplyMax(0, close - close[1])
```

```pine_script
// Determine from an input option what the cross // above level of the momentum indicator should // be, with a maximum of 0 momCrossLevel = ApplyMax(5, crossLevelInput * 2)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the position size, with a maximum of 500 shares positionSize = ApplyMax(500, strategy.equity * 0.015)
```

```pine_script
// For the stop distance away from the current price, // don't use a distance that's more than 20 ticks stopDistance = ApplyMax(20 * syminfo.mintick, math.abs(low - low[1]))
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Maximum value example", format=format.volume) // ApplyMax() applies the given maximum to the range // of the specified value. Returns value unchanged // when it's below the maximum. ApplyMax(value, maximum) => math.min(maximum, value) // Compute the 1-bar difference in volume, with a maximum // of 0 (so we're only interested in bars with declining volume) volumeChange = ApplyMax(0, volume - volume[1]) // Create a columns plot with the volume changes plot(series=volumeChange, style=plot.style_columns, title="Volume change")
```

```pine_script
// ApplyRange() applies the given minimum and maximum // to the range of the specified value. Returns value // unchanged when it's inside the minimum-maximum range. ApplyRange(value, minimum, maximum) => math.min(math.max(value, minimum), maximum)
```

```pine_script
// Get the 14-bar Average True Range (ATR) with a minimum // of 5 points and a maximum value of 25 points. atrValue = ApplyRange(ta.atr(14), 5, 25)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the position size with a minimum of 10 shares // and a maximum of 2,500 shares. positionSize = ApplyRange(0.01 * strategy.equity, 10, 2500)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the recent price range recentRange = ta.highest(high, 20) - ta.lowest(low, 20) // Determine profit target distance from current price. But don't // place target more than 100 ticks or less than 20 ticks away. targetDistance = ApplyRange(recentRange, 20 * syminfo.mintick, 100 * syminfo.mintick)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Range (minimum-maximum) example", format=format.volume) // ApplyRange() applies the given minimum and maximum // to the range of the specified value. Returns value // unchanged when it's inside the minimum-maximum range. ApplyRange(value, minimum, maximum) => math.min(math.max(value, minimum), maximum) // Calculate the 1-bar difference in volume, but exclude outliers // by using a minimum of -10k and a maximum of 10k volumeChange = ApplyRange(volume - volume[1], -10000, 10000) // Show the bounded volume change with a columns plot plot(series=volumeChange, style=plot.style_columns, title="Volume change")
```

- Set a minimum value.
- Set a maximum value.
- Set a minimum to maximum range.

- Get largest value from numbers explains how Pine Script gets the largest digit from several.
- Fetch smallest number from group clarifies how code retrieves the smallest among several numbers.
- Round up and down in Pine Script shows how indicators and strategies round to a whole number.
- Make whole number input clarifies how code adds an integer input option to the script.

lessons.title: How to make a colour input in TradingView’s Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to make a colour input in TradingView’s Pine Script?

## Default pattern

### input.color() arguments

## Quick examples

### Basic example

### Use the input variable

### Elaborate example

## Features of input.color()

## Example script

## Summary

### References

Pine Script inputs are what configure a strategy’s or indicator’s settings. With the input.color() function we make a colour input [1] [4] [5] .

In the code, we use such an input in the same way as a regular colour [5] . That means a colour input can do things like:

A colour input looks like so in the script’s settings:

The input.color() function has several arguments that configure the colour input. The arguments we can use are [1] :

No matter how we use input.color(), it always returns the colour the manual setting currently has [see 1] . When we store that colour in a variable, other script code can use that variable to find out what the input is currently set to.

This is what the input.color() arguments do [1] [5] :

The one argument that’s always required is defval [1] . The others are optional and we can safely ignore them.

Also useful to know is that each argument requires a constant value [1] . We cannot use a dynamic value or conditional expression to set an input argument.

Enough theory, right? Let’s examine some code to see how we make colour inputs.

Though input.color() has several arguments, most of the time we only use a few. Let’s say we want an input that configures the colour of a line plot. In that case we do:

This snippet uses input.color() with two arguments. The first is color.teal. That sets the input’s default to the teal standard colour variable. The other argument, title, has a value of "Plot Colour". That sets the text that shows as a label before the input.

To give the code access to the input’s current value, we assign what input.color() returns to a variable. With that plotColour variable the script can look up what manual colour we gave the input.

Here’s what the colour input looks like:

When we click on the teal square, TradingView opens a window from where we select other colours. That’s the same colour widget we see elsewhere in TradingView whenever we pick a colour [4] .

To simplify the code we can omit the title keyword argument. That gets us the exact same input, but with a little less code typing:

I like to use the title argument to clarify which of the possible string arguments is set. But this is also a personal bias. So feel free to use what you like the most.

After we store the value that input.color() returns in a variable, other script code refers to that variable to get the input’s current setting.

To see how that works, let’s expand the previous example:

Here we first make a colour input with input.color(). We assign its value to the plotColour variable.

Then the plot() function shows a Weighted Moving Average on the chart. We calculate that 20-bar average with the ta.wma() function. For the plot’s colour we use the plotColour variable, which holds whichever colour we set the manual option to.

This way the input’s current colour shows up in the plotted line on the chart.

There are more arguments to input.color() than a default and name (title). This is how we code a more elaborate colour input:

The input that the input.color() function makes here is orange (color.orange) by default. Its descriptive name is ‘Label Colour’.

The tooltip argument adds a descriptive text to the input. When we hover the mouse over the tooltip icon that’s behind the input, a small popup window tells us more information about this input.

We use the group argument to make a category heading for the input. That heading is named ‘Label Settings’.

Here’s how this colour input looks:

Let’s explore how a full script can use the input.color() function. The indicator below calculates two Simple Moving Averages (SMAs). Based on how those averages and the close price compare, the chart’s background gets one of three colours.

For that the input.color() function makes three colour inputs. We’ll use green for prices above the moving averages, red for prices below the averages, and blue when prices move between the SMAs.

The indicator’s full code is:

We start with the indicator() function. With title we name the script. And overlay makes the script appear on the chart’s instrument.

Then we make three colour inputs with the input.color() function:

Each input’s default is a transparent colour made with the color.new() function. The first input uses green (color.green) with 85% transparency. It’s name is ‘Bullish Background’ and we’ll use it for moving average buy signals.

The next input uses a standard value of 90% transparent blue (color.blue). This is the background colour for when prices neither trend up nor down. The third and last colour input is initially red (color.red). That 85% transparent background applies when prices move below the moving averages. We’ll use the bullColor, sideColor, and bearColor variables to access these inputs’ colours later.

But first the indicator calculates the fast and slow moving average with the ta.sma() function. The plot() function then plots both averages on the chart. Since we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes them as regular line plots. Those lines show in orange (color.orange) and blue (color.blue).

Next we determine the background colour. To do so we pick one of three colour inputs:

This cascaded if statement first tests if the close is above the slow average with the fast average above the slow one. In that situation we use the bullColor variable, which holds the colour of the ‘Bullish Background’ input.

When the first condition tests false, the next looks if the close is under the slow average (with the fast average above the slow one). If that’s so we use the bearColor variable, which is the colour from the ‘Bearish Background’ input. Otherwise, as the default, we use sideColor. That’s the variable from the ‘Neutral Background’ input.

After we pick the colour, Pine Script’s bgcolor() function applies that colour to the chart’s background.

The three colour input options that the script makes look like this:

And on the chart, the different background colours based on the moving averages show as follows:

```pine_script
input.color(defval, title, tooltip, inline, group, confirm)
```

```pine_script
// Make a colour input that configures a plot colour plotColour = input.color(color.teal, title="Plot Colour")
```

```pine_script
// Make a colour input that configures a plot colour plotColour = input.color(color.teal, "Plot Colour") // without optional 'title'
```

```pine_script
// Make a colour input that configures a plot colour plotColour = input.color(color.teal, title="Plot Colour") // Plot a Weighted Moving Average with the input's colour plot(ta.wma(close, 20), color=plotColour, title="WMA")
```

```pine_script
// Create an input that specifies the background colour of text labels labelColour = input.color(color.orange, title="Label Colour", tooltip="Sets the background colour of up and down labels.", group="Label Settings")
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Colour input example", overlay=true) // Create inputs to configure the background colour signals bullColor = input.color(color.new(color.green, 85), title="Bullish Background") sideColor = input.color(color.new(color.blue, 90), title="Neutral Background") bearColor = input.color(color.new(color.red, 85), title="Bearish Background") // Calculate and plot the Simple Moving Averages (SMAs) fastAverage = ta.sma(close, 9) slowAverage = ta.sma(close, 21) plot(fastAverage, color=color.orange, title="Fast Average") plot(slowAverage, color=color.blue, title="Slow Average") // Compare price and averages to determine background colour backgroundColour = if close > slowAverage and fastAverage > slowAverage bullColor else if close < slowAverage and fastAverage < slowAverage bearColor else sideColor // Apply chosen background to the chart bgcolor(backgroundColour, title="Trend Background")
```

```pine_script
// Create inputs to configure the background colour signals bullColor = input.color(color.new(color.green, 85), title="Bullish Background") sideColor = input.color(color.new(color.blue, 90), title="Neutral Background") bearColor = input.color(color.new(color.red, 85), title="Bearish Background")
```

```pine_script
// Compare price and averages to determine background colour backgroundColour = if close > slowAverage and fastAverage > slowAverage bullColor else if close < slowAverage and fastAverage < slowAverage bearColor else sideColor
```

- Set the colour of plots (like lines, shapes, and characters), backgrounds (coloured backgrounds and background fills, for instance), and drawings (such as labels and trend lines).
- Create a new colour by adding transparency or from a colour gradient.
- And be selected from several colours, like we do with the conditional operator and if/else statement, for example.

- defval sets the input’s standard value. Thanks to this default value the script can calculate with the input the moment we add it to the chart. The possible colours for the default are:Standard colour variables (like color.yellow or color.blue).Hexadecimal colour values (such as #ebebeb and #AAF0D1).RGB colours (like color.rgb(210, 94, 35)).And a transparent colour (for example, color.new(color.orange, 20) and color.new(#FDBCB4, 75)).
- Standard colour variables (like color.yellow or color.blue).
- Hexadecimal colour values (such as #ebebeb and #AAF0D1).
- RGB colours (like color.rgb(210, 94, 35)).
- And a transparent colour (for example, color.new(color.orange, 20) and color.new(#FDBCB4, 75)).
- title set’s the input’s name. This text shows before the input as a label, and often is something that describes the input.
- tooltip sets the text for the tooltip window. That small popup window shows when we hover the mouse over the tooltip icon.
- inline defines the input’s inline ID. Inputs with the same text ID appear on the same line in the settings window. Without this argument, each input has its own line (and one shows below the other).
- group tells what text to use as the input’s heading. When multiple inputs use the same group, TradingView combines them under that heading.
- confirm has TradingView ask the user to confirm the input when the script is added to the chart (true). If we don’t set this argument, such a confirmation doesn’t happen.

- Standard colour variables (like color.yellow or color.blue).
- Hexadecimal colour values (such as #ebebeb and #AAF0D1).
- RGB colours (like color.rgb(210, 94, 35)).
- And a transparent colour (for example, color.new(color.orange, 20) and color.new(#FDBCB4, 75)).

- Most of the time, we can also configure a script’s colour with the ‘Style’ tab of the script’s settings window. But, depending on the script’s code, that tab doesn’t always show all colours the script uses [5] . When that happens, the input.color() function is the only way to change a script’s colours by hand [5] .
- In Pine Script version 4, we made colour inputs with the input.color value for the input() function [2] . Other than this change, the behaviour of colour inputs didn’t change in Pine Script version 5. In Pine Script 3 and earlier, colour inputs weren’t available [3] .

- The input.color() function adds a colour option to the ‘Inputs’ tab of the script’s settings window.
- input.color() only requires one argument: its default value. Usually, we set the title argument too; that shows a descriptive text label before the input.
- Other arguments include group (makes a grouped heading above the inputs) and inline, which places several inputs on the same line in the settings window.

1. TradingView (n.d. a). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on January 21, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView (n.d. b). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on January 21, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/
1. TradingView (n.d. c). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on January 21, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v3/
1. TradingView Blog (2020, September 17). Create Color Inputs in Pine. Retrieved on January 21, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/blog/en/create-color-inputs-in-pine-20751/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Inputs. Retrieved on January 21, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Inputs.html

lessons.title: Make a Pine Script input to select a TradingView instrument symbol
lessons.clean_text:
# Make a Pine Script input to select a TradingView instrument symbol

## Default pattern

### input.symbol() arguments

## Quick examples

### Basic example

### How to use input variable

### Elaborate example

## Features of input.symbol()

## Example script

## Summary

### References

There are multiple inputs in Pine Script. The input.symbol() function is one of them. This input adds a symbol search box to the script’s settings window, from where we select a specific instrument [1] [4] . That instrument finder works just like TradingView’s regular instrument search window [4] .

When we find and select an instrument with a symbol input, our script can use that symbol in multiple ways. Most often we combine the symbol with a function that loads background data [see 4] .

This is how a symbol input looks in TradingView:

We can configure the input.symbol() function with several arguments. These are all that are available [1] :

Regardless of which arguments we use, input.symbol() always returns the symbol of the instrument we selected [see 1] . We get that value as a string that consists of the exchange and symbol itself [see 1] , such as "NASDAQ:QQQ".

When we store that string in a variable, other script code can use that variable whenever it needs to know the input’s current value. That’s how code can tell which instrument we selected in the script’s settings.

Here’s what the input.symbol() arguments do [1] [4] :

Of the above list, the only argument we have to use each time is defval [1] . The others are optional, and some, like confirm, we’ll practically never use.

By the way, each argument requires a constant value [1] . We can’t configure the input with a dynamic variable or conditional expression.

With the theory out of the way, let’s do some coding!

While input.symbol() has several arguments, we often only use a couple. Say we want an option to select an instrument. In that case we do:

Here we call input.symbol() with two arguments. The first, "NASDAQ:QQQ", is the input’s default. The other, "Instrument Symbol", is the label that appears before the input in the script’s settings.

This is what the QQQ symbol input looks like:

We can simplify the code example if we leave out the title keyword argument. That still gets us the same input, but with a little less keyboard typing:

I like to use title so it’s explicit which string argument is set. But that’s also a habitual bias. So feel free to adopt the style that works best for you.

We store the value that input.symbol() returns in a variable. That way other script code can use that variable to see which instrument we selected in the script’s settings.

Here’s a quick example of how that works:

This snippet first makes a symbol input with input.symbol(). We put its value in the otherSymbol variable.

Then the request.security() function uses that variable to get the selected instrument’s close price. There are three additional arguments that function uses. timeframe.period loads background data with the same resolution as the chart. close[1] gets us the previous close from that instrument. And barmerge.lookahead_on synchronises the bars of that other instrument with the chart’s bars.

Next the plot() function makes an orange (color.orange) line with that other instrument’s close price. This is how the instrument we choose in the script’s settings shows on the chart.

The input.symbol() function has more options than a default and name (title). Here’s a more elaborate way to use the function:

This symbol input has "SPX" (the S&P 500 index) as its default. The input’s label is ‘Other Symbol’.

The group argument adds a category heading. That header is named ‘Additional Instruments’. If we were to give other inputs the same group name, TradingView puts them together below that heading.

With the tooltip argument we add a description to the input. When the user hovers the mouse over the input’s tooltip icon, a small popup window displays that description.

Here’s what this symbol input looks like:

Worth pointing out is that TradingView automatically adds the exchange prefix to a symbol input (when we haven’t defined one).

As the image shows, our "SPX" symbol input uses the standard SP exchange. If TradingView shouldn’t add the symbol’s default exchange, we need to include our desired exchange in input.symbol()’s default value.

Let’s see how a full script uses the input.symbol() function. The indicator below calculates the correlation between the chart’s instrument and another instrument. It plots that statistic as an area plot, with horizontal plots for key levels of 1, 0, and -1.

We use a symbol input to select that other instrument. The input starts with the S&P 500 index (SP:SPX), but of course we can change this in the script’s settings.

The indicator’s entire code is:

We begin with the indicator() function. That names the script.

Then we include the custom BackgroundData() function to load background data in an accurate manner, without repainting.

Next we add three inputs to the script:

The input.symbol() function makes the first input. Its name is ‘Other Symbol’ and its default is "SP:SPX". To make the input’s value available to other script code, we store its value in the otherSymbol variable.

The next input is a source input (input.source()). This one specifies which price data to compare. The third and last input is made by input.int(). This integer input sets the correlation’s calculation length. With the priceData and corLength variables we track the inputs’ values.

Then we load data and calculate the correlation:

Here the BackgroundData() function fetches the other symbol’s data. We give this function three values to work with. The otherSymbol variable, which holds our symbol input’s value, tells which instrument to load. timeframe.period makes it use the same time frame as the chart. And the priceData input variable specifies the kind of data. We put the loaded background data in the symbolData variable.

The ta.correlation() function then calculates the correlation. We provide it three values. The first, priceData, is the input with price data of the chart’s instrument. The second, symbolData, is the other instrument’s data we just loaded. And the third, corLength, is the input that sets the correlation’s calculation length.

With the correlation computed, the script does it other tasks. That first has the plot() function show the correlation on the chart, as an area plot in #71BC78 (the hexadecimal colour for fern green).

The last bit of code are three hline() function calls. This makes horizontal plots at the 1, 0, and -1 levels, which help interpret the correlation. These solid lines show in the #73A9C2 colour (moonstone blue).

Let’s see what the code made! The script’s three inputs look like:

The correlation that the script plots appears as follows:

```pine_script
input.symbol(defval, title, tooltip, inline, group, confirm)
```

```pine_script
// Specify the symbol of the other instrument to analyse otherSymbol = input.symbol("NASDAQ:QQQ", title="Instrument Symbol")
```

```pine_script
// Specify the symbol of the other instrument to analyse otherSymbol = input.symbol("NASDAQ:QQQ", "Instrument Symbol") // without optional 'title' ^^^
```

```pine_script
// Specify the symbol of the other instrument to analyse otherSymbol = input.symbol("NASDAQ:QQQ", title="Instrument Symbol") // Get (and plot) the close price of the specified instrument symbolClose = request.security(otherSymbol, timeframe.period, close[1], lookahead=barmerge.lookahead_on) plot(symbolClose, color=color.orange, title="Symbol Close")
```

```pine_script
// Create a symbol input that says for which instrument to load data otherSymbol = input.symbol("SPX", title="Other Symbol", group="Additional Instruments", tooltip="Selects which other " + "instrument's data to load in the background.")
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Symbol input example") // BackgroundData() loads data with the request.security() function in // the background, without repainting. BackgroundData(symbol, timeframe, data) => isLive = barstate.isrealtime request.security(symbol, timeframe, data[isLive ? 1 : 0])[isLive ? 0 : 1] // Create inputs otherSymbol = input.symbol("SP:SPX", title="Other Symbol") priceData = input.source(close, title="Price Data") corLength = input.int(90, title="Correlation Length") // Get data and calculate correlation symbolData = BackgroundData(otherSymbol, timeframe.period, priceData) correlation = ta.correlation(priceData, symbolData, corLength) // Plot the correlation and its range lines plot(correlation, color=#71BC78, style=plot.style_area, title="Correlation") hline(1, color=#73A9C2, linestyle=hline.style_solid) hline(0, color=#73A9C2, linestyle=hline.style_solid) hline(-1, color=#73A9C2, linestyle=hline.style_solid)
```

```pine_script
// Create inputs otherSymbol = input.symbol("SP:SPX", title="Other Symbol") priceData = input.source(close, title="Price Data") corLength = input.int(90, title="Correlation Length")
```

```pine_script
// Get data and calculate correlation symbolData = BackgroundData(otherSymbol, timeframe.period, priceData) correlation = ta.correlation(priceData, symbolData, corLength)
```

- defval sets the default value. This gives the input (and script) a value to work with, even when we haven’t configured the input. There are two ways to set that standard value:With just a symbol (like "EURUSD"), in which case TradingView uses the default exchange for that symbol.With an exchange and symbol, such as "OANDA:EURUSD".
- With just a symbol (like "EURUSD"), in which case TradingView uses the default exchange for that symbol.
- With an exchange and symbol, such as "OANDA:EURUSD".
- title is the label name. This string shows before the input in the script’s settings window, and usually describes what the input configures.
- tooltip says what text shows when we hover the mouse over the input’s tooltip icon. This way we add additional information to the input.
- inline gives the input a text-based ID. TradingView places inputs with the same ID on a single row in the script’s settings. Without this argument, each input shows on its own row, one below the other.
- group sets the text that appears above the input as a heading. When multiple inputs have the same heading, TradingView groups them together.
- confirm tells if we should confirm the input when we add the script to the chart (true). With the default false such confirmation is unnecessary.

- With just a symbol (like "EURUSD"), in which case TradingView uses the default exchange for that symbol.
- With an exchange and symbol, such as "OANDA:EURUSD".

- We can’t turn a symbol input into a drop-down menu [see 1] . If we want a list with pre-defined symbols to choose from, we make a text input with the input.string() function instead.
- In Pine Script version 4, we made symbol search inputs with the input.symbol value for the input() function [2] . And in Pine Script version 3 and earlier, we made those inputs with the symbol value for the input() function [3] . Despite these code differences, the input’s features didn’t change.

- The input.symbol() function adds an instrument (symbol) search field to the ‘Inputs’ tab of the script’s settings window.
- input.symbol() only has one required argument: its default value. We often set its title argument too; that places a label before the input.
- Other useful arguments include group (to make a categorised heading) and tooltip, which adds a descriptive popup window to the input.

1. TradingView (n.d. a). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on January 27, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView (n.d. b). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on January 27, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/
1. TradingView (n.d. c). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on January 27, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v3/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Inputs. Retrieved on January 26, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Inputs.html

lessons.title: Introduction to TradingView’s operators
lessons.clean_text:
# Introduction to TradingView’s operators

## What are operators, operands, and expressions?

## Classifying operators based on their number of operands

## Standard TradingView operators: assignment and arithmetic

## Accessing values from previous bars in TradingView

## Creating if/else statements in TradingView Pine

## TradingView’s operators for true/false values

## Creating custom functions in TradingView Pine

## TradingView’s order of operations

### References

Coding scripts in TradingView require operators. But what are operators and which ones are there?

Before looking at this chapter’s articles, let’s discuss some terminology first. An operator is a code element that performs a certain action on one or several values to create a different value (Stephens, 2014). The values that an operator ‘operates on’ are called operands (Sharp, 2013).

So when we for example add two numbers together, then the plus sign (+) is the operator that performs an action (addition in this case) on several values (our two numbers). The new value that’s created by the addition operator is the sum of the two numbers.

The difference between operators and operands is visually highlighted in the image below:

As we can see, one statement (a line of code) can have several operators. Which operator is evaluated first depends on the operators’ priority.

Another term that you’ll see in this chapter and in the TradingView documentation (see TradingView, n.d.) is expression. An expression is a piece of code that returns a value (Albahari & Albahari, 2012), and these often contain an operator. Examples of expressions are 45 * 9.12 (returns 410.4), ema(close, 20) (returns the 20-period Exponential Moving Average of closing prices), and (high - low) / 2. That latter example has two expressions in fact; high - low returns the bar’s range and (high - low) / 2 returns the value of the bar’s range divided by two.

Operators can be classified as unary, binary, or ternary depending on the number of operands that an operator acts upon (Albahari & Albahari, 2012; Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.; Sempf, Sphar, & Davis, 2010):

Now that we’ve the terminology clear, let’s look at this chapter’s articles.

We start by exploring TradingView’s assignment operator that stores values in variables and assigns values to a function’s keyword arguments. Next is a closer look at the arithmetic operators:

Practically every script uses historical data in its calculations. That’s possible with the history referencing operator:

TradingView doesn’t have if/else statements that programming languages typically have (see Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). We can, however, imitate if/else behaviour with an operator and a function:

In the chapter’s next part we discuss how to work with Boolean true/false values in TradingView Pine:

After that we explore creating functions so that we can program repeating calculations with less code:

We end the chapter with the following:

Albahari, J. & Albahari, B. (2012). C# 5.0 in a Nutshell: The Definitive Reference (5th edition). Sebastopol, CA: O’Reilly Media.

Pine Script Language Tutorial (n.d.). Retrieved on August 13, 2015, from https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sCfC873xJEMV7MGzt1L70JTStTE9kcG2q-LDuWWkBeY/

Sempf, B., Sphar, C., & Davis, S.R. (2010). C# 2010 All-In-One for Dummies. Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons.

Sharp, J. (2013). Microsoft Visual C# 2013 Step by Step. Microsoft Press.

Stephens, R. (2014). C# 5.0 Programmer Reference. Indianapolis, IN: John Wiley & Sons.

TradingView (n.d.). Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on September 23, 2015, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

- An unary operator works on a single value. There are three operators in TradingView that can be used unary: addition (+), subtraction (-), and the history referencing operator ([ ]). -close, for example, returns the negative value of the closing price.
- Most TradingView operators are binary, which means that they operate on two values: one on the left and one on the right. Examples of these operators are the multiplication operator (like close[1] * 2) and the greater than comparison operator (close > open).
- There’s one ternary operator in TradingView that takes three operands: the conditional ternary operator (?:). This operator’s first operand is a condition that causes the second operand to be returned when true and the third returned when it evaluates to false.

- First we examine the addition (+), subtraction (-), division (/), and multiplication (*) arithmetic operators and look at how + and - can be used on a single operand.
- Then we discuss how strings can be joined together with + in adding strings with the addition operator.
- In working with the modulus operator we use the % operator to return the remainder of integer division.

- In retrieving data from previous bars we discuss how this history referencing operator ([ ]) works and how it retrieves historical values from built-in variables, user-made variables, built-in functions, and custom functions.

- The conditional ternary operator (?:) has three operands: a true/false condition and two other operands of which one is returned depending on the condition. That makes ?: act like a concise if/else statement.

- In TradingView’s comparison operators we discuss how to compare numbers against each other (like greater than or less than or equal to) such that we end up with a true/false value.
- Then in combining true/false values with logical operators we look at how multiple true/false expressions can be combined into a single true or false value.

- The function declaration operator (=>) allows us to create our own single-line and multi-line functions.

- In the priority of operators in TradingView Pine we discuss the order in which operators are evaluated and how we can change that order with parentheses.

lessons.title: How many bars does a TradingView chart have?
lessons.clean_text:
# How many bars does a TradingView chart have?

## Account sets bar limits

## Historical data depends on exchange

## Show number of bars on chart

### Example charts

## Summary

### References

When we open a chart, TradingView loads a certain number of bars. That bar quantity affects the chart’s date range and also the amount of historical data. More bars means more historical data and a larger date range. But how many? Let’s take a look.

How many bars appear on charts depends on your TradingView account [1] [2] [3] :

Besides upgrading to a different account, the bar amount isn’t something we can increase ourselves. It’s TradingView who decides how many bars appear on a particular chart.

The bar limits mentioned above aren’t exact numbers. From one instrument to the next, the bar amount can differ with several thousands. Furthermore, TradingView continuously increases the data amount (and it sometimes takes a while before new limits are announced.) At the time of writing, Premium users often get charts that already have more than 30,000 bars.

In addition to TradingView’s limits, two other factors influence the number of bars. The first is the instrument’s age. The longer an instrument trades, the more price data there is. While a stock with an IPO last year only has little data.

What also matters is the instrument’s exchange or data source. Let’s explore this one a bit closer.

Different TradingView instruments have various amounts of historical data. This makes sense when we talk about two different instruments (BTCUSD versus ETHUSD for example). But it also happens with the same symbol.

That happens because not every data source has the same historical data amount. Some exchanges in TradingView have little data for a particular symbol, whereas others have a lot of data for the same symbol.

That behaviour occurs with relatively new instruments like cryptocurrencies. But also with instruments that are decades old.

Take EURUSD for instance. The Euro officially started trading on January 1, 1999. That would give plenty of historical data, right? Not necessarily.

If we chart EURUSD from the CAPITALCOM exchange, TradingView only shows bars that go back to 2017:

But if we plot the same EURUSD symbol from the IDC exchange, data even goes back to January 1971 (based on back-adjusting US Dollar prices):

So for the same instrument, these two data sources show a huge difference in historical data. What this means is the following:

When we plan to do a historical chart analysis or backtest, it’s a good idea to first load the same instrument from different exchanges and see which gives the most historical data. Else we might end up missing several years of data.

The discussion above raises an important question. If the number of bars differ per symbol and data source, how can we see how many bars a particular chart has?

That information isn’t inside TradingView’s user interface. But we can code a custom script that tells us. This Pine Script indicator creates a text label with the number of bars on the chart:

This code does four things: define the script settings, make a label, create the label text, and then update the label on each bar. Let’s discuss each.

First we configure the indicator with the indicator() function. That names the script and overlays it on top of the chart’s instrument.

Then we make the label with the label.new() function:

This text label points to the left (label.style_label_left). It has a white text (color.white) against a teal-coloured background (color.teal). size.large makes the label, and its font size, bigger than normal.

The label’s coordinates are na for now, but we’ll update them later to a real chart location.

We store the label’s identifier in the barsLabel variable. That variable is declared with the var keyword. That keyword makes the variable just one time, which also means that our label is made once.

Next we generate the label text:

This if statement checks barstate.islast. That variable is true on the chart’s last bar. On that last bar we can tell how many bars the chart has.

On the final bar we make the labelText variable and set it to a text value. That text is from several string pieces joined with the + operator. We include the instrument’s symbol with the syminfo.ticker variable (such as EURUSD or MSFT).

We also include bar_index. That variable returns the current bar number. On the last bar, that tells the chart’s total number of bars. We add 1 because bar_index starts counting from zero, and not with 1 for the first bar.

Because that bar number isn’t text, the str.tostring() function converts it into a piece of text. Through the "##,###" format string we show the bar count with a thousands separator (,), such as 22,238 and 1,823. That makes for easier reading.

The last bit of code updates the label:

We do two things here. First we update the label’s text to the string variable we just made (labelText). We use the label.set_text() function for that.

Then we update the label’s location with the label.set_xy() function. That new spot is one bar to the right of the current bar (bar_index + 1) at the close price coordinate.

Let’s see how the indicator looks on the chart. On a daily GBPUSD chart, the script tells there are over 7,300 bars:

When the chart’s time frame is 30 minutes, there are over 31,000 GBPUSD bars (for a Premium account):

Try the script yourself and see how much bars your favourite instruments have.

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Display number of bars on chart", overlay=true) // Make one label to show the chart's bar count var barsLabel = label.new(x=na, y=na, style=label.style_label_left, color=color.teal, textcolor=color.white, size=size.large) // On the last bar, update the label if barstate.islast labelText = "This " + syminfo.ticker + " chart has\n" + str.tostring(bar_index + 1, "##,###") + " price bars" label.set_text(id=barsLabel, text=labelText) label.set_xy(id=barsLabel, x=bar_index + 1, y=close)
```

```pine_script
// Make label once to show the chart's bar count var barsLabel = label.new(x=na, y=na, style=label.style_label_left, color=color.teal, textcolor=color.white, size=size.large)
```

```pine_script
if barstate.islast labelText = "This " + syminfo.ticker + " chart has\n" + str.tostring(bar_index + 1, "##,###") + " price bars"
```

```pine_script
if barstate.islast // ... label.set_text(id=barsLabel, text=labelText) label.set_xy(id=barsLabel, x=bar_index + 1, y=close)
```

- Free users get over 5,000 bars on their chart.
- Pro and Pro+ users receive more than 10,000 bars on the chart.
- And Premium users get charts with over 20,000 bars.

- How much bars a chart has depends on the TradingView account. Free users get more than 5,000 bars, Pro and Pro+ users over 10,000 bars, and Premium users receive in excess of 20,000 bars.
- Not every exchange has the same amount of historical data. Some data sources have much less data than others for the same symbol.
- Pine Script can tell the number of bars on the chart with bar_index. That variable returns the current bar number.

1. TradingView.com (n.d.). Plans for every level of ambition. Retrieved on September 11, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/gopro/
1. TradingView Blog (2020, July 8). Introducing Additional Bars on TradingView Charts. Retrieved on September 11, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/blog/en/additional-bars-on-tradingview-charts-19428/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Execution model. Retrieved on September 11, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v4/language/Execution_model.html

lessons.title: How to make a pip-based input in TradingView’s Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to make a pip-based input in TradingView’s Pine Script?

## Step 1: Make the pip input

## Step 2: Use the input’s value with code

## Example script

## Summary

An instrument’s tick size indicates the price change for one pip. A script that works with pips can define that amount in the code directly.

What’s easier, however, is to configure the pip amount with an input. That saves the hassle of editing the code repeatedly. Such an input can help with a stop-loss or profit target order based on a number of pips. Or with a price and breakout filter.

To make a pip-based input we perform two steps:

Let’s look at the code for each step.

First we make an input that sets the pip amount. Then turn its value into the price change equivalent of those pips.

The code for this step is:

This code makes an integer input option with the input.int() function. Its default value is 2. The title argument names the input ‘Pips Offset’. And minval ensures the input’s value is always zero or more.

While this input sets the number of pips, we don’t know yet how much price change that pip count is. For that we multiply the input with the custom GetPipSize() function. That one returns the instrument’s pip size.

Take EUR/USD for example. With the input set to 5, multiplying those 5 pips with GetPipSize() returns EUR/USD’s price scale equivalent, which is 0.00100 (since EUR/USD trades in fractional pips).

We store the input’s pip size in the pipOffsetInput variable for use later.

The input option we made here looks like this:

Now we use the pip-based input somewhere in our script. Where? That depends on what code should use the pip amount.

Let’s assume we create a price band around the Exponential Moving Average (EMA). We want to set the bands’ distance with the number of pips from the input option.

In that case we’d use the input’s value like this:

Here the ta.ema() function first calculates the EMA. That function runs on close prices (close) and a length of 40 bars. We put its value in the emaValue variable.

To calculate the upper band we increase that variable with the number of pips from the input (pipOffsetInput). For the lower band we decrease the moving average with that input’s pip amount.

By using the input variable here, the code uses the option’s current value. And if we change the input’s value by hand, then this code updates with it.

Let’s see how a full script can use a pip-based input. The indicator below makes a 40-bar Exponential Moving Average price band like we mentioned above. The pip input defines the upper and lower band distance to the moving average.

When prices move away from the noise and break outside the band, we colour the chart’s background. We’ll use green for buy signals and red for sell setups.

The indicator’s code is:

First we configure the indicator with the indicator() function. That names the script and places it on top of the chart’s instrument.

Then we include the GetPipSize() custom function.

Next we make a pip-based input as we discussed above.

Then the ta.ema() function calculates the 40-bar Exponential Moving Average of close prices (close). To get the upper band we increase that moving average with the pip input’s value. And for the lower band we decrease that average with the pip’s value.

The plot() function plots those three values on the chart. The upper and lower band show in orange (color.orange) and the EMA displays in aqua (color.aqua). Since we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes regular line plots by default.

To highlight signals on the chart we use the bgcolor() function. The conditional operator (?:) decides which colour to use. First the operator sees if the close is above the upper band. When that’s so, we use green (color.green) set to 85% transparency with the color.new() function.

Else the second conditional operator checks if the close is below the lower band. In that situation we use a transparent red (color.red). When the second condition is neither true, we turn off the background colour with na.

On the chart, the indicator initially makes a price band 2 pips above and 2 pips below the moving average. That looks like:

If we increase the ‘Pips Offset’ input option to 8, the price bands widen and we remove a lot of noise from the chart. Luckily the GBP/USD breakout signal still happens:

```pine_script
// STEP 1. Make an input that specifies the number of ticks. // Then multiply its value with GetPipSize() to turn the pips into // the instrument's price scale value. pipOffsetInput = input.int(2, title="Pips Offset", minval=0) * GetPipSize()
```

```pine_script
// STEP 2. Use the pip-derived price value in the code. // Here we base an upper and lower band a pip amount around the EMA. emaValue = ta.ema(close, 40) upperBand = emaValue + pipOffsetInput lowerBand = emaValue - pipOffsetInput
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Pip-based input example", overlay=true) // GetPipSize() returns the instrument's pip size. Returns the // tick size for non-forex instruments, and the pip size (based // on whole pips) for forex currency pairs. GetPipSize() => syminfo.mintick * (syminfo.type == "forex" ? 10 : 1) // STEP 1. Make an input that specifies the number of ticks. // Then multiply its value with GetPipSize() to turn the pips into // the instrument's price scale value. pipOffsetInput = input.int(2, title="Pips Offset", minval=0) * GetPipSize() // Calculate the 40-bar Exponential Moving Average and its bands emaValue = ta.ema(close, 40) // STEP 2. Use the pip-derived price value in the code. // Here we base an upper and lower band a pip amount around the EMA. upperBand = emaValue + pipOffsetInput lowerBand = emaValue - pipOffsetInput // Plot the average and its bands plot(upperBand, color=color.orange, title="Upper Band") plot(emaValue, color=color.aqua, title="Average") plot(lowerBand, color=color.orange, title="Lower Band") // Highlight breakouts with a coloured background bgcolor(close > upperBand ? color.new(color.green, 85) : close < lowerBand ? color.new(color.red, 85) : na)
```

1. Make the input:Code an integer input that defines the number of pips.Multiply the input’s value with the custom GetPipSize() function. That translates the number of pips into the instrument’s price scale. (For example, 10 pips becomes 0.0010 for EUR/USD and 0.10 for USD/JPY.)
1. Code an integer input that defines the number of pips.
1. Multiply the input’s value with the custom GetPipSize() function. That translates the number of pips into the instrument’s price scale. (For example, 10 pips becomes 0.0010 for EUR/USD and 0.10 for USD/JPY.)
1. Use the input’s value in the script’s code, at the location of where we want to work with the pip amount.

- Code an integer input that defines the number of pips.
- Multiply the input’s value with the custom GetPipSize() function. That translates the number of pips into the instrument’s price scale. (For example, 10 pips becomes 0.0010 for EUR/USD and 0.10 for USD/JPY.)

- To make a pip-based input we first code an integer input option with the input.int() function.
- Then multiply that input’s value with the custom GetPipSize() function. That turns the pips into a price change value.
- And finally use that price amount somewhere in the script.

lessons.title: Get the mode from a TradingView Pine Script array: here’s how
lessons.clean_text:
# Get the mode from a TradingView Pine Script array: here’s how

## Quick example

## Step-by-step array.mode() example

## Ways to use array.mode()

## Example script

## Features of array.mode()

## Summary

### References

After we create an array and add elements to it, Pine Script’s array.mode() function returns the mode from the array’s elements [1] . That mode is the value that occurs most often inside the array.

The function has this standard pattern [1] :

array.mode() returns one of two possible values [1] [2] :

To get an array’s most common element, we first need an integer array or float array.

When we stored the identifier of such an array in the myArray variable earlier, then to get the array’s most common element like so:

As a side note, the id keyword argument of array.mode() is optional. I use those keywords to make code easier to follow. But you can skip them to reduce keyboard typing. This is totally valid Pine Script code too:

For a closer look at the array.mode() function, let’s go through an example together.

To get an array’s most common value, we first create an array:

This snippet makes an integer array with the array.new_int() function. We put its identifier in the myArray variable to access the variable later.

Because we didn’t gave the array an initial size, it starts empty:

Since an empty array doesn’t have a mode, let’s add a couple elements:

Here the array.push() function adds two elements to the end of the array. The myArray variable tells that function which array to modify.

Our array now has this content:

With some values in the array, let’s try to find the array’s mode:

Here we call the array.mode() function to get the array’s most common element. We identify the array to analyse with the myArray variable. And store the outcome of array.mode() in the dataMode variable.

But since every element has the same frequency, there isn’t one that happens most often. So instead array.mode() returns na:

Let’s fix that by adding a few more elements to the array:

Here the array.unshift() function adds new elements to the start of the array. We again use the myArray variable to tell Pine Script which array to change.

The array now has these elements:

To get the new, updated mode we call the array.mode() function again:

Now array.mode() returns this value:

Good! This value is indeed the most common value in the array.

The array.mode() function returns the value of the element with the highest frequency. That helps because it saves the hassle of looping over the array and finding the mode ourselves.

Besides getting an array’s mode, however, there aren’t other tasks for which we also use the array.mode() function.

Let’s look at a script that uses the array.mode() function. The indicator below adds the closing price of each bar to an array. On the chart’s last bar we get the array’s most common value. That tells what price happened the most often on the entire chart.

The indicator’s code is:

First we configure the indicator with the indicator() function. That names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then we make a float array with the array.new_float() function. We store its identifier in the closePrices variable to access the array later. Because we declare that variable with the var keyword, Pine Script makes the variable (and its associated array) just once. That makes the array’s elements persist from one bar to the next.

With the array.push() function we fill the array. That adds each bar’s close (close) to the array’s end. With the closePrices variable we tell Pine Script which array to modify.

Next an if statement checks barstate.islast. That variable is true on the chart’s last bar. When the script gets to that bar, we make a text label with the label.new() function. That label shows at the bar’s high (bar_index, high).

The drawing has a salmon red background, which we set with the #FFE4E1 hexadecimal colour. Its letters show in black (color.black) with a large font (size.large).

The label’s text combines a literal string with the array’s mode. To get that value we call the array.mode() function on the closePrices array. That, however, returns a number whereas the label requires text. So we turn that numerical value into text with Pine Script’s str.tostring() function. With the "0.00" format string that conversion happens to a number with two decimal digits.

When the script runs on the chart, it draws a text label that tells the most frequent closing price. For Bitcoin (BTCUSD), that’s the value of 5.00 from a long time ago:

```pine_script
array.mode(id)
```

```pine_script
// Get most common value from 'myArray' array dataMode = array.mode(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
// Get most common value from 'myArray' array dataMode = array.mode(myArray) // without optional 'id=' part
```

```pine_script
// Create a new, empty array myArray = array.new_int()
```

```pine_script
[ ]
```

```pine_script
// Add two values to the array array.push(id=myArray, value=4) array.push(id=myArray, value=32)
```

```pine_script
[4, 32]
```

```pine_script
// Get most common value of array dataMode = array.mode(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
NaN
```

```pine_script
// Add more values to the array array.unshift(id=myArray, value=4) array.unshift(id=myArray, value=12) array.unshift(id=myArray, value=-7)
```

```pine_script
[-7, 12, 4, 4, 32]
```

```pine_script
// Get array's mode value dataMode := array.mode(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
4
```

