```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="array.mode() example script", overlay=true) // Make a persistent array to store all the chart's closing prices var closePrices = array.new_float() // Add the closing price of each bar to the array array.push(id=closePrices, value=close) // On the last bar, show the most common close with a label if barstate.islast label.new(x=bar_index, y=high, color=#FFE4E1, textcolor=color.black, size=size.large, text="Most common close price:\n" + str.tostring(array.mode(id=closePrices), "0.00"))
```

- id is the identifier of the integer array or float array from which we want the mode.That ID is returned by the function that made that array earlier, like the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.With that identifier we tell Pine Script which array to work with. Without it, Pine Script doesn’t know what array we had in mind.
- That ID is returned by the function that made that array earlier, like the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.
- With that identifier we tell Pine Script which array to work with. Without it, Pine Script doesn’t know what array we had in mind.

- That ID is returned by the function that made that array earlier, like the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.
- With that identifier we tell Pine Script which array to work with. Without it, Pine Script doesn’t know what array we had in mind.

- The value in the array that occurs most often (meaning, with the highest frequency) as an integer or float value.When several values appear with the same frequency in the array, array.mode() returns the one with the lowest value.
- When several values appear with the same frequency in the array, array.mode() returns the one with the lowest value.
- Or otherwise the na value. This value is returned when:The array has no elements (is empty).The array has no mode. This happens when all numbers in the array appear with the same frequency (for instance, every number only occurs once).The array only contains na values. (When there are some na values among actual numbers, array.mode() ignores those na values and computes with the available numbers instead.)
- The array has no elements (is empty).
- The array has no mode. This happens when all numbers in the array appear with the same frequency (for instance, every number only occurs once).
- The array only contains na values. (When there are some na values among actual numbers, array.mode() ignores those na values and computes with the available numbers instead.)

- When several values appear with the same frequency in the array, array.mode() returns the one with the lowest value.

- The array has no elements (is empty).
- The array has no mode. This happens when all numbers in the array appear with the same frequency (for instance, every number only occurs once).
- The array only contains na values. (When there are some na values among actual numbers, array.mode() ignores those na values and computes with the available numbers instead.)

- array.mode() requires an integer array or float array. When we run this statistic function with a different type of array (like a colour or label array), Pine Script generates the ‘cannot call ‘array.mode’ with argument ‘id=(…)’. An argument of ‘(…)’ type was used but a ‘float[]’ is expected.’ error.

- The array.mode() function returns the mode (most common) value of all numbers in an integer or float array.
- When the array is empty, has no mode, or only contains na elements, then array.mode() returns na.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on October 10, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Arrays. Retrieved on October 10, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/language/Arrays.html

lessons.title: How to see if a value crossed over or under another in TradingView Pine?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to see if a value crossed over or under another in TradingView Pine?

## See if values cross each other in TradingView Pine

## See if a value crosses over another

### Example: see if series crosses over another

### Example: see if series crosses over fixed level

## See if value crosses under another

### Example: see if series dropped below another series

### Example: see if series fell below a fixed value

## Look for a cross in any direction

### Example: see if series crosses other series

### Example: see if series crosses single value

## How historical and real-time data influences crosses

## Summary

### References

A rather common task in TradingView Pine scripts is to see if some value crossed another. We might for instance want to check if a 10-bar moving average crossed the 30-bar SMA. Or see whether the Relative Strength Index (RSI) crossed below 30. Let’s see how we code these things.

What makes variables in TradingView Pine unique is that they don’t contain just a single value. Instead they hold a series of values (TradingView Wiki, 2017), with one value for each price bar. This makes it for instance possible to fetch the previous bar’s Average True Range (ATR) value. We can also use a variable series to see if the values crossed some fixed value or another variable series.

TradingView has three helpful functions to see if some value crossed another:

Let’s take a closer look at these functions and how we can use them in our scripts.

The built-in ta.crossover() function checks if one series of values crosses over another (TradingView, n.d.). When there’s a cross above, ta.crossover() returns true. Without such a cross over, the function returns false.

There are two ways to use ta.crossover():

And so there are also two ways to execute ta.crossover():

The first statement of this snippet uses ta.crossover() to see if one series of values crossed over another. With the second statement we look if the current value of a series crossed over some fixed value (like a profit target, calculated value, or input option).

Two things have to happen before ta.crossover() sees a crossover (TradingView, n.d.):

When these two things occur, ta.crossover() returns true. Else the function returns false.

Now let’s look at two example scripts to see the two ways to use the ta.crossover() function.

The first way to use ta.crossover() is to see if one series of values crossed over another. Say we plot a 14-bar Relative Strength Index (RSI) with a 3-bar Exponential Moving Average (EMA). Now we want our indicator to highlight bars on which the oscillator crosses over its moving average. One way to do that is with the ta.crossover() function.

Here’s how that looks when we highlight those crosses with a coloured background:

The code of the example indicator is:

Let’s see what goes on here. First we define the indicator’s properties with the indicator() function. Then we calculate the RSI and its EMA:

Then we highlight the background when the RSI crosses over its moving average:

We use the bgcolor() function to highlight crosses with a coloured background. But we only want to highlight bars on which a crossover happened. And so we use the ta.crossover() function with the rsiValue and rsiEMA arguments. That returns true when the 14-bar RSI crossed over its 5-bar EMA (and false otherwise).

When that kind of cross occurs, TradingView’s conditional operator (?:) returns the fuchsia (color.fuchsia) colour. That colour is then used by bgcolor() to colour the background. Without a RSI-EMA cross, the conditional operator returns na. That value disables the coloured background. The effect is that we only highlight those bars on which a crossover occurred.

After that use the plot() function to display the RSI and its moving average as a regular line plot:

And with the hline() function we make two overbought and oversold lines:

The other application of ta.crossover() checks if a series of values crosses over some fixed value. Say we plot a Relative Strength Index (RSI) and want to highlight those bars on which the RSI crossed over its neutral 50 level. One way to implement that is with the ta.crossover() function.

Here’s how those crossovers look when we highlight them with a coloured background:

And this is what the indicator’s code looks like:

Here we first define the indicator’s settings with the indicator() function. Then we calculate the 14-bar RSI:

Next we colour the chart’s background when the RSI goes above 50:

Here we use the bgcolor() function to highlight the chart’s background. But we don’t colour the background of each price bar. Instead we use the conditional operator (?:) and ta.crossover(). When that function returns true, we make the background purple (color.purple). Else we use na to disable the coloured background.

We use the ta.crossover() function here with the rsiValue and 50 arguments. That has the function return true when the 14-bar RSI crossed over the value of 50 on the current bar (compared to the previous bar). Without such a cross, ta.crossover() returns false instead (and we don’t get a coloured background).

We also use the plot() function to show the RSI values as a regular line plot:

And with the hline() function we make horizontal lines for three RSI values:

TradingView’s ta.crossunder() function checks if one series of values crosses below another (TradingView, n.d.). With a cross below, ta.crossunder() returns true. Without such a cross, the function returns false instead.

There are two ways to use ta.crossunder():

In terms of code, there are two ways to execute ta.crossunder():

The first statement uses ta.crossunder() to see if one series of values crossed below another. With the second statement we check if the current value of a series crosses below some fixed value (like a profit target, calculated value, or input option).

Two things have to happen before ta.crossunder() sees a crossunder (TradingView, n.d.):

When these two things happen, ta.crossunder() returns true. Else the function returns false.

Now let’s look at two example indicators to see how we can use the ta.crossunder() function.

The first way to use ta.crossunder() is to see whether one series of values crossed below another. Say we plot the Stochastics’ %K and %D lines. Now we want to highlight those bars on which the %K line dropped below %D. One way to implement this is with the ta.crossunder() function.

Here’s how those line crosses look when we colour their background:

The code of this example indicator is:

Here we first define the indicator’s properties with the indicator() function. Then we calculate both Stochastics lines:

Next ta.crossunder() sees whether one of those lines crossed another:

We call the ta.crossunder() function here with two arguments. The first, the stochK variable, holds the value of the Stochastics %K. The other, stochD, has the Stochastics %D value. Now when the %K line drops below %K, ta.crossunder() returns true. When such a cross doesn’t materialise, ta.crossunder() returns false.

We store the outcome of ta.crossunder() in the crossBelow variable. Next we use that variable when we colour the chart’s background:

Since we don’t want to colour the background of every bar, we use TradingView’s conditional operator (?:). That operator evaluates the crossBelow variable. When true, we use the red (color.red) colour with bgcolor(). Else we have na turn off the coloured background. This way we only highlight those bars on which the %K line dropped below the %D line.

Next we use the plot() function to show both Stochastics lines as regular line plots:

And with the hline() function we make the oscillator’s overbought and oversold lines:

The other way to use ta.crossunder() is to see if a series of values crosses below a fixed value. This way we can, for instance, monitor whether the Stochastics %K drops below 20 or 80. When we highlight those instances, this is what the chart looks like:

The indicator’s code that makes this happen is:

We first define the indicator properties with the indicator() function. Then we calculate the Stochastics %K and %D values:

Then we look if the Stochastic %K line dropped below 20 or 80:

Here we first have the ta.crossunder() function check if the value of the stochK variable dropped below the fixed value of 20. When it did, ta.crossunder() returns true (and false otherwise). We store this true/false outcome in the osCross variable for use later.

Next the ta.crossunder() function looks if the stochK variable fell below the value of 80. If that’s the case, the function returns true (and gives false otherwise). This outcome we put in the obCross variable.

Next we colour the background based on those two crosses:

This code first makes the backgroundColour variable. With a small cascaded if statement we give this variable one of two colours.

The first if condition checks if the osCross variable is true. When it is, code returns teal (color.teal) with 80% transparency set by the color.new() function. Pine Script then stores this value in the backgroundColour variable.

If that condition tests false, the second if condition runs. Here we see if the obCross variable is true. When that’s so, code returns orange (color.orange) with 80% transparency from the color.new() function.

Should that second condition also be false, then Pine Script implicitly returns the na value (which is then stored in the backgroundColour variable). This na value has the effect of disabling the coloured background. So there’s no coloured background when the Stochastics didn’t fall below 20 or 80.

Finally, we call the bgcolor() function to perform the actual background colouring. We pass this function the backgroundColour variable. That applies one of the two colours – or else the na value – to the chart’s background.

After that we plot two regular line plots with the plot() function:

And we use the hline() function to make two horizontal lines for the overbought and oversold levels:

When the direction of the cross doesn’t matter, we use TradingView’s ta.cross() function. That function looks if two values cross each other, regardless of direction (TradingView, n.d.). When they cross, ta.cross() returns true. And when there’s no crossover or crossunder, ta.cross() returns false.

There are two ways to use the ta.cross() function:

And so there are two ways to execute ta.cross():

The first statement uses ta.cross() to see if one series of values crossed another (either up or down). With the second statement we see if a variable series crossed above or below some fixed value.

Here’s what ta.cross() considers a crossover and crossunder (TradingView, n.d.):

When such a crossover or crossunder happens, ta.cross() returns true. Without these crosses, the function returns false.

To see how we use the ta.cross() function in practice, let’s look at two example indicators.

The first way to use ta.cross() is to see if some series of values crossed another. Say we have an indicator that plots two moving averages, and we want to highlight those bars on which the averages cross (regardless of whether they cross above or below each other).

Here’s how such an indicator looks on the chart:

The code of the example indicator is:

First we define the indicator’s properties with the indicator() function. Then we calculate two Simple Moving Averages (SMAs):

We calculate the moving averages with the ta.sma() function, which we run here on closing prices (close) with a length of 15 and 35 bars. We store these values in the fastMA and slowMA variables for use later.

Then we highlight bars where the moving averages crossed:

We highlight those ‘cross bars’ with a shape. For that we use plotshape(), a function that draws a shape on the chart whenever its first argument is true. Here we set that argument to the ta.cross() function with the fastMA and slowMA variables as arguments. That makes ta.cross() return true when the 15-bar SMA crosses the 35-bar average.

When that cross happens, the true value from ta.cross() has plotshape() make a shape on the chart. The type of shape we use is a diamond (shape.diamond) coloured red (color.red).

The last bit of indicator code uses the plot() function to plot both moving averages as a regular line plot:

The other way to use the ta.cross() function is to see if a variable series crossed over a fixed value, like a price target, oscillator reading, or resistance level. Let’s say we monitor Bitcoin (BTC/USD) prices, and want our TradingView script to highlight bars where the 30-bar Simple Moving Average (SMA) crossed $6,440.

Here’s how such an example indicator looks on the chart:

To highlight those crosses we use this code:

First we define the indicator’s settings with the indicator() function. Then we use the ta.sma() function to calculate a 30-bar average of closing prices:

Next we colour the chart’s background for those bars where a cross happened:

We colour the chart’s background from top to bottom with the bgcolor() function. Here the conditional operator (?:) can either make the background lime green (color.lime) or disabled (na) when no cross happened. We make that green colour 90% transparent with the color.new() function.

The condition we evaluate is the ta.cross() function with smaValue and 6440 as arguments. This makes the function return true when the 35-bar SMA crosses over or under the 6,440 value. When that cross happened, we get a green background with 90% transparency. Else we turn off the background with na.

The indicator’s last statement uses the plot() function to show the 35-bar SMA as a regular line plot on the chart:

The three cross functions (ta.crossover(), ta.crossunder(), and ta.cross()) always compare the current bar’s value with that of the previous bar. What that ‘current bar value’ is depends on which data that price bar uses:

This also means that in real-time we might see a cross during one price update, but then with the next real-time price update see that cross disappear. And then a later price update in the same bar might have the cross appear again.

One thing that the cross functions cannot do, is compare the current real-time tick with the previous tick. Instead they always compare the current price with the last value on the previous bar (the closing value).

TradingView has three built-in functions that monitor for crosses. With the ta.crossover() function we check if a series of values crossed over another series (or crossed above a certain value). This function makes it possible to see if a 10-bar SMA crossed over a 20-bar average, or whether the Relative Strength Index (RSI) moved above the 80 level.

The ta.crossunder() function checks if a series of values crossed below another series (or moved below a certain value). This way we can code scenarios where the 10-bar moving average fell below the 30-bar SMA. Or see whether the Commodity Channel Index (CCI) crossed under -100.

With the ta.cross() function we monitor if one series of values crossed another (or crossed a certain value). This makes it possible to program the situation where the Stochastics %K line crosses the %D line. Or evaluate whether an indicator crossed some value.

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on October 18, 2018, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

TradingView Wiki (2017, June 16). Type System. Retrieved on October 18, 2018, from https://www.tradingview.com/wiki/Type_System

```pine_script
ta.crossover(seriesA, seriesB) ta.crossover(series, value)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Crossover example", overlay=false) // Calculate values rsiValue = ta.rsi(close, 14) rsiEMA = ta.ema(rsiValue, 5) // Highlight when the RSI crosses over its EMA bgcolor(ta.crossover(rsiValue, rsiEMA) ? color.new(color.fuchsia, 90) : na) // Plot values and show overbought and oversold levels plot(rsiValue, color=color.orange) plot(rsiEMA, color=color.teal, linewidth=2) hline(30) hline(70)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate values rsiValue = ta.rsi(close, 14) rsiEMA = ta.ema(rsiValue, 5)
```

```pine_script
// Highlight when the RSI crosses over its EMA bgcolor(ta.crossover(rsiValue, rsiEMA) ? color.new(color.fuchsia, 90) : na)
```

```pine_script
// Plot values and show overbought and oversold levels plot(rsiValue, color=color.orange) plot(rsiEMA, color=color.teal, linewidth=2)
```

```pine_script
hline(30) hline(70)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Crossover example", overlay=false) // Calculate RSI rsiValue = ta.rsi(close, 14) // Highlight the chart when RSI moves above 50 bgcolor(ta.crossover(rsiValue, 50) ? color.new(color.purple, 80): na) // Display RSI values of the chart plot(rsiValue, color=color.teal) // Plot RSI signal levels hline(30) hline(50, linestyle=hline.style_solid, color=color.new(color.gray, 60)) hline(70)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate RSI rsiValue = ta.rsi(close, 14)
```

```pine_script
// Highlight the chart when RSI moves above 50 bgcolor(ta.crossover(rsiValue, 50) ? color.new(color.purple, 80): na)
```

```pine_script
// Display RSI values of the chart plot(rsiValue, color=color.teal)
```

```pine_script
// Plot RSI signal levels hline(30) hline(50, linestyle=hline.style_solid, color=color.new(color.gray, 60)) hline(70)
```

```pine_script
ta.crossunder(seriesA, seriesB) ta.crossunder(series, value)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Crossunder example", overlay=false) // Calculate values stochK = ta.sma(ta.stoch(close, high, low, 14), 3) stochD = ta.sma(stochK, 3) // Determine crossunder conditions crossBelow = ta.crossunder(stochK, stochD) bgcolor(crossBelow ? color.new(color.red, 80) : na) // Plot Stochastics lines plot(stochK, color=color.orange) plot(stochD, color=color.teal) hline(20) hline(80)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate values stochK = ta.sma(ta.stoch(close, high, low, 14), 3) stochD = ta.sma(stochK, 3)
```

```pine_script
crossBelow = ta.crossunder(stochK, stochD)
```

```pine_script
bgcolor(crossBelow ? color.new(color.red, 80) : na)
```

```pine_script
// Plot Stochastics lines plot(stochK, color=color.orange) plot(stochD, color=color.teal)
```

```pine_script
hline(20) hline(80)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Crossunder example", overlay=false) // Calculate values stochK = ta.sma(ta.stoch(close, high, low, 14), 3) stochD = ta.sma(stochK, 3) // Colour the background when %K crosses under 20 or 80 osCross = ta.crossunder(stochK, 20) obCross = ta.crossunder(stochK, 80) backgroundColour = if osCross color.new(color.teal, 80) else if obCross color.new(color.orange, 80) bgcolor(backgroundColour) // Plot Stochastics lines plot(stochK, color=color.orange) plot(stochD, color=color.teal) hline(20) hline(80)
```

```pine_script
stochK = ta.sma(ta.stoch(close, high, low, 14), 3) stochD = ta.sma(stochK, 3)
```

```pine_script
osCross = ta.crossunder(stochK, 20) obCross = ta.crossunder(stochK, 80)
```

```pine_script
backgroundColour = if osCross color.new(color.teal, 80) else if obCross color.new(color.orange, 80) bgcolor(backgroundColour)
```

```pine_script
// Plot Stochastics lines plot(stochK, color=color.orange) plot(stochD, color=color.teal)
```

```pine_script
hline(20) hline(80)
```

```pine_script
ta.cross(seriesA, seriesB) ta.cross(series, value)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Cross example", overlay=true) // Calculate moving averages fastMA = ta.sma(close, 15) slowMA = ta.sma(close, 35) // Highlight crosses with a shape plotshape(ta.cross(fastMA, slowMA), style=shape.diamond, color=color.red) // Plot values plot(fastMA, color=color.teal) plot(slowMA, color=color.orange, linewidth=2)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate moving averages fastMA = ta.sma(close, 15) slowMA = ta.sma(close, 35)
```

```pine_script
// Highlight crosses with a shape plotshape(ta.cross(fastMA, slowMA), style=shape.diamond, color=color.red)
```

```pine_script
// Plot values plot(fastMA, color=color.teal) plot(slowMA, color=color.orange, linewidth=2)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Cross example", overlay=true) // Calculate simple moving average smaValue = ta.sma(close, 30) // Highlight when SMA crosses $6,440 bgcolor(ta.cross(smaValue, 6440) ? color.new(color.lime, 90) : na) // Plot SMA on the chart plot(smaValue, color=color.orange, linewidth=2)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate simple moving average smaValue = ta.sma(close, 30)
```

```pine_script
// Highlight when SMA crosses $6,440 bgcolor(ta.cross(smaValue, 6440) ? color.new(color.lime, 90) : na)
```

```pine_script
// Plot SMA on the chart plot(smaValue, color=color.orange, linewidth=2)
```

- ta.crossover() tells us whether a variable series crossed over another series or some fixed value.
- ta.crossunder() says if a variable series crossed under another series or some fixed value.
- And ta.cross() informs whether a variable series crossed over or under another series or some fixed value.

- The function can check if some series of values crossed over another. This way we for instance monitor if a 10-bar Simple Moving Average (SMA) crossed over a 20-bar SMA.
- The function can also see if a variable series crossed over a fixed value. This makes it possible to see if the Relative Strength Index (RSI) crossed over 80.

- On the previous bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be less than or equal to that of the second argument.
- And on the current bar the value of the function’s first argument has to be greater than the second argument.

- The function can check if some series of values crossed below another. This way we can see whether a 25-bar Exponential Moving Average (EMA) dropped below a 50-bar EMA.
- The function can also check if a variable series crosses below a fixed value. This makes it possible to see whether the Stochastics dropped below 20.

- On the previous bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be greater than or equal to that of the second argument.
- And on the current bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be less than that of the second argument.

- The function can see if some series of values crossed another. This makes it possible to see if a 15-bar Simple Moving Average (SMA) crossed the 35-bar SMA.
- The function can also see if a variable series crossed over a fixed value. This way we can see whether a moving average crosses some price level, or if the Relative Strength Index (RSI) crossed the neutral 50 level.

- For a crossover:On the previous bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be less than or equal to that of the second argument.And on the current bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be greater than that of the second argument.
- On the previous bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be less than or equal to that of the second argument.
- And on the current bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be greater than that of the second argument.
- For a crossunder:On the previous bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be greater than or equal to that of the second argument.And on the current bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be less than that of the second argument.
- On the previous bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be greater than or equal to that of the second argument.
- And on the current bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be less than that of the second argument.

- On the previous bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be less than or equal to that of the second argument.
- And on the current bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be greater than that of the second argument.

- On the previous bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be greater than or equal to that of the second argument.
- And on the current bar, the value of the function’s first argument has to be less than that of the second argument.

- When the bar is a historical bar, the cross functions compare the bar’s value at the close with the closing value of the previous bar.
- When the current bar is a real-time bar, the functions compare the current real-time price update with the closing value of the previous bar.

lessons.title: Get an open order’s entry price in TradingView’s Pine Script
lessons.clean_text:
# Get an open order’s entry price in TradingView’s Pine Script

## Default pattern

## Quick examples

## Features of strategy.opentrades.entry_price()

## Example strategy

## Summary

### References

When a Pine Script strategy has an open position, we get the entry price for a particular open order with the strategy.opentrades.entry_price() function [1] . That’s how the strategy finds out at which prices its simulated orders filled.

The function has this definition [1] :

There are two things that strategy.opentrades.entry_price() can return:

There are several things we can do with the strategy.opentrades.entry_price() function.

To know the price of the first open order (that is, the order opened first, so the oldest one), we do:

And if we want to know the entry price of the last open order (meaning, the most recent entry), we use strategy.opentrades.entry_price() like this:

Another option is to loop over all open trades. That way we work with the entry price of each open order.

Say we want the average entry price. For that we make a for loop that goes through all open orders (strategy.opentrades - 1). Inside the loop we add all entry prices together. After the loop is over, we calculate the average by dividing that sum with the number of open orders.

Here’s how that looks in Pine Script code:

By the way, this example shows how to loop over all open orders. But if we’re after the average entry price, a more efficient way is to use the strategy.position_avg_price variable.

Let’s see how a Pine Script strategy can use entry prices. The script below trades a Simple Moving Average (SMA) and Exponential Moving Average (EMA) from the long side. We exit the position when the faster average crosses below the slower one.

With the strategy.opentrades.entry_price() function we plot the last entry price on the chart. That makes it easy to see how current prices trade in relation to the last entry price.

The strategy’s full code is:

First we configure the strategy with the strategy() function. That names the script and places it on top of the chart’s instrument. With pyramiding we allow up to 3 entries in the same direction.

Next the ta.ema() function calculates a 20-bar Exponential Moving Average. And the ta.sma() function computes a 50-bar Simple Moving Average. Both process the instrument’s close prices (close).

The plot() function then shows both averages on the chart. The EMA appears in orange (color.orange) and the SMA uses teal (color.teal). Since we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes regular lines by default.

Next an if statement sees if the bar closed above the EMA and whether that average is above the SMA. When those things are the case, the if code calls the strategy.entry() function to open a long trade (strategy.long).

The second if statement has the ta.crossunder() function see if the EMA crossed under the SMA. If that happened, the strategy.close() function exits the long trade.

Then we fetch the strategy’s most recent entry price:

We use the strategy.opentrades.entry_price() function to get the last entry price. To specify the trade number we use strategy.opentrades - 1. No matter how many (or few) open trades the strategy has, that always gets us the last order number.

To show that entry price on the chart we call the plot() function. This makes a circles plot (plot.style_circles) that shows in fuchsia (color.fuchsia). Because strategy.opentrades.entry_price() returns na when the strategy has no open position, the plot is also turned off when the strategy is flat.

When we add the strategy to the chart, it plots solid circles to show the entry price. And when there’s a new entry, the circle updates itself:

```pine_script
strategy.opentrades.entry_price(trade_num)
```

```pine_script
// Get price of initial entry in position initialEntryPrice = strategy.opentrades.entry_price(0)
```

```pine_script
// Get price of last entry in position: lastEntryPrice = strategy.opentrades.entry_price(strategy.opentrades - 1)
```

```pine_script
// Collect the entry prices from all open trades priceSum = 0.0 for tradeNumber = 0 to strategy.opentrades - 1 priceSum += strategy.opentrades.entry_price(tradeNumber) // Calculate the average entry price avgEntryPrice = priceSum / strategy.opentrades
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Get last open entry price", pyramiding=3, overlay=true) // Calculate and plot moving average emaValue = ta.ema(close, 20) smaValue = ta.sma(close, 50) plot(emaValue, color=color.orange, title="EMA") plot(smaValue, color=color.teal, linewidth=2, title="SMA") // Generate trades if close > emaValue and emaValue > smaValue strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if ta.crossunder(emaValue, smaValue) strategy.close("Enter Long") // Get price of last entry in current position lastEntryPrice = strategy.opentrades.entry_price(strategy.opentrades - 1) // Display last entry price on the chart plot(lastEntryPrice, color=color.fuchsia, style=plot.style_circles, linewidth=3, title="Last entry price")
```

```pine_script
// Get price of last entry in current position lastEntryPrice = strategy.opentrades.entry_price(strategy.opentrades - 1)
```

- trade_num is the open order number. This integer tells Pine Script from which open order we want the entry price.This value is zero-based; the order opened first has number 0, the second order 1, the third 2, and so on.We get the total number of open orders with strategy.opentrades - 1. We use this amount to loop over all open orders and to get the last (most recent) entry order.
- This value is zero-based; the order opened first has number 0, the second order 1, the third 2, and so on.
- We get the total number of open orders with strategy.opentrades - 1. We use this amount to loop over all open orders and to get the last (most recent) entry order.

- This value is zero-based; the order opened first has number 0, the second order 1, the third 2, and so on.
- We get the total number of open orders with strategy.opentrades - 1. We use this amount to loop over all open orders and to get the last (most recent) entry order.

- A floating-point value with the entry price of the specified open order [1] .
- Or else the na value. The function returns this value when:The order number is incorrect. An incorrect number is less than zero (0) or greater than the current value of strategy.opentrades - 1.The strategy has no open orders at the moment.
- The order number is incorrect. An incorrect number is less than zero (0) or greater than the current value of strategy.opentrades - 1.
- The strategy has no open orders at the moment.

- The order number is incorrect. An incorrect number is less than zero (0) or greater than the current value of strategy.opentrades - 1.
- The strategy has no open orders at the moment.

- The strategy.opentrades.entry_price() function only returns entry prices of open orders. As soon as the strategy closes a particular order, that order’s data isn’t accessible with the function anymore. Instead we’ll have to query the closed trade data with the strategy.closedtrades.entry_price() function.
- strategy.opentrades.entry_price() is available since Pine Script version 5 [see 1] . But the function isn’t in version 4 and earlier [2] . When your strategy code begins with //@version=4 (or uses a lower version number), then the script cannot use strategy.opentrades.entry_price().

- The strategy.opentrades.entry_price() function returns the entry price for a particular open order.
- With an order number we tell the function from which order we want the entry price.
- That number starts at zero (0) for the entry order opened first. And it ends at strategy.opentrades - 1 for the last (most recent) entry.

1. TradingView (n.d. a). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on May 11, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView (n.d. b). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on May 11, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/

lessons.title: The Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy coded for TradingView

# The Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy coded for TradingView

## Trend following with the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy for TradingView

## Trading rules for the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy

## Code the Double Donchian Channel Breakout trading strategy for TradingView

### Step 1: Define strategy settings and input options

#### Inputs for the Donchian Channels length

#### Inputs for position sizing

### Step 2: Calculate trading strategy values

#### Calculate Donchian Channels values

#### Calculate strategy’s position size

#### Determine maximum position size

#### Determine long and short order sizes

#### Figure out trade window to flatten strategy

### Step 3: Output the strategy’s data and visualise signals

#### Plot entry Donchian Channel

#### Plot exits Donchian Channel

### Step 4: Code the long trading rules

### Step 5: Program the short trading conditions

### Step 6: Open a trading position with entry orders

### Step 7: Close market positions with exit orders

#### Submit Donchian Channel exit orders

#### Flatten strategy when backtest ends

## Performance of the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy for TradingView

### Ideas for improvement and new strategies

## Full code: the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy for TradingView

### References

A Donchian Channel is a popular tool with trend-following strategies. But that highest high and lowest low band does have a disadvantage: it can take a long time before the opposite band is crossed and the exit signal generates. Let’s see how we can address that issue by doubling the Donchian Channels.

In Trend Following (2006), Michael Covel shares his research and insights into some of the world’s best traders. One thing those top performers have in common is their trading methodology. All are trend followers, a trading approach with a seemingly simple goal: capture the majority of an up or down move for profit (Covel, 2006).

What makes trend followers different is that they don’t speculate when and where trends begin. Instead they simply monitor price. They know in advance what price moves define a trend, and only after a trend begins do they open positions (Covel, 2006). This does mean they always miss the start of a trend. And they neither get out near the top. But this can work fine: there’s plenty of money in the main move of big trends.

Most trend-following strategies share a few characteristics (Covel, 2006). They detect major trends by looking at price changes. Profitable positions are kept open until the trend ends. That makes profits run. Losing trades are closed at a predefined stop-loss level. That cuts losses short. And with position sizing the amount that each position loses is kept in balance.

One trend-following strategy is the Double Donchian Channel Breakout. This strategy is an attempt to improve the Donchian Channel Breakout strategy. That script kept losing positions open for a long time while we waited for price to cross the opposite Donchian Channel band. That not only made losses worse, it also reduced open profits considerably before the exit signal happened.

To fix that problem the Double Donchian Channel Breakout uses not one but two Donchian Channels (Covel, 2006). One has a long lookback period (and therefore a wider range), which we use for entries. The other band uses a short lookback period (and thus captures a smaller range of price action). That band is then used for exits. Let’s see what the strategy’s trading rules are.

While Covel (2006) discusses several trend-following strategies, he only makes a short mention of the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy. So to come up with the trading rules below I combined the Donchian Channel Breakout strategy with some common sense and risk management principles from Covel’s (2006) book.

That gives the following trading rules:

The risk of positions we trade with this strategy is based on the 40-bar highest high and lowest low. When market volatility is low, that measure of risk is small. A small estimated risk can lead to a big calculated position size. And that becomes a problem when prices gap against our position, leading to losses much greater than intended.

To prevent the strategy from trading positions that are too big, we limit the exposure of each position to 10% of equity. That way not more than one tenth of the strategy’s equity is used for margin requirements.

The other strategies that Covel (2006) shares all use daily data from various US futures, including currencies (British Pound, Japanese Yen, Euro), commodities (crude oil, gold, silver, corn, wheat), soft commodities (coffee, sugar), and financials (S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, T-Note 5yr). So to test the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy we’ll also use daily futures data.

Now let’s turn the strategy’s rules into a proper TradingView strategy. An efficient way to do so is with a template. That adds structure and provides a framework for the different steps to code. Plus it breaks the programming task up into smaller, easier-to-manage portions.

This is the template we’ll use for the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy:

If you want to follow along with the code discussion below, make a new strategy script in TradingView’s Pine Editor. Then paste in the above template. (If you just want the finished code, see the end of this article for the complete strategy.)

For an idea of what the code we’re going to write actually does, here’s how the finished Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy looks on the chart:

Now let’s start and code a TradingView strategy based on two Donchian Channels.

In the first step we define the strategy settings and its input options. For the settings we call the strategy() function:

Here we use the title argument to name the script. With overlay set to true we have the strategy overlay on the chart’s instrument (and not appear in a subpanel).

We disable pyramiding (pyramiding=0) based on the strategy’s rules. With initial_capital we give the script a starting capital of 100,000 in the currency of the chart’s instrument.

For the costs we use 4 currency (commission_value=4) per single order (commission_type=strategy.commission.cash_per_order). These costs are a bit high, but it’s safer to overestimate than underestimate trading costs. For market and stop orders we use 2 ticks slippage (slippage=2).

Next we make several input options to easily configure the strategy’s parameters. The first input sets the Donchian Channel lengths:

These two inputs are integer inputs, which we code with the input.int() function. We name the first ‘Short Donchian Channel Length’ and give it a starting value of 40. The other, ‘Long Donchian Channel Length’, begins with a value of 100.

We store these inputs in the fastLen and slowLen variables. That way we can easily reference the input’s current value by using the variable.

Then we make inputs for the strategy’s position sizing:

The first option is a true/false input made with the input.bool() function. We name it ‘Use Position Sizing?’ and turn it on by default (true). Its current value we store in the usePosSize variable. With this variable we have the strategy later use a default order size of 1 or the computed position size.

The next two inputs are floating-point inputs. Those are made with Pine Script’s input.float() function. The first, named ‘Max Position Risk %’, has a default of 2. We use this setting to define how much equity to risk per trade. The other is named ‘Max Position Exposure %’. This one defines how much strategy equity we invest into a position’s margin requirements. Note that we multiply both inputs with 0.01 to express these percentage-based settings into a decimal value.

The last input is named ‘Margin %’. This integer input (input.int()) estimates the margin rate for the chart’s instrument. Unfortunately we can’t access that data in TradingView Pine at this time. So we have to guess what the instrument’s margin rate is. For a typical futures contract that rate varies between 5 to 15% of the total contract value (Kowalski, 2018). And so we use a default value of 10 here.

Next we calculate the strategy’s data. There are three things we need to figure out: the Donchian Channel values, the position size, and the strategy’s trade window.

First we program the Donchian Channels:

We first compute the 100-bar highest high of the slow Donchian Channel. For that we call the ta.highest() function, which we execute on high prices (high) for slowLen bars (the input variable we set to 100 earlier). We put that 100-bar highest high in the ubSlow variable.

Then we calculate the 100-bar lowest low. We do that with TradingView’s ta.lowest() function, which uses low prices (low) and a length of slowLen here. We store the lower band of the slow Donchian Channel in the lbSlow variable.

Next we calculate the fast Donchian Channel. We run the ta.highest() function on high data for fastLen bars, the input variable we set to 40 earlier. To get the fast lower band we have ta.lowest() calculate the 40-bar lowest low. We store these values in the ubFast and lbFast variables.

Note that we place the history referencing operator ([]) with a value of 1 behind each ta.highest() and ta.lowest() function call. This calculates the highest high and lowest low from the previous bars, without including the current bar’s data.

Then we figure out the strategy’s position size. For that we first calculate the percentage of equity we want to risk and the estimated trade risk:

To calculate the risk equity we multiply the maxRisk input variable with strategy.equity, a variable that returns the sum of the strategy’s initial capital, close trade profit, and open position profit (TradingView, n.d.). Since maxRisk has a default of 2%, multiplying it with strategy.equity tells us we can risk for that two percent.

For the trade risk estimation we make two calculations. The risk of a long trade is the price difference between the current price (close) and the 40-bar lowest low (lbFast). A short trade, on the other hand, has a price risk of the 40-bar highest high (ubFast) minus the current price.

We multiply both price differences with syminfo.pointvalue. That variable returns how much one full point of price movement is worth in currency terms. (For the E-mini S&P 500 future it returns 50, for instance.) This translate the price difference into a cash amount. We store those values in the riskLong and riskShort variables.

Next we figure out the maximum position size we’re allowed to trade:

First we multiply the maxExposure input variable with strategy.equity. That tells us how many percent of the strategy’s equity we can invest in a position’s margin requirements. If the strategy’s equity is $100,000 and the ‘Max Position Exposure %’ input option set to 10, then we can’t invest more than $10,000 in margin.

We divide that value with the marginPerc input variable times the current price (close) times syminfo.pointvalue. That input variable returns our estimation of the margin rate for the chart’s instrument. When we combined with the instrument’s total contract value (close * syminfo.pointvalue) we know how much margin we need to reverse for 1 contract.

So to get the maximum allowed position size we divide how much we want to invest in margin with the margin requirement of one contract. We do that division inside the math.floor() function to round the outcome down to the nearest integer.

Then we use the values we just figured out to determine the actual order sizes:

To give the longPosSize variable its value we first have TradingView’s conditional operator (?:) evaluate the usePosSize input variable. That variable returns true when the ‘Use Position Sizing?’ input option is enabled (and gives false when that checkbox is turned off).

When true, we calculate the long order size as follows. First we divide the riskEquity variable with riskLong. That tells us how many long contracts we can trade for our preferred maximum risk amount. We use the math.floor() function to round the outcome down to the nearest integer. This way we never trade a position that’s a bit too big.

Then we have the math.min() function return the smallest of two values: either the calculated long order size or the maxPos variable value. With this we never trade a position that’s bigger than the maximum allowed position size.

Should the usePosSize input variable be false, then the ‘Use Position Sizing?’ input option is disabled, and we have the conditional operator simply return 1. That way we always trade one contract when position sizing is turned off.

The way we set the value of the shortPosSize variable is very similar, except here we use the riskShort variable as a proxy for the short trade’s risk.

The last thing we need to compute in this step is the strategy’s trade window:

The purpose of the strategy’s trade window is to make the script go flat when the backtest ends. That way there are no open trades in the performance report and values in the ‘Strategy Tester’ window don’t fluctuate when the market is open. But how does the strategy know that the backtest is about to end?

For that we define the tradeAllowed variable. The value of that variable is based on two logical expressions joined with the and operator. The first checks if the bar’s opening time (time) is less than or equal to (<=) the timenow variable minus 86,400,000 times 5. That 86.4 million value is the number of milliseconds in a day, and both time and timenow are also based on milliseconds.

So when we subtract 5 times the number of milliseconds in day from now, and then require that the price bar is before that point in time, we in effect make the strategy only trade till 5 days before the current date and time. Those five days give the strategy enough time to close trades and account for days the market is closed.

The other expression that sets the value of the tradeAllowed variable is bar_index > slowLen. TradingView’s bar_index variable returns the current bar number (TradingView, n.d.), and here we want that bar number to be bigger than the ‘Long Donchian Channel Length’ input option. This makes the strategy begin trading only after there’s enough price data to calculate both Donchian Channels (so 100 price bars at most).

For the third step we output the strategy’s data on the chart. That way we can track its behaviour and verify trade setups.

First we plot the Donchian Channel we use to enter trades:

We display the Donchian Channel’s bands with TradingView’s plot() function. The first plot() function call shows the 100-bar highest high (ubSlow). plot() makes a regular line plot by default, and here we make that line color.green. With the linewidth argument set to 2 the line is a bit bigger than normal.

The second plot() statement shows the 100-bar lowest low (lbSlow). We make that Donchian Channel band appear in the color.red colour with a line thicker than normal (linewidth=2).

Then we display the Donchian Channel used for exits:

To not clutter the chart with several lines, we only show part of the short-term Donchian Channel when there’s an open position. So when the script is long we plot the 40-bar lowest low. And when the strategy has an open short position we display the 40-bar highest high.

To use conditional plotting we set the first argument of the above two plot() function calls with the conditional operator (?:). For the first plot() call that operator evaluates if the strategy.position_size variable is less than (<) 0, which happens when the strategy is short (TradingView, n.d.).

In that case the conditional operator returns the ubFast variable, and the function then shows that 40-bar highest high on the chart. We show those values with a color.teal coloured line with breaks plot. When the script is flat or long, the conditional operator returns na to disable the plot.

The second plot() statement shows the 40-bar lowest low (lbFast) values when the strategy.position_size variable is greater than (>) 0. That happens when the strategy is long. In that case those low values show as an color.orange line with breaks. When the strategy is not long, we use na to disable the plotted line on those bars.

Next we turn the strategy’s long trading rules into TradingView code. Since the strategy has us enter trades with a stop order, there’s are no enter long and enter short conditions. Instead we simply send stop orders when the strategy is flat to open a trade.

The strategy’s exit long rule is something we need to code, however. That rule closes longs when prices drop below the lower band of the 40-bar Donchian Channel. Here’s how we code that:

The exitLong variable we make here gets its true/false value from two expressions joined with the and operator. For the first we use the ta.crossunder() function, which with the close and lbFast arguments returns true when the closing price dropped below the 40-bar lowest low (and else returns false).

The second requirement before we exit a long position is that strategy.position_size is greater than 0. With this check the strategy will only send an exit long order when there’s an open long position. If we’d sell when the strategy is flat or short, we’d only get a (bigger) short position.

The Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy closes short positions when prices rise above the 40-bar Donchian Channel. We code that as follows:

The value of the enterShort variable depends on two expressions joined with the and operator. The first is the ta.crossover() function with the close and ubFast variables as arguments. That returns true when the closing price crossed over the 40-bar highest high (and false otherwise).

The other requirement is that the script is short to begin with (strategy.position_size < 0). This way we don’t buy to cover a short position when the strategy is actually flat or already long.

For the sixth step we write the strategy code that opens positions. Here’s how we submit the script’s entry stop orders:

Since the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy opens trades with stop orders, we send both an enter long order and enter short order whenever the strategy is flat. We’ll use a OCA (One-Cancels-All) order group so that once an order fills, TradingView automatically cancels the other pending stop order.

To make that happen the above if statement first checks if the strategy is flat. That happens when the strategy.position_size variable equals (==) 0. We also want the current bar to be inside the strategy’s backtest window. That occurs when the tradeAllowed variable is true.

When those two things happen, the two strategy.entry() statements indented below if execute. The first strategy.entry() function opens a long trade (strategy.long) named ‘EL’ (id="EL"). We submit that order for longPosSize contracts, the variable that we earlier set to the computed position size (or give a value of 1 when position sizing is off).

We send that enter long order for the ubSlow + syminfo.mintick stop price. This places the stop at the 100-bar highest high (ubSlow) plus a single tick, which we get with TradingView’s syminfo.mintick variable. And so the smallest price fluctuation above the 100-bar Donchian Channel gets us into a long position.

The second strategy.entry() function call is much like the first. This time the function sends an enter short order (strategy.short) for shortPosSize contracts. We name that order ‘ES’. For the stop price we use the 100-bar lowest low (lbSlow) minus one tick (syminfo.mintick). This way the tiniest price move under the 100-bar Donchian Channel opens a short trade.

To not have both stop orders compute after one fills, we use a OCA order group (oca_type=strategy.oca.cancel) for both. Since both strategy.entry() orders use the same OCA group name (oca_name="EntryOrders"), TradingView knows they belong together. So when one fills the other gets cancelled.

In this last step we close trades. There are two ways the strategy closes positions. The first is when the exit long or short signal happens. The other is when the backtest ends, when we flatten the strategy.

So first we submit the strategy’s exit long and short trades:

The first if statement checks exitLong. Earlier we set that variable to true when the closing price dropped under the 40-bar lowest low while the strategy is long. When that happens this if statement executes the strategy.close() function. That function closes the entry orders we earlier named "EL". The name of this exit order is "XL".

The second if statement looks up exitShort. That variable holds true when prices broke above the 40-bar highest high while the strategy is short. In that case strategy.close() exits the "ES" entry orders. This exit we name "XS".

The last line of the strategy exits trades when the backtest ends:

This if statement closes all open orders with the strategy.close_all() function, and thus flattens the strategy, when not tradeAllowed is true.

Earlier we set the tradeAllowed variable to true when the script calculated on a price bar that happened after the first 100 bars but before 5 days from the current date and time. Outside that window the variable is false. But now at the end of the backtest we want a true value so that strategy.close_all() kicks into effect.

To make that happen we place the not logical operator before tradeAllowed. This returns true when tradeAllowed is false, and gives false when tradeAllowed is true.

Note that, since we also check the value of tradeAllowed before we open a trade, the strategy completely stops trading when that variable is false. This way the script only trades (and backtests) the time window we defined earlier.

Let’s begin our look at the strategy’s performance with something that the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy does well. True to its nature as a trend-following strategy, the script performs very well when prices experience a long trend.

An example of that is the following E-mini S&P 500 future chart. Here the strategy when long around 2,200 and closed the profitable long trade a whopping 400 points later:

Of course, the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy also has its weaknesses. The strategy suffers when prices move sideways. That environment gives losses because trends don’t start, prices move in the ‘wrong’ direction, and trends don’t continue long enough for a profitable exit.

An example of that underperformance is the chart below. Here the Double Donchian Channel Breakout had several losing trades in a row because E-mini S&P 500 future prices fluctuated up and down over the course of several months:

The backtest performance of the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy shows below. The results are positive for both the E-mini S&P 500 future and EuroStoxx 50 future. Plus the win percentage is decent for a basic trend-following strategy.

Problematic is, however, the low number of trades. With around 2 trades per year there’s not enough data to say with confidence that the strategy does (or doesn’t) work. The results in the table below are even without position sizing so that the strategy could trade every signal it got. But that didn’t gave many trades, so further testing is still needed.

While the backtest results are not horrible, we can probable make Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy better. Here are some ideas that you might find valuable to explore further:

See the Donchian Channel Breakout strategy for another take on the popular Donchian Channel indicator.

Other TradingView strategies that also trade high and low breakouts include the Donchian Trend strategy and the Donchian Trend with Time Exit strategy.

The complete code of the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy shows below. For more information and explanations refer to the discussion above.

Covel, M.W. (2006). Trend Following: How Great Traders Make Millions in Up or Down Markets (2nd edition). Upper Saddle River, NJ: FT Press.

Kowalski, C. (2018, August 30). Learn About Futures Margin. Retrieved on October 11, 2018, from https://www.thebalance.com/all-about-futures-margin-on-futures-contracts-809390

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on November 6, 2018, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

```pine_script
//@version=5 // Step 1. Define strategy settings // Step 2. Calculate strategy values // Step 3. Output strategy data // Step 4. Determine long trading conditions // Step 5. Code short trading conditions // Step 6. Submit entry orders // Step 7. Submit exit orders
```

```pine_script
// Step 1. Define strategy settings strategy(title="Double Donchian Channel Breakout", overlay=true, pyramiding=0, initial_capital=100000, commission_type=strategy.commission.cash_per_order, commission_value=4, slippage=2)
```

```pine_script
// Donchian Channel inputs fastLen = input.int(40, title="Short Donchian Channel Length") slowLen = input.int(100, title="Long Donchian Channel Length")
```

```pine_script
// Position sizing inputs usePosSize = input.bool(true, title="Use Position Sizing?") maxRisk = input.float(2, title="Max Position Risk %", step=.25, minval=0.25, maxval=15) * 0.01 maxExposure = input.float(10, title="Max Position Exposure %", step=1, minval=1, maxval=100) * 0.01 marginPerc = input.int(10, title="Margin %", minval=1, maxval=100) * 0.01
```

```pine_script
// Step 2. Calculate strategy values // Calculate the Donchian Channel values ubSlow = ta.highest(high, slowLen)[1] lbSlow = ta.lowest(low, slowLen)[1] ubFast = ta.highest(high, fastLen)[1] lbFast = ta.lowest(low, fastLen)[1]
```

```pine_script
// Calculate position size riskEquity = maxRisk * strategy.equity riskLong = (close - lbFast) * syminfo.pointvalue riskShort = (ubFast - close) * syminfo.pointvalue
```

```pine_script
maxPos = math.floor((maxExposure * strategy.equity) / (marginPerc * (close * syminfo.pointvalue)))
```

```pine_script
longPosSize = usePosSize ? math.min(math.floor(riskEquity / riskLong), maxPos) : 1 shortPosSize = usePosSize ? math.min(math.floor(riskEquity / riskShort), maxPos) : 1
```

```pine_script
// Determine trade window tradeAllowed = time <= timenow - (86400000 * 5) and bar_index > slowLen and longPosSize > 0 and shortPosSize > 0
```

```pine_script
// Step 3. Output strategy data // Plot Donchian Channel for entries plot(ubSlow, color=color.green, linewidth=2, title="Slow DoCh - Upperband") plot(lbSlow, color=color.red, linewidth=2, title="Slow DoCh - Lowerband")
```

```pine_script
// Plot Donchian Channel for exits plot(strategy.position_size < 0 ? ubFast : na, color=color.teal, style=plot.style_linebr, linewidth=2, title="Fast DoCh - Upperband") plot(strategy.position_size > 0 ? lbFast : na, color=color.orange, style=plot.style_linebr, linewidth=2, title="Fast DoCh - Lowerband")
```

```pine_script
// Step 4. Determine long trading conditions exitLong = ta.crossunder(close, lbFast) and strategy.position_size > 0
```

```pine_script
// Step 5. Code short trading conditions exitShort = ta.crossover(close, ubFast) and strategy.position_size < 0
```

```pine_script
// Step 6. Submit entry orders if strategy.position_size == 0 and tradeAllowed strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long, qty=longPosSize, stop=ubSlow + syminfo.mintick, oca_type=strategy.oca.cancel, oca_name="EntryOrders") strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short, qty=shortPosSize, stop=lbSlow - syminfo.mintick, oca_type=strategy.oca.cancel, oca_name="EntryOrders")
```

```pine_script
// Step 7. Submit exit orders if exitLong strategy.close("EL", comment="XL") if exitShort strategy.close("ES", comment="XS")
```

```pine_script
if not tradeAllowed strategy.close_all()
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 // Step 1. Define strategy settings strategy(title="Double Donchian Channel Breakout", overlay=true, pyramiding=0, initial_capital=100000, commission_type=strategy.commission.cash_per_order, commission_value=4, slippage=2) // Donchian Channel inputs fastLen = input.int(40, title="Short Donchian Channel Length") slowLen = input.int(100, title="Long Donchian Channel Length") // Position sizing inputs usePosSize = input.bool(true, title="Use Position Sizing?") maxRisk = input.float(2, title="Max Position Risk %", step=.25, minval=0.25, maxval=15) * 0.01 maxExposure = input.float(10, title="Max Position Exposure %", step=1, minval=1, maxval=100) * 0.01 marginPerc = input.int(10, title="Margin %", minval=1, maxval=100) * 0.01 // Step 2. Calculate strategy values // Calculate the Donchian Channel values ubSlow = ta.highest(high, slowLen)[1] lbSlow = ta.lowest(low, slowLen)[1] ubFast = ta.highest(high, fastLen)[1] lbFast = ta.lowest(low, fastLen)[1] // Calculate position size riskEquity = maxRisk * strategy.equity riskLong = (close - lbFast) * syminfo.pointvalue riskShort = (ubFast - close) * syminfo.pointvalue maxPos = math.floor((maxExposure * strategy.equity) / (marginPerc * (close * syminfo.pointvalue))) longPosSize = usePosSize ? math.min(math.floor(riskEquity / riskLong), maxPos) : 1 shortPosSize = usePosSize ? math.min(math.floor(riskEquity / riskShort), maxPos) : 1 // Determine trade window tradeAllowed = time <= timenow - (86400000 * 5) and bar_index > slowLen and longPosSize > 0 and shortPosSize > 0 // Step 3. Output strategy data // Plot Donchian Channel for entries plot(ubSlow, color=color.green, linewidth=2, title="Slow DoCh - Upperband") plot(lbSlow, color=color.red, linewidth=2, title="Slow DoCh - Lowerband") // Plot Donchian Channel for exits plot(strategy.position_size < 0 ? ubFast : na, color=color.teal, style=plot.style_linebr, linewidth=2, title="Fast DoCh - Upperband") plot(strategy.position_size > 0 ? lbFast : na, color=color.orange, style=plot.style_linebr, linewidth=2, title="Fast DoCh - Lowerband") // Step 4. Determine long trading conditions exitLong = ta.crossunder(close, lbFast) and strategy.position_size > 0 // Step 5. Code short trading conditions exitShort = ta.crossover(close, ubFast) and strategy.position_size < 0 // Step 6. Submit entry orders if strategy.position_size == 0 and tradeAllowed strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long, qty=longPosSize, stop=ubSlow + syminfo.mintick, oca_type=strategy.oca.cancel, oca_name="EntryOrders") strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short, qty=shortPosSize, stop=lbSlow - syminfo.mintick, oca_type=strategy.oca.cancel, oca_name="EntryOrders") // Step 7. Submit exit orders if exitLong strategy.close("EL", comment="XL") if exitShort strategy.close("ES", comment="XS") if not tradeAllowed strategy.close_all()
```

| Performance metric | E-mini S&P 500 future (ES) | EuroStoxx 50 future (FESX) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| First trade | 1998-02-03 | 1999-01-04 |
| Last trade | 2018-11-04 | 2018-11-01 |
| Time frame | 1 day | 1 day |
| Net profit | $2,716 | €38,172 |
| Gross profit | $97,840 | €62,248 |
| Gross loss | -$95,124 | -€24,076 |
| Max drawdown | $25,755 | €9,944 |
| Profit factor | 1.029 | 2.585 |
| Total trades | 48 | 36 |
| Win percentage | 41.67% | 52.78% |
| Avg trade | $56.58 | €1,060 |
| Avg win trade | $4,892 | €3,276 |
| Avg losing trade | -$3,397 | -€1,416 |
| Win/loss ratio | 1.44 | 2.313 |
| Commission paid | $384 | €288 |
| Slippage | 2 ticks | 2 ticks |

- Enter long:Go long with a stop order when prices cross the upper Donchian Channel band of 100 bars.
- Go long with a stop order when prices cross the upper Donchian Channel band of 100 bars.
- Exit long:Close any long positions when prices drop below the lower Donchian Channel band of 40 bars.
- Close any long positions when prices drop below the lower Donchian Channel band of 40 bars.
- Enter short:Go short with a stop order when prices cross the lower Donchian Channel band of 100 bars.
- Go short with a stop order when prices cross the lower Donchian Channel band of 100 bars.
- Exit short:Close short positions when prices rise above the upper Donchian Channel band of 40 bars.
- Close short positions when prices rise above the upper Donchian Channel band of 40 bars.
- Position sizing:The initial risk of each position (difference between entry price and 40-bar Donchian Channel) is 2% of equity.The maximum exposure (that is, the margin to equity ratio) for a single position is 10% of equity.
- The initial risk of each position (difference between entry price and 40-bar Donchian Channel) is 2% of equity.
- The maximum exposure (that is, the margin to equity ratio) for a single position is 10% of equity.

- Go long with a stop order when prices cross the upper Donchian Channel band of 100 bars.

- Close any long positions when prices drop below the lower Donchian Channel band of 40 bars.

- Go short with a stop order when prices cross the lower Donchian Channel band of 100 bars.

- Close short positions when prices rise above the upper Donchian Channel band of 40 bars.

- The initial risk of each position (difference between entry price and 40-bar Donchian Channel) is 2% of equity.
- The maximum exposure (that is, the margin to equity ratio) for a single position is 10% of equity.

- Like most trend-following strategies, the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy underperforms when markets move sideways. If we can filter out those unfavourable market conditions the strategy’s performance becomes a whole lot better. Perhaps an ADX or Average True Range (ATR) filter helps. We can also use moving averages, like the Donchian Trend strategy also does.
- The parameter settings for the Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy are chosen based on strategies shared by Covel (2006). Odds are they’re not the most optimal and that the strategy can perform better with other parameters values.
- The strategy enters trades when prices move one tick above the 100-bar highest high and lowest low. This has us open positions as soon as possible. But not every peek outside a Donchian Channel is a valid breakout. Perhaps the strategy’s performance improves when we require that a bar closes a certain amount of ticks beyond the Donchian Channel.
- The Double Donchian Channel Breakout strategy has no explicit stop, though the script does close trades when prices move against us. But the strategy might be easier to trade once we know there’s a stop in the market protecting against big losses. And with a time stop we can weed out those setups that go nowhere.
- More ways to improve the strategy include risk management. We could, for instance, stop trading based on a losing day streak. Or limit the strategy’s maximum position size. See the risk management category for more ideas and inspiration.

lessons.title: Format times and dates with pattern letters of Pine Script’s
lessons.clean_text:
# Format times and dates with pattern letters of Pine Script’s

## ‘Pattern letters’ explained

### Pattern letter versus format specifier

### Pattern letters example

## Pattern letters

## Common pattern letters

## Quick examples

### Format date with pattern letters

### Format time with pattern letters

## Example script

## Summary

### References

Pine Script’s str.format() function is a powerful function to create strings from a template that uses dynamic values.

Those dynamic values can include time values. There are two ways to control how a time appears in the string str.format() makes. We can use a pre-defined format specifier. Or we make our own time format with pattern letters. Let’s see what this latter is about.

A pattern letter is an alphabetical character that specifies how a time value should appear in text [see 1] . They are used by the str.format() function and the str.format_time() function [3] .

Each letter refers to a specific time or date component. Through that mechanism they precisely control how the string str.format() makes appears [4] .

Pine Script has several pattern letters to choose from. Which ones we use, and how we combine them, defines how code turns a time value into a date and/or time string. They provide great precision and flexibility for how a time value appears in text.

With a format specifier, Pine Script decides how a time or date appears as text. This is convenient and requires little work on our part. And is a great option when a predefined format generates the exact output we want. But it doesn’t allow for customisation.

A pattern letter lets us precisely define how a time or date shows in the string. There’s optimal flexibility as we can micromanage the exact output. And we don’t have to use Pine Script’s default American-based time and date style. But it does require a bit more work to define the exact pattern we want to use.

We place pattern letters after the time or date format specifier in the placeholder [2] [3] [4] . We put those placeholders in the format string of the str.format() function.

This means to use pattern letters, we always use the time or date format specifier. (It doesn’t matter which one.) We can’t use pattern letters otherwise.

Here’s a quick example to clarify:

The {0,time,HH:mm:ss} placeholder we got here has three components:

With the HH:mm:ss pattern letters, a time value can show in the string as "12:45:00", "04:22:47", and "22:00:00".

Let’s see what pattern letters we can choose from.

The str.format() function uses these pattern letters to specify how a time or date appears in the generated string [1] :

We not only use these pattern letters with the str.format() function, but also with the str.format_time() function. This latter function makes a string from a time value that uses a specific time zone.

The str.format() function uses any whitespace before, inside, or after the pattern letters to generate the string exactly as specified. Meaning, this whitespace isn’t ignored. With " E ", for example, we get the abbreviated day of week name with two spaces before and after.

Besides pattern letters, str.format() also supports pattern digits. Those precisely control how numbers appear in the function’s generated string.

To include literal text around pattern letters in the placeholder, we escape that text with single quotes (' and '). Else str.format() tries to interpret that text as pattern letters.

These are popular ways to turn a time value into a formatted string with pattern letters:

Now we know what pattern letters there are, let’s use a couple with the str.format() function.

To show a time value as a date of year, we can have str.format() use the MMMM, d, and yyyy pattern letters. Here’s how that looks:

The format string of str.format() has three placeholders:

When str.format() combines the format string’s literal text with the three placeholders and the pattern letters, the generated string is something like:

To display a time value as a time of day text, we can use the HH, mm, and ss pattern letters. Here’s a str.format() example:

The format string of str.format() has three placeholders:

When str.format() combines the literal text in the format string with the three placeholders, the function generates a string such as:

Let’s see how pattern letters work in a full script. The indicator below makes a text label with three pieces of data: the instrument’s symbol, the bar’s time of day and date, and the bar’s range in ticks of the instrument.

The str.format() function generates that label text. Its placeholders use pattern letters to show the time of day with a 24-hour clock. And the date of year with the day name, day of month, month number, and year. Thanks to pattern letters we precisely control how those values appear in the text.

The full code of the indicator is:

We start with the indicator() function. title names the script . And overlay shows the indicator on the chart’s instrument.

Then an if statement sees if the script calculates on one of the chart’s last bars. When it does, the barstate.islast variable returns true (and the if code executes).

That code first calculates the bar’s range in ticks. This first subtracts the bar’s low from its high, and then divide with syminfo.mintick. That variable returns the instrument’s minimum price change. We put the resulting value in the tickRange variable.

Next the str.format() function generates the label text:

The format string that str.format() uses has four placeholders:

Note how the + operator combines two pieces of the format string together. That string can be several lines of source code thanks to string concatenation. This makes the code easier to work with, and makes no difference for how str.format() operates.

The last bit of code has the label.new() function draw a text label on the chart. This label shows one bar to the right (bar_index + 1), at the bar’s midpoint price (hl2). Its style points to the left (label.style_label_left). The label colour is teal (color.teal) with white (color.white) letters. The drawing is bigger than normal (size.large). And the text argument set to the labelText variable shows the str.format() output in the label.

On the chart, the indicator’s label shows the symbol, the bar’s time and data, and the bar’s range. Thanks to pattern letters, the time value shows exactly as we specified:

```pine_script
str.format("Bar open time: {0,time,HH:mm:ss}", time)
```

```pine_script
// Say above which price a particular instrument closed on a certain date signalText = str.format("{0} closed at {1} on {2,date,MMMM d, yyyy}.", syminfo.tickerid, close, time)
```

```pine_script
"NASDAQ:NFLX closed at 295.72 on October 28, 2022."
```

```pine_script
// Generate text with the instrument's volume at a certain time of day signalText = str.format("{0}''s volume is {1} at {2,time,HH:mm:ss}.", syminfo.ticker, volume, time)
```

```pine_script
"BTCUSD's volume is 517.958 at 12:00:00."
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Example of str.format() pattern letters", overlay=true) // On any of the chart's last bars, create a text label if barstate.islast // Calculate the current bar's range in ticks of the instrument tickRange = (high - low) / syminfo.mintick // Generate the label's text with symbol, time, date, and tick range labelText = str.format("{0}\n{1,time,H:mm:ss}\n{1,date,E d-M-yy}" + "\nRange:\n{2,number} ticks", syminfo.ticker, time, tickRange) // Draw a label to show the generated text on the chart label.new(bar_index + 1, hl2, style=label.style_label_left, color=color.teal, textcolor=color.white, size=size.large, text=labelText)
```

```pine_script
// Generate the label's text with symbol, time, date, and tick range labelText = str.format("{0}\n{1,time,H:mm:ss}\n{1,date,E d-M-yy}" + "\nRange:\n{2,number} ticks", syminfo.ticker, time, tickRange)
```

| Letter(s) | Meaning | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| d | Day number of month, ranging from 1 to 31. | 9 |
| dd | Day number of month, with a leading zero for single-digit dates (01 to 31). | 09 |
| E | Day of week name, abbreviated to a three-character format (Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun). | Tue |
| EEEE | Day of week name (Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday). | Tuesday |
| D | Day number of year, which counts from 1 for January 1st to 365 for December 31th (and one more in leap years). | 270 |
| DD | Day number of year, with leading zero for single-digit day counts (01 to 365 or 366). | 09 |
| DDD | Day number of year as a three-digit number, with leading zero(s) for day counts below 100 (from 001 to 365 or 366). | 092 |
| M | Month number of year, ranging from 1 to 12. | 9 |
