| MM | Month number of year as a two-digit value, with leading zero for single-digit months (01 to 12). | 09 |
| MMM | Month name of year, abbreviated to three characters (Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov, Dec). | Sep |
| MMMM | Month of name year (January, February, March, April, May, June, July, August, September, October, November, December). | September |
| y | Year as a four-digit number. | 2023 |
| yy | Year as a two-digit number, showing the two rightmost numbers of the year (23 for 2023 and 12 for 712). Possible range is 00 to 99. | 22 |
| yyyy | Year as a four-digit number, with leading zero(s) for years before 1000. (Years beyond 9999 show with five digits.) | 0224 |
| Y | Week-based year as a four-digit number. This year is extrapolated from a 52-week year. A date like December 31, 2024, which happens in week 53 of the calendar year, is given the year 2025 since on that date the 52-week year was over. | 2022 |
| YY | Week-based year as a two-digit number, showing the two rightmost numbers for the year (00 to 99). | 22 |
| YYYY | Week-based year as a four-digit number, with leading zero(s) for years before 1000. | 0190 |
| w | Week number of year, from 1 to 53. | 6 |
| ww | Week number of year, with leading zero for single-digit week numbers (01 to 53). | 06 |
| W | Week number of month, from 1 to 6. Based on how many calendar weeks passed in the month. New calendar weeks start on Sunday. If the 1st of the month happens on Saturday, then the 2nd of that month (Sunday) is week 2 – even though it’s only the month’s second day. | 5 |
| F | Week number of month, from 1 to 5. Based on how many days actually passed in the month. The first 7 days of the month are week 1, the next 7 days week 2, and so on. | 4 |
| G | Era of a date. Is ‘AD’ (Anno Domini) for years since Jesus Christ and ‘BC’ (Before Christ) for earlier years. Dates with negative years, the time period before year zero, use ‘BC’. | AD |
| K | Hours of a time. Uses a 12-hour clock; values start with 0 and end with 11. | 6 |
| KK | Hours of a time. Based on a 12-hour clock. Adds a leading zero for single-digit hours; values go from 00 to 11. | 06 |
| h | Clock hour of time. Uses a 12-hour clock; values range from 1 to 12. | 6 |
| hh | Clock hour of time. Based on a 12-hour clock. Adds a leading zero for single-digit hours; ranges from 01 to 12. | 06 |
| H | Hours of a time. Uses a 24-hour clock; values go from 0 to 23. | 14 |
| HH | Hours of a time. Based on a 24-hour clock. Adds a leading zero for single-digit hours; values fall between 00 to 23. | 09 |
| k | Clock hour of time. Uses a 24-hour clock; range of values is 1 to 24. | 4 |
| kk | Clock hour of time. Based on a 24-hour clock. Adds a leading zero for single-digit hours; values go from 01 to 24. | 04 |
| m | Minute of hour, from 0 to 59. | 9 |
| mm | Minute of hour, with a leading zero for single-digit minutes. Ranges from 00 to 59. | 09 |
| s | Seconds of minute, from 0 to 59. | 5 |
| ss | Seconds of minute, with leading zero for single-digit seconds. Values range from 00 to 59. | 05 |
| S | Milliseconds of a time, from 0 to 999. | 32 |
| SS | Milliseconds of a time, with one leading zero for single-digit milliseconds. The value range is 00 to 999. | 04 |
| SSS | Milliseconds of a time, with up to two leading zeros for milliseconds below 100. Values go from 000 to 999. | 004 |
| a | Signifies if time happened before or after noon, with ‘AM’ or ‘PM’ respectively. | AM |
| z | Time zone name for a time, abbreviated. | UTC |
| zzzz | Time zone name for a time. | Coordinated Universal Time |
| X | Time zone offset for a time. The ‘Z’ value means: zero offset to UTC. | Z |
| Z | Time zone offset for a time. Formatted as hours and minutes without a colon (:) in between. The value is ‘+0000’ when the offset to the UTC time zone is zero. | +0000 |

| Pattern letters | Description |
| --- | --- |
| K:mm a | Shows the hours based on a 12-hour clock, the minutes with leading zero for single-digit minutes, and the AM/PM indication. |
| HH:mm | Shows the hours from a 24-hour clock (with leading zero for single-digit hours), and the minutes with leading zero for single-digit minutes. |
| HH:mm:ss | Shows the hours from a 24-hour clock with leading zero for single-digit hours, the minutes with leading zero for single-digit minutes, and the seconds with leading zero for single-digit seconds. |
| HH:mm:ss.SSS | Shows the hours based on a 24-hour clock with leading zero for single-digit hours, the minutes with leading zero for single-digit minutes, the seconds with leading zero for single-digit seconds, and the time’s milliseconds with up to two leading zeros for milliseconds below 100. |
| M/d/yy | Shows the month number of year, the day number of month, and the two-digit year separated with forward slashes (/). |
| M/d/yyyy | Shows the month number of year, the day number of month, and the year as a four-digit number, all separated with /. |
| d-M-yy | Shows the day number of month, the month number of year, and the two-digit year, separated with -. |
| d-M-yyyy | Shows the day number of month, the month number of year, and the four-digit year, separated with -. |

- 0 is the placeholder index. This index refers to the first value in the argument list: the time variable.
- time is the format specifier. This lets Pine Script know it should format the value using time and date formatting.
- HH:mm:ss are the pattern letters that tell exactly how the time value should appear. Their meaning is:HH is the hours of the time, based on a 24-hour clock and a leading zero for single-digit hours (00 to 23).: is the literal colon (:) that separates hours from minutes (and minutes from seconds).mm is the minutes of the hour, with leading zero for single-digit minutes (00 to 59).ss is the seconds of the minute, with a zero in front of single-digit seconds (00 to 59).
- HH is the hours of the time, based on a 24-hour clock and a leading zero for single-digit hours (00 to 23).
- : is the literal colon (:) that separates hours from minutes (and minutes from seconds).
- mm is the minutes of the hour, with leading zero for single-digit minutes (00 to 59).
- ss is the seconds of the minute, with a zero in front of single-digit seconds (00 to 59).

- HH is the hours of the time, based on a 24-hour clock and a leading zero for single-digit hours (00 to 23).
- : is the literal colon (:) that separates hours from minutes (and minutes from seconds).
- mm is the minutes of the hour, with leading zero for single-digit minutes (00 to 59).
- ss is the seconds of the minute, with a zero in front of single-digit seconds (00 to 59).

- {0} is a placeholder with no format specifier. The 0 placeholder index refers to the first value in the argument list. That’s the syminfo.tickerid variable, which returns the instrument’s data source and ticker (meaning, symbol).
- {1} is another placeholder without any formatting. Its 1 index gets the second value from the argument list. That’s the close variable.
- {2,date,MMMM d',' yyyy} is a placeholder with the date format specifier and the MMMM d',' yyyy pattern letters. The placeholder index of 2 refers to the time variable, which returns the bar’s open time. The pattern letters mean:MMMM is the full month name of the year, such as January, August, and December.d is the day number of the month, ranging from 1 to 31.yyyy is the year as four-digit number.
- MMMM is the full month name of the year, such as January, August, and December.
- d is the day number of the month, ranging from 1 to 31.
- yyyy is the year as four-digit number.

- MMMM is the full month name of the year, such as January, August, and December.
- d is the day number of the month, ranging from 1 to 31.
- yyyy is the year as four-digit number.

- {0} is a placeholder with no format specifier. The 0 placeholder index refers to the first value in the argument list. That syminfo.ticker variable returns the instrument’s ticker (meaning, symbol).
- {1} is another placeholder with no specified formatting. The placeholder index of 1 gets the volume variable from the argument list.
- {2,time,HH:mm:ss} is a placeholder with the time format specifier. Its 2 placeholder index gets the time variable, which returns the bar’s open time. The HH:mm:ss pattern letters define the precise format for that time. Those letters mean:HH are the hours from a 24-hour clock, with leading zero for single-digit hours, and ranges from 00 to 23.mm are the minutes with leading zero for single-digit minutes, from 00 to 59.ss are the seconds, with leading zero for single-digit seconds, ranging from 00 to 59.
- HH are the hours from a 24-hour clock, with leading zero for single-digit hours, and ranges from 00 to 23.
- mm are the minutes with leading zero for single-digit minutes, from 00 to 59.
- ss are the seconds, with leading zero for single-digit seconds, ranging from 00 to 59.

- HH are the hours from a 24-hour clock, with leading zero for single-digit hours, and ranges from 00 to 23.
- mm are the minutes with leading zero for single-digit minutes, from 00 to 59.
- ss are the seconds, with leading zero for single-digit seconds, ranging from 00 to 59.

- {0} is a placeholder with no format specifier. So Pine Script uses default formatting for its value. The placeholder index of 0 refers to the first value in the argument list: the syminfo.ticker variable, which returns the instrument’s ticker (meaning, its symbol).
- {1,time,H:mm:ss} is a placeholder with the time format specifier. Its placeholder index of 1 fetches the time variable in the argument list. That variable returns the bar’s open time. The H:mm:ss pattern letters format that time. Those letters mean:H is the hours of the time, based on a 24-hour clock. This ranges from 0 to 23.mm are the minutes of a time, with leading zero for single-digit minutes (ranging from 00 to 59).ss are the seconds of the time, with leading zero for single-digit seconds (00 to 59).
- H is the hours of the time, based on a 24-hour clock. This ranges from 0 to 23.
- mm are the minutes of a time, with leading zero for single-digit minutes (ranging from 00 to 59).
- ss are the seconds of the time, with leading zero for single-digit seconds (00 to 59).
- {1,date,E d-M-yy} is a placeholder with the date format specifier. Its placeholder index of 1 also retrieves the time variable from the argument list. The E d-M-yy pattern letters format that time. Those mean:E is the abbreviated, three-letter day of week name, from Mon to Sun.d is the day of month number, ranging from 1 to 31.MM is the month number of the year, with leading zero for single-digit months (01 to 12).yy is the two-digit year, showing the last two digits of the year (22 for 2022).
- E is the abbreviated, three-letter day of week name, from Mon to Sun.
- d is the day of month number, ranging from 1 to 31.
- MM is the month number of the year, with leading zero for single-digit months (01 to 12).
- yy is the two-digit year, showing the last two digits of the year (22 for 2022).
- {2,number} is a placeholder with the number format specifier. Its placeholder index of 2 gets the third value in str.format()’s argument list: the tickRange variable we computed earlier.

- H is the hours of the time, based on a 24-hour clock. This ranges from 0 to 23.
- mm are the minutes of a time, with leading zero for single-digit minutes (ranging from 00 to 59).
- ss are the seconds of the time, with leading zero for single-digit seconds (00 to 59).

- E is the abbreviated, three-letter day of week name, from Mon to Sun.
- d is the day of month number, ranging from 1 to 31.
- MM is the month number of the year, with leading zero for single-digit months (01 to 12).
- yy is the two-digit year, showing the last two digits of the year (22 for 2022).

- The str.format() function makes strings by including dynamic values with a specific formatting in a template.
- A pattern letter says how a time value should show in the string str.format() makes. With them we precisely control how a time of day or date appears.
- We put pattern letters in a placeholder, behind the time or date format specifier.
- What pattern letters we use, how many, and in which sequence, specifies how the time shows in str.format()’s output.
- Pattern letters are optional but very handy. They provide complete control over how a time value appears in the string. With them we aren’t stuck with a American-based, predefined time format.

1. Java Docs (n.d. a). Class DateTimeFormatter. Retrieved on November 2, 2022, from https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/time/format/DateTimeFormatter.html
1. Java Docs (n.d. b). Class MessageFormat. Retrieved on October 31, 2022, from https://docs.oracle.com/javase/10/docs/api/java/text/MessageFormat.html
1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on October 31, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Blog (2021, May 21). New features in Pine Script: str.format() and array.from(). Retrieved on November 2, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/blog/en/new-pine-funtions-for-strings-and-arrays-24554/

lessons.title: How does a chart’s time zone setting affect TradingView scripts?
lessons.clean_text:
# How does a chart’s time zone setting affect TradingView scripts?

## Example 1: Colour the first price bar of the calendar day

## Example 2: Highlight price bars in a specific trading session

### References

Situation: You want to get a better understanding of why setting the chart’s time zone to ‘Exchange’ is important when using the TradingView built-in variables for date and time. Let’s look at a couple of examples for that.

The example indicator below checks if the current price bar is the first of the day and, when it is, colours that bar’s background. When the bar isn’t the first of the day, we use the na variable to make the background transparent.

With that we can expect that only the first bar each day has a coloured background. Even though the example is just a few lines, we use the same logic in more complex scripts (like trading the opening gap or tracking the daily high and low).

The code for colouring the background of the day’s first bar is:

When we add this example to a Crude Oil CFD chart set to ‘Exchange’ time zone, the chart looks like:

Here the script behaves like it should: the highlighted bars coincide with the date stamp on the horizontal time axis. That is, the bars that mark the 7th, 8th, 9th, and 10th day of the month all have their background colour set to teal.

Now when we change the chart’s time zone setting to UTC+1, the chart looks like this:

If we only consider price action, then the same price bars are still highlighted as the first bar of the day. This happens because the dayofmonth variable works with the date in the exchange’s time zone (TradingView, n.d.), and that didn’t change when we adjusted the chart’s time zone setting.

What did change was how much the time axis shifted. Now the highlighted bars don’t correspond to the first bar of the day if we look at the chart’s time axis. Instead, the day’s first bar now happens several hours after the day started. If we didn’t knew better, we’d say the script is broken. And if the script should trade based on the first bar of the day, we’d be in for a surprise with trades happening at the ‘wrong’ times.

In the second example indicator we programmatically determine if a price bar falls in a specific trading session. When it does, we colour the chart’s background. This way the indicator colours the background of all prices happening between 9:30 and 16:00. The code for this is:

When this example indicator is added to a AUD/CAD 30-minute chart that has its time zone set to ‘Exchange’, it looks like:

Here we see that the script starts colouring the background orange after the 9:30 bar up until (and including) the 16:00 bar. This way the indicator correctly identifies all price action happening in that time range.

Now if we adjust the chart’s time zone from UTC-5 (the exchange time for AUD/CAD) to UTC+3, the chart changes to:

Again, if we only look at price action then the same bars are highlighted. This happens because the time() function works based on the instrument’s exchange time zone (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.), and the time zone in which the instrument quotes didn’t change when we made the visual chart adjustment.

But looking at the bar time stamps, the horizontal time axis now shows that the bars between 18:00 till 00:00 have an orange background. If we didn’t take into consideration which TradingView functions and variables work with ‘Exchange’ time, it would seem we have a programming error in our script or that there’s a TradingView bug somewhere. But in fact, the script works as intended.

When we use a time zone different from ‘Exchange’, our indicator or strategy still works correctly – even though it doesn’t seem like that when looking at the bar times. To prevent confusion and a lot of time spend looking for programming errors, consider always using the ‘Exchange’ time zone setting on all of your charts.

We can also take advantage of the fact that TradingView scripts calculate with the instrument’s time zone instead of the chart’s time zone setting. For instance, say we colour the chart’s background of a certain time range (like in the previous example). If we then change the chart’s time zone setting, we can easily see how the exchange time corresponds to that other time zone.

Pine Script Language Tutorial (n.d.). Retrieved on December 3, 2016, from https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sCfC873xJEMV7MGzt1L70JTStTE9kcG2q-LDuWWkBeY/

TradingView (n.d.). Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on May 8, 2017, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Recipe - First bar of day", overlay=true) // When the current bar's day of month differs from // that of the previous bar, the current bar is the // first of the day firstBar = dayofmonth != dayofmonth[1] // Determine the background colour colourOfBg = firstBar ? color.teal : na // Perform the background colouring bgcolor(color.new(colourOfBg, 90))
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Recipe - Highlight a trading session", overlay=true) // Whenever the `time()` function returns a value different than // `na`, the current bar falls in the session. Our `inSession` // variable is `true` then. inSession = not na(time(timeframe.period, "0930-1600")[1]) // Set the colour to color.orange when the bar falls inside the // session and `na` (meaning, no colour) when it doesn't colourOfBg = inSession ? color.orange : na // Colour the chart's background bgcolor(color.new(colourOfBg, 85))
```

lessons.title: How to get the profit factor of TradingView Pine Script strategies?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to get the profit factor of TradingView Pine Script strategies?

## Custom function

### Function examples

## Example strategy

## Summary

### References

Every trading strategy has losses, but a profitable strategy makes up those losses with sufficient gains. A popular way to relate the strategy’s losses-to-gains is the profit factor.

The profit factor shows the ratio between the strategy’s gross profit and gross loss [see 2] . That is, it tells the amount of currency made in relation to the currency amount lost [2] .

We calculate the profit factor as follows [see 2] :

When the gross profit is bigger than its gross loss, the strategy achieved a net profit and its profit factor is bigger than 1.

At the moment, Pine Script doesn’t have a variable that returns the strategy’s profit factor [see 1] . But that isn’t a problem; we can calculate it ourselves.

Here’s a custom function that does so:

This ProfitFactor() function returns the strategy’s current profit factor. Inside the function we divide the result of all profitable trades (its gross profit) with the sum of all losing trades (its gross loss). We get those values with Pine Script’s strategy.grossprofit and strategy.grossloss variables. The result is the strategy’s profit factor.

When the strategy hasn’t done a losing trade yet, strategy.grossloss is zero. In that case the ProfitFactor() function returns the na value. To properly calculate the profit factor the strategy needs at least one unprofitable trade.

After we copy/paste the above function in the strategy’s code, there are several ways to use it.

The first and most basic is to simply call the function when we want to know the strategy’s current profit factor. We do that like so:

The ProfitFactor() function returns a value on each bar. That means we can also compare its current value against that of a previous bar. So to know if the profit factor increased on the current bar, we do:

While the strategy tests the chart’s historical bars, it can make decisions based on the current profit factor. That’s how it can adapt to a good (or bad) profit factor.

We can, for example, reduce the strategy’s default order size with 40% while the profit factor is negative:

It’s also possible to use ProfitFactor() with other Pine Script functions. For instance, to find out if the profit factor crossed below 1, we combine our custom function with the ta.crossunder() function:

To visually track a strategy’s profit factor we plot it on the chart. We do that with the plot() function:

Let’s use the profit factor in a full strategy. The script below trades the 20-bar Bollinger Bands. When prices cross above the upper band, we go long. With a drop below the lower band we open a short position.

At the end of the chart, when the strategy finished its historical test, we make a text label that shows the profit factor. That label is green when the strategy is profitable (profit factor above 1) and red otherwise.

The strategy’s code is:

We first call the strategy() function. That gives the script a name. And overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then we include the custom ProfitFactor() function we defined above.

Next the ta.bb() function calculates a 20-bar Bollinger Bands with a 2.0 standard deviation. We store its values in the middleLine, upperBand, and lowerBand variables. Then the plot() function shows each on the chart as a regular line plot.

After that three if statements generate the strategy’s trades. The first has the ta.crossover() function see if prices crossed above the upper Bollinger Band. When they did, we execute the strategy.entry() function to open a long trade (strategy.long).

The second if statement checks with the ta.crossunder() function if prices went below the lower band. If that’s so, strategy.entry() opens a short trade (strategy.short).

The third if statement closes trades. This one asks the ta.cross() function to see if prices crossed above or below the middle line. When that happens, the strategy.close_all() function exits any open trade.

The last bit of code is what shows the profit factor on the chart:

This if statement checks the barstate.islastconfirmedhistory variable. That one is true on the chart’s last historical bar.

Inside the if statement we first determine the label colour. We call the ProfitFactor() function to see if the profit factor is above 1. When it is, we use the #AAF0D1 hexadecimal colour (which is magic mint green). Else we use #F4C2C2 (tea rose red).

Then we build the label’s text. We combine several literal strings with three variables: the gross profit (strategy.grossprofit), the gross loss (strategy.grossloss), and profit factor (ProfitFactor()). Because these variables return numbers, while the label expects text, we make the numbers into text with the str.tostring() function.

Then we make the label with the label.new() function. It shows three bars to the right (bar_index + 3) and at the bar’s midpoint (hl2). The label points left (label.style_label_left). And its colour and text are what we defined just a moment ago.

When we add the script to a chart, the label it makes with the profit factor appears like this:

```pine_script
// ProfitFactor() returns the strategy's current profit factor, // including the effect of commissions and slippage (when set). // Note: when the strategy hasn't done a losing trade yet, the // gross loss is zero and that makes the function return 'na'. ProfitFactor() => strategy.grossprofit / strategy.grossloss
```

```pine_script
// Get current profit factor pfResult = ProfitFactor()
```

```pine_script
// Compare profit factor against previous bar if ProfitFactor() > ProfitFactor()[1] // Profit factor increased!
```

```pine_script
defaultOrderSize = 100 // Reduce size when profit factor is below 1 if ProfitFactor() < 1 defaultOrderSize := 60 // Open a long position with prices above the 20-bar EMA if close > ta.ema(close, 20) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long, qty=defaultOrderSize)
```

```pine_script
// See if profit factor turns negative if ta.crossunder(ProfitFactor(), 1) label.new(x=bar_index, y=high, text="PF < 1")
```

```pine_script
// Plot profit factor on the chart plot(ProfitFactor(), style=plot.style_columns, title="Profit Factor")
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Profit factor example", overlay=true) // ProfitFactor() returns the strategy's current profit factor, // including the effect of commissions and slippage (when set). // Note: when the strategy hasn't done a losing trade yet, the // gross loss is zero and the function returns 'na'. ProfitFactor() => strategy.grossprofit / strategy.grossloss // Calculate Bollinger Bands [middleLine, upperBand, lowerBand] = ta.bb(close, 20, 2.0) // Plot Bollinger Bands plot(upperBand, color=color.teal, title="Upper Band") plot(middleLine, color=color.gray, title="Middle line") plot(lowerBand, color=color.teal, title="Lower Band") // Generate entry trades if ta.crossover(close, upperBand) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if ta.crossunder(close, lowerBand) strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short) // Generate exit trades if ta.cross(close, middleLine) strategy.close_all(comment="Exit Position") // On the last historical bar, make a label with the strategy's profit factor if barstate.islastconfirmedhistory labelColour = ProfitFactor() > 1 ? #AAF0D1 : #F4C2C2 labelText = "Gross profit: " + str.tostring(strategy.grossprofit, "##,###.00") + "\nGross loss: " + str.tostring(strategy.grossloss, "##,###.00") + "\n\nProfit factor: " + str.tostring(ProfitFactor(), "0.000") label.new(x=bar_index + 3, y=hl2, style=label.style_label_left, color=labelColour, text=labelText)
```

```pine_script
// On the last historical bar, make a label with the strategy's profit factor if barstate.islastconfirmedhistory labelColour = ProfitFactor() > 1 ? #AAF0D1 : #F4C2C2 labelText = "Gross profit: " + str.tostring(strategy.grossprofit, "##,###.00") + "\nGross loss: " + str.tostring(strategy.grossloss, "##,###.00") + "\n\nProfit factor: " + str.tostring(ProfitFactor(), "0.000") label.new(x=bar_index + 3, y=hl2, style=label.style_label_left, color=labelColour, text=labelText)
```

- gross profit is the cumulative profit of all winning trades. This value includes commission and slippage costs.
- gross loss is the cumulative loss of all losing trades, including commission and slippage.

- The profit factor says how the strategy’s total losses compare with its total wins.
- A profit factor above 1 means the strategy is profitable: it made more money than it lost. Losing strategies have a profit factor below 1.
- To calculate the profit factor, we divide the strategy’s gross profit with the absolute value of its gross loss.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on January 16, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Help (n.d.). How are Strategy Tester Report values calculated and what do they mean?. Retrieved on January 26, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/support/solutions/43000561856-how-are-strategy-tester-report-values-calculated-and-what-do-they-mean/

lessons.title: How to configure TradingView Pine Script strategies by hand?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to configure TradingView Pine Script strategies by hand?

## Open strategy settings

## Manual strategy settings

### Initial capital

### Base currency

### Order size

### Pyramiding

### Commission

### Verify price for limit orders

### Slippage

### Margin for long and short positions

### Recalculate after order is filled

### Recalculate on every tick

## Strategy settings versus code

## Summary

### References

There are two ways to configure TradingView strategies. The strategy() function specifies strategy behaviour with code. And there are manual strategy settings in TradingView’s user interface.

Manual settings have a couple benefits. They quickly change a strategy feature, without having to edit code. And they can configure the same strategy differently on multiple charts.

Let’s explore those manual settings. First we’ll open the strategy settings. Then we’ll examine what each setting means.

We configure TradingView strategies by hand with the ‘Properties’ tab of the strategy’s settings window.

There are several ways to open that window. Three common approaches are:

There are three tabs in the strategy’s settings window. The ‘Properties’ tab has general settings, which we discuss in this lesson. The two other tabs, ‘Inputs’ and ‘Style’, configure the strategy’s input options and visual style.

These are the settings in that ‘Properties’ tab:

The settings we can configure here by hand are:

Let’s see what each setting is about.

The ‘Initial capital’ setting specifies how much funds the strategy starts with [1] . This is the cash our strategy has at the start of the backtest.

The initial capital impacts the performance metrics TradingView reports at the end of the backtest. It also affects the strategy’s equity. That equity, in turn, is used for margin and default order size (when we size orders based on percentage of equity). This way more initial capital allows for trading bigger positions.

The initial capital is in the strategy’s currency [1] . When that currency is different from the instrument’s currency, TradingView does automatic currency conversions to keep the strategy’s results accurate.

If we don’t set the initial capital, our strategy begins trading with 1 million [1] .

The ‘Base currency’ option says which currency the strategy calculates with [1] .

That currency is used for the performance results in the ‘Strategy Tester’ window, like net profit and drawdown [1] . It also impacts strategy equity. And it influences the default order size, when we size orders based on cash or percentage of equity. Finally, the strategy’s commission is also in this currency.

The currencies in the ‘Base currency’ drop-down menu are:

With the ‘Default’ option, the strategy uses the same currency as the chart’s instrument and no currency conversion takes place [1] .

The ‘Order size’ setting configures the strategy’s default order size.

There are three order sizing approaches in this setting’s drop-down menu [1] :

Whichever default size we choose, its quantity has to be the instrument’s minimum allowed size or more [1] . Otherwise the order won’t execute. We cannot, for instance, trade 0.5 contracts of the E-mini S&P 500 future.

Also worth knowing: the ‘Order size’ setting configures the default order size. The strategy uses this order size when its code doesn’t send an order with a custom size. Else that custom size overrides the default order size.

The ‘Pyramiding’ setting says how many entries in the same direction may occur [1] .

With ‘Pyramiding’ set to 1, the strategy can open only one long or short order at a time [1] . When that trade is open, and additional entry signals occur, the strategy won’t trade those.

When we set ‘Pyramiding’ to 2 or more, then this value is the maximum number of entries in the same direction [1] .

The ‘Commission’ setting defines how much trading fees are paid with each trade [1] .

The different commission options are [1] :

The commission we configure applies to both entries and exits [1] .

The ‘Verify price for limit orders’ setting controls when TradingView considers a limit order filled.

With its default value of 0 ticks, TradingView simulates limit order fills as soon as the bar’s price touches the limit price [1] . This isn’t very realistic, as it can fill orders on the bar’s exact high or low.

However, when we give the ‘Verify price for limit orders’ setting a value of 1 or higher, then limit orders only fill when the market price exceeds the limit price by that given number of ticks [1] . (If the bar doesn’t reach this tick offset, the order won’t fill.)

The result are more strict conditions for when limit orders fill [1] . Which makes the strategy’s backtest more accurate, since in real life limit orders don’t fill every time.

The ‘Slippage’ setting specifies how much slippage (in ticks) the strategy should add to market and stop orders [1] .

We configure slippage to account for the trading spread [1] . Due to the bid/ask difference, we buy at the higher price and sell at the lower price. So when our strategy simulates an order, in reality we may have been filled 1 or 2 ticks worse (and sometimes more). With the ‘Slippage’ setting we account for these trading costs.

The ‘Margin for long positions’ and ‘Margin for short positions’ settings configure the margin percentage of long and short orders. That percentage is how much of the order’s value has to be funded by strategy equity [see 1] .

Our broker might, for instance, have a margin percentage of 10% for the S&P 500 index CFD. In that case, to buy or sell $1,000 worth of SPX we only need $100 (10%) of equity. And, conversely, if we have $500 equity, we can buy $5,000 worth of SPX, but not more.

The ‘Margin for long positions’ and ‘Margin for short positions’ settings ensure the strategy doesn’t open orders bigger than it can afford (so not more than $5k in the example above).

To not use margin, we set the ‘Margin for long positions’ and ‘Margin for short positions’ settings both to 100. The strategy then has fund the entire position with its own strategy equity [see 1] .

The ‘Recalculate after order is filled’ setting makes the strategy do an extra calculation during an incomplete bar, after an order fills on that bar [1] .

The extra intra-bar calculation makes the strategy faster. Rather than wait till the bar closes, the strategy can now take an action inside the bar too.

That ‘inside the bar’-calculation happens in addition to the regular, on bar close calculation [1] . And the bonus calculation happens on historical as well as real-time bars [1] .

The ‘Recalculate on every tick’ setting lets the strategy calculate with each update of a real-time bar, like an indicator also does [1] .

That calculation happens with every price and volume update. On a high time frame, that can mean hundreds of calculations per real-time bar. During each of those calculations, the strategy can perform its actions (like generating orders). This makes the strategy very fast.

There’s a downside, though. The ‘Recalculate on every tick’ setting doesn’t change how the strategy behaves on historical bars [1] . Those bars still have just one calculation, when the bar closes.

That’s an issue. Because it makes the strategy’s historical performance give an unrealistic picture of how the strategy behaves in real-time [1] . As such, we shouldn’t trust the backtest results at all when ‘Recalculate on every tick’ is turned on.

Besides the manual settings discussed above, we can also configure strategies with code. We do so with the strategy() function.

The manual settings, however, override how code configured the setting. So no matter how strategy() defined a setting, the manual option takes precedence.

This makes it possible to quickly change the strategy’s settings by hand. And to configure the same strategy differently on multiple charts.

- Hover the mouse over the strategy name in the top left of the chart. This expands the strategy’s name with several options. Click on the three dots (...) to open the strategy’s context menu. There select ‘Settings’.

- Right-click the strategy’s name in the top left of the chart. This opens the strategy’s context menu. From that menu, choose ‘Settings’.

- Double-click the strategy name in the top left of the chart. That also opens the strategy settings.

- Initial capital
- Base currency
- Order size
- Pyramiding
- Commission
- Verify price for limit orders
- Slippage
- Margin for long positions
- Margin for short positions
- Recalculate after order is filled
- Recalculate on every tick

- Default
- USD (United States dollar)
- EUR (Euro)
- AUD (Australian dollar)
- GBP (British pound sterling)
- NZD (New Zealand dollar)
- CAD (Canadian dollar)
- CHF (Swiss franc)
- HKD (Hong Kong dollar)
- JPY (Japanese yen)
- NOK (Norwegian krone)
- RUB (Russian ruble)
- SEK (Swedish krona)
- SGD (Singapore dollar)
- TRY (Turkish lira)
- ZAR (South African rand)
- BTC (Bitcoin)
- ETH (Ethereum)
- MYR (Malaysian ringgit)
- KRW (South Korean won)

- ‘Contracts’ sets the order size a fixed number of contracts, shares, or units.
- ‘USD’ (or the currency we selected with the ‘Base currency’ setting) sets the order size to a certain cash amount. TradingView divides that cash with the instrument’s price to get the order quantity.
- ‘% of equity’ uses a percentage of strategy equity. TradingView takes that available equity the moment the order generates and divides it with the instrument’s price to get the order quantity.

- ‘%’ sets commission to a percentage of the order’s cash value. With this commission type, how much trading fees we pay varies with each transaction.
- ‘USD per contract’ (or whichever currency the strategy uses) sets the commission to a currency amount for each contract, share, or unit traded by an order.
- ‘USD per order’ (or whichever currency the strategy uses) sets the commission to a fixed currency amount per order, regardless of that order’s size.

- We configure TradingView strategies with code or by hand.
- The strategy’s manual options are in the ‘Properties’ tab of the strategy’s settings window.
- The settings in the ‘Properties’ tab override how strategy() configured the strategy.

1. TradingView Help (n.d.). Strategy properties. Retrieved on March 31, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/support/solutions/43000628599-strategy-properties/

lessons.title: TradingView’s if/else statement: make code decisions between two options
lessons.clean_text:
# TradingView’s if/else statement: make code decisions between two options

## Execute one of two codes: TradingView’s if-then-else statement

### Default pattern: if/else statement in TradingView Pine

### Quick examples: TradingView’s if/else statements

## Example scripts: if/else behaviour in TradingView scripts

### Example: set plot colour with if/else

### Example: use if/else to calculate a profit target

## Summary

### References

With an if statement our indicator or strategy can make basic decisions. But what if our script should choose between two options? For that scenario we use an if/else statement. Let’s see how.

Often our strategies take one of two actions based on a situation. For instance, “if the bar closed above the 10-bar EMA, then use that EMA as the long stop price; else, keep the stop at the 20-bar lowest low”. To implement that if-then-else behaviour we use an if/else statement.

An if/else statement gives a script the ability to choose between one of two paths. This way an indicator or strategy makes decisions based on whichever data it has available. Thanks to that feature our code doesn’t run in a straight line, but rather handles different situations differently.

With an if/else statement always one of two things happen (Liberty & MacDonald, 2009; Matthes, 2016). The situation we programmed happened, or it didn’t. Either way one part of the if/else statement executes. But never both; it’s always the ‘if’ or otherwise the ’else’.

If/else statements make a lot of things possible. They can submit orders, draw trend lines, place text labels, and of course calculate values. Let’s see how we make them in TradingView Pine.

If/else statements require TradingView Pine version 2 or higher (TradingView, n.d.). If your indicator or strategy script begins with //@version=2 or higher, you can use if/else statements.

The basic pattern of an if/else statement is (TradingView, n.d.; TradingView Docs, n.d.):

As we can see, there are several parts to an if/else statement:

So with an if/else statement always one of two paths executes. When the condition is true, our code under if runs. Should the condition be false, the else code executes (TradingView, n.d.).

To put it differently, the if code looks for a specific situation. And the else code handles all other situations. There’s also a possible disadvantage here. Since else triggers for every other situation, it can execute in more situations than we originally expected.

Note that the indentation of an if/else statement matters a lot. It’s how TradingView determines which code belongs to the if and else portions, and which code doesn’t belong to the if/else statement at all. If we get the indentation wrong, we either get a programming error or our script behaves strangely.

Let’s see how if/else statements work with a couple of examples. Here an if/else statement makes a decision about which order to send:

The condition we evaluate here is whether the bar’s closing price (close) is greater than (>) the 10-bar exponential moving average. We get that latter value with the ta.ema(close, 10) expression.

When the bar is indeed above that average, the if code executes. There we have the strategy.entry() function open a long trade (strategy.long).

If the bar is at or below that average, the else code runs. That code has strategy.entry() open a short trade (strategy.short). So with this if/else statement our script either sends a long or short order.

The snippet above executes the strategy.entry() function conditionally. While this works fine, we cannot use every TradingView function inside an if/else statement.

Here’s another if/else statement example:

The plotColour variable we make here gets one of two possible colours: color.green or color.red. With an if/else statement we set that variable to a conditional value.

The if condition looks if the bar’s close (close) is above (>) the 10-bar simple moving average (ta.sma(close, 10)). When it is, we return the color.green colour with the if/else statement. That colour then gets stored in our plotColour variable.

When the bar closed on or below the moving average, the else code triggers. There we return the color.red colour to the plotColour variable. So after this if/else statement, that variable either holds the red or green colour. We can then use that variable to, for instance, plot on the chart.

Now that we know how TradingView’s if/else statements work, let’s explore an indicator and strategy that uses them.

The example indicator below calculates a 20-bar simple moving average. We compare the bar’s close against that average in an if/else statement to figure out the plot colour. The we plot those values as a circles plot.

The indicator’s complete code is:

The for us relevant code is the if/else statement:

Here we make the plotColour variable. With the = operator we give this variable its value. That value is not the same on every bar, however.

The if/else statement checks if the bar’s close is above (>) the simple moving average. When it is, we return the color.green colour as the value for our plotColour variable. Otherwise (else), we use color.red as the variable’s value.

After the if/else statement ran, we either use red or green as the plot colour. (To apply that colour on the chart we then use the plotColour variable with the plot() function.) Here’s how the coloured moving average plot looks like:

The example strategy below calculates a 12-bar exponential moving average. When prices cross over that average, we go long. To exit the position we use a profit target. That target is something we calculate with an if/else statement.

The strategy’s full code is:

Let’s explore the if/else statement a bit closer:

The priceTarget variable we make here gets one of two possible values. An if/else statement determines which value it gets. For the true/false condition we look if the bar’s closing price (close) is above (>) the 12-bar exponential moving average.

When that tests true, we set the profit target to 10% above the current moving average value. When the condition is false, the else code sets the profit target to 1% under the average. This way our profit target depends on how the bar’s close relates to the moving average.

Here’s how the strategy looks on the chart:

An if/else statement evaluates a true/false condition. When the condition is true, code indented below the if keyword runs. When the condition is false (meaning, in all other cases), the else code executes. That way an if/else statement runs one of two paths.

Thanks to them our indicator or strategy can make if-then/else decisions. But we do need to get the indentation right. Statements under the if and else keywords are indented with 4 spaces or 1 Tab. To measure that indentation, look at the line with the if keyword. (So if that line is already indented with 1 Tab, then the if and else code gets indented with 2 Tabs.)

Liberty, J., & MacDonald, B. (2009). Learning C# 3.0: Master the Fundamentals of C# 3.0. Sebastopol, CA: O’Reilly Media.

Matthes, E. (2016). Python Crash Course: A Hands-On, Project-Based Introduction to Programming. San Francisco, CA: No Starch Press.

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on August 5, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

TradingView Docs (n.d.). Expressions, declarations and statements. Retrieved on August 5, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v4/language/Expressions_declarations_and_statements.html

```pine_script
[myVariable =] if condition // code that runs when 'condition' is true else // code that executes when 'condition' is false
```

```pine_script
// Go long when the bar closes above the 10-bar EMA, else go short if close > ta.ema(close, 10) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) else strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

```pine_script
// Set the `plotColour` to green or red conditionally plotColour = if close > ta.sma(close, 10) color.color.green else color.color.red
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="If/else statement example", overlay=true) // Calculate simple moving average smaValue = ta.sma(close, 20) // Figure out plot colour plotColour = if close > smaValue color.green else color.red // Plot SMA plot(smaValue, color=plotColour, style=plot.style_circles, linewidth=3)
```

```pine_script
// Figure out plot colour plotColour = if close > smaValue color.green else color.red
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="If/else statement example", overlay=true) // Calculate exponential moving average emaValue = ta.ema(close, 12) plot(emaValue, color=color.lime, linewidth=2) // Set profit target with if/else priceTarget = if close > emaValue emaValue * 1.10 else emaValue * 0.99 // Submit orders if ta.crossover(close, emaValue) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) strategy.exit("Exit Long", limit=priceTarget)
```

```pine_script
// Set profit target with if/else priceTarget = if close > emaValue emaValue * 1.10 else emaValue * 0.99
```

- The if keyword, which is followed by a true/false condition.
- Below if we have one or multiple lines of code. Those statements are indented with 4 spaces or 1 Tab. This code executes whenever the condition tests true.
- The else keyword.
- Under else there’s another block of code (again indented with 4 spaces or 1 Tab). The line or lines of code we have here run when the condition tests false.
- Optionally we can store the value returned by the if/else statement in a variable.

lessons.title: How to code a time with date input in TradingView’s Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to code a time with date input in TradingView’s Pine Script?

## Default pattern

### input.time() arguments

## Quick examples

### Basic example

### Use the input variable

### Elaborate example

## Features of input.time()

## Example script

## Summary

### References

Pine Script has multiple input options. With the input.time() function we make an input that selects a time and date in the script’s settings window [1] . We use this input to easily configure a particular point in time.

A time input can do several things. It can set the time and date on which the script should do a particular action, like create a drawing [1] . Or it marks the start point for a script’s calculations, like the VWAP or start of backtest. And, with two time inputs, we define a date range for the script’s actions, like generating trades [4] and collecting price-based information.

This is how a time input looks in the script’s settings:

The input.time() function has several arguments that configure the input. These are the ones available [1] :

After it makes the input, input.time() returns a time value in milliseconds that corresponds to its time and date [1] [5] .

When we store that value in a variable, the other script code can then use that variable to find out what the input is set to. That’s how the script can tell which time and date we configured in the settings.

Here’s what the different input.time() arguments are about [1] [4] [5] :

Of these arguments, only defval needs a value [1] . The others are optional, and an argument like inline we rarely use with time inputs.

Also useful to know: each argument requires a constant value [1] . We can’t configure an input with a dynamic variable or a conditional expression.

That’s enough input.time() theory! Let’s look at some code.

Often we use input.time() with two arguments. Say we want to select the time and date for a vertical trend line. For that we make a time input like so:

Here we specify two values for input.time(). The first sets the input’s default. That needs to be a time in milliseconds, which is annoying. Luckily, the timestamp() function can turn an easy-to-read date into milliseconds. Here it sets the default to February 1, 2022, at 12:00 o’clock.

The other value we set is "Line Location". This title argument is the label that appears before the input.

Here’s how this time input looks like:

We can shorten the example if we leave out the optional title keyword argument. That still makes the same input, but with a little less code:

Though title is optional, I still type it to clarify which string argument is set. But that’s my preference; feel free to use a style you like the most.

When we store the value that input.time() returns in a variable, other script code can use that variable to get the input’s current value.

To see that in action, let’s expand the previous example to:

This code first makes a time input with the input.time() function. We store its value in the lineDate variable.

Then an if statement checks the barstate.islastconfirmedhistory variable. That one is true on the last historical bar. When the script gets there, the line.new() function makes a new trend line.

That line’s time coordinates are the lineDate variable, which is the input with a default of February 1, 2022. For its price coordinates we use the high and low. When we extend the trend line in both directions (extend.both), it becomes a vertical line.

With the line at the location of the lineData variable, each time we change the input by hand, that variable’s value changes – which then modifies the line’s location.

The input.time() function has more arguments than a default and name (title). Here’s a more expansive example:

This input has a default of 12:45 on February 1, 2022, set by the timestamp() function. The input’s label is ‘Start Date’.

The group argument creates a category heading above the input. If several inputs have the same group name, TradingView combines them below that header.

The tooltip argument adds a helpful description to the input. This shows an informational icon behind the input. When we hover the mouse over it, a pop-up window appears with the explanation.

This richer input appears as follows:

Let’s see how a full script uses the input.time() function. The indicator below calculates a Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP).

A time input sets the time and date of when the VWAP starts to calculate. From that date onward, the script collects volumes, prices, and the VWAP standard deviation. We plot those on the chart to see how this weighted average developed over time.

The indicator’s full code is:

We start with the indicator() function. That names the script with title. And overlay makes the script appear on top of the chart’s instrument.

Then we code two inputs:

The input.time() function makes a time input. Its standard value is the timestamp() function with the "1 Jan 2022" literal string. This doesn’t set the time of day, so that time defaults to midnight (00:00). We name the input ‘VWAP Start Date’ and track its value with the vwapStartDate variable.

Next the input.source() function makes a price data source input. This one sets the data we calculate the VWAP with. Its default is hlc3, which is the average of the bar’s high, low, and close. We put the input’s value in the vwapData variable.

With the inputs made we create three persistent variables (var). Those will track the VWAP’s data. Then we collect the VWAP data:

This if statement checks if the bar’s open time (time) is greater than or equal to (>=) the date and time we configured with the ‘VWAP Start Date’ input (vwapStartDate). This comparison is true for all bars later in time than the input’s value.

For those bars, we collect the sums of price data times volume, volume itself, and volume squared.

After the if statement the code calculates with those values. To get the VWAP it divides the sum of prices times volume (sumPrices) with the total volume (sumVolume). Then it calculates the VWAP variance and subsequently its standard deviation. We need that latter for the upper and lower band around the VWAP.

The script’s last bit of code plots the data on the chart with the plot() function. The VWAP shows in blue (color.blue) and the bands use orange (color.orange). Since we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes regular line plots by default.

The two inputs that the indicator makes look like this:

The VWAP that starts at the input’s time and date looks so on the chart:

```pine_script
input.time(defval, title, tooltip, inline, group, confirm)
```

```pine_script
// Create an input that specifies on which time and date // the script should make a vertical line lineDate = input.time(timestamp("1 Feb 2022 12:00"), title="Line Location")
```

```pine_script
// Create an input that specifies on which time and date // the script should make a vertical line lineDate = input.time(timestamp("1 Feb 2022 12:00"), "Line Location") // without optional 'title' ^^^
```

```pine_script
// Create an input that specifies on which time and date // the script should make a vertical line lineDate = input.time(timestamp("1 Feb 2022 12:00"), title="Line Location") // Once, on the chart's last bar, draw the vertical line at the // input's specified time and date if barstate.islastconfirmedhistory line.new(x1=lineDate, y1=high, x2=lineDate, y2=low, extend=extend.both, color=color.orange, width=2, xloc=xloc.bar_time)
```

```pine_script
// Create a time input that configures when the strategy // backtest should start. startDateTime = input.time(timestamp("1 Feb 2022 12:45"), title="Start Date", group="Strategy Date Range", tooltip="Specifies the date and time from which on " + "the strategy simulates buy and sell trades.")
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Time input example", overlay=true) // Create inputs vwapStartDate = input.time(timestamp("1 Jan 2022"), title="VWAP Start Date") vwapData = input.source(hlc3, title="VWAP Data Source") // Calculate VWAP var sumPrices = 0.0 var sumVolume = 0.0 var powVolume = 0.0 if time >= vwapStartDate sumPrices += vwapData * volume sumVolume += volume powVolume += volume * math.pow(vwapData, 2) vwapValue = sumPrices / sumVolume vwapVariance = math.max(powVolume / sumVolume - math.pow(vwapValue, 2), 0) vwapStdDev = math.sqrt(vwapVariance) // Plot the VWAP and its standard deviation bands plot(vwapValue, color=color.blue, title="VWAP") plot(vwapValue + vwapStdDev, color=color.orange, title="Upper Band") plot(vwapValue - vwapStdDev, color=color.orange, title="Lower Band")
```

```pine_script
// Create inputs vwapStartDate = input.time(timestamp("1 Jan 2022"), title="VWAP Start Date") vwapData = input.source(hlc3, title="VWAP Data Source")
```

```pine_script
if time >= vwapStartDate sumPrices += vwapData * volume sumVolume += volume powVolume += volume * math.pow(vwapData, 2)
```

- defval sets the input’s standard value. Thanks to this default, the script can calculate with the input even when we haven’t set it yet. There are two ways to set a default time:An integer time value, which is a milliseconds amount. This requires huge, cumbersome numbers to work with.The timestamp() function, which turns a literal time and date text into a time value for us. This is easy to read and quick to implement.
- An integer time value, which is a milliseconds amount. This requires huge, cumbersome numbers to work with.
- The timestamp() function, which turns a literal time and date text into a time value for us. This is easy to read and quick to implement.
- title is the input’s name. This text appears before the input in the script’s settings. It often describes what the input configures.
- tooltip says what text shows in a small pop-up window when the user hovers the mouse over the input’s tooltip icon.
- inline gives the input a text-based ID. TradingView shows inputs with the same ID on a single line in the script’s settings. Otherwise each input shows on its own line, one below the other.
- group sets the heading name above the input. When more than one input has the same heading text, TradingView groups them together as a category.
- confirm set to true shows the input’s interactive mode when the script is added to the chart. This guides the user to click on the chart and select a time and date that way.

- An integer time value, which is a milliseconds amount. This requires huge, cumbersome numbers to work with.
- The timestamp() function, which turns a literal time and date text into a time value for us. This is easy to read and quick to implement.

- The default for a time input cannot be the current time and date. That standard value has to be a constant, and that excludes the ever-changing current time.
- A time input’s default, when set with the timestamp() function, can also be a time and date in a particular time zone [1] [4] . TradingView automatically adjust that time and date into the chart’s time zone [4] .
- If we make two time inputs for a start and end date, a good idea is to set the end date several years in the future. This way the script always continues till the end of the chart in the next years. (Otherwise, we’d have to modify the script’s code every now and then.)
- In Pine Script version 4, we made time inputs with the input.time value for the input() function [2] . Besides this difference, time inputs didn’t change in behaviour. In Pine Script 3 and earlier, those inputs weren’t available [3] .

- The input.time() function adds a time and date option to the ‘Inputs’ tab of the script’s settings.
- input.time() has one required argument: a default value. That value is a literal milliseconds amount. Or the more convenient time and date value returned by the timestamp() function.
- We usually set title argument too; that creates a text label before the input with its name.
- Other useful arguments are group (adds a heading to the input) and tooltip, which creates a pop-up window with customised information.

1. TradingView (n.d. a). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on February 3, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView (n.d. b). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on February 3, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/
1. TradingView (n.d. c). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on February 3, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v3/
1. TradingView Blog (2020, December 1). New Parameter for Date Input Added to Pine. Retrieved on February 3, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/blog/en/new-parameter-for-date-input-added-to-pine-21812/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Inputs. Retrieved on February 3, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Inputs.html

lessons.title: Stop trading based on a maximum loss: TradingView’s
lessons.clean_text:
# Stop trading based on a maximum loss: TradingView’s

## Stop a TradingView strategy based on maximum drawdown

## Features of TradingView’s strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function

### Drawdown is calculated based on strategy equity

### Losses can be worse than the ‘maximum’ drawdown

### This risk management function stops all trading

### The risk management function is active all the time

### Cannot be set with a true/false condition

### But can be set with an input option

#### Set maximum cash drawdown with an input

#### Set maximum % loss with an input

### Only affects the current strategy script

## Examples of the strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function

### Example: stop trading after a drawdown of $2,500

### Example: see the maximum drawdown develop on the chart

## Strengths and weaknesses of strategy.risk.max_drawdown()

## Other built-in TradingView risk management functions

## Summary

### References

No matter how good a strategy is, each trading strategy has drawdowns. One way to limit that risk is to stop trading at a certain maximum drawdown level. Let’s see how we code that in TradingView with the strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function.

A strategy that loses a lot of money is a serious risk. Even when our strategy loses ‘just’ 40%, we’ll need to achieve returns greater than 66% just to break-even. Unsurprisingly, it’s easier to prevent a big loss than recover from it.

With the strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function our TradingView strategy automatically stops trading once a certain maximum drawdown is reached (TradingView, n.d.).

That function has the following default pattern (TradingView, n.d.):

Here’s what those arguments mean (TradingView, n.d.):

Once the maximum drawdown is reached, strategy.risk.max_drawdown() stops all trading.

So we can’t open new trades with the strategy.entry() function, but can neither use the strategy.order() function to submit trades. (This is noteworthy because not all risk management functions prevent strategy.order() from trading.)

Should we measure drawdown in percentage of equity, then strategy.risk.max_drawdown() also stops trading when strategy equity becomes negative (TradingView, n.d.). So the maximum allowed loss with the strategy.percent_of_equity setting is -100%. (With the strategy.cash option a negative strategy equity is allowed.)

Say we want to limit our loss to 5,000 in the currency of the chart’s instrument. For that we use the risk management function like so:

If we don’t want to lose more than 10% of equity, we use the function like this:

Let’s take a closer look at how strategy.risk.max_drawdown() behaves and what its features are.

TradingView measures the maximum drawdown with the strategy’s equity. That equity is the sum of the strategy’s initial capital, closed trade profit (including commission and slippage), and the profit/loss of the open position.

So drawdown is partly based on paper profits and losses, which we haven’t realised yet. It’s even possible for a strategy to be in significant drawdown when it doesn’t have a single closed losing trade.

Once our strategy reaches the maximum drawdown, strategy.risk.max_drawdown() closes the open position with a market order. That order can fill with slippage, which makes our ‘maximum’ drawdown worse than we wanted.

The market can also gap against our position. In that scenario we also get losses bigger than our ‘maximum’ drawdown. This makes it a good idea to set a smaller maximum drawdown than the biggest loss you want or can take.

Once our strategy reaches the maximum drawdown we defined with strategy.risk.max_drawdown(), the following happens (TradingView, n.d.; TradingView Wiki, 2018):

When we include strategy.risk.max_drawdown() in our strategy, it influences how the script behaves during each script calculation (TradingView Wiki, 2018). So our strategy will always respect the maximum drawdown defined by that risk management function.

That also means that the full backtest and all real-time signals are affected by strategy.risk.max_drawdown(). There’s no quick way to disable this risk management function. To turn it off we have to remove the strategy.risk.max_drawdown() line from our code, or comment it out with the Ctrl + / keyboard shortcut.

At this time, we cannot execute strategy.risk.max_drawdown() conditionally. So we can’t enable this function based on a condition with the if statement, like a close above a 20-bar exponential moving average:

Neither can we set the function’s arguments based on a condition, like so:

Both these code examples generate a TradingView error. And so once we use strategy.risk.max_drawdown(), the function uses the same maximum drawdown for the entire backtest period. This makes it impossible to adapt the risk management function based on strategy conditions or price volatility.

TradingView doesn’t have a manual setting for a strategy’s maximum drawdown at this time. But we can make our own input option. That way we can configure the maximum drawdown that strategy.risk.max_drawdown() uses without having to edit the strategy’s code each time. Let’s see how.

To set the maximum drawdown based on currency, we’ll need to make an integer or float input option. And then use that input’s current value with strategy.risk.max_drawdown(). Here’s an example:

Here we have the input.int() function make an integer input. We give this custom setting a default of 2,500 and track its value with the maxDd variable.

Then we call strategy.risk.max_drawdown(). For the maximum drawdown we use that maxDd input variable. And with type=strategy.cash that drawdown is based on the strategy’s cash.

To configure the strategy’s maximum drawdown as a percentage of equity we’ll also make an integer or floating-point input. And then use strategy.risk.max_drawdown() with that input’s value. For example:

Here the input.int() function first makes an integer input. We use that function’s minval and maxval arguments to set the option’s allowed range to 1-100.

Then we execute strategy.risk.max_drawdown() with the maxPercDd input variable. We use type=strategy.percent_of_equity to calculate the drawdown based on percentage of equity.

While the strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function affects the entire strategy script, its influence also stops there.

The risk management function doesn’t know how TradingView strategies on other charts perform. Neither does it know about manual trades we opened inside TradingView. Or the position we have at the broker.

So when strategy.risk.max_drawdown() stops our strategy from sending new orders, we can still trade the chart’s instrument from inside TradingView with manual orders. And strategies on other charts, even when they trade the same instrument, also remain active.

Now we know the characteristics of strategy.risk.max_drawdown(), let’s see how we use this risk management function in a complete TradingView strategy script.

The example script below trades 20-bar highest high and lowest low breakouts. We’ll also submit stops, and base them on the midpoint between those extreme prices. We use strategy.risk.max_drawdown() to make the strategy stop after 2,500 is lost.

Here’s the strategy’s code:

First we define the strategy’s settings with the strategy() function. Then we calculate the highest high and lowest low with the ta.highest() and ta.lowest() functions. We then use the plot() function to show those values as a circles plot.

Next we limit the strategy’s maximum drawdown:

To implement that money management rule we execute the strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function. We set its value argument to 2500 and use strategy.cash for the type argument. This makes the strategy stop after losing 2,500 or more in the currency of the chart’s instrument.

Then we submit two entry orders:

The first if statement uses the ta.crossover() function to see if the bar’s close crossed the 20-bar highest high. When it did, we call the strategy.entry() function to open a long trade (strategy.long).

The other if statement uses the ta.crossunder() function. That way we see if the close fell below the 20-bar lowest low. If that happened, we have strategy.entry() open a short trade (strategy.short).

Here’s the thing about these two if statements. While they only compare the close against the highest high or lowest low, they implicitly depend on the strategy’s drawdown. That’s because when our strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function activates, these strategy.entry() orders aren’t submitted anymore.

So when your if statements don’t behave like they should, it might also be that the code they execute is ‘disabled’ by a risk management function.

This is how the above example strategy looks on the chart:

Here the strategy reached its maximum drawdown on the 22th of the month. From that point on, no EUR/USD trades were generated. This prevented further strategy losses, but also made the 2,500 loss final.

The example strategy below trades a 20-bar and 50-bar Simple Moving Average (SMA). When the fast moving average crosses over the slow one, we go long. If the faster goes under the slower one, we short. With strategy.risk.max_drawdown() we limit the drawdown to 10,000 in the currency of the chart’s instrument.

We also plot the strategy’s maximum drawdown on the chart. This way we can see how the drawdown develops over time.

The strategy’s full code is:

We first define the strategy settings with the strategy() function. Then we calculate two moving average with the ta.sma() function.

Then we implement the risk management rule:

This has us execute the strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function. We set the function’s value argument to 10000. And with type=strategy.cash have the drawdown calculate on a currency amount. This way when losses reach 10,000 our strategy stops.

Next we trade the moving average crosses:

The first if statement has the ta.crossover() function check whether the fast moving average crossed above the slow one. When it did, we execute the strategy.entry() function to open a long trade (strategy.long).

The other if statement uses the ta.crossunder() function to see if the fast simple moving average fell under the slow one. In that case we have strategy.entry() open a short trade (strategy.short).

Now we make the code that tracks the strategy’s drawdown. First we calculate some values:

Here we make the maxEquity variable. Then we set the variable to the biggest value of either the strategy’s current equity (strategy.equity) or the previous bar value of maxEquity. This makes the maximum equity either increase on the current bar, or remain the same. (This way the variable behaves like a high watermark, the highest strategy equity ever achieved.)

To calculate the strategy’s current drawdown, we subtract that highest equity value (maxEquity) from the strategy’s current equity (strategy.equity). We track that outcome with the drawdown variable.

Next we display the strategy’s drawdown:

Here the plot() function displays the drawdown variable. We show those values as a regular line plot. And with the linewidth argument make that line a bit thicker than normal.

To help interpret that line we colour the plot conditionally. We have the conditional operator (?:) check if the current drawdown is greater than the previous bar value (drawdown > drawdown[1]). When it is, we make the plot green (color.green). When the drawdown remained the same or got more negative, we display that line in the red (color.red) colour.

Here’s how the strategy and its drawdown line look:

Because our strategy has no real edge, it’s no surprise that the drawdown line slowly ticks down. Then when the drawdown reaches $10,000 (the EuroStoxx CFD quotes in US dollar), TradingView sends an exit order to close the open long position.

But that market order filled at an unfavourable price. And so the strategy’s final drawdown is worse than we configured with strategy.risk.max_drawdown(). Once the strategy was flat, it didn’t execute a single trade anymore.

To recap this article, let’s see what strategy.risk.max_drawdown() does well and what its limitations are. The strong features of this risk management function are:

There are also a few limitations with strategy.risk.max_drawdown():

In this article we looked at how the strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function limits the strategy’s losses. But TradingView also has other risk management functions:

We can use each risk management rule separately, or combine several risk rules together. See the risk management category for an overview of ways to limit trading risks in TradingView.

The strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function stops our strategy when a predefined maximum drawdown is reached.

More precisely, when the drawdown limit is reached TradingView first cancels all pending orders. Then it submits a market order to close the open position. After that TradingView prevents that the strategy generates any trade during the backtest period and real-time signals.

The strategy.risk.max_drawdown() function has two arguments. The first, value, specifies how much we’re willing to lose at most. The other, type, defines how to measure the maximum drawdown. It’s either based on a percentage of strategy equity (strategy.percent_of_equity) or a cash amount in the currency of the chart’s instrument (strategy.cash).

strategy.risk.max_drawdown() measures a drawdown based on how the strategy’s equity develops. That equity is the sum of initial capital, closed trades profit, and open position profit/loss. Because of that latter, the strategy doesn’t need to close trades before the maximum drawdown changes. All it takes is an open position that’s gets (more) unprofitable.

We can’t set strategy.risk.max_drawdown() conditionally. It remains active during the entire backtest, and also uses the same maximum drawdown for each bar.

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on April 15, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

TradingView Wiki (2018, February 19). Strategies. Retrieved on April 14, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/wiki/Strategies

```pine_script
strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value, type)
```

```pine_script
strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value=5000, type=strategy.cash)
```

```pine_script
strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value=10, type=strategy.percent_of_equity)
```

```pine_script
if close > ta.ema(close, 20) strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value=2000, type=strategy.cash)
```

```pine_script
strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value=close > ta.ema(close, 20) ? 2000 : 5000, type=strategy.cash)
```

```pine_script
// Input that sets the strategy's maximum drawdown (in currency) maxDd = input.int(2500, title="Max Drawdown (Cash)", minval=1) // Use the input's value with the maximum drawdown function strategy.risk.max_drawdown(maxDd, type=strategy.cash)
```

```pine_script
// Input for the strategy's maximum drawdown (in % of strategy equity) maxPercDd = input.int(10, title="Max Drawdown (%)", minval=1, maxval=100) // Use the input's value with the maximum drawdown function strategy.risk.max_drawdown(maxPercDd, type=strategy.percent_of_equity)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Max Drawdown example", overlay=true) // Calculate highest high and lowest low hiHighs = ta.highest(high, 20)[1] loLows = ta.lowest(low, 20)[1] midPoint = (hiHighs + loLows) / 2 // Show 20-bar highs and lows on the chart plot(hiHighs, style=plot.style_circles, color=color.green, linewidth=2) plot(loLows, style=plot.style_circles, color=color.red, linewidth=2) plot(midPoint, color=color.orange) // Stop trading after losing more than 2,500 currency strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value=2500, type=strategy.cash) // Submit entry orders if ta.crossover(close, hiHighs) strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long, qty=50000) if ta.crossunder(close, loLows) strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short, qty=50000) // Submit stop orders if strategy.position_size > 0 strategy.exit("XL", stop=midPoint) if strategy.position_size < 0 strategy.exit("XS", stop=midPoint)
```

```pine_script
// Stop trading after losing more than 2,500 currency strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value=2500, type=strategy.cash)
```

```pine_script
// Submit entry orders if ta.crossover(close, hiHighs) strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long, qty=50000) if ta.crossunder(close, loLows) strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short, qty=50000)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Limit the strategy drawdown", overlay=false) // Calculate moving averages fastMA = ta.sma(close, 20) slowMA = ta.sma(close, 50) // Limit the maximum drawdown to 10k strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value=10000, type=strategy.cash) // Submit orders if ta.crossover(fastMA, slowMA) strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long, qty=10) if ta.crossunder(fastMA, slowMA) strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short, qty=10) // Calculate strategy drawdown maxEquity = 0.0 maxEquity := math.max(strategy.equity, nz(maxEquity[1])) drawdown = strategy.equity - maxEquity // Plot the strategy's drawdown plot(drawdown, linewidth=2, color=drawdown > drawdown[1] ? color.green : color.red)
```

```pine_script
// Limit the maximum drawdown to 10k strategy.risk.max_drawdown(value=10000, type=strategy.cash)
```

```pine_script
// Submit orders if ta.crossover(fastMA, slowMA) strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long, qty=10) if ta.crossunder(fastMA, slowMA) strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short, qty=10)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate strategy drawdown maxEquity = 0.0 maxEquity := math.max(strategy.equity, nz(maxEquity[1])) drawdown = strategy.equity - maxEquity
```

```pine_script
// Plot the strategy's drawdown plot(drawdown, linewidth=2, color=drawdown > drawdown[1] ? color.green : color.red)
```

| Argument | Description |
| --- | --- |
| value | Required float argument that sets the maximum drawdown. This value is either in the currency of the chart’s instrument. Or it’s a percentage of equity, with an allowed range of 0 till 100. |
| type | Required argument that specifies how the drawdown is measured. Possible values are strategy.cash (for a drawdown measured in money lost) or strategy.percent_of_equity. That latter measures the drawdown in percentage of equity lost. |

- All pending strategy orders are cancelled.
- If the strategy has an open position, TradingView submits a market order to close that entire position.
- After that the strategy will not submit a trade anymore.

- strategy.risk.max_drawdown() makes it easy to limit the strategy’s loss based on cash or strategy equity.
- The function virtually guarantees that trading actually stops when our strategy reaches its maximum drawdown. (If we code something ourselves, there’s always the chance of a mistake.)
