- When we set this risk management function with an input option, we can easily tweak the maximum drawdown value without having to edit the strategy’s code.

- This risk management function can’t be set with a condition. It instead keeps active for the entire backtest period, and also uses the same maximum drawdown value every time. This way we can’t account for changes in strategy conditions and price volatility.
- The risk management function stops trading for the entire backtest period and all real-time signals. That doesn’t give the strategy a chance to recover. It instead guarantees that the maximum drawdown is final. This approach is too harsh when our strategy only temporarily suffers due to unexpected market conditions.
- strategy.risk.max_drawdown() doesn’t consider the number of trades. A loss of $2,500 with 3 trades might be anomaly (like a flash crash or central bank intervention), but the same loss over 75 trades definitely signals a problem with the strategy’s edge.

- With TradingView’s strategy.risk.allow_entry_in() function we disable long or short entries. That way our script only trades from one side.
- The strategy.risk.max_cons_loss_days() function limits the number of losing days in a row. Once that number is reached, TradingView stops the strategy.
- With strategy.risk.max_position_size() we limit the strategy’s maximum position size. This prevents that our script trades too enthusiastic.
- The strategy.risk.max_intraday_filled_orders() function limits the number of intra-day trades. When that number is reached, TradingView calls it a day and activates the strategy again tomorrow.
- And the strategy.risk.max_intraday_loss() function limits the strategy’s maximum intra-day loss. When that drawdown is reached, the strategy gets a fresh start the next day.

lessons.title: Configure with Pine Script how TradingView strategies calculate order size
lessons.clean_text:
# Configure with Pine Script how TradingView strategies calculate order size

## Default pattern

### Fixed quantity

### Cash amount

### Percentage of equity

## Features of default_qty_type

## Default order size isn’t position size

## Example strategy

### Example charts

## Manual setting for default order type

## Summary

### References

Pine Script’s default_qty_type setting defines how the strategy calculates the default order size [see 1] .

The possible values for default_qty_type are [1] :

We usually combine the default_qty_type setting with Pine Script’s default_qty_value setting. Their difference is:

To say how our strategy should calculate its default order size, we set the default_qty_type argument of the strategy() function. There are three ways to do that.

To set the default order size to a fixed quantity, we give the default_qty_type setting the strategy.fixed value:

This is, however, not necessary: strategy.fixed is already the default for default_qty_type [1] . If we leave it out, we get the same result:

Sometimes it’s useful to set strategy.fixed explicitly. It tells the readers of our code that we picked that default order type deliberately, and not accidentally forgot to change the default.

To size orders with a certain cash amount, we set the default_qty_type setting to strategy.cash:

And to base the default order size on a percentage of strategy equity, we give the default_qty_type setting the value of strategy.percent_of_equity:

Order size and position size aren’t always the same thing.

They can be, when we don’t use pyramiding. With only one entry per long or short direction, that entry order is the entire position. Which makes the size of that order also the position size. And the default_qty_type setting then determines the position size.

But that doesn’t apply when we scale into positions. With pyramiding enabled, TradingView executes the default order size several times. That makes the position size a multiple of the default order size. The total number of entries in the same direction then determines what the strategy’s total position size is.

To prevent that the position size becomes too big, we adjust the default order quantity (default_qty_value) to the maximum number of entries in the same direction. Say we want to invest 2,500 cash per position and set pyramiding to 5. In that case, default_qty_value has to be 500. When the five pyramid orders fill, the entire position size is about 2,500 currency.

Let’s see how a strategy can use the default_qty_type setting. The script below trades two Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs). When the fast average crosses above the slow one, we open a long trade. And when the fast average drops below the slow one, we short the market.

We first use the strategy with a fixed quantity per order. Later we switch to a percentage of equity and see how that changes the strategy.

The strategy’s code is:

We first configure the strategy with the strategy() function. That names the strategy. And overlays it on the chart’s instrument. The default_qty_value setting set to 10 makes the strategy trade 10 contracts, shares, units, or lots per order. (Because we don’t set default_qty_type yet, that default size is a fixed quantity.)

Then the ta.ema() function calculates two EMAs. The first has a length of 7 bars and the other uses a period of 21 bars. Both calculate with close prices (close).

Next the plot() function shows both moving averages on the chart. Because we don’t set the plot type, they display as regular line plots. Their colours are orange (color.orange) and teal (color.teal).

Two if statements submit the strategy’s orders. The first has the ta.crossover() function see if the fast average crossed above the slow one. When it did, the if code runs. That has the strategy.entry() function open a long trade (strategy.long).

The other if statement uses the ta.crossunder() function. That’s how it sees if the fast average dropped below the slow one. In that scenario, strategy.entry() opens a short trade (strategy.short).

Let’s see how the strategy looks on the chart. Because we haven’t set the default order type, the strategy uses a fixed quantity per trade. With the default_qty_value setting we set that amount to 10 contracts, shares, units, or lots. And so each order trades 10 contracts:

(The chart shows 20 contracts because the order first closes the open 10 contracts, and then opens another 10 in the opposite direction. )

Now let’s change the default order type. To do that we add the default_qty_type setting to the strategy() function, with a value of strategy.percent_of_equity:

This makes the strategy invest 10% of equity in each order. After we save this code change, TradingView recalculates the strategy. The script still trades the same setups, but with larger order sizes:

Besides the default_qty_type setting we can also configure the default order type by hand.

We do that in the ‘Properties’ tab of the strategy’s settings window. There the ‘Order size’ setting defines the standard order size. With the second input field (the drop-down menu) we choose how the default order size is calculated:

When we use this manual option, we override the value of the default_qty_type setting.

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="My Strategy", default_qty_type=strategy.fixed, default_qty_value=5)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="My Strategy", default_qty_value=5) // (Strategy now uses fixed quantity by default)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="My Strategy", default_qty_type=strategy.cash, default_qty_value=5000)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="My Strategy", default_qty_type=strategy.percent_of_equity, default_qty_value=15)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Default quantity type example", overlay=true, default_qty_value=10) // Calculate and plot EMAs fastEMA = ta.ema(close, 7) slowEMA = ta.ema(close, 21) plot(fastEMA, color=color.orange, linewidth=2, title="Fast EMA") plot(slowEMA, color=color.teal, linewidth=2, title="Slow EMA") // Generate trades if ta.crossover(fastEMA, slowEMA) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if ta.crossunder(fastEMA, slowEMA) strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

```pine_script
strategy(title="Default quantity type example", overlay=true, default_qty_value=10, default_qty_type=strategy.percent_of_equity)
```

- strategy.fixed sizes orders based on a fixed quantity. This has every order use the same size, like 10 contracts or 250 shares.
- strategy.cash sizes orders based on a certain amount of cash. Such as $5,000 or €10,000. That cash amount divided by the instrument’s price is the order size.
- strategy.percent_of_equity sizes orders based on a percentage of strategy equity. Like 10 or 25 percent. That portion of the strategy’s equity divided by the instrument’s price is the order size.

- default_qty_type says how the default order size is calculated.
- default_qty_value says what quantity the default order size has.

- The default_qty_type setting requires one of the constant values discussed above. It cannot use a dynamic value. As a result the strategy uses the same default order type during the entire backtest. (We can, of course, calculate dynamic order sizes ourselves.)
- The default_qty_value setting has a default of 1. If we set the default order type with default_qty_type, we got to change default_qty_value too. Else our strategy uses a default size of 1 currency or 1% equity, and that’s often not enough to open a trade.

- The default_qty_type setting says how the strategy should calculate its default order size.
- Pine Script has three default order types: fixed quantity (strategy.fixed), cash per trade (strategy.cash), and percentage of equity (strategy.percent_of_equity).
- While default_qty_type says how the default order size is calculated, it’s the default_qty_value setting that says what the default order size is.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on December 1, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/

lessons.title: How to size TradingView labels with a Pine Script input?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to size TradingView labels with a Pine Script input?

## Steps required

### Step 1: Make input option

### Step 2: Turn input into size.*

### Step 3: Set label size

#### Option 1: New label

#### Option 2: Existing label

## Example script

### Example images

## Summary

Pine Script labels can use various sizes. Usually the indicator or strategy defines in its code what label size to use. But sometimes it’s useful to configure this size ourselves, by hand in the script settings. How to do that?

To make that happen we create an input option. When that input specifies the label size, other code can use the input’s value to size labels. When the script runs on the chart, we then easily change the label size through the input settings.

There are several ways to implement this. One approach is with a drop-down menu. Let’s see how we make that.

To make a drop-down input that sets label size, we do the following:

Let’s go through these steps and explore their code.

The input allows the script user to change the label size by hand. To make one we use the input.string() function:

This code calls input.string() with three arguments. The first, "default", is the input’s standard value. When the user doesn’t configure the input, this value is what the script uses instead.

With the title argument we name the input. This text appears before the input in the script’s settings window.

The options argument specifies a list of values to choose from. This makes the input show a drop-down menu. We include values that range from ‘very small’ to ‘very large’.

By the way, these size descriptions can be any string, as long as each size has its own unique name. We can use a different language than English and even use icons and emojis for the sizes. Neither are 5 different sizes required; if our script only needs three label sizes, then we define just three in the options list. (Just keep in mind that the default value of input.string() always has to be a value that appears in the options list.)

We store the input’s value in the labelSizeInput variable. Later code uses this variable to access the input’s current value.

In the previous step, we made a drop-down input with several string values for label sizes. There’s one problem though; label sizes don’t use strings but size.* constants instead (such as size.normal and size.huge).

So in this second step, we convert the input’s value into a size.* value:

This switch statement processes the labelSizeInput variable of the previous step. This variable holds whichever value the input’s drop-down menu is currently set to.

In the switch statement, Pine Script matches each possible value of that input option with a corresponding size.* constant. This maps the drop-down items to their proper size. "small", for instance, becomes size.small and "very large" is turned into size.huge.

So the switch statement picks the right size.* value based on the input’s current value (taken from the labelSizeInput variable). It returns that size.* value, which we store in the labelSizeOption variable for the next step.

At this point we have a variable with a size.* label size that matches the input option’s value. Now we use that variable with a label function so the chosen size actually shows on the chart.

There are two ways to implement this. We can make a new label with the size configured in the input. Or we can change an existing label’s size to that of the input.

That first approach has the label start off with the right size. This is what we typically use. The alternative is useful when we’d like to change the label size in a specific situation (meaning, when a certain condition happened).

To make a new label with the size set by the input, we do:

Here the label.new() function draws a new label. This label appears at the high of 7 bars ago (bar_index[7], high[7]). Its colour is orange (color.orange) with white (color.white) letters.

For the label size we use the labelSizeOption variable. This variable is what the switch statement above set to a size.* constant.

The label’s text shows the value of the labelSizeInput variable. We made this input variable in the first step. This lets the label show which value from the drop-down menu we choose.

The other option is to change an existing label to the input’s size:

Here the label.new() function first makes a new label. We place that label at the high of 20 bars ago (bar_index[20], high[20]). We set the colour of this label to teal (color.teal).

We store the label identifier that label.new() returns in the myLabel variable. This way we can access the label later.

Next we call the label.set_size() function to change the size of an existing label. We pass this function two arguments. The first, myLabel, is the variable with the label identifier. That way the function knows which label to modify. The other, labelSizeOption, is the variable with the size.* value from the input. That’s how the input’s value changes the label size.

The indicator below combines the three steps above into a single script. The code makes two labels. Both have their size set with the input option. To translate that input into something the labels can use, we follow the three steps we discussed above.

The indicator’s entire code is:

We start with the indicator() function. That configures the script. title names the script. And overlay shows the indicator overlaid on the chart’s instrument.

Then we make the input option with the input.string() function. We store its current value in the labelSizeInput variable.

Next a switch statement determines the size.* constant based on that variable’s value. The value switch picks is what we store in the labelSizeOption variable.

Then an if statement checks if the script processes the last historical bar (barstate.islastconfirmedhistory). That bar only happens once on the chart. This way the if statement’s code, and the labels it makes, only runs once.

Inside that if block the label.new() function makes two labels. The first one starts with the label size set by the input. The other label later has its size changed with the label.set_size() function.

Let’s see how the above script looks. The input appears like so:

When we add the indicator to the chart, it uses the ‘default’ label size by default. That has the two example label show as follows:

If we use the input to change the label size to ’large’, the labels on the chart change to:

```pine_script
// STEP 1. Make a text-based input with different label sizes labelSizeInput = input.string("default", title="Label Size", options=["very small", "small", "default", "large", "very large"])
```

```pine_script
// STEP 2. Translate the input's possible values into size.* constants labelSizeOption = switch labelSizeInput "very small" => size.tiny "small" => size.small "default" => size.normal "large" => size.large "very large" => size.huge
```

```pine_script
// Make a new label with the size derived from the input label.new(bar_index[7], high[7], color=color.orange, textcolor=color.white, size=labelSizeOption, text="Label size:\n" + labelSizeInput)
```

```pine_script
// Or customise an existing label's size to that of the input myLabel = label.new(bar_index[20], high[20], color=color.teal) label.set_size(myLabel, labelSizeOption)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Set label size with input (elaborate)", overlay=true) // STEP 1. Make a text-based input with different label sizes labelSizeInput = input.string("default", title="Label Size", options=["very small", "small", "default", "large", "very large"]) // STEP 2. Translate the input's possible values into size.* constants labelSizeOption = switch labelSizeInput "very small" => size.tiny "small" => size.small "default" => size.normal "large" => size.large "very large" => size.huge // STEP 3. Use that translated size value with the Pine Script label if barstate.islastconfirmedhistory // Make a new label with the size derived from the input label.new(bar_index[7], high[7], color=color.orange, textcolor=color.white, size=labelSizeOption, text="Label size:\n" + labelSizeInput) // Or customise an existing label's size to that of the input myLabel = label.new(bar_index[20], high[20], color=color.teal) label.set_size(myLabel, labelSizeOption)
```

1. Make a string input with label sizes in a drop-down menu.
1. Turn the drop-down menu’s value into a proper label size value.
1. Use that converted value to change the label size with:The label.new() function when we make a label.Or the label.set_size() function to change an existing label’s size.
1. The label.new() function when we make a label.
1. Or the label.set_size() function to change an existing label’s size.

- The label.new() function when we make a label.
- Or the label.set_size() function to change an existing label’s size.

- To configure a label size with an input, we do three things.
- First make a string input with a drop-down list. This defines the different sizes to choose from.
- Then turn the input’s string value into a proper label size value.
- And finally use that label size with the label.new() or label.set_size() function.

# TradingView Pine Script programming tutorials

There are 40 chapters, 506 lessons, 1440 images, and 872k words in total.

Hi there, I’m Jos! On TradingCode I combine my passion for programming with my interest in financial markets.

I write about things I’ve learned to help others succeed. Trading is challenging enough already; hopefully, I can help you with the programming part.

I got started programming trading scripts back in 2011. Since then I’ve coded plenty of indicators and strategies.

I wrote my first TradingView script back in 2014. In the time since I coded hundreds of indicators and strategies in Pine Script.

lessons.title: Set TradingView label text with Pine Script code: here’s how
lessons.clean_text:
# Set TradingView label text with Pine Script code: here’s how

## Default pattern

## Quick example

### Example chart

### Keyword arguments

## Ways to use label.set_text()

## Example script

## Features of label.set_text()

## Summary

### References

After our Pine Script code made a label, the label.set_text() function sets the text of that label [1] [2] .

When the label doesn’t have text already, label.set_text() gives the label text to show. And if the label already got text, then this function overwrites that text.

To do its work, the function has to know which label to modify and what text to use. Let’s take a closer look.

The function’s standard pattern is [1] :

The label.set_text() function doesn’t return a value we can use or inspect.

Let’s see how we work with the label.set_text() function. Before we can modify a label’s text, we a label first. So let’s make one:

Here the label.new() function draws a label at the close price (close), three bars to the right of the current bar (bar_index + 3). The label’s background is black (color.black) with white (color.white) letters. The label style points left (label.style_label_left) and its size is bigger than normal (size.large).

We store the identifier that label.new() returns in a variable. That way we can access the label later.

Next we set the label text with the label.set_text() function:

We call label.set_text() with two arguments. The first, myLabel, is the variable with the label identifier. This tells the function which label to modify.

The other argument is a string. This text is what the label’s new text should become. We use a literal string combined (+) with the bar’s close price (close).

Since that price is a number, while label.set_text() expects a string, we convert the number to a textual string with Pine Script’s str.tostring() function. That makes it compatible with what label.set_text() requires.

The label that the quick example above makes looks like so:

Here’s how we combine the example code snippets above into a full script:

First we call the indicator() function. That names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Next an if statement looks if the script processes the last price bar (barstate.islast). When it does, Pine Script executes the code we talked about above: label.new() makes a new label and then label.set_text() sets that label’s text.

The id and text keyword arguments of label.set_text() are optional. I’ve added them to the examples above to clarify what the function does. But we can leave them out. That reduces the keyboard typing required.

Without the optional id and text keyword arguments, label.set_text() looks like:

The label.set_text() function can do three things:

If we want to set a label’s text just once, and that text won’t change, then we could also set that text with the label.new() function when we make the label. Doing so requires less code typing.

There are two ways to execute label.set_text(). We can place the function inside an if statement or switch statement. That only executes the function, and thus update the label’s text, in particular situations.

The alternative is to call the function on every bar. With no condition that filters its execution, label.set_text() updates the label’s text all the time. This way the label can show the latest price or indicator value.

To get the label’s text, rather than set it, we use the label.get_text() function.

Let’s learn how label.set_text() works in a full script. The indicator below plots the Money Flow Index (MFI). When this oscillator crosses above 80, we make a sell signal label. If it goes below 20, we draw a label with a buy signal.

We’re primarily interested in the latest buy and sell signal. So when a new signal happens, we have label.set_text() remove the text of the previous label. This way only the last ‘Buy’ and ‘Sell’ texts show on the chart.

The indicator’s code is:

We begin with the indicator() function. That configures the script. With title we name the script.

Next the ta.mfi() function calculates the Money Flow Index (MFI). Its data is the high, low, and close average (hlc3) and a length of 14 bars.

Then the plot() function shows the MFI on the chart. We don’t set the plot type. So Pine Script make a regular line plot by default. This line shows in teal (color.teal).

After that the hline() function makes two horizontal plots. These show the MFI overbought and oversold levels. We place those at 80 and 20.

Next we make two persistent variables with the var keyword:

These mfiBuyLabel and mfiSellLabel label variables will later hold the label identifiers. The reason to make them var variables is so they repeat their value on each bar (unless we update the variable to a different value).

Based on how we use these variables later, their previous bar value (mfiBuyLabel[1] and mfiSellLabel[1]) returns the identifier of the previous buy and sell label, respectively.

And the reason we declare these variables before the if statements that use them, is so that Pine Script can properly store the variable’s value on each bar.

Then we make the first Money Flow Index-based label:

This if statement lets the ta.crossover() function check if the MFI (mfiValue) crossed above 80. When it did, code first makes a new label with the label.new() function.

That label shows at the current bar’s Money Flow Index value (bar_index, mfiValue). Because earlier the indicator() function didn’t overlay the script, this label appears in a chart panel below the chart’s instrument.

The label’s colour is #FDBCB4, the hexadecimal colour value for melon red. With the text argument the label’s text becomes ‘Sell’.

That message should only show with the most recent label, and not with the previous ones. So the label.set_text() function removes the text from the last label. We give the function two arguments. The first, mfiSellLabel[1], returns the identifier of the previous sell label.

This works because a var variable repeats its last value on each bar (unless we update the variable to a different value). And so the previous value of mfiSellLabel returns the identifier of when we made the last sell label.

The other label.set_text() argument is an empty string (""). This removes the text from that previous label.

Since we remove the previous label’s text each time we make a new label, all previous labels are without the ‘Sell’ text message.

Then we look for the Money Flow Index buy signal:

This if statement has the ta.crossunder() function see if the MFI crossed below 20. The script does two things when it did.

First the label.new() function makes a new label. This label shows at the current bar’s MFI (bar_index, mfiValue). Its style points up (label.style_label_up). The label colour is the hexadecimal colour for tea green (#D0F0C0). ‘Buy’ is the label’s text.

Since the previous label shouldn’t keep its text, we call label.set_text(). We get the identifier of that last label with mfiBuyLabel[1]. With an empty string ("") the function removes that label’s text. This way only the last buy signal label shows the text ‘Buy’.

The chart shows ‘Buy’ and ‘Sell’ texts for the two most recent Money Flow Index cross signals. The earlier signals that happened before the last two don’t have text, and instead show as empty labels:

```pine_script
label.set_text(id, text)
```

```pine_script
// Create a label three bars to the right. It appears in black & // white and points to the left. myLabel = label.new(x=bar_index + 3, y=close, color=color.black, textcolor=color.white, style=label.style_label_left, size=size.large)
```

```pine_script
// Update the label's text to include the current close price label.set_text(id=myLabel, text="Last price: " + str.tostring(close))
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Quick label.set_text() example", overlay=true) // On the chart's last bar, make a label and set its text if barstate.islast // Create a label three bars to the right. It appears in black & // white and points to the left. myLabel = label.new(x=bar_index + 3, y=close, color=color.black, textcolor=color.white, style=label.style_label_left, size=size.large) // Update the label's text to include the current close price label.set_text(id=myLabel, text="Last price: " + str.tostring(close))
```

```pine_script
// Update the label's text to include the current close price label.set_text(myLabel, "Last price: " + str.tostring(close)) // ^ ^ without optional 'id=' and 'text='
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Change label's text") // Calculate and plot the Money Flow Index mfiValue = ta.mfi(hlc3, 14) plot(mfiValue, color=color.teal, title="Money Flow Index") hline(80, title="Overbought") hline(20, title="Oversold") // Create persistent label variables (needed to access past labels) var label mfiBuyLabel = na var label mfiSellLabel = na // When the MFI crosses above 80, make a sell label if ta.crossover(mfiValue, 80) mfiSellLabel := label.new(bar_index, mfiValue, color=#FDBCB4, text="Sell") // Remove text of the previous sell signal label.set_text(mfiSellLabel[1], "") // Create a buy label when the MFI moves below 20 if ta.crossunder(mfiValue, 20) mfiBuyLabel := label.new(bar_index, mfiValue, style=label.style_label_up, color=#D0F0C0, text="Buy") // Remove text of the previous buy signal label.set_text(mfiBuyLabel[1], "")
```

```pine_script
// Create persistent label variables (needed to access past labels) var label mfiBuyLabel = na var label mfiSellLabel = na
```

```pine_script
// When the MFI crosses above 80, make a sell label if ta.crossover(mfiValue, 80) mfiSellLabel := label.new(bar_index, mfiValue, color=#FDBCB4, text="Sell") // Remove text of the previous sell signal label.set_text(mfiSellLabel[1], "")
```

```pine_script
// Create a buy label when the MFI moves below 20 if ta.crossunder(mfiValue, 20) mfiBuyLabel := label.new(bar_index, mfiValue, style=label.style_label_up, color=#D0F0C0, text="Buy") // Remove text of the previous buy signal label.set_text(mfiBuyLabel[1], "")
```

- id defines the identifier of the label whose text we want to modify.Thanks to this identifier the function knows which label to access. Without an identifier, Pine Script doesn’t understand which label we want to change.The label.new() function returns this identifier when we make the label.When this argument is (accidentally) the na value, the function can’t identify the label. It won’t change the text of a label then. But at least the function doesn’t error with na.
- Thanks to this identifier the function knows which label to access. Without an identifier, Pine Script doesn’t understand which label we want to change.
- The label.new() function returns this identifier when we make the label.
- When this argument is (accidentally) the na value, the function can’t identify the label. It won’t change the text of a label then. But at least the function doesn’t error with na.
- text is a string (piece of text) with the label’s new text.The maximum label text length is 4,096 characters. With a longer string, Pine Script triggers the ‘string is too long’ error.If this argument is the na value or an empty string (""), then the function removes the label existing text. That is, it empties the label’s text content.
- The maximum label text length is 4,096 characters. With a longer string, Pine Script triggers the ‘string is too long’ error.
- If this argument is the na value or an empty string (""), then the function removes the label existing text. That is, it empties the label’s text content.

- Thanks to this identifier the function knows which label to access. Without an identifier, Pine Script doesn’t understand which label we want to change.
- The label.new() function returns this identifier when we make the label.
- When this argument is (accidentally) the na value, the function can’t identify the label. It won’t change the text of a label then. But at least the function doesn’t error with na.

- The maximum label text length is 4,096 characters. With a longer string, Pine Script triggers the ‘string is too long’ error.
- If this argument is the na value or an empty string (""), then the function removes the label existing text. That is, it empties the label’s text content.

- When the label doesn’t have text, this function gives the label text to show.
- When the label has text, the function can change that text to something else.
- And the function can remove the label’s entire text.

- Pine Script labels don’t automatically wrap their text when the lines are long. We do that ourselves when we strategically place the newline character (\n) in the text. Wherever that character appears, Pine Script adds a new line (that is, the equivalent of pressing the Enter key).
- The maximum length for the label’s text is 4,096 characters. While Pine Script sometimes allows a longer string, it’s unknown if those exceptions remain and in which situations they apply. It’s therefore best to keep the string length below 4 thousand characters. If the string is too long, Pine Script generates the ‘string is too long’ error message (and we cannot use the script).
- We cannot control a label’s font. This includes the font family, font size, and font style (bold or italic, for instance). These style decisions are made by TradingView. What we can do is change the label size with the label.set_size() function. When the label becomes bigger or smaller, the text’s font size grows or shrinks with it.

- The label.set_text() function sets the text content of a specified label.
- The function needs two things. First a label identifier that tells which label to modify. We get this identifier from the label.new() function when we make the label.
- The other argument is a piece of text (string) with the label’s new content.
- A label text’s maximum length is 4,096 characters. With the \n escape character we include newlines in this string.
- To remove a label’s text, label.set_text() sets the text to an empty string ("").

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on August 3, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Text and shapes. Retrieved on August 3, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Text_and_shapes.html

lessons.title: How to use plot placeholders with TradingView’s strategy alerts?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to use plot placeholders with TradingView’s strategy alerts?

## Plot placeholders with strategy alerts

## Two plot placeholder options

## Plot placeholders with strategy alerts

### Plot placeholders in ‘Create Alert’

### Plot placeholders with //@strategy_alert_message

### Full script

## Limitation of plot placeholders

## Summary

### References

One way to make strategy alerts more dynamic are plot placeholders. These include a plot’s current value in an alert message. That lets us, for example, show stop values and target levels when the strategy generates a new alert with a simulated order fill. Let’s take a closer look.

Plot placeholders are pieces of text between double braces ({{ and }}) inside an alert message. When the alert triggers, TradingView replaces the plot placeholder with the dynamic value of the corresponding plot. That is one way to get dynamic numbers in an alert message.

Plot placeholders also work with alert conditions that an indicator makes. If you used them before with an indicator, you’ll know how they work with strategies. For TradingView it makes no difference if we use plot placeholders with an indicator or strategy alert.

As discussed in the plot placeholders lesson, there are two ways to include a plot’s value in an alert message [1] [2] [3] :

Both these styles include the plot’s current value in the alert message. Let’s see how that works.

There are two ways to use plot placeholders in strategy alerts:

Let’s take a closer look at each.

The first option is to type plot placeholders in the ‘Create Alert’ window when we make the strategy alert. This works with any strategy script that plots data. And it doesn’t require us to make code changes.

Say we want to use this alert message text with our strategy alerts:

This message includes the value of the plot named ‘SuperTrend’.

When we don’t know the strategy’s plot names, we open the chart’s ‘Data Window’. There we see the name of each plot:

First we click on the ‘Data Window’ icon in the right-hand side bar. Then we look for the script’s name in the ‘Data Window’. Here that is ‘My example script’. Finally we look for the plot names. In the image above the script has three plots: ‘Fast’, ‘Medium’, and ‘Slow’.

To type plot names in the strategy alert message, we first add the strategy script to the chart. Then we open the chart’s ‘Create Alert’ window. There we first select the strategy’s name in the ‘Condition’ field.

Then in the ‘Message’ field we type the earlier message text. Next we click ‘Create’ to make this alert.

Now each time the strategy simulates an order fill, an alert message shows with the value from the SuperTrend plot:

The second way to use plot placeholders is with the //@strategy_alert_message comment.

We place that comment somewhere in our strategy script. TradingView then uses its value for the default alert message text that hows in the ‘Create Alert’ window.

When that comment includes plot placeholders, we set up a standard alert text that includes the current value of plots. This makes it easier and faster to configure the alert later. That’s because it removes the need to customise the alert text by hand each time.

Let’s assume we want to use this alert message:

To know what plot names to use, we either look in the strategy code to what title argument the plot functions use. Or we add the strategy to the chart and inspect the ‘Data Window’. (Like we did above with typing the plot placeholders manually.)

To use the above message as the default alert text, we add the //@strategy_alert_message comment somewhere to our strategy script. (A good place is between the version comment and the strategy() function.) And we set that comment to what the default alert message should be.

Here’s how that looks:

Next we save the strategy script and add it to the chart (when it wasn’t already there). Then we open the chart’s ‘Create Alert’ window.

In that window’s ‘Condition’ field we select the name of the strategy script. Once we do, TradingView pre-fills the alert message to the text we gave the //@strategy_alert_message comment. Now all there’s left to do is click the ‘Create’ button to make the alert:

Then when the strategy simulates an order fill, TradingView generates an alert message like:

Here we see that TradingView replaced both placeholders in the alert message with their corresponding dynamic values.

The entire script used in the example above is:

To set the default alert message we first use the //@strategy_alert_message comment. Then we configure the script with the strategy() function. This names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Next the ta.highest() function calculate the 20-bar highest high. And the ta.lowest() function returns the lowest low over the same period. We store these values in the highestHigh and lowestLow variables.

Then the plot() function plots both values on the chart. The highest high shows in green (color.green) and red (color.red) is the lowest low colour. We don’t set the plot type. So Pine Script makes regular line plots by default.

After that we generate the strategy orders. First an if statement tests if the bar’s high (high) is above the 20-bar highest high. When it is, the strategy.entry() function opens a long trade (strategy.long).

The other if statement looks if the bar’s low (low) is below the 20-bar lowest low. In that scenario, strategy.entry() opens a short trade (strategy.short).

On the chart, the strategy looks like:

Plot placeholders have an important disadvantage: they don’t work inside text of the alert_message argument of order functions. When we use a plot placeholder there, its literal text shows in the alert message. And so TradingView won’t substitute the placeholder with the current plot value.

That’s unfortunate because strategy alerts from order functions have several benefits. They let us make different alert messages for different orders. That’s how we use one alert message for entry orders and another message with exit orders. Or use special messages with profit target exits and stop-loss exits.

Luckily, strategy alerts we create from the alert_message argument can include any dynamic value, provided we convert that value into a string. That’s how we include any value the strategy script has access to in the alert message. And that way we don’t have to use plot placeholders to insert dynamic values into alert messages.

```pine_script
New {{ticker}} signal. Stop is {{plot("SuperTrend")}}.
```

```pine_script
New {{ticker}} entry signal. Last price is {{close}}. Highest high at {{plot("Highest High")}} and lowest low at {{plot("Lowest Low")}}.
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 //@strategy_alert_message New {{ticker}} entry signal. Last price is {{close}}. Highest high at {{plot("Highest High")}} and lowest low at {{plot("Lowest Low")}}. strategy(title="Plot placeholders in strategy comment", overlay=true) // Other strategy code here..
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 //@strategy_alert_message New {{ticker}} entry signal. Last price is {{close}}. Highest high at {{plot("Highest High")}} and lowest low at {{plot("Lowest Low")}}. strategy(title="Plot placeholders in strategy comment", overlay=true) // Calculate and plot the highest high and lowest low highestHigh = ta.highest(high, 20)[1] lowestLow = ta.lowest(low, 20)[1] plot(highestHigh, color=color.green, title="Highest High") plot(lowestLow, color=color.red, title="Lowest Low") // Generate the breakout trades if high > highestHigh strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if low < lowestLow strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

- {{plot_N}} includes a plot’s value based on that plot’s number. This implicit number counts from zero (0) for the first plot the script makes. So we include the first plot with {{plot_0}}, get the second with {{plot_1}}, and so on. The highest plot number TradingView allows is {{plot_19}} so this style can only reference the first 20 plots.
- {{plot("name")}} inserts a plot’s value based on that plot’s name. Such as {{plot("EMA")}} and {{plot("Highest Close")}}. This name is identical to the title argument of the plot function that made the plot. This placeholder always uses double quotes (" and ") around the plot name.

- Manually type plot placeholders in the ‘Create Alert’ window.
- Define a default alert message with the //@strategy_alert_message comment that includes plot placeholders.

- Plot placeholders let us include the current value of plots in an alert message. This is one way to get dynamic values in alert messages.
- We reference plots by (such as {{plot_0}}) or by name ({{plot("SMA")}}).
- Strategy alerts have two options for plot placeholders.
- We can manually type the plot placeholder in the ‘Create Alert’ window when we make the strategy alert.
- Or we use the plot placeholder in the //@strategy_alert_message comment and configure the default alert message that way.
- Plot placeholders restrict dynamic values in alert messages to data the script plotted. A better alternative is the alert_message argument of order functions. That lets us use any strategy variable or value in the alert message.

1. TradingView Blog (2019, October 31). Introducing variables in Alerts. Retrieved on March 31, 2023, from https://www.tradingview.com/blog/en/introducing-variables-in-alerts-14880/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Alerts. Retrieved on March 31, 2023, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Alerts.html
1. TradingView Help (n.d.). How to use a variable value in alert. Retrieved on March 31, 2023, from https://www.tradingview.com/support/solutions/43000531021-how-to-use-a-variable-value-in-alert/

lessons.title: How to round to full hundreds (100s) in TradingView’s Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to round to full hundreds (100s) in TradingView’s Pine Script?

## Round up and down to hundreds

### Example values

## Round up to nearest full 100

### Example values

## Round down to nearest full 100

### Example values

## Example script

## Summary

Pine Script can round in several ways. The standard functions for rounding take a fractional value and turn it into a whole number. That makes 842.90 into 843 and 372.25 becomes 372.

But how about rounding a value to full hundreds? That would turn 842 into 800 and convert 372 to 400. This way we round a position size down to the nearest full 100 shares. Or display volume in full hundreds for easy reading.

There are three different ways to round to hundreds. Let’s discuss each.

The first option is to round up and down. When the last two digits before the decimal point (.) are 50 or higher, we round up; else, we round down. This turns 363 into 400 and makes 348.7 into 300.

This custom function rounds up and down to the nearest full 100s:

There’s one argument (value) for this RoundToHundreds() function. Inside the function we first divide that argument with 100.0. (When we divide with an integer, Pine Script throws away the fractional value. So we use 100.0 instead.) That moves the decimal point (.) two places to the left. Doing so turns 520 into 5.20.

Then we round that figure to a whole number with Pine Script’s math.round() function. That makes 5.20 into 5. Then we multiply with 100 to move the decimal point back two places to the right. The result is a rounded value with the same number scale as the original argument.

To use this function, we call it with a numerical value or variable. For example:

Here’s what the RoundToHundreds() function returns for several other values:

As we can see, when we round to hundreds, the rounded value can become more (983 becomes 1000). But the value can also be rounded down (102 gets turned into 100).

Another approach is to always round up to the next full hundred. That way both 620 and 673 become 700.

Here’s a custom function that implements the required code:

This RoundToHundredsUp() function also has one argument (value). Inside the function we first divide with 100.0 to move the decimal point. Next we round up to a whole number with Pine Script’s math.ceil() function. Then we multiply with 100 to arrive back at the same number scale as the function’s argument uses.

To use this function, we call it with a number, like this:

Here’s what the RoundToHundredsUp() function returns for other values:

As this shows, the rounded value always becomes more (bigger). Meaning, a positive value like 102 gets rounded to 200. And a negative value (like -329) becomes less negative (-300).

The third and last approach is to always round down to the nearest full hundred. That turns 119 and 189 both into 100.

When we calculate a position size, this approach makes sure we don’t end up buying too many shares.

This custom function rounds down to the next hundred:

Inside this RoundToHundredsDown() function, we first divide the value argument with 100.0. That moves the decimal point two spots. Then we round down with Pine Script’s math.floor() function. We multiply that outcome with 100 to move the decimal point (.) back to its original place.

To use the function, simply call it with a numerical value or variable. For instance:

And this is what the RoundToHundredsDown() function returns for various other values:

When we round down to hundreds, the rounded value is always less (smaller) or else unchanged. For positive values, that means a less positive value (983 becomes 900). And for negative values, it means a more negative value (-4.2 rounds to -100).

Let’s use the custom rounding functions in practice. The indicator below plots a Simple Moving Average (SMA). The RoundToHundreds() function rounds that average up and down to the nearest full hundred.

The script’s full code is:

With the indicator() function we first define the indicator’s settings. This names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then we insert the custom RoundToHundreds() function, which we discussed above. (We can also use the RoundToHundredsDown() or RoundToHundredsUp() function here, of course.)

Next we calculate the SMA with Pine Script’s ta.sma() function. We store that 20-bar average in the smaValue variable. The plot() function then shows that variable’s value on the chart. Since we didn’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes a regular line plot by default. That line shows in silver grey (color.silver).

We then make another plot. This one shows the average rounded to full hundreds. To get that value we call the RoundToHundreds() function with the smaValue variable. This solid line shows in navy blue (color.navy).

Here’s how the indicator looks on the chart:

As this Tesla chart shows, once the moving average rose above 450, the rounded average jumped from 400 to the 500 level. A similar thing happened when the average crossed above 550 and 650.

```pine_script
// RoundToHundreds() rounds the specified value up or down to the // nearest full hundreds (100s) value. RoundToHundreds(value) => math.round(value / 100.0) * 100
```

```pine_script
RoundToHundreds(102) // 100 RoundToHundreds(190.95) // 200 RoundToHundreds(983) // 1000
```

```pine_script
// RoundToHundredsUp() rounds the specified value up to the next // full hundreds (100s) value. RoundToHundredsUp(value) => math.ceil(value / 100.0) * 100
```

```pine_script
RoundToHundredsUp(102) // 200 RoundToHundredsUp(190.95) // 200 RoundToHundredsUp(983) // 1000
```

```pine_script
// RoundToHundredsDown() rounds the specified value down to the next // full hundreds (100s) value. RoundToHundredsDown(value) => math.floor(value / 100.0) * 100
```

```pine_script
RoundToHundredsDown(102) // 100 RoundToHundredsDown(190.95) // 100 RoundToHundredsDown(983) // 900
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Round to hundreds example", overlay=true) // RoundToHundreds() rounds the specified value up or down to the // nearest full hundreds (100s) value. RoundToHundreds(value) => math.round(value / 100.0) * 100 // Calculate and plot a Simple Moving Average smaValue = ta.sma(close, 20) plot(smaValue, color=color.silver, linewidth=2, title="SMA") // Display the average rounded up and down to hundreds plot(RoundToHundreds(smaValue), color=color.navy, linewidth=3, title="SMA Rounded to Hundreds")
```

| Original value | Rounded up or down to hundreds |
| --- | --- |
| 3 | 0 |
| 75 | 100 |
| 102 | 100 |
| 190.95 | 200 |
| 983 | 1000 |
| 4312 | 4300 |
| 0.01 | 0 |
| -0.01 | 0 |
| -4.2 | 0 |
| -8.75 | 0 |
| -329 | -300 |
| -992 | -1000 |
| na | Error; cannot call auser-defined function with na. |
| "1.83" | Error; cannot call thisfunction with a string value. |

| Original value | Rounded up to hundreds |
| --- | --- |
| 3 | 100 |
| 75 | 100 |
| 102 | 200 |
| 190.95 | 200 |
| 983 | 1000 |
| 4312 | 4400 |
| 0.01 | 100 |
| -0.01 | 0 |
| -4.2 | 0 |
| -8.75 | 0 |
| -329 | -300 |
| -992 | -900 |
| na | Error; cannot call auser-defined function with na. |
| "1.83" | Error; cannot call thisfunction with a string value. |

| Original value | Rounded down to hundreds |
| --- | --- |
| 3 | 0 |
| 75 | 0 |
| 102 | 100 |
| 190.95 | 100 |
| 983 | 900 |
| 4312 | 4300 |
| 0.01 | 0 |
| -0.01 | -100 |
| -4.2 | -100 |
| -8.75 | -100 |
| -329 | -400 |
| -992 | -1000 |
| na | Error; cannot call auser-defined function with na. |
| "1.83" | Error; cannot call thisfunction with a string value. |

- To round to full hundreds, we first divide the value to round with 100.0. Then round that outcome, followed by multiplying with 100 again.
- The rounding function we use determines if we round up and down (what the math.round() function does), always up (done with the math.ceil() function), or always down (the math.floor() function).

lessons.title: Do an extra TradingView strategy calculation after an order fills
lessons.clean_text:
# Do an extra TradingView strategy calculation after an order fills

## Default pattern

## Features of calc_on_order_fills

## Example strategy

### Example charts

## Manual setting

## Summary

### References

Pine Script’s calc_on_order_fills setting makes the strategy perform an additional intra-bar calculation after an order fills [1] [2] [3] [4] . In the TradingView strategy settings, this feature is called ‘Recalculate After Order Is Filled’.

An intra-bar calculation is when the script does a calculation inside an incomplete bar, so before the trading period of that bar ends. This is different from a closed bar calculation. With that latter the strategy can’t trade the bar anymore (since it’s trading period is over).

Why bother with intra-bar calculations? Well, calc_on_order_fills has a couple benefits:

So calc_on_order_fills makes the strategy faster. We don’t wait till the bar ends before doing some action. Instead we do that task during (that is, inside) the bar.

We configure the calc_on_order_fills setting with the strategy() function. Its value is true or false based on what behaviour we want.

To make the strategy do an extra intra-bar calculation after an order fills, we set calc_on_order_fills to true:

If we want the strategy to only calculate on closed bars, we have two options. We can either set calc_on_order_fills to false:

However, the default for calc_on_order_fills is already false [1] . It’s much more common to leave out calc_on_order_fills from strategy(), which also disables this feature:

Let’s see a full strategy use the calc_on_order_fills setting. The script below trades two Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs). When the fast average is above the slow one for two bars, we go long. And if two bars in a row have the fast EMA below the slow average, we go short. We allow up to 3 entries in the same direction.

First we’ll use the strategy without the calc_on_order_fills setting. Then we’ll enable this feature and see how that changes the strategy.

The strategy’s code is:

We start with the strategy() function. That configures the strategy. With title we name the script. overlay makes the strategy appear on the chart’s instrument. And with pyramiding we allow up to three entries in the same direction.

Next the ta.ema() function calculates two exponential moving averages. The first has a length of 10 bars; the other a period of 30. Both run on close prices (close).

The plot() function then shows both averages on the chart. The fast average shows in orange (color.orange) and the slower one in teal (color.teal). We don’t set the plot type, so Pine Script makes regular line plots by default.

Then we evaluate trade conditions. The first if statement checks if the fast average is above the slow average on the current (fastEMA > slowEMA) and previous (fastEMA[1] > slowEMA[1]) bar. When that’s so, the strategy.entry() function opens a long trade (strategy.long).

The other if statement looks for the opposite pattern: the fast average below the slow one for two consecutive bars. If that’s the case, strategy.entry() opens a short trade (strategy.short).

Let’s see what the strategy does. Recall that we haven’t used calc_on_order_fills yet. So the strategy does one calculation per bar, at the close.

here’s how the strategy looks on the chart:

There are three bars here with an order. Here’s what happens:

Now let’s use the calc_on_order_fills setting and see how that changes the strategy. We modify the strategy() function to include calc_on_order_fills with a true value:

After we save this code change, TradingView reloads the strategy. Now it does the following on the chart:

Rather than one order per bar, calc_on_order_fills fills 3 orders on the same bar.

This time here’s what happens:

The result? The strategy scales a lot quicker into the position thanks to the extra calculations calc_on_order_fills triggers.

Rather than wait several hours before the strategy opens its full position, those trades now execute within a single 1-hour bar.

Besides the calc_on_order_fills setting, this strategy feature also has a manual setting.

We find that option in the ‘Properties’ tab of the strategy’s settings window. There the ‘Recalculate After Order Is Filled’ setting tells if the strategy should do an intra-bar calculation each time an order fills:

This manual setting overrides how we configured calc_on_order_fills.

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="My Strategy", calc_on_order_fills=true)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="My Strategy", calc_on_order_fills=false)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="My Strategy")
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Calculate on order fills example", overlay=true, pyramiding=3) // Calculate and plot two Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs) fastEMA = ta.ema(close, 10) slowEMA = ta.ema(close, 30) plot(fastEMA, color=color.orange, title="Fast EMA") plot(slowEMA, color=color.teal, linewidth=2, title="Slow EMA") // Open a trade with bullish and bearish EMA patterns if fastEMA > slowEMA and fastEMA[1] > slowEMA[1] strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if fastEMA < slowEMA and fastEMA[1] < slowEMA[1] strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

```pine_script
strategy(title="Calculate on order fills example", overlay=true, pyramiding=3, calc_on_order_fills=true)
```

- Scale faster in or out a position. Rather than wait till the bar closes to send the next order, we submit a new order right after the previous one fills.
- Update stop-loss and profit target orders right after an entry order fills.
- Generate alerts with the alert() function immediately after an order fills. This gives an extra alert opportunity besides strategy alerts.

- calc_on_order_fills requires a constant true or false value [1] . We cannot use a condition of variable here. So the setting is either on or off all the time.
- To make decisions based on the type of calculation, the barstate.isconfirmed variable tells if the strategy calculates inside a bar or on a closed bar.
- calc_on_order_fills’s intra-bar calculation happens in addition to the standard closed bar calculation [1] . That regular calculation still happens. So with calc_on_order_fills, price bars have at least one, but possibly more, calculations.
- calc_on_order_fills can have a cumulative effect: when an order fills, an extra script calculation happens. If that calculation triggers another order which then fills during the same bar, yet another script calculation happens. And so on.
- The higher the chart’s time frame, the greater potential benefit calc_on_order_fills has. That’s because on those time frames, there’s a lot of time between when an order fills and the bar closes (which is when the strategy calculation normally happens).
- When calc_on_order_fills’s extra calculation happens depends on the bar’s price data:On historical data, the additional calculation happens on the bar’s open, high, or low.On real-time data, that extra script calculation happens with the next price or volume update.
- On historical data, the additional calculation happens on the bar’s open, high, or low.
- On real-time data, that extra script calculation happens with the next price or volume update.
- To quickly scale in and out of a position with calc_on_order_fills, the extra script calculation has to submit another (market) order. This requires that order’s condition to be true during the intra-bar calculation. Otherwise, the extra orders simply won’t generate.This sometimes requires changes to the strategy code. Say we generate the first order based on a bar pattern (like an inside bar). When this order fills on the next bar’s open, the bar pattern isn’t valid on that bar. So additional orders won’t generate.
- This sometimes requires changes to the strategy code. Say we generate the first order based on a bar pattern (like an inside bar). When this order fills on the next bar’s open, the bar pattern isn’t valid on that bar. So additional orders won’t generate.
- At the moment, we can’t combine calc_on_order_fills with the process_orders_on_close setting. When we try, the strategy goes back to one script calculation on historical bars.

- On historical data, the additional calculation happens on the bar’s open, high, or low.
- On real-time data, that extra script calculation happens with the next price or volume update.

- This sometimes requires changes to the strategy code. Say we generate the first order based on a bar pattern (like an inside bar). When this order fills on the next bar’s open, the bar pattern isn’t valid on that bar. So additional orders won’t generate.

- With the fast EMA above the slow EMA for two bars, the strategy generates its first market order. Since this order generates on a closed bar, it fills at the next bar’s open.
- On that next bar, the fast average is still above the slow one. So the strategy generates another market order on the closed bar. That order also fills a bar later.
- On the third bar, the long condition is still present. The strategy generates its last order (on a closed bar), which executes at the next bar’s open.

- With the fast average above the slow average for two bars, the strategy generates its first market order. This calculation still happens on a closed bar, so the order fills the next bar. (This is the same behaviour as earlier.)
- On that next bar’s open, the order fills. This triggers the first intra-bar calculation. During that calculation, the fast average is still above the slow one. So the order condition is still true. That has the strategy submit the next market order.
- That additional order fills immediately at the bar’s open. This triggers the second intra-bar calculation. With the fast average still above the slow one, the order condition remains true. So the strategy generates another market order.
- That third order fills at the bar’s low. Now yet another intra-bar calculation happens. However, at this point the strategy reached its pyramiding limit. So no additional order generates and the strategy is done.

- The calc_on_order_fills setting makes the strategy do an intra-bar calculation each time an order fills.
- Those extra calculations make it possible to perform additional tasks, like submitting another order.
- On real-time bars, the strategy does the intra-bar calculation with the next price or volume update. On historical bars, those calculations happen at the open, high, and low.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on March 22, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Strategies. Retrieved on March 22, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Strategies.html
1. TradingView Help (n.d. a). Strategy produces unrealistically good results by peeking into the future. Retrieved on March 22, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/support/solutions/43000614705-strategy-produces-unrealistically-good-results-by-peeking-into-the-future/
1. TradingView Help (n.d. b). Strategy properties. Retrieved on March 22, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/support/solutions/43000628599-strategy-properties/

lessons.title: Get the Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP) in TradingView Pine Script
lessons.clean_text:
# Get the Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP) in TradingView Pine Script

## Default pattern

## Quick examples

## Features of ta.vwap()

## Example script

## Summary

### References

In Pine Script, we get the Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP) with the ta.vwap() function [1] .

The VWAP is an intra-day average that begins at the start of each trading day. During the session, it weights each bar’s value against that bar’s volume. This gives bars with high volume more weight in the VWAP’s value. Bars with low volume, on the other hand, contribute little to the moving average’s value.

The function has this definition [1] :

The ta.vwap() function returns one of two things:

So to get the VWAP we tell ta.vwap() which data to process. Let’s look at some ways to do that.

The typical way to use VWAP is to process each bar’s average high, low, and close price (which we get from the hlc3 variable). Meaning, to compute the volume-weighted average price of (high + low + close) / 3 we do:

Besides that bar price, the VWAP can calculate on any variable that returns a series of values. To get the VWAP of close prices, we have the ta.vwap() function process the close variable:

The ta.vwap() function isn’t limited to built-in variables, however. This function can process any expression that has a value on each bar. Say we want the 14-bar close change as a volume-weighted average. In that case we call ta.vwap() like so:

Another possibility is to calculate the VWAP on data that another function returns. Let’s assume we’re looking to smooth the Rate of Change (ROC). For that we call the ta.vwap() function on ROC values:

If our script doesn’t need the standalone ROC values, we can also put the ta.roc() function directly inside the ta.vwap() function. That gives the same result but with less code typing:

Let’s see how a complete script uses the ta.vwap() function. The indicator below calculates the Volume-Weighted Average Price of close prices. Around that VWAP it makes a percentage-based price band.

When prices move above that band, we see that as a positive sign and colour the chart’s bars green. Should prices move below that band, red price bars signal the downtrend.

The indicator’s full code is:

We start with the indicator() function. That configures the script’s name and overlays it on the instrument.

Then we make two inputs. The first, a source input (input.source()), defines the price data to calculate the VWAP on. The other, a float input (input.float()), specifies the percentage offset for the upper and lower band.

We calculate the Volume-Weighted Average Price next:

This code executes the ta.vwap() function on the averageData input variable. That variable defaults to the instrument’s close prices. To calculate the upper and lower band we increase and decrease the VWAP with the percentage set by the bandOffset input variable (whose standard value is 0.05%).

With the data computed the script goes on with other tasks. First it makes three plots with the plot() function. This shows the VWAP as an orange (color.orange) regular line plot, while the upper and lower band display in blue (color.blue).

Then a cascaded if statement finds out what colour to give the chart’s bars. First it sees if the bar’s close is above the upper band. When that’s so, we pick green (color.green). Else the second condition looks if the close is under the lower band. For that situation we use red (color.red). Otherwise, when the bar trades inside the bands, we work with grey (color.gray).

The indicator’s last line of code is the barcolor() function. That applies the chosen bar colour to the chart.

On the chart, the indicator plots the Volume-Weighted Average Price and two percentage-based bands. Bars that are outside that range are made green or red to signal up- and downtrends:

See all moving averages in Pine Script for which other moving averages we can code.

```pine_script
ta.vwap(source)
```

```pine_script
// Get the volume-weighted average price of hlc3 hlcVwap = ta.vwap(hlc3) plot(hlcVwap)
```

```pine_script
// Compute the volume-weighted average closing price closeVwap = ta.vwap(close) plot(closeVwap)
```

```pine_script
// Get the VWAP of the 14-bar difference in close price closeDiffVwap = ta.vwap(close - close[14]) plot(closeDiffVwap)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the 8-bar Rate of Change and smooth with the VWAP rocValue = ta.roc(close, 8) rocVwap = ta.vwap(rocValue) plot(rocValue) plot(rocVwap, color=color.orange)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the 8-bar Rate of Change and smooth with the VWAP rocVwap = ta.vwap(ta.roc(close, 8)) plot(rocVwap, color=color.orange)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Volume-Weighted Average Price example", overlay=true) // Input options averageData = input.source(close, title="Average Data Source") bandOffset = input.float(0.05, title="Price Band Offset (%)", step=0.05) / 100 // Calculate the volume-weighted average price and its 'noise' bands vwapData = ta.vwap(averageData) upperBand = vwapData * (1 + bandOffset) lowerBand = vwapData * (1 - bandOffset) // Plot the average and its band plot(vwapData, color=color.orange, title="VWAP") plot(upperBand, color=color.blue, title="Upper band") plot(lowerBand, color=color.blue, title="Lower band") // Colour the chart's bars with prices outside the band barColour = if close > upperBand color.green else if close < lowerBand color.red else color.gray barcolor(barColour)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the volume-weighted average price vwapData = ta.vwap(averageData) upperBand = vwapData * (1 + bandOffset) lowerBand = vwapData * (1 - bandOffset)
```

- source is the series of numerical values to process. This is the (price) data we want to calculate the VWAP on.

- The VWAP as a floating-point value [1] .
- Or else the na value. This happens when the instrument doesn’t have volume data. (Spot forex and indexes are examples of TradingView instruments without volume.)

- The period of the VWAP is anchored to the start of the intra-day session. On the first intra-day bar, the average has a length of 1. Then on the next bar a length of 2. The following bar a lookback of 3. And so on. This way the VWAP includes all price data of the day, no matter how many bars the day has.
- At the start of each day, the VWAP starts its calculations anew. This is different from a regular moving average, which always calculates on the same number of bars (such as the last 30 price bars).
- The ta.vwap() function cannot calculate over a longer time period than the day’s session. If we want to reset the VWAP at a different point than the day’s open, we got to calculate that VWAP ourselves.
- Besides ta.vwap(), another volume-based average is the Volume-Weighted Moving Average (VWMA). This moving average always calculates a certain number of recent bars. The VWAP, on the other hand, starts when the trading day starts.

- The ta.vwap() function calculates the Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP).
- That function needs to know which data to calculate on. Its length is the number of bars since the session started.
- In contrast with moving averages, the VWAP doesn’t calculate on a fixed number of bars. It instead begins when the intra-day session starts (and resets the next time the market opens).

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on January 2, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/

lessons.title: How to get the largest value from a Pine Script array?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to get the largest value from a Pine Script array?

## Quick example

## Step-by-step array.max() example

## Ways to use array.max()

## Example script

## Features of array.max()

## Summary

### References

After we made an array and add elements to it, the array.max() function returns the largest value in the array [1] .

The function has this default pattern [1] :

array.max() returns one of two possible values [1] [2] :

To get an array’s maximum, we first need an integer array or floating-point array.

If we made such an array earlier and stored its identifier in the myArray variable, then we get that array’s biggest value like this:

By the way, the id keyword argument of array.max() is optional. I use those keywords to make the code examples easier to follow. But for less keyboard typing you can leave them out. This code works fine too:

For a detailed look at array.max(), let’s go through a step-by-step example together.

To get an array’s biggest value we need an array first. So let’s make one:

Here the array.new_int() function makes an integer array. We store the identifier of this whole number array in the myArray variable. With that variable we’ll access the array later.

Since we didn’t gave the array a size, it starts empty:

Getting the maximum now is pointless – we’ll only get a na value for our efforts. So let’s add some numbers to the array:

Here we call Pine Script’s array.push() function three times. Each time the function adds a number to the end of the array.

At this point our array has three elements:

Good! Now let’s get the biggest value:

To get the largest value we call the array.max() function. With the myArray variable we specify from which array to get that value.

We store that maximum value in the dataMax variable. That one has a value of:

Correct! This value is the array’s current maximum. If we change the array’s elements, its maximum might also change. To get that new maximum we execute array.max() again. Let’s see.

Let’s change the array by adding two new elements:

Here the array.unshift() function adds a new element to the start of the array. We call the function twice to add two new numbers. We again use the myArray variable to tell Pine Script which array to modify.

With the new values the array has these numbers:

To get the fresh maximum we call array.max() again:

This gets us the largest value of:

The array.max() function returns the biggest value from an integer array or float array. That’s handy because otherwise we have to loop over the array and find the maximum that way.

We can combine the array’s maximum with its minimum (which the array.min() function returns) to find the array’s range. But that difference between the highest and lowest value is also what the array.range() function returns. So we usually don’t use the array.max() function for that.

Let’s see how a script can use the array.max() function. The indicator below stores the volume of each bar in an array.

After we collect data for all bars on the chart, we use the array.max() function to get the highest volume a single bar had.

The script’s code is:

First we configure the indicator with the indicator() function. This names the script (with title) and overlays it on the chart’s instrument (overlay).

Then we make a new array with the array.new_float() function. We store the array’s identifier in the volumeData variable. Else we wouldn’t be able to access the array later.

The array variable is, by the way, a var one. This has Pine Script make the variable (and the associated array) only once, at the start of the chart. That way the array keeps its elements from one bar to the next.

Next we fill the array with the array.push() function. This adds the bar’s volume (volume) to the volumeData array. Since this code runs on every bar, it includes each bar’s volume in the array. And at the end of the chart we’ve collected data from all bars.

Speaking of, next an if statement sees if the script arrived on the last bar. It did when the barstate.islast variable is true. In that case we make a text label with the label.new() function. That label shows on the current bar’s high (bar_index, high).

Its background is light green, set with the #D0F0C0 hexadecimal colour. The label’s letters show in black (color.black) with a large font (size.large).

For its text we combine a literal string with the highest volume. To get that maximum we call the array.max() function on the volumeData array. But returns a number while the label wants text. So we make that value into text with the str.tostring() function. With Pine Script’s volume formatting (format.volume) the volume shows with ‘K’ and ‘M’ suffixes for thousands and millions, when needed.

When we run the script on the chart, its label tells what the highest single bar volume was:

```pine_script
array.max(id)
```

```pine_script
// Get largest value from the 'myArray' array dataMaximum = array.max(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
// Get largest value from the 'myArray' array dataMaximum = array.max(myArray) // without optional 'id=' part
```

```pine_script
// Create an new, empty array myArray = array.new_int()
```

```pine_script
[ ]
```

```pine_script
// Add values to the array array.push(id=myArray, value=27) array.push(id=myArray, value=-10) array.push(id=myArray, value=84)
```

```pine_script
[27, -10, 84]
```

```pine_script
// Get the array's maximum value dataMax = array.max(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
84
```

```pine_script
// Add two more values array.unshift(id=myArray, value=10 * 2) array.unshift(id=myArray, value=105)
```

```pine_script
[105, 20, 27, -10, 84]
```

```pine_script
// Get the array's updated maximum dataMax := array.max(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
105
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="array.max() example script", overlay=true) // Make a persistent array var volumeData = array.new_float() // On each bar, add that bar's volume to the array array.push(id=volumeData, value=volume) // On the last bar, show the biggest volume value if barstate.islast label.new(x=bar_index, y=high, color=#D0F0C0, textcolor=color.black, size=size.large, text="Highest volume reading:\n" + str.tostring(array.max(id=volumeData), format.volume))
```

- id is the identifier of the integer array or float array from which we want the biggest value.This ID is returned by the function that created the array, such as the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.That identifier tells Pine Script which array we want to work with. Without it, Pine Script doesn’t know what array to use.
- This ID is returned by the function that created the array, such as the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.
- That identifier tells Pine Script which array we want to work with. Without it, Pine Script doesn’t know what array to use.

- This ID is returned by the function that created the array, such as the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.
- That identifier tells Pine Script which array we want to work with. Without it, Pine Script doesn’t know what array to use.

- An integer or float value that represents the largest value in the array.
- Or else the na value. This happens when:The array is empty and has no elements.All elements in the array are na. (When there are actual numbers besides na values, array.max() ignores those na values and returns the biggest value from the available numbers.)
- The array is empty and has no elements.
- All elements in the array are na. (When there are actual numbers besides na values, array.max() ignores those na values and returns the biggest value from the available numbers.)

- The array is empty and has no elements.
- All elements in the array are na. (When there are actual numbers besides na values, array.max() ignores those na values and returns the biggest value from the available numbers.)

- array.max() only works with integer arrays or float arrays. If we call this array statistic function with a different type of array, Pine Script generates the ‘cannot call ‘array.max’ with argument ‘id=(…)’. An argument of ‘(…)’ type was used but a ‘float[]’ is expected.’ error.

- The array.max() function returns the largest value from an integer or float array.
- When the array is empty or only has na elements, array.max() returns na.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on October 10, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Arrays. Retrieved on October 10, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/language/Arrays.html

lessons.title: Get the TradingView chart’s lowest value with Pine Script code
lessons.clean_text:
# Get the TradingView chart’s lowest value with Pine Script code

## Custom function

### Using the function
