## Example script

TradingView charts cover a wide date range and can have tens of thousands of bars. With that amount of data it’s hard to see the chart’s lowest low. So how can Pine Script code show us that value?

To get the chart’s lowest value we do in essence two things:

When we update the variable each time the chart reaches a lower value, then on the last bar we have the chart’s lowest value. Let’s see how the code for that looks.

To get the chart’s lowest value we use this function:

This custom GetChartLowest() function has one argument: dataSeries. That argument specifies from which series of data to get the lowest value.

What makes the function useful is that it doesn’t care about which data it gets. GetChartLowest() can work with low prices, close prices, moving average values, volume values, or whichever data we want to analyse. Even data with na values is no problem. If we don’t tell the function which data to use, it works with low prices (low).

The function’s code first makes the chartLowest variable. This variable tracks the lowest value of the function’s argument. It’s a var variable, so its value persists from one bar to the next (even when we don’t update the variable on every bar). The variable’s initial value is dataSeries. It gets that value from the function’s argument on the chart’s first bar.

Then an if statement sees if the dataSeries argument’s current value is below (<) the chartLowest variable. Because that variable’s initial value might be na, we wrap it inside the nz() function. When it’s indeed NaN, Pine Script uses 1e10 instead. With that high 10,000,000,000 default value, the value of dataSeries is sure to be under that default.

When the argument’s value is indeed less than the chart’s lowest value (or 10 billion), we update the chartLowest variable to the current value of dataSeries. That updates the variable with each new low.

The last statement in the GetChartLowest() function is just the chartLowest variable. That’s how the function returns that variable’s value.

The function returns the lowest value for the chart’s date range. That range depends on how many bars the chart has. Not every chart and time frame contains the instrument’s entire history.

Because of that, the chart’s lowest value is not the same as the all-time low.

Sometimes people call the chart’s lowest low the ‘all-time low’, but that’s usually not accurate. Only on daily and higher time frames is there a good chance that the chart has the full price history.

Let’s see what using the function is like. If we want the chart’s lowest low, we simply call GetChartLowest() without an argument:

The function can also return the lowest value of any variable. Say we calculated a moving average and stored its value in the myAverage variable. To get the chart’s lowest moving average value we do:

The GetChartLowest() function isn’t limited to variables, though. We can also pass this function another function (though that other function needs to return a series of data). GetChartLowest() then returns the lowest value of that function.

For example, to get the 20-bar lowest Exponential Moving Average for the entire chart we do:

For accurate results, the GetChartLowest() function has to execute on each bar. When it doesn’t run on a particular bar, it misses that bar’s data. And when that bar made a new lowest value, the function doesn’t know about it. That makes the function’s result incorrect.

To execute the function on each bar, we don’t place it inside if statements, switch statements, or loops. Instead we call it from an unindented block of code.

Let’s see how a script can use the above function. The indicator below uses GetChartLowest() to get the chart’s lowest low, lowest EMA, and lowest close on Friday. We display those lowest levels with plots on the chart.

The indicator’s full code is:

This script first configures its settings with the indicator() function. That names the indicator and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then we include the custom GetChartLowest() function that we discussed above.

Next we plot the chart’s lowest low price:

This code makes a plot with the plot() function. The plotted data is GetChartLowest(). Without an argument given, that function returns the chart’s lowest low. That price is what plot() shows as a regular line plot in red (color.red).

Then we show the lowest Exponential Moving Average:

First the ta.ema() function calculates the 20-bar EMA. That function runs on close prices for a length of 20 bars. We store the returned average in the emaValue variable. The plot() function then shows that average as an orange (color.orange) line plot.

To show the chart’s highest EMA we make another plot. This time we plot the value of the GetChartLowest() function, which processes the emaValue variable here. That fuchsia (color.fuchsia) line tells what the lowest moving average is.

The last bit of code shows the chart’s lowest close that happened on a Friday:

Here we first make the closeFriday variable. Its gets one of two values with the conditional operator (?:). That operator first evaluates if the current day of week (dayofweek) equals (==) Friday (dayofweek.friday).

When that’s the case, the conditional operator returns the close variable as the value for the closeFriday variable. Otherwise, for other days of week, the operator returns na.

This code is by the way random. The close prices on Friday aren’t special. What matter is that this code creates a variable with values on some bars, and gaps for missing data (the na values) on other bars. That will show how the GetChartLowest() function still tracks the chart’s lowest value, even when the data contains na values.

To show the lowest close reached on Fridays we call the plot() function. The data to plot is the GetChartLowest() function that processes the closeFriday variable. Those lowest values show as a teal (color.teal) coloured line plot.

When the script runs on the chart, it shows the chart’s lowest low, lowest moving average, and lowest close on Fridays. For Bitcoin (BTCUSD), those prices are quite a distance away from the current prices:

```pine_script
// GetChartLowest() returns the lowest value of the given series // argument for the entire chart. That `dataSeries` argument is // optional and defaults to the bar low prices when not set. // Note: call this function on every bar for calculation consistency. // Also, the function's output depends on how many bars TradingView // loaded on the chart. GetChartLowest(dataSeries = low) => var chartLowest = dataSeries if dataSeries < nz(chartLowest, 1e10) chartLowest := dataSeries chartLowest
```

```pine_script
// Returns lowest low of chart chartLow = GetChartLowest()
```

```pine_script
// Returns the lowest value that the 'myAverage' // variable reached on the chart avgChartLow = GetChartLowest(myAverage)
```

```pine_script
// Returns the lowest 20-bar EMA of the chart emaChartLow = GetChartLowest(ta.ema(close, 20))
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Lowest value of chart", overlay=true) // GetChartLowest() returns the lowest value of the given series // argument for the entire chart. That `dataSeries` argument is // optional and defaults to the bar low prices when not set. // Note: call this function on every bar for calculation consistency. // Also, the function's output depends on how many bars TradingView // loaded on the chart. GetChartLowest(dataSeries = low) => var chartLowest = dataSeries if dataSeries < nz(chartLowest, 1e10) chartLowest := dataSeries chartLowest // Display the lowest low of the chart plot(series=GetChartLowest(), color=color.red, linewidth=2, title="Lowest Low Chart") // Show the chart's lowest 20-bar EMA value emaValue = ta.ema(close, 20) plot(series=emaValue, color=color.orange, title="EMA") plot(series=GetChartLowest(emaValue), color=color.fuchsia, linewidth=2, title="Lowest EMA Chart") // Plot the chart's lowest close reached on Fridays closeFriday = dayofweek == dayofweek.friday ? close : na plot(series=GetChartLowest(closeFriday), color=color.teal, linewidth=2, title="Lowest Friday Close Chart")
```

```pine_script
// Display the lowest low of the chart plot(series=GetChartLowest(), color=color.red, linewidth=2, title="Lowest Low Chart")
```

```pine_script
// Show the chart's lowest 20-bar EMA value emaValue = ta.ema(close, 20) plot(series=emaValue, color=color.orange, title="EMA") plot(series=GetChartLowest(emaValue), color=color.fuchsia, linewidth=2, title="Lowest EMA Chart")
```

```pine_script
// Plot the chart's lowest close reached on Fridays closeFriday = dayofweek == dayofweek.friday ? close : na plot(series=GetChartLowest(closeFriday), color=color.teal, linewidth=2, title="Lowest Friday Close Chart")
```

1. Create a variable that will hold the lowest value.
1. Update that variable when the current value goes under the variable’s value.

lessons.title: What settings do TradingView indicators and strategies share?
lessons.clean_text:
# What settings do TradingView indicators and strategies share?

## Unique indicator settings

## Unique strategy settings

## Summary

### References

TradingView’s Pine Script language can code several script types. The most common two are indicators and strategies (libraries are the third script type).

While indicators and strategies differ, they also share several settings. To configure indicator settings we use the indicator() function. And with the strategy() function we set up strategies. Those two functions share several settings.

These settings we can use with indicators and strategies [1] :

The settings above are those that indicators and strategies share. But each script type also has its unique settings.

The nice thing about the settings listed above is that they have the same effect in both script types. So when we know how a setting works with an indicator, we also know how it works with a strategy script (and vice versa).

There are, however, some settings that Pine Script indicators have that strategies don’t have. Those are:

These two options cannot be used with a trading strategy. If we want to use them, we got to make an indicator script instead.

TradingView strategies have a lot more unique settings than indicators do. Since strategies are more complex and have more features, they have more script settings too.

These are the settings that a strategy can use, but aren’t available to an indicator:

Since none of the above settings are possible in indicator scripts, the only way to use them is by creating a TradingView strategy.

- The timeframe setting makes it possible for an indicator to calculate on a different time frame.
- The timeframe_gaps option specifies how gaps from that different time frame should be combined with the current chart’s data.

- The pyramiding setting defines how many entries in the same direction are allowed.
- The calc_on_order_fills option has the strategy perform an extra script calculation each time an order fills.
- The calc_on_every_tick setting makes the strategy calculate with every incoming real-time tick.
- The backtest_fill_limits_assumption option says how many ticks prices need to move past a limit order before that order is considered filled.
- The default_qty_type setting specifies how the default order size should be calculated.
- The default_qty_value option gives the value of the default order size.
- The initial_capital setting is what sets the strategy’s initial trading capital.
- The currency option defines the strategy’s account currency.
- The slippage setting says how many ticks of slippage stop and market orders have.
- The commission_type setting defines how commission for orders is calculated.
- The commission_value setting spits out how much commission each order incurs.
- The process_orders_on_close setting has the strategy fill orders on closed bars, rather than wait till the next bar’s open.
- The close_entries_rule option says in which sequence exit orders scale out of a position.

- TradingView’s indicators and strategies share several settings.
- Those common settings work the same for each script type.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on December 2, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/

lessons.title: How to exit unprofitable trades with a percentage-based stop loss in TradingView?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to exit unprofitable trades with a percentage-based stop loss in TradingView?

## Submit TradingView stops based on percentage price changes

## Overview: steps to submit percentage-based stops

### Step 1. Optional: set the maximum loss percentage with inputs

### Step 2. Use the percentage to calculate the stop-loss price

### Step 3. Submit the actual stop-loss orders

## Example strategy: trade with percentage stops

### References

Stop-loss orders prevent that our strategy loses too much money. TradingView already supports for tick and price-based stops. But there’s no feature for percentage-based stops. Let’s see how we code those ourselves.

With a stop-loss order we specify a price at which we want to close our trade. Then when the market reaches that price, TradingView (or our broker) generates a market order to immediately close the trade. That way we limit risks by not getting a bigger loss.

To have our code submit stops we use the strategy.order() function or the strategy.exit() function. While that latter function is flexible, it only supports price-based and tick-based stops (TradingView, n.d.). So it can’t generate percentage-based stops for us.

But those stops are quite helpful. A percentage stop always places our exit the same relative distance from the entry price. That especially matters during a long-term backtest where the instrument increased or decreased a lot in value. In that scenario a tick-based stop is sometimes close and sometimes far away. But a percentage stop keeps the same distance.

Luckily a bit of custom code can make our strategy use percentage-based stops. Let’s see how.

To have our TradingView strategy submit percentage-based stop-loss orders, we do the following:

Let’s examine these steps and the code they require.

When we make an input option, we can easily configure a percentage stop. It also saves us from having to edit strategy code ever time we want to change the stop.

For maximum flexibility we’ll make an input for long and short stops:

We make these inputs with the input.float() function. That makes for two floating-point inputs. The first, ‘Long Stop Loss (%)’, has a default of 1 (so 1%). It’s minimum is 0 to prevent we accidentally use a negative value. We track this input’s value with the longLossPerc variable.

The other input, ‘Short Stop Loss (%)’, also starts at 1%. And it also has a minimum of 0. That way no negative value can mess up our later calculations. We’ll reference this input’s value later on with the shortLossPerc variable.

Note that we multiply each input’s value with 0.01. Here’s why. With these inputs we configure a percentage, like 3% or 12%. But the calculations in the second step are easier when the percentages are expressed as decimal values (so 0.03 and 0.12). And so we multiply the percentage with 0.01 to get a decimal value. (We could also have divided with 100; that gives the same outcome.)

Now that we know the stop percentages we can figure out the actual stop price:

To calculate the stop prices we use strategy.position_avg_price. That variable returns the average entry price of the currently open market position (TradingView, n.d.).

For the long stop we multiply strategy.position_avg_price with 1 minus longLossPerc. That latter is the input variable from the previous step. We subtract the input’s value from 1 to calculate with a percentage decrease. (A long stop is placed under the entry price.)

Let’s make that more concrete. Say the entry price is 2,230.50 and the long stop is 5% (so 0.05 as a decimal value). If we take 0.05 from 1, we’ll have 0.95. When we multiply the entry price with that value, we get 2,118.975. That price is 5% under our entry. And so that’s where our long stop should be.

(Here’s another way to think about it. If our entry price is 100% for the full value. Now if we want to lose 5% at most, our stop should be at the 95% level. And we get that level when we multiply the entry price with 0.95.)

For the short stop we perform a similar calculation. Except now the stop price is a certain percentage above the entry. So we multiply strategy.position_avg_price with 1 plus shortLossPerc (the input variable from the previous step).

We store the actual stop prices in the longStopPrice and shortStopPrice variables for the next step.

There’s a nice benefit to strategy.position_avg_price. When our strategy scales in or out of a position, then the strategy.position_avg_price variable updates to reflect the then-current entry price. When that happens our stop prices automatically update as well. And that way our stops remain at the correct level, even with multiple entries and exits.

Now that we know the stop prices we can submit the actual orders. We use the strategy.exit() function for that like so:

The first if statement checks if the strategy is long. That’s the case whenever the strategy.position_size variable is bigger than (>) 0. In that scenario we have strategy.exit() submit an order named ‘XL STP’ (exit long stop) based on the percentage stop we determined earlier (longStopPrice).

The second if test is similar. This one sees if the strategy is short (strategy.position_size < 0). When it is, we have strategy.exit() generate the ‘XS STP’ order at the shortStopPrice stop price. That variable is the percentage-based short stop from the previous step.

What’s interesting is that strategy.exit() originally didn’t support percentage-based stops. But the function does have a stop argument, which accepts a price-based stop value. By figuring out what price a percentage stop would have, we could still implement this behaviour in TradingView. (And you can use the same principle to generate dozens of stop types.)

We can also send long and short stops with TradingView’s strategy.order() function. But if we don’t watch out this function can also open a new trade in the other direction. And we’ll have to calculate the order size ourselves. So to not complicate things we used strategy.exit() in this article.

The strategy below has the three steps we discussed above. It generates trades with a 20-bar and 60-bar Simple Moving Average (SMA). When the fast average crosses over the slow on, we go long. When there’s a cross below we initiate a short trade.

But we don’t want to lose too much. So we use a 1 percent long and short stop. To see and track those stop values we have the plot() function display them with a crosses plot.

The entire strategy code is:

Let’s see how the strategy performs. The chart below shows a short trade in a S&P 500 CFD. That trade filled at 2,805.6. Not long thereafter we unfortunately got stopped out at 2,833.7. That gave a loss of 28.2 points, or -1.005% away from our entry price:

For another example we set the strategy’s stop to 3%. On the chart below the script went long just near the peak at 2,781.6. That trade didn’t go well, and a few days later we got stopped out at 2,698.1. The result was a loss of 83.5 points or -3.0019%.

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on May 28, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

```pine_script
// Set stop loss level with input options (optional) longLossPerc = input.float(1, title="Long Stop Loss (%)", minval=0.0, step=0.1) * 0.01 shortLossPerc = input.float(1, title="Short Stop Loss (%)", minval=0.0, step=0.1) * 0.01
```

```pine_script
// Determine stop loss price longStopPrice = strategy.position_avg_price * (1 - longLossPerc) shortStopPrice = strategy.position_avg_price * (1 + shortLossPerc)
```

```pine_script
// Submit exit orders based on calculated stop loss price if strategy.position_size > 0 strategy.exit("XL STP", stop=longStopPrice) if strategy.position_size < 0 strategy.exit("XS STP", stop=shortStopPrice)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Stop loss (% of instrument price)", overlay=true, pyramiding=3) // STEP 1: // Set stop loss level with input options (optional) longLossPerc = input.float(1, title="Long Stop Loss (%)", minval=0.0, step=0.1) * 0.01 shortLossPerc = input.float(1, title="Short Stop Loss (%)", minval=0.0, step=0.1) * 0.01 // Calculate moving averages fastSMA = ta.sma(close, 20) slowSMA = ta.sma(close, 60) // Calculate trading conditions enterLong = ta.crossover(fastSMA, slowSMA) enterShort = ta.crossunder(fastSMA, slowSMA) // Plot moving averages plot(fastSMA, color=color.teal) plot(slowSMA, color=color.orange) // STEP 2: // Determine stop loss price longStopPrice = strategy.position_avg_price * (1 - longLossPerc) shortStopPrice = strategy.position_avg_price * (1 + shortLossPerc) // Plot stop loss values for confirmation plot(strategy.position_size > 0 ? longStopPrice : na, color=color.red, style=plot.style_cross, linewidth=2, title="Long Stop Loss") plot(strategy.position_size < 0 ? shortStopPrice : na, color=color.red, style=plot.style_cross, linewidth=2, title="Short Stop Loss") // Submit entry orders if enterLong strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long) if enterShort strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short) // STEP 3: // Submit exit orders based on calculated stop loss price if strategy.position_size > 0 strategy.exit("XL STP", stop=longStopPrice) if strategy.position_size < 0 strategy.exit("XS STP", stop=shortStopPrice)
```

1. Optional: create an input that configures the stop-loss percentage.
1. Use a percentage to calculate the actual stop price.
1. Submit the stop-loss order for the computed price.

lessons.title: TradingView’s nested if statement: if inside another
lessons.clean_text:
# TradingView’s nested if statement: if inside another

## If statements inside other if and else code

### Default pattern: if inside another if statement

### Default pattern: if statement inside an else code block

## Quick examples: TradingView’s nested if statement

## Example TradingView scripts

### Highlight moving average crosses with a nested if statement

### Send and manage strategy orders with nested if statements

## Nested if statement replaced with if/else code

## Summary

### References

TradingView’s if statement evaluates a condition that, when true, runs the code indented below if. But not every scenario can be coded with a single if statement. Let’s see how we combine them together.

With an if statement we execute TradingView code based on a true/false condition (TradingView, n.d.; TradingView Docs, n.d.). That way our indicator or strategy makes decisions.

But sometimes one if statement isn’t precise enough to implement our script logic. In that case we can combine if statements. One option is a nested if statement. With a nested if statement we place one if statement inside another (e.g., Liberty & MacDonald, 2009).

When we do, not only the first if statement has to test true, but the second one also got to be true before our code runs. Let’s take a look.

In TradingView, the default pattern of an if statement inside another if statement looks like:

Here the first if statement evaluates firstCondition. When found true, the code indented below if runs. There we have a line of code that always runs when that condition is true.

But there’s also a second if statement. This nested one evaluates secondCondition. When that also tests true, the code of this second if statement runs.

Now here’s the thing. TradingView only tests the second if statement when the first one tests true. And so before our nested if statement’s code runs, both firstCondition and secondCondition have to test true.

There’s also a second way to make a nested if statement. And that’s by putting an if statement inside the else block of an if/else statement. Here’s how that looks:

This if/else statement tests firstCondition. When true, the code under the first if run and TradingView is done with this if/else statement.

But when that condition is false, the else code runs. That part starts with some code that always runs when firstCondition is false. Then there’s a nested if statement. This one tests secondCondition. When found true, the code of this if statement runs.

Now before our nested if statement runs, two things have to happen: firstCondition has to be false and secondCondition has to be true. Without that, the nested if statement never executes.

How we indent if statements matters a lot. A nested if statement has to be indented with an additional 4 spaces or 1 tab, relative to the level of the top if statement.

If we got the indentation wrong – even by a single space – then either TradingView errors or our code generates unexpected results.

Let’s see how we can use nested if statements in practice. Here we set a variable with two if conditions:

This plotColour variable gets one of two values. If the bar’s close is above the open, the variable gets the color.blue colour.

When the close is above the open and the close is higher than the previous close (close[1]), then the nested if statement returns color.orange. That colour is then stored in the plotColour variable.

Here’s another way to use a nested if statement:

Here the first if statement checks if the bar’s volume (volume) is greater than (>) the 10-bar simple moving average of volume. When it is, the code indented below if runs.

There we got a nested if statement that checks the enterLong variable. When that variable is true, we have the strategy.entry() function open a long trade (strategy.long).

There’s also a second nested if statement. This one checks enterShort. Should that variable be true, the strategy.entry() function opens a short trade (strategy.short).

Because those two nested if statements depend on the top if statement, no entry order is submitted unless the volume is above its 10-bar average. Even when enterLong or enterShort is true, we still need the volume to be above its moving average.

Above we executed the strategy.entry() function inside an if statement. This works, but not every TradingView function can run inside an if statement.

See functions we can execute in if statements for more.

Now let’s see how complete TradingView scripts can use nested if statements. Below we first discuss an example indicator; after that we turn our focus on an example strategy.

Let’s say we trade based on prices that cross a moving average. But we don’t want to hold positions over the weekend. So we don’t take buy and sell signals on Friday. Let’s see how nested if statements can help to implement that logic.

The indicator’s full code is:

We first set the indicator properties with the indicator() function. Then we calculate an Exponential Moving Average (EMA) with the ta.ema() function. We store that average’s value in the emaValue variable.

Next we plot that moving average on the chart with the plot() function. Those values display as a regular line plot.

Then we monitor when prices cross the moving average:

This highlightColour variable determines what colour to give the chart when a crossover or crossunder happens. Since we only want to generate signals when prices cross the exponential moving average, the ta.cross() function checks if the close price (close) crossed the average (emaValue).

When that tests true, a nested if statement runs. This one checks if the current bar’s day of week (dayofweek) is different than (!=) Friday (dayofweek.friday).

Should the cross indeed happened on a different day than Friday, the nested if returns the color.orange colour. That value is then stored in the highlightColour variable. (When there’s no cross, this if statement implicitly returns the na value.)

The indicator’s last line of code then executes the bgcolor() function to apply that colour to the chart. This is how that looks:

Here we see that the script highlights several moving average crosses. But on the 26th, which was a Friday, the crosses aren’t highlighted. That’s thanks to the nested if statement we coded above.

Now let’s see how a strategy can use nested if statements. Let’s say we want to open trades based on how the close compares to 10 bars ago, but only when the strategy is flat.

We close those positions with limit orders. But the strategy should only send those exit orders when there’s an open trade. Let’s see how nested if statements help implement that logic.

The strategy’s complete code is:

First we set the strategy’s options with the strategy() function. Then we calculate a moving average with the ta.sma() function. Next we have the plot() function display that average as a regular line plot.

Then an if/else statement calculates a price target based on the highest high and lowest low. We store that exit level in the priceTarget variable.

With that out of the way we look for trading opportunities:

Here we have an if statement that checks if the strategy is flat. That’s the case when the strategy.position_size variable equals (==) zero (0). When it does, two nested if statements run.

The first one looks if the current price (close) is above (>) the close from 10 bars ago (close[10]). When that tests true, the strategy.entry() function opens a long trade (strategy.long).

The other nested if statement sees if the current price is under (<) the close 10 bars ago. In that case we make strategy.entry() open a short trade (strategy.short).

Because the two nested if statements are indented below the top if statement, they only run when the strategy is flat. It also means the strategy cannot open additional trades if there’s already an open position.

The last part of the strategy’s code handles open trades. For that we also use nested if statements:

Here the first if statement sees if the strategy is currently long or short. That’s the case when strategy.position_size has a value different than (!=) 0. Our script evaluates two nested if statements in that scenario.

The first checks if the strategy is long. That happens when strategy.position_size’s value is above zero. In that situation the strategy.exit() function submits a limit order to close the position. The price of that order is the priceTarget variable we made earlier.

The second nested if statement looks if the strategy is short (strategy.position_size < 0). When it is, the strategy.exit() function closes the open short position with a limit order for the priceTarget price.

With those if statements indented below another if statement, they only execute when the first if condition tests true. So they run when the strategy has an open position (strategy.position_size != 0). But otherwise they don’t execute.

This is how those nested if statements make the strategy behave on the chart:

So a nested if statement makes it possible to evaluate complex conditions. But we don’t always need an extra if statement for that. The reason why is that we can combine several conditions into a single if test. We do that with TradingView’s logical and operator.

Let’s say we have the following nested if statement:

Here we got an if/else statement with nested if statement. There are two possible paths through this code.

The first sees if the close is above the open (close > open). Then the nested if statement checks whether the bar closed higher than the previous bar (close > close[1]). When those two things are the case, we close the ‘Enter Long’ order with the strategy.close() function.

The else portion, on the other hand, simply closes the ‘Enter Short’ order with strategy.close(). This code runs when close > open tests false.

But here’s the thing. The nested if statement isn’t really necessary. There’s no code between the first and second if test. Instead, the second if condition simply evaluates an additional condition. This means we combine the two together, like this:

This code still executes strategy.close() in the same situations. But now without the additional nested if statement. As a result there’s less indentation and the code is overall easier to read.

A nested if statement is an if statement placed put inside another if or if/else statement. Since nested if statements only run depending on the preceding if or else code, they make it possible to handle complex situations.

There are generally two kind of nested if statements we can make. The first is to place an if statement inside another if code block. That makes the nested if statement only run when the preceding if condition tests true.

The other option is to put the if statement inside an else code block. This way the nested if statement only runs when the preceding condition tests false.

TradingView uses indentation to see which code belongs to which if statement. If we don’t use multiples of 4 spaces (or 1 tab), then TradingView cannot interpret our code.

Also, we don’t always need a nested if statement. If there’s no code between an if condition and the subsequent nested if statement, the code can be rewritten into a single if condition with the and logical operator.

Liberty, J., & MacDonald, B. (2009). Learning C# 3.0: Master the Fundamentals of C# 3.0. Sebastopol, CA: O’Reilly Media.

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on November 22, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/

TradingView Docs (n.d.). Expressions, declarations and statements. Retrieved on November 22, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v4/language/Expressions_declarations_and_statements.html

```pine_script
[myVariable =] if firstCondition // Code that runs when 'firstCondition' is true if secondCondition // Code that runs when both conditions are true
```

```pine_script
[myVariable =] if firstCondition // Code that runs when 'firstCondition' is true else // Code that runs when 'firstCondition' is false if secondCondition // Code that runs when 'firstCondition' is false // and 'secondCondition' is true
```

```pine_script
// Update 'plotColour' variable plotColour = if close > open color.blue if close > close[1] color.orange
```

```pine_script
// Only trade when volume is above SMA if volume > ta.sma(volume, 10) if enterLong strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if enterShort strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Nested if statement example", overlay=true) // Calculate and plot exponential moving average emaValue = ta.ema(ta.ema(close, 10), 20) // Plot moving average on the chart plot(emaValue, color=color.teal, linewidth=2) // Check for crossovers, but not on Friday highlightColour = if ta.cross(close, emaValue) if dayofweek != dayofweek.friday color.orange // Highlight the moving average crosses bgcolor(highlightColour)
```

```pine_script
// Check for crossovers, but not on Friday highlightColour = if ta.cross(close, emaValue) if dayofweek != dayofweek.friday color.orange
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Nested if statement example", overlay=true) // Calculate an EMA smaValue = ta.sma(close, 75) // Plot the moving average on the chart plot(smaValue, color=color.teal, linewidth=2) // Calculate profit target highestHigh = ta.highest(high, 20)[1] lowestLow = ta.lowest(low, 20)[1] priceTarget = if close > smaValue highestHigh * 1.005 else lowestLow * 0.995 // When the strategy is flat, look for the trading // setup and submit entry orders if strategy.position_size == 0 if close > close[10] strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if close < close[10] strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short) // Submit the strategy's exit orders if strategy.position_size != 0 if strategy.position_size > 0 strategy.exit("Exit Long", limit=priceTarget) if strategy.position_size < 0 strategy.exit("Exit Short", limit=priceTarget)
```

```pine_script
// When the strategy is flat, look for the trading // setup and submit entry orders if strategy.position_size == 0 if close > close[10] strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if close < close[10] strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

```pine_script
// Submit the strategy's exit orders if strategy.position_size != 0 if strategy.position_size > 0 strategy.exit("Exit Long", limit=priceTarget) if strategy.position_size < 0 strategy.exit("Exit Short", limit=priceTarget)
```

```pine_script
if close > open if close > close[1] strategy.close("Enter Long") else strategy.close("Enter Short")
```

```pine_script
if close > open and close > close[1] strategy.close("Enter Long") else strategy.close("Enter Short")
```

lessons.title: Understand the two order phases of TradingView Pine strategy scripts
lessons.clean_text:
# Understand the two order phases of TradingView Pine strategy scripts

## Two steps before a TradingView strategy order fills

## Order generating phase: check conditions and approve orders

### TradingView evaluates orders in isolation

### How to prevent TradingView approving too many orders?

## Order executing phase: execute the order and report back

## Summary

### References

To make our strategy script trade we simply submit an order with a TradingView order function . But before a piece of code results in an actual filled order, there are two steps TradingView has to take. Let’s see what happens behind the scenes or our strategy scripts.

There are several TradingView order functions that have our strategy script trade signals, take profits, and cover losses. These orders seem to be automatically filled by TradingView on the chart. But behind the scenes there are two phases that each order goes through.

When we know how TradingView handles and processes orders, we better understand strategy behaviour. Plus we prevent that we’re surprised by what sometimes looks to be a TradingView bug.

Before an order that we generate with a strategy script is submitted, here are the two phases it goes through (TradingView Wiki, 2018):

These two steps apply to every order our strategy script submits. And these two phases happen with historical backtesting, real-time forward-testing, and with actual live trading.

While these two order phases sound theoretical, there are definitely practical consequences. For example, did you know that when you send several orders at the same time you can get a position much bigger than you wanted? The reason why this happens is because of TradingView’s order phases. So let’s take a closer look.

When our strategy script executes an order function , that order first goes into the order generating phase. During this phase the order’s conditions are checked to see if the order should actually be generated (TradingView Wiki, 2018). When the order is approved during this phase, it goes on to the order execution phase. Else the order command is discarded and our order won’t be submitted.

During the order generating phase, TradingView of course looks at the actual order conditions we programmed in our code. But there are also other things that TradingView considers:

Not every strategy order function has the same order conditions. For instance, the strategy.order() function isn’t affected by the strategy.risk.allow_entry_in() risk management function. And neither do the strategy’s pyramiding settings matter for that order function.

So which conditions TradingView checks before it approves the order also depends on which order function we use (other factors are the strategy settings and risk management rules).

When all conditions like the ones mentioned above are satisfied, the order is placed; when one or more aren’t satisfied, the order isn’t placed (TradingView Wiki, 2018).

So during the order generating phase it’s not only our strategy code that determines whether an order gets send. What also matters are the strategy settings (like pyramiding) and the context (is there, for instance, an open trade to close from?).

The order generating phase has a couple of important characteristics. Let’s look at each one in turn.

The most important feature of the order generating phase is that each order is evaluated individually (TradingView Wiki, 2018). So TradingView takes a single order, and looks if that order checks all boxes. When it does, the order is approved and send to the order execution phase.

But what TradingView doesn’t do is evaluate how several orders submitted at the same time affect the strategy’s settings. Say that our strategy script uses the following code to execute 2 entry orders at the same time:

Here the strategy() function disables pyramiding (pyramiding=0). Then an if statement looks if the bar’s close is above the 10-bar EMA. When it is, we execute the strategy.entry() function to submit two entry orders in the long directory (strategy.long).

Even though we call strategy.entry() twice, only one of those entry orders should fill. We don’t allow pyramiding after all. But that is not what happens. Instead TradingView considers each order individually during the order generating phase. Since each individual order is approved – there isn’t yet an open market position and we do allow one entry in the same direction – TradingView ends up submitting both orders.

But of course, the combined effect of those two orders is a bigger position than we want. However TradingView doesn’t take into account what happens when all orders in the order generating phase actually fill (TradingView Wiki, 2018).

When our strategy already has an open position, then any new order is discarded in the order generating phase. The situation we describe above happens when several orders are individually all approved, but have a combined effect that’s opposed to the strategy settings.

Another way to think about this feature is the following. Because there are two different phases – order generating and order execution phase – orders that are approved in the generating phase are not necessarily the same orders that will get approved once we arrive at the order execution stage.

So when we submit several orders at the same time, TradingView can approve each of them separately – even when their combined effect would violate the strategy settings. So how do we handle this behaviour?

There’s no way to make TradingView evaluate orders differently during the order generating phase. But what we can do is not submit several orders at the same time. So instead of sending several entry orders we combine their order size into a single strategy.entry() function call.

For exit orders we’ll need to make sure that their conditions don’t overlap. That way during each script calculation, at most one exit order gets generated. Else we might sell or buy more than intended, which makes us end up with an open position in the other direction.

Luckily, for exit orders we only need to concern ourselves with the strategy.order() function. The other order functions, like the strategy.exit() function and the strategy.close() function, can only close from an open market position. strategy.order(), on the other hand, can also open a new position in the other direction.

In the second phase TradingView executes the order (TradingView Wiki, 2018). After this TradingView reports back. That way our strategy script can use that information with its next script calculation.

By the time an order arrives in the order execution phase, TradingView already approved the order’s conditions. And so practically every order in the order execution phase gets executed. There are two exceptions to this: orders that belong to an order group, and the pending orders that our strategy script cancels at a later point.

When orders are tied together in an order group , as soon as one fills the others are cancelled or reduced in size. This feature does require that TradingView has enough time to act. Say we use an OCA order group, but each order has nearly the same execution price (like the high and high + 2 ticks). When one order fills, there’s likely not enough time in the order execution phase to cancel the other orders.

There are two ways an order can leave the order execution phase:

When we cancel an order but then later resubmit it, it starts again in the order generating phase. When approved there, TradingView moves the order to the order execution phase where it can be filled.

Our strategy script submits orders when it executes one of TradingView’s order functions. But before an order fills it goes through two phases: the order generating and the order execution phase.

During the order generating phase TradingView evaluates the order features, strategy settings, and risk management rules. When all of these approve the order, it moves to the next stage.

In the order execution phase TradingView executes the order and reports back. In this phase TradingView can also cancel or modify orders that belong to an order group. Our script can cancel orders in this phase as well.

An important feature of these phases is the following. During the order generating phase, TradingView evaluates each order in isolation. So when we submit several entry orders at the same time, and our strategy does not allow pyramiding, each of them is approved individually. Only when TradingView executes them in the next phase will we end up with a position bigger than indented. But at that point TradingView has already approved and filled the orders.

TradingView Wiki (2018, February 19). Strategies. Retrieved on March 18, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/wiki/Strategies

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Strategy example", pyramiding=0) if close > ta.ema(close, 10) strategy.entry("EL1", strategy.long) strategy.entry("EL2", strategy.long)
```

- During the order generating phase TradingView checks the order conditions, strategy settings, and risk management rules. Based on how that evaluation goes, TradingView either approves or declines the order.
- During the order execution phase TradingView submits the actual order and oversees its execution. After the order fills TradingView reports back to the strategy.

- When we submit an entry order, do the strategy’s pyramiding settings allow an (additional) entry?
- Is there a risk management function that forbids the order to go through?
- When we use an order function to close a position, is there an actual open position to exit from?
- Is there already a pending order that has the same order identifier? When there is, TradingView modifies that pending order. Else it submits a new order.
- When we use an order function to close from a particular entry, is there actually an open trade with the specified order identifier?
- When we use an order function that can reverse a position, is there an open position to begin with? Or should the function open a new trade?

- The order gets filled, after which TradingView reports the fill details back to the strategy.
- The order gets cancelled. This is something our strategy can do or TradingView itself based on order groups .

lessons.title: How to exit trades with a stop-loss order in Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to exit trades with a stop-loss order in Pine Script?

## Long stop exit

### Quick examples

### When to generate long stop

## Short stop exit

### Quick examples

### When to generate short stop

## Example strategy

## Summary

### References

TradingView strategies generate stop-loss orders with the strategy.exit() function [1] . That’s how the strategy closes its virtual position with a market order at a certain loss amount.

We can use the strategy.exit() function in several ways. To exit with a stop order, these function arguments are required [see 1] :

What isn’t required is cancel the stop order with either strategy.cancel() or strategy.cancel_all().

When we attach a stop to a particular entry with from_entry, TradingView automatically cancels the stop when that particular entry is closed. So if another order closes the trade before the stop loss could, TradingView deletes the stop for us.

Let’s use the three steps and code a long and short stop-loss exit.

The steps above give two ways to make a protective long stop: with ticks or based on price. Let’s take a look.

For the first approach, a ticks-based stop loss, we use the strategy.exit() function like this:

Here strategy.exit() makes an exit order named "Exit Long". We tell it to close the "Enter Long" entry orders. That attaches the order to that particular entry. Otherwise, Pine Script applies this stop to whichever open position the strategy has (even if that’s a short position).

The loss argument set to 150 puts the stop loss 150 ticks away from the position’s entry price. (TradingView automatically calculates the correct instrument price based on that tick amount.)

The other approach is a stop that uses a specific price. Here’s how that looks:

This code snippet also makes an exit order named "Exit Long". We again close the ‘Enter Long’ entry orders.

With the stop argument we set the stop loss 2% below the current bar’s low.

The two code examples above lack when to send the stop-loss order. As they are now, they execute strategy.exit() on every bar. That isn’t the best option.

Instead we better generate the stop loss at the same time we generate the entry order. This has two important benefits:

Here’s how code looks when we generate the stop at the same time as the entry order:

This if statement has the ta.crossover() function see if the bar’s high crossed above the 20-bar highest high (ta.highest()). When that cross happened, the if statement’s condition is true (and its code runs).

That code first calls the strategy.entry() function to generate a long (strategy.long) entry order named "Enter Long". This makes for a market order entry.

Next the strategy.exit() function executes. We name this exit order "Exit Long" and tell it to close from the ‘Enter Long’ entry. The loss argument places the stop loss 150 ticks away from the average entry price of the ‘Enter Long’ order(s).

Behind the scenes, TradingView first generates the entry order. Then it waits until that order fills, after which it immediately generates the exit order. Thanks to this smart behaviour, there’s no problem with us executing strategy.exit() before there’s an entry order to close from.

Just as with the long stop, Pine Script can also generate a short stop-loss order in two ways: based on ticks or price. Let’s see.

The first option, a stop loss a number of ticks away, uses the strategy.exit() function like so:

Here strategy.exit() makes an exit order named "Exit Short". This order closes from the "Enter Short" entry order(s). The loss argument set to 235 puts the stop loss 235 ticks above the entry price of the ‘Enter Short’ orders.

The alternative is to place the stop at a specific price. Here’s how that looks:

This code also generates an exit order named "Exit Short". Again we exit from the ‘Enter Short’ entry orders.

This time, however, the stop argument places the stop price 2% above the bar’s high.

Just as with the long stop, we best generate the short stop at the same time we generate the short entry order. Because:

In terms of code, here’s how it looks when we generate the stop and entry orders at the same time:

This if statement makes the ta.crossunder() function check if the bar’s low crossed below the 20-bar lowest low (ta.lowest()). When that’s so, ta.crossunder() returns true and the if code block executes.

There we first call the strategy.entry() function. This opens a short trade (strategy.short) named "Enter Short". Based on how strategy.entry() works, this generate a market order entry.

Next we execute the strategy.exit() function. This exit order’s name is "Exit Short" and it closes the ‘Enter Short’ entry order(s). The loss argument puts the stop 235 ticks way from the entry price. (We don’t know yet what the entry price will be, but TradingView automatically figures this out for us.)

As with the long example, behind the scenes TradingView waits to generate the strategy.exit() exit until there’s an entry named ‘Enter Short’. This makes it no problem to generate the exit at the same time as entry.

Let’s have a full strategy generate stop-loss orders with the strategy.exit() function. The script below trades two Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs).

When the fast average crosses above the slow one, the strategy goes long. If the average drops beneath the slow average, the strategy opens a short trade. Both entries get a protective stop from strategy.exit(). That stop is a specific price, placed 10 ticks away from the slow average.

The strategy’s entire code is:

The code starts with the strategy() function. That names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Next the ta.ema() function calculates two Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs). The first has a length of 10 bars, while the other uses a period of 30. Both calculate on the instrument’s close prices (close).

The plot() function then shows both averages on the chart. Given we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes regular line plots. The fast average shows in orange (color.orange) and the slow one uses teal (color.teal).

Then we generate the long entry and long stop orders:

This if statement looks with the ta.crossover() function if the fast average crossed above the slow one. If that’s so, the if code block executes.

That first generates an entry order with the strategy.entry() function. This order’s name is "Enter Long". The strategy.long value tells Pine Script to open a long trade.

Next the strategy.exit() function generates the stop-loss exit. We name this order "Exit Long". The from_entry argument makes it exit from the ‘Enter Long’ order we just generated. The stop argument specifies the order’s stop price. That value is the slow Exponential Moving Average minus 10 ticks (syminfo.mintick).

Note that this doesn’t update the stop to the moving average on each bar. Only when the cross happens (and the if statement’s code executes), is the stop price set.

Next we make the short entry as well as its stop:

This if statement has the ta.crossunder() function test if the fast EMA crossed below the slow EMA. In that scenario, the if code block executes.

That first calls strategy.entry(). This makes the "Enter Short" entry order. The strategy.short value tells Pine Script to open a short trade.

Next the strategy.exit() function executes. "Exit Short" is the name of this order. The from_entry argument connects this exit to the ‘Enter Short’ order made in the previous line of code. With the stop argument this stop is placed at the EMA plus 10 ticks (syminfo.mintick).

On the chart, the strategy trades the two moving averages and their protective stop-loss orders as follows:

```pine_script
// Generate a stop based for the 'Enter Long' entries with a loss of 150 ticks strategy.exit("Exit Long", from_entry="Enter Long", loss=150)
```

```pine_script
// Submit a stop loss for the 'Enter Long' entries that's 2% below the bar's low strategy.exit("Exit Long", from_entry="Enter Long", stop=low * 0.98)
```

```pine_script
// When prices cross above the 20-bar highest high, go long with // a stop loss 150 ticks below the entry price if ta.crossover(high, ta.highest(high, 20)[1]) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) strategy.exit("Exit Long", from_entry="Enter Long", loss=150)
```

```pine_script
// Generate a stop for the 'Enter Short' entries based on a loss of 235 ticks strategy.exit("Exit Short", from_entry="Enter Short", loss=235)
```

```pine_script
// Submit a stop for the 'Enter Short' entries that's 2% above the bar's high strategy.exit("Exit Short", from_entry="Enter Short", stop=high * 1.02)
```

```pine_script
// When prices cross below the 20-bar lowest low, initiate a short trade // with a stop loss 235 ticks below the entry price if ta.crossunder(low, ta.lowest(low, 20)[1]) strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short) strategy.exit("Exit Short", from_entry="Enter Short", loss=235)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Stop order exits", overlay=true) // Calculate and plot the Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs) fastEMA = ta.ema(close, 10) slowEMA = ta.ema(close, 30) plot(fastEMA, color=color.orange, title="Fast EMA") plot(slowEMA, color=color.teal, linewidth=2, title="Slow EMA") // Go long when the fast average crosses above the slow one if ta.crossover(fastEMA, slowEMA) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) strategy.exit("Exit Long", from_entry="Enter Long", stop=slowEMA - syminfo.mintick * 10) // Go short when the fast average crosses below the slow average if ta.crossunder(fastEMA, slowEMA) strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short) strategy.exit("Exit Short", from_entry="Enter Short", stop=slowEMA + syminfo.mintick * 10)
```

```pine_script
// Go long when the fast average crosses above the slow one if ta.crossover(fastEMA, slowEMA) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) strategy.exit("Exit Long", from_entry="Enter Long", stop=slowEMA - syminfo.mintick * 10)
```

```pine_script
// Go short when the fast average crosses below the slow average if ta.crossunder(fastEMA, slowEMA) strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short) strategy.exit("Exit Short", from_entry="Enter Short", stop=slowEMA + syminfo.mintick * 10)
```

1. Set the order identifier (that is, order name) with the first argument. We can, for instance, use "Exit Long" and "Exit Short" to name the long and short exit. Or, of course, use any other name.
1. Use the from_entry argument to say which entry order to exit from. This way Pine Script knows to which entry order(s) the stop-loss order applies.
1. Specify the stop-loss level. There are two possible arguments:loss for a stop loss a number of ticks away from the position’s entry price.stop to set the stop loss with an actual instrument price.
1. loss for a stop loss a number of ticks away from the position’s entry price.
1. stop to set the stop loss with an actual instrument price.

- loss for a stop loss a number of ticks away from the position’s entry price.
- stop to set the stop loss with an actual instrument price.

- The stop protects the position as soon as the entry order fills. (If we wait to generate the stop when strategy is long, there’s a one-bar delay and no protective stop on the entry bar.)
- It prevents the stop price from always changing (and thus never execute). Say the stop price is 8% below the low. But we generate (update) the order on each bar. That makes the stop always 8% below each bar, and in effect move away from the market. So we want to generate this stop when needed, and not keep moving its price away.

- It makes the stop order active as soon as the entry order fills. That protects our trade on the entry bar, and the stop can exit immediately when needed.
- It prevents the stop price keep changing on each bar, which has it ‘run away’ from the market without it getting filled.

- TradingView strategies create stop-loss orders with the strategy.exit() function.
- For such an order, that function needs three arguments. First is the order identifier (that is, order name).
- Then the from_entry argument connects the exit order to a particular entry order.
- For the stop itself, the loss argument sets the stop a number of ticks away from the entry price. Or, alternatively, we set the stop at a specific price with the stop argument.
- We generate the stop-loss order at the same time as the entry order. That way, when the entry order fills, TradingView activates the stop immediately to protect the trade against severe losses.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on May 6, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/

lessons.title: How to get the standard deviation from a TradingView Pine Script array?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to get the standard deviation from a TradingView Pine Script array?

## Quick example

## Step-by-step array.stdev() example

## Ways to use array.stdev()

## Example script

## Features of array.stdev()

## Summary

### References

After we make an array and add elements to it, Pine Script’s array.stdev() function returns the standard deviation of the array’s elements [1] .

The function has this default pattern [1] :

array.stdev() returns one of two possible values [1] [2] :

To get an array’s standard deviation, we first need an integer array or float array.

When we made such an array and put its identifier in the myArray variable, then we get that array’s standard deviation like so:

Just so you know, the id keyword argument of array.stdev() is optional. My examples use those keywords to make the code easier to follow. But without them there’s less code to type. This is completely valid Pine Script code too:

Let’s go through an example together to see how the array.stdev() function works.

To get the array’s standard deviation, we first have to make an array:

Here the array.new_float() function makes a new float array. We store the array’s identifier in the myArray variable. With that variable we’ll access the array later.

Since we didn’t gave the array a size, it starts empty:

If we try to get the standard deviation of an empty array, array.stdev() returns the na value. That isn’t very useful.

So let’s add a couple elements to the array:

This snippet executes the array.push() function three times. Each time that adds a new element to the array’s end. The myArray variable tells Pine Script which array to work with.

With the new elements added, the array’s content is:

Now we got some numbers to work with. So let’s get the standard deviation:

Here we execute the array.stdev() function. We use the myArray variable to identify the array. And store the returned standard deviation in the dataStDev variable.

The standard deviation stored in that variable is:

When we change an array’s elements (add, remove, or adjust element values), then the array’s standard deviation changes too. But to get that updated value, we got to call array.stdev() again. Let’s see how that goes.

First we add a few more elements to the array:

This code executes the array.unshift() function twice. Each time that adds a new element to the start of the myArray array.

That changes the array’s content to:

Now to get the new, updated standard deviation we execute array.stdev() again:

The standard deviation for the updated array is:

So the array.stdev() function gets us the standard deviation of all numbers in an array. That’s useful and more efficient than the alternative, which is to loop over the array and compute the statistic ourselves.

We’ll often use array.stdev() together with other array statistic functions, such as array.avg() for the array’s average and array.range() for the range.

But other than getting the standard deviation from an array, there aren’t other situation in which we also use the array.stdev() function.

Let’s see how a script can use array.stdev(). The indicator below stores the open prices of all bars in an array. On the chart’s last bar it makes a text label with the standard deviation of those open prices.

The indicator’s full code is:

First we configure the indicator with Pine Script’s indicator() function. That names the script and overlays it on the chart.

Then the array.new_float() function makes a new float array. We store the array’s identifier in the openPrices variable for access later.

That variable is declared with the var keyword. That has Pine Script make the variable (and its associated array) just once, rather than creating a new array on each bar. This way the array’s elements persist from one bar to the next.

Next the array.push() function adds the current bar’s open (open) to the array. We use the openPrices variable to tell Pine Script which array to modify. Since this code run on every bar, each bar’s open is added to the array.

Then an if statement looks up the barstate.islast variable. That variable is true on the chart’s last bar. When the script gets to that bar, the if code makes a new text label with the label.new() function.

That label shows at the last bar’s midpoint price (bar_index, hl2). Its style points left (label.style_label_left). Its background is light cyan blue, specified with the #E0FFFF hexadecimal colour. The label’s letters show in black (color.black) with a large font (size.large).

The label’s text is the combination of a literal string with the standard deviation. To get that value we call the array.stdev() function and use the openPrices variable to identify the array. That function, however, returns a number while the label requires text. To fix that incompatibility we convert the standard deviation to text with the str.tostring() function. The "0.0000" format string shows that number with 4 decimal digits.

When we run the script on the chart, it tells what the standard deviation of open prices is. Like on this 1-hour silver chart, for example:

```pine_script
array.stdev(id)
```

```pine_script
// Get standard deviation of the 'myArray' array dataStDev = array.stdev(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
// Get standard deviation of the 'myArray' array dataStDev = array.stdev(myArray) // without optional 'id=' part
```

```pine_script
// Create a new, empty array myArray = array.new_float()
```

```pine_script
[ ]
```

```pine_script
// Add some real values to the array array.push(id=myArray, value=1.149) array.push(id=myArray, value=0.238) array.push(id=myArray, value=-2.075)
```

```pine_script
[1.149, 0.238, -2.075]
```

```pine_script
// Get the array's standard deviation dataStDev = array.stdev(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
1.3570419628
```

```pine_script
// Add a few more elements array.unshift(id=myArray, value=0.1039) array.unshift(id=myArray, value=-0.007)
```

```pine_script
[-0.007, 0.1039, 1.149, 0.238, -2.075]
```

```pine_script
// Retrieve the array's updated standard deviation dataStDev := array.stdev(id=myArray)
```

```pine_script
1.0605125523
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="array.stdev() example script", overlay=true) // Make a persistent array for storing all open prices var openPrices = array.new_float() // On each bar, add that bar's open price to the array array.push(id=openPrices, value=open) // On the last bar, show the standard deviation of // the open prices with a text label if barstate.islast label.new(x=bar_index, y=hl2, style=label.style_label_left, color=#E0FFFF, textcolor=color.black, size=size.large, text="Standard deviation\nof open prices:\n" + str.tostring(array.stdev(id=openPrices), "0.0000"))
```

- id is the identifier of the integer array or float array from which we want the standard deviation.This ID is a value returned by the function that made the array earlier, like the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.That identifier tells Pine Script from which array to get the standard deviation. Without the identifier, Pine Script doesn’t know which array to use.
- This ID is a value returned by the function that made the array earlier, like the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.
- That identifier tells Pine Script from which array to get the standard deviation. Without the identifier, Pine Script doesn’t know which array to use.

- This ID is a value returned by the function that made the array earlier, like the array.new_int() or array.new_float() function.
- That identifier tells Pine Script from which array to get the standard deviation. Without the identifier, Pine Script doesn’t know which array to use.

- The standard deviation of the array’s elements as an integer or float value.
- Or else a na value. This value is returned when:The array is empty and doesn’t have elements.The array only contains na elements. (When the array has actual numbers besides na values, then array.stdev() ignores those na values and calculates with the available numbers.)
- The array is empty and doesn’t have elements.
- The array only contains na elements. (When the array has actual numbers besides na values, then array.stdev() ignores those na values and calculates with the available numbers.)

- The array is empty and doesn’t have elements.
- The array only contains na elements. (When the array has actual numbers besides na values, then array.stdev() ignores those na values and calculates with the available numbers.)

- In statistics, there are two ways to calculate a standard deviation [3] . One is based on a sample and estimates the standard deviation in the population. The other is the actual measure of the standard deviation in the entire population. This latter approach is what array.stdev() uses.
- array.stdev() needs an integer array or else a float array. When we execute this statistic function with a different type of array (like a label or Boolean array), Pine Script generates the ‘cannot call ‘array.stdev’ with argument ‘id=(…)’. An argument of ‘(…)’ type was used but a ‘float[]’ is expected.’ error.

- The array.stdev() function returns the standard deviation of an integer or float array.
- When the array is empty or only contains na elements, then array.stdev() returns na.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on October 10, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Arrays. Retrieved on October 10, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/language/Arrays.html
1. Urdan, T.C. (2010). Statistics in Plain English (3rd Edition). New York, NY: Routledge Taylor & Francis Group.

lessons.title: See if Pine Script string ends with substring (without case differences)
lessons.clean_text:
# See if Pine Script string ends with substring (without case differences)

## Custom function

## Function examples

## Quick example

## Function features

## Example script

## Summary

### References

Sometimes code has to test if a string ends with a smaller string (the substring), without the impact of letter case. That is, Pine Script should treat small letters (‘a’ to ‘z’) and big letters (‘A’ to ‘Z’) the same. And look at the meaning of text, rather than the size of letters. This lets code see that ‘GBPUSD’ ends with ‘usd’, though it won’t find ’eur’ at the end of ‘ETHUSD’.

The thing is, Pine Script has no function that makes such case-independent comparisons [see 1] . Luckily, that’s no issue: we can code this ourselves. Let’s see how.

This custom function looks if a string ends with another string. The comparison ignores the difference between small and big letters:

This StrEndsWith() function has two string parameters: source and substring. The first is the string whose end we want to test. The second is the string to look for. This smaller string is what we want to find at the end of the larger string.

The function’s code is a single statement that calls two different string functions. Let’s see what str.lower() does first.

To remove case differences between the source and substring strings, we call str.lower() on both. This gives the lowercased version of those strings. When both strings only have small letters, there’s no case difference between them anymore.

Next we use those lowercased strings with the str.endswith() function. This tests if the lowercased source string ends with the lowercased substring. When that’s so, str.endswith() returns true. If the second string isn’t at the end of the first, we get a false result.

Because both strings only have small letters in the range ‘a’ to ‘z’, Pine Script compares the strings based on their actual meaning. And not on the size of their letters. This result is a case-insensitive comparison that says if the content of one string appears at the end of another string.

These are examples of the StrEndsWith() function and the result it generates:

Say we got a string and want to know this text ends with another string, independent of case. To test this, we call the StrEndsWith() function:

We give StrEndsWith() two arguments. First is the syminfo.ticker variable. This variable returns the ticker (meaning, symbol) of the chart’s instrument. This is the string to search in. The other argument the "perp" literal string. This text we want to find.

This has the function see if the ticker ends with ‘perp’, regardless of case. So ‘PERP’ and ‘Perp’, as well as other variants, are a match.

We put the function’s true/false result in the endsWithPerp variable. Now there are several things we can do with this variable. One option is to colour the chart’s background:

This code calls the bgcolor() function. The conditional operator (?:) inside the function’s parentheses says which, if any, colour to use. To make that decision, the operator evaluates the endsWithPerp variable.

When that variable is true, the operator returns fuchsia (color.fuchsia) with 90% transparency from the color.new() function. Should the variable be false, the operator returns the na value. That disables the coloured background.

And so for instruments whose ticker ends with ‘perp’ (or any casing alternative), the chart’s background is fuchsia. Instruments without that substring at the end of their ticker don’t get a coloured background.

Let’s use the StrEndsWith() function in a full script. The indicator below looks for stocks that trade on exchanges in the United Kingdom, Germany, and America.

To get that information, StrEndsWith() looks if the instrument’s time zone ends with ’london’, ‘berlin’, or ’new york’. Because this is a case-insensitive test, it doesn’t matter how TradingView encoded the time zone name: ‘London’, ‘LONDON’, and ’london’ (and other casing alternatives) are all sufficient to identify an UK stock.

The indicator’s full code is:

First we the indicator() function. This names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then we include the StrEndsWith() function that we discussed above.

Next we code the first conditions. We look for a stock a London-based time zone:

This if statement checks two things. First the script has to calculate on one of the chart’s last bars. We get that information from the barstate.islast variable. Next the chart’s instrument has to be a stock. This is the case when its asset type (syminfo.type) equals (==) "stock".

We combine both evaluations with the and operator. This requires both to be true before their combination is true too. With one or both false, the combination is false too.

With a true combination, the if statement’s code executes. This code contains a cascaded if statement. Its first if condition has the StrEndsWith() function test if the instrument’s time zone (syminfo.timezone) ends with "london". When the time zone name ends with that phrase, code indented below if executes.

That code makes a text label with the label.new() function. This label shows at the bar’s high (bar_index, high). Its text says we got a British stock. The label’s colour is teal (color.teal) with white (color.white) letters.

When the instrument doesn’t trade in the London time zone, the next portion of the cascaded if statement runs:

This if condition has StrEndsWith() evaluate if the instrument’s time zone ends with "berlin", regardless of case.

When it does, the label.new() function makes a new label. This text label tells we got a German stock. Its colour is fuchsia (color.fuchsia) and its letters are white (color.white).

Should the instrument neither trade in the Berlin time zone, Pine Script moves on to the final if condition:

We again code a condition with the StrEndsWith() function. Now the function tests if the instrument’s time zone ends with "new_york". When it does, label.new() makes a text label with the message this is an American stock. That label’s colour is lime green (color.lime).

On the chart, the script recognises stocks that trade in the London, Berlin, and New York time zones. On the BMW stock chart below, the script correctly says this is a German stock:

```pine_script
// StrEndsWith() returns 'true' or 'false' to indicate if the specified // source string ends with the given substring, regardless of case // differences. StrEndsWith(string source, string substring) => str.endswith(str.lower(source), str.lower(substring))
```

```pine_script
// Test if instrument ticker (symbol) ends with 'perp' (perpetual future) endsWithPerp = StrEndsWith(syminfo.ticker, "perp")
```

```pine_script
// Turn the background fuchsia when the string ends with 'perp' bgcolor(endsWithPerp ? color.new(color.fuchsia, 90) : na)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="String ends with substring (case insensitive)", overlay=true) // StrEndsWith() returns 'true' or 'false' to indicate if the specified // source string ends with the given substring, regardless of case // differences. StrEndsWith(string source, string substring) => str.endswith(str.lower(source), str.lower(substring)) // On the last bar, see if the chart's instrument is a stock if barstate.islast and syminfo.type == "stock" // Look in which country this stock trades, based on its time zone if StrEndsWith(syminfo.timezone, "london") label.new(bar_index, high, "This is a British stock!", color=color.teal, textcolor=color.white) else if StrEndsWith(syminfo.timezone, "berlin") label.new(bar_index, high, "This is a German stock!", color=color.fuchsia, textcolor=color.white) else if StrEndsWith(syminfo.timezone, "new_york") label.new(bar_index, high, "This is an American stock!", color=color.lime)
```

```pine_script
// On the last bar, see if the chart's instrument is a stock if barstate.islast and syminfo.type == "stock" // Look in which country this stock trades, based on its time zone if StrEndsWith(syminfo.timezone, "london") label.new(bar_index, high, "This is a British stock!", color=color.teal, textcolor=color.white)
```

```pine_script
else if StrEndsWith(syminfo.timezone, "berlin") label.new(bar_index, high, "This is a German stock!", color=color.fuchsia, textcolor=color.white)
```

```pine_script
else if StrEndsWith(syminfo.timezone, "new_york") label.new(bar_index, high, "This is an American stock!", color=color.lime)
```

| Code example | Result |
| --- | --- |
| StrEndsWith("XRPUSD", "USD") | true. The "XRPUSD" string ends with the phrase "USD". |
| StrEndsWith("XRPUSD", "usd") | true. The letters of "usd" appear at the end of the "XRPUSD" string. Since the function ignores case differences, "usd", "Usd", "USD", and other casing alternatives are considered the same. What matters is that the letters match, not their casing. |
| StrEndsWith("XRPUSD", "xru") | false. While "xru" happens inside the string, those three characters aren’t the last of the string. So the function returns false. |
| StrEndsWith("0.35675", "675") | true. The "0.35675" text ends with the "675" characters. |
| StrEndsWith("XRP USD", " USD") | false. While the phrase "USD" appears in both strings, we look for a phrase prefixed with two spaces. But the source string only has one space before "USD". So this comparison results in false. |

- The StrEndsWith() function works with any expression that returns a string. This includes variables and the return value from functions. But also individual array and matrix elements.
- This is how StrEndsWith() behaves with empty strings and the na value:When the source string and substring are both empty ("") or na, the function returns true. As such, both StrEndsWith("", "") and StrEndsWith(na, na) are true.When the substring is empty or na, but the source string is a non-empty string, the function returns true. That is, StrEndsWith("EURUSD", "") and StrEndsWith("USDJPY", na) are both true.When the source string is empty or na, but the substring is a non-empty string, the function returns false. Meaning, StrEndsWith("", "EUR") and StrEndsWith(na, "GBP") are both false.
