In those cases losses happen because trends fail to start, fail to continue long enough for a profitable exit, or because prices simply go in the wrong direction.

An example is the chart below. Here the E-mini S&P 500 future moved sideways for several months. During that time the SMA Crossover Pyramiding strategy got four losing trades in a row:

The performance graph of the strategy (with one additional entry based on the trading rules) shows below. A few things are worth noting. The strategy’s drawdowns are modest in relation to the net profit. Another thing is that the big up spikes in equity show that the strategy relies on big trades. That unfortunately also means that missing one or two trades can ruin a year of trading.

The strategy also has relative long periods in which not much happens. We can see that in the first 50 trades or so, where the strategy remained closed to break even. If we would have given up by then thinking the strategy is a failure, we’d miss the big up swing afterwards.

The backtest results show in the table below. These results use one additional entry (so up to 2 trades in the same direction). But the results are without position sizing. That way the strategy could trade every signal it got, which maximised the number of trades in the backtest report.

The strategy was profitable on both the E-mini S&P 500 future and EuroStoxx 50 future. The win percentage is decent of an unoptimised long-term trend-following strategy. Plus the strategy’s drawdown is relatively small – just one third of the strategy’s net profit.

Furthermore, the strategy has 3 times as much profit as the regular SMA Crossover strategy, which doesn’t scale into positions (but is otherwise the same as the SMA Crossover Pyramiding strategy). The drawdown in the E-mini S&P 500 future is, however, just $7k bigger. So from that perspective we traded positions that are bigger and got three times as much profit, while we barely increased the strategy’s risks.

One concern is the low number of trades. With just over 2 trades per year there’s not that much data to go on. To really understand whether the SMA Crossover Pyramiding strategy works (or not) we’ll have to do more testing.

While the initial backtest is profitable, there are still things we can explore for better results and a deeper understanding of the strategy. Here are some ideas you might find valuable to explore further:

For TradingView strategies that are like the SMA Crossover Pyramiding strategy, see the SMA Crossover strategy and the SMA Crossover Weekly trend-following strategy.

Two other trend-following strategies that also use moving averages are the Triple Moving Average strategy and the Dual Moving Average strategy.

The complete code of the SMA Crossover Pyramiding strategy shows below. For details and an explanation of the code, see the discussion above.

Covel, M.W. (2006). Trend Following: How Great Traders Make Millions in Up or Down Markets (2nd edition). Upper Saddle River, NJ: FT Press.

Kowalski, C. (2018, August 30). Learn About Futures Margin. Retrieved on October 11, 2018, from https://www.thebalance.com/all-about-futures-margin-on-futures-contracts-809390

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on November 16, 2018, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

```pine_script
//@version=5 // Step 1. Define strategy settings // Step 2. Calculate strategy values // Step 3. Determine long trading conditions // Step 4. Code short trading conditions // Step 5. Output strategy data // Step 6. Submit entry orders // Step 7. Submit exit orders
```

```pine_script
// Step 1. Define strategy settings strategy(title="SMA Crossover Pyramiding", overlay=true, pyramiding=0, initial_capital=100000, commission_type=strategy.commission.cash_per_order, commission_value=4, slippage=2)
```

```pine_script
// Moving average inputs fastLen = input.int(50, title="Fast SMA Length") slowLen = input.int(100, title="Slow SMA Length")
```

```pine_script
// Stop loss inputs atrLen = input.int(10, title="ATR Length") longStpMult = input.float(4, title="Long Stop Multiple") shortStpMult = input.float(4, title="Short Stop Multiple")
```

```pine_script
// Position sizing inputs usePosSize = input.bool(true, title="Use Position Sizing?") maxRisk = input.float(2, title="Max Position Risk %") * 0.01 maxExposure = input.float(10, title="Max Position Exposure %") * 0.01 marginPerc = input.int(10, title="Margin %") * 0.01
```

```pine_script
// Pyramiding settings longThres = input.float(1, title="Scale Long Profit %") shortThres = input.float(1, title="Scale Short Profit %") maxLongEntries = input.int(2, title="Max Long Entries") maxShortEntries = input.int(2, title="Max Short Entries")
```

```pine_script
// Backtest time range settings endMonth = input.int(11, title="End Month Backtest", minval=1, maxval=12) endYear = input.int(2018, title="End Year Backtest", minval=1950, maxval=2030)
```

```pine_script
// Step 2. Calculate strategy values // Calculate moving averages and ATR fastMA = ta.sma(close, fastLen) slowMA = ta.sma(close, slowLen) atrValue = ta.atr(atrLen)
```

```pine_script
// Determine backtest window tradeWindow = time <= timestamp(endYear, endMonth, 1, 0, 0)
```

```pine_script
// Determine position size riskEquity = maxRisk * strategy.equity riskLong = (longStpMult * atrValue) * syminfo.pointvalue riskShort = (shortStpMult * atrValue) * syminfo.pointvalue maxPos = math.floor((maxExposure * strategy.equity) / (marginPerc * close * syminfo.pointvalue)) posSizeLong = usePosSize ? math.min(math.floor(riskEquity / riskLong), maxPos) : 1 posSizeShort = usePosSize ? math.min(math.floor(riskEquity / riskShort), maxPos) : 1
```

```pine_script
// Compute trade profits (is NaN when flat) tradeProfit = ((close - strategy.position_avg_price) / strategy.position_avg_price) * 100
```

```pine_script
// Calculate the number of entries into positions longEntries = 0 longEntries := (strategy.position_size < 1) ? 0 : (strategy.position_size > strategy.position_size[1]) ? longEntries[1] + 1 : longEntries[1] shortEntries = 0 shortEntries := (strategy.position_size > -1) ? 0 : (strategy.position_size < strategy.position_size[1]) ? shortEntries[1] + 1 : shortEntries[1]
```

```pine_script
// Step 3. Determine long trading conditions enterLong = ta.crossover(fastMA, slowMA) and strategy.position_size < 1 and tradeWindow
```

```pine_script
extraLong = strategy.position_size > 0 and longEntries < maxLongEntries and tradeProfit / longEntries > longThres and tradeWindow
```

```pine_script
// Calculate long stop and update stop price // when there's a long or additional long signal longStop = 0.0 longStop := enterLong or extraLong ? close - (atrValue * longStpMult) : longStop[1]
```

```pine_script
// Step 4. Code short trading conditions enterShort = ta.crossunder(fastMA, slowMA) and strategy.position_size > -1 and tradeWindow
```

```pine_script
extraShort = strategy.position_size < 0 and shortEntries < maxShortEntries and tradeProfit / shortEntries < shortThres * -1 and tradeWindow
```

```pine_script
// Calculate short stop and update stop price // when there's a short or additional short signal shortStop = 0.0 shortStop := enterShort or extraShort ? close + (atrValue * shortStpMult) : shortStop[1]
```

```pine_script
// Step 5. Output strategy data // Show moving averages on the chart plot(fastMA, color=color.orange, title="Fast SMA") plot(slowMA, color=color.teal, title="Slow SMA", linewidth=2)
```

```pine_script
// Plot stop levels plot(strategy.position_size > 0 ? longStop : na, color=color.green, style=plot.style_cross, linewidth=2, title="Long Stop Price") plot(strategy.position_size < 0 ? shortStop : na, color=color.red, style=plot.style_cross, linewidth=2, title="Short Stop Price")
```

```pine_script
// Step 6. Submit entry orders // Send the strategy's initial entry orders if enterLong strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long, qty=posSizeLong) if enterShort strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short, qty=posSizeShort)
```

```pine_script
// Send the additional entry orders if extraLong strategy.order("EL Extra", strategy.long, qty=posSizeLong) if extraShort strategy.order("ES Extra", strategy.short, qty=posSizeShort)
```

```pine_script
// Step 7. Submit exit orders if strategy.position_size > 0 strategy.exit("XL", stop=longStop) if strategy.position_size < 0 strategy.exit("XS", stop=shortStop)
```

```pine_script
if not tradeWindow strategy.close_all()
```

Calculate strategy values // Calculate moving averages and ATR fastMA = ta.sma(close, fastLen) slowMA = ta.sma(close, slowLen) atrValue = ta.atr(atrLen) // Determine backtest window tradeWindow = time <= timestamp(endYear, endMonth, 1, 0, 0) // Determine position size riskEquity = maxRisk * strategy.equity riskLong = (longStpMult * atrValue) * syminfo.pointvalue riskShort = (shortStpMult * atrValue) * syminfo.pointvalue maxPos = math.floor((maxExposure * strategy.equity) / (marginPerc * close * syminfo.pointvalue)) posSizeLong = usePosSize ? math.min(math.floor(riskEquity / riskLong), maxPos) : 1 posSizeShort = usePosSize ? math.min(math.floor(riskEquity / riskShort), maxPos) : 1 // Compute trade profits (is NaN when flat) tradeProfit = ((close - strategy.position_avg_price) / strategy.position_avg_price) * 100 // Calculate the number of entries into positions longEntries = 0 longEntries := (strategy.position_size < 1) ? 0 : (strategy.position_size > strategy.position_size[1]) ? longEntries[1] + 1 : longEntries[1] shortEntries = 0 shortEntries := (strategy.position_size > -1) ? 0 : (strategy.position_size < strategy.position_size[1]) ? shortEntries[1] + 1 : shortEntries[1] // Step 3. Determine long trading conditions enterLong = ta.crossover(fastMA, slowMA) and strategy.position_size < 1 and tradeWindow extraLong = strategy.position_size > 0 and longEntries < maxLongEntries and tradeProfit / longEntries > longThres and tradeWindow // Calculate long stop and update stop price // when there's a long or additional long signal longStop = 0.0 longStop := enterLong or extraLong ? close - (atrValue * longStpMult) : longStop[1] // Step 4. Code short trading conditions enterShort = ta.crossunder(fastMA, slowMA) and strategy.position_size > -1 and tradeWindow extraShort = strategy.position_size < 0 and shortEntries < maxShortEntries and tradeProfit / shortEntries < shortThres * -1 and tradeWindow // Calculate short stop and update stop price // when there's a short or additional short signal shortStop = 0.0 shortStop := enterShort or extraShort ? close + (atrValue * shortStpMult) : shortStop[1] // Step 5. Output strategy data // Show moving averages on the chart plot(fastMA, color=color.orange, title="Fast SMA") plot(slowMA, color=color.teal, title="Slow SMA", linewidth=2) // Plot stop levels plot(strategy.position_size > 0 ? longStop : na, color=color.green, style=plot.style_cross, linewidth=2, title="Long Stop Price") plot(strategy.position_size < 0 ? shortStop : na, color=color.red, style=plot.style_cross, linewidth=2, title="Short Stop Price") // Step 6. Submit entry orders // Send the strategy's initial entry orders if enterLong strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long, qty=posSizeLong) if enterShort strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short, qty=posSizeShort) // Send the additional entry orders if extraLong strategy.order("EL Extra", strategy.long, qty=posSizeLong) if extraShort strategy.order("ES Extra", strategy.short, qty=posSizeShort) // Step 7. Submit exit orders if strategy.position_size > 0 strategy.exit("XL", stop=longStop) if strategy.position_size < 0 strategy.exit("XS", stop=shortStop) if not tradeWindow strategy.close_all()

| Performance metric | E-mini S&P 500 future (ES) | EuroStoxx 50 future (FESX) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| First trade | 1998-09-07 | 1998-12-22 |
| Last trade | 2018-10-23 | 2018-11-02 |
| Time frame | 1 day | 1 day |
| Net profit | $94,144 | €90,330 |
| Gross profit | $273,550 | €179,631 |
| Gross loss | -$179,406 | -€89,301 |
| Max drawdown | $33,215 | €31,903 |
| Profit factor | 1.525 | 2.012 |
| Total trades | 80 | 96 |
| Win percentage | 38.75% | 47.92% |
| Avg trade | $1,176 | €940 |
| Avg win trade | $8,824 | €3,905 |
| Avg losing trade | -$3,661 | -€1,786 |
| Win/loss ratio | 2.41 | 2.186 |
| Commission paid | $468 | €500 |
| Slippage | 2 ticks | 2 ticks |

- Enter long:Go long (and exit any open short positions) with a market order the next day at the open when the 50-period Simple Moving Average (SMA) crosses over the 100-period SMA.Enter one additional long position the next day with a market order when prices increase 1% from the entry price.
- Go long (and exit any open short positions) with a market order the next day at the open when the 50-period Simple Moving Average (SMA) crosses over the 100-period SMA.
- Enter one additional long position the next day with a market order when prices increase 1% from the entry price.
- Exit long:Close the entire long position based on the following stop loss: entry price - 4 times the 10-bar Average True Range (ATR).
- Close the entire long position based on the following stop loss: entry price - 4 times the 10-bar Average True Range (ATR).
- Enter short:Go short (and close any open longs) with a market order the next day at the open when the 50-bar SMA crosses under the 100-bar SMA.Enter one additional short position the next day with a market order when prices decrease with 1% from the entry price.
- Go short (and close any open longs) with a market order the next day at the open when the 50-bar SMA crosses under the 100-bar SMA.
- Enter one additional short position the next day with a market order when prices decrease with 1% from the entry price.
- Exit short:Close the entire short position with the following stop loss: entry price + 4 times the 10-bar Average True Range (ATR).
- Close the entire short position with the following stop loss: entry price + 4 times the 10-bar Average True Range (ATR).
- Position sizing:The initial risk for each position (difference between entry price and stop-loss price) is 2% of equity.The maximum exposure (that is, the margin to equity ratio) for a single position is 10% of equity.
- The initial risk for each position (difference between entry price and stop-loss price) is 2% of equity.
- The maximum exposure (that is, the margin to equity ratio) for a single position is 10% of equity.

- Go long (and exit any open short positions) with a market order the next day at the open when the 50-period Simple Moving Average (SMA) crosses over the 100-period SMA.
- Enter one additional long position the next day with a market order when prices increase 1% from the entry price.

- Close the entire long position based on the following stop loss: entry price - 4 times the 10-bar Average True Range (ATR).

- Go short (and close any open longs) with a market order the next day at the open when the 50-bar SMA crosses under the 100-bar SMA.
- Enter one additional short position the next day with a market order when prices decrease with 1% from the entry price.

- Close the entire short position with the following stop loss: entry price + 4 times the 10-bar Average True Range (ATR).

- The initial risk for each position (difference between entry price and stop-loss price) is 2% of equity.
- The maximum exposure (that is, the margin to equity ratio) for a single position is 10% of equity.

- One problem of the SMA Crossover Pyramiding strategy is that the moving average length makes the strategy slow to respond. While a 50-bar SMA does filter out noise in the market, its length also means we have to wait a decent amount of time before an exit signal happens. At that point we likely already gave up some profit or saw losses get worse. Perhaps we can use a 50/100-bar cross for entries, but use a shorter (and therefore quicker) moving average to close positions.
- Covel (2006) reports an optimisation which shows that the strategy’s best performance happens with a length of 30-40 bars for the fast moving average and 80-100 bars for the slow SMA. We probably want to do our own tests to see which parameter settings are optimal, also because Covel’s (2006) tests happened more than a decade ago.
- Like most trend-following strategies, the SMA Crossover Pyramiding strategy performs poorly when markets move sideways. If we can filter out those market conditions, the strategy’s performance increases for the better. Things we can try are volume filters, Average True Range (ATR) filters, the ADX, or higher time frame analysis.
- The strategy’s stop loss uses a fixed price. While that gives positions plenty of room to move, it also means we give up a decent amount of profit before the stop gets hit. Perhaps a trailing stop can help the strategy to close losing positions sooner, and don’t give up as much profit.
- When we expand on the strategy with additional trading rules and filters, it might be a good idea to temporarily disable the pyramiding and position sizing. That way we can better see how those changes affect the strategy’s performance, without the influence of the strategy’s pyramiding.
- We can also make the strategy better by taking less risks. For instance, with a maximum position size we prevent trades that are too big. And with a maximum losing day streak we prevent a long drawdown. See the risk management category for other ideas.

lessons.title: Look if TradingView script runs on weekly time frame with Pine Script
lessons.clean_text:
# Look if TradingView script runs on weekly time frame with Pine Script

## timeframe.isweekly values for different time frames

### Based on time interval, not chart type

## Compared with other time frame variables

## Example indicator

## Ways to use timeframe.isweekly

## Features of timeframe.isweekly

## Summary

### References

Pine Script’s timeframe.isweekly variable tells if an indicator or strategy script calculates on a weekly time frame.

timeframe.isweekly returns one of two values [1] :

So the value of timeframe.isweekly depends on the chart’s time frame. Here’s what the variable returns for different TradingView time frames:

The value of timeframe.isweekly depends on the time interval TradingView used to create the bars. When those price bars are made from weekly data, timeframe.isweekly is true.

The variable doesn’t depend on the chart type that plots those bars. Even uncommon chart options like Kagi and Line Break are based on a time interval for each bar (such as 1 week or 1 month).

So for timeframe.isweekly our chart type doesn’t matter. Because for bars, candlesticks, Heikin Ashi, Renko, Line Break, Kagi, and Point & Figure charts, the variable returns true or false as the above table shows.

Pine Script has several time frame variables. When our code runs on a weekly time frame, then this is what those other variables return:

To summarise, on a weekly time frame (timeframe.isweekly) the timeframe.isdwm variable is also true. But all other time frame variables are false on weekly bars.

For an idea of how we can use timeframe.isweekly, the complete indicator below creates an informative text label with this time frame variable:

The code first sets up the indicator’s settings with the indicator() function. That names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then an if statement checks barstate.islastconfirmedhistory. That variable is true on the last historical bar. We use it here so our code runs just once, at the end of the chart. That way we don’t get a label on every bar.

In that if statement we first determine the label’s text. So we make the labelText variable and set its value with an if/else statement. Its if condition evaluates timeframe.isweekly. On a weekly time frame, that code sets the variable’s text to a message about the weekly chart. For other time frames, the else code says the chart isn’t a weekly one.

Then we add more text to the labelText variable. That text includes the time frame’s period (timeframe.period). The addition assignment operator (+=) adds the new text to the end of the variable’s existing content.

Finally we make the text label with the label.new() function. This label appears at the bar’s close (bar_index, close). It points to the lower right (label.style_label_lower_right). The background is orange (color.orange), set to 40% transparency with the color.new() function. Its letters are black (color.black) with a large size (size.large). The label’s text is the labelText variable.

On a weekly chart, the indicator’s label confirms that time frame. It also tells the time frame period. For this weekly PayPal chart, that’s one week (‘W’):

When we switch the chart to something else, the label’s content updates accordingly. Like on this 30-minute chart for instance:

There are several things we can do with timeframe.isweekly, including:

In addition to using timeframe.isweekly on its own, we can also combine it with Pine Script’s other time frame variables. That’s how we code specific features for different time frames, like the daily, weekly, and monthly charts.

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Weekly time frame example", overlay=true) // On the last historical bar, create a label with time frame info if barstate.islastconfirmedhistory // Determine the label's text labelText = if timeframe.isweekly "We got a weekly\nchart here!" else "This isn't a\nweekly price chart" labelText += "\n\nChart period is:\n" + timeframe.period // Create the label itself label.new(x=bar_index, y=close, style=label.style_label_lower_right, color=color.new(color.orange, 40), textcolor=color.black, size=size.large, text=labelText)
```

| Chart time frame | timeframe.isweekly value |
| --- | --- |
| 1 second | false |
| 5 seconds | false |
| 15 seconds | false |
| 30 seconds | false |
| 1 minute | false |
| 3 minutes | false |
| 5 minutes | false |
| 15 minutes | false |
| 30 minutes | false |
| 45 minutes | false |
| 1 hour | false |
| 2 hours | false |
| 3 hours | false |
| 4 hours | false |
| 1 day | false |
| 5 days | false |
| 1 week | → true ← |
| 1 month | false |
| 1 quarter (3 months) | false |
| 1 year (12 months) | false |
| 10 ranges | false |
| 100 ranges | false |

| Variable | When timeframe.isweekly is true |
| --- | --- |
| timeframe.ismonthly | false |
| timeframe.isdaily | false |
| timeframe.isminutes | false |
| timeframe.isseconds | false |
| timeframe.isdwm | → true ← |
| timeframe.isintraday | false |

- true when the script runs on a weekly time frame. These are the 1 week to 52 week chart resolutions.
- false when the script runs on a different time frame than weekly.

- Change script calculations based on the time frame. On a weekly chart, we can calculate price targets differently than on intra-day charts.
- Check if the script runs on a suitable time frame. If our code is a short-term trading idea, we may not want to give users signals on weekly (and higher) charts.
- Adapt the script settings to the time frame. On the weekly chart we can use a shorter period RSI than on daily charts.
- Automatically load a higher time frame in the background. On weekly bars, our code can fetch monthly data for its higher time frame moving averages.

- timeframe.isweekly tells if the script runs on weekly bars. But it doesn’t say how many weeks each bar lasts. That’s something the timeframe.multiplier variable tells us. With timeframe.isweekly true, the value of timeframe.multiplier is how many weeks each bar lasts. Like 1 for 1-week and 4 for 4-week charts.
- The timeframe.period variable returns the chart’s period and time frame. For weekly charts that variable returns a string with the letter ‘W’. Such as "2W" for a 2-week chart. This variable is what we typically use to load background data with the same time frame as the chart.
- While it’s possible to place the history operator ([]) behind timeframe.isweekly, that isn’t necessary: the variable returns the same value on each bar.
- timeframe.isweekly is available to both indicators and strategies. Furthermore, we can access this variable on any possible time frame.
- In Pine Script version 3 and earlier, the timeframe.isweekly variable was named isweekly [2] .

- The timeframe.isweekly variable returns true when the script runs on a weekly time frame. That are the 1 week to 52 week chart resolutions.
- When the chart doesn’t use weekly data, timeframe.isweekly is false.
- timeframe.isweekly doesn’t depend on the chart type. It also works on less common charts like Point & Figure and Line Break.

1. TradingView (n.d. a). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on September 4, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/
1. TradingView (n.d. b). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on September 4, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v3/

lessons.title: Give price bars different colours with TradingView’s Pine Script
lessons.clean_text:
# Give price bars different colours with TradingView’s Pine Script

## Three ways to pick colours

## Cascaded if statement

## Switch statement

## Conditional operators

## Summary

### References

The standard behaviour of Pine Script’s barcolor() function is to give each price bar the same colour. While useful, sometimes we want to give specific bars certain colours. That makes it easy to spot certain situations and trading setups.

Thankfully, barcolor() can use multiple colours for different scenarios. That requires two things for every situation we want to highlight:

Because a price bar can only have one colour, our code has to decide which colour to give a bar when.

For bars we don’t want to colour we use the na value. Bars that have that particular value have their coloured bar turned off [see 1] . That way we only colour those bars we’re interested in.

A TradingView script can choose between colours in three ways:

Let’s implement each approach with a script to see how it works. Let’s get started!

Probably the easiest way to pick a colour is with the cascaded if statement. The indicator below calculate two Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs). Then a series of if conditions compare the current bar’s close against the averages. Based on the outcome of those true/false comparisons we give the bar one of four possible colours.

The indicator’s full code is:

First the indicator calls the indicator() function. That sets the script’s name and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then the ta.ema() function calculates two moving averages. These run on close prices (close) and a length of 15 and 45 bars. We store their values in the emaFast and emaSlow variables.

Next the plot() function shows both averages on the chart. Since we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes them into regular line plots. Those lines show in orange (color.orange) and blue (color.blue).

Then we determine the bar colour:

This code makes the emaBarColour variable. Its value depends on a cascaded if statement. The first true condition gets to decide which value the variable gets. Let’s go through the different conditions.

First we see if the bar’s close (close) is above (>) the fast average (emaFast) and if the close is above the slow average (emaSlow). When both things occurred at the same time, the condition is true and the if code block returns the color.lime colour. That value Pine Script then stores in the emaBarColour variable.

If the bar didn’t close above both averages, we move on to the next condition. That one tests if the close (close) is below (<) the fast (emaFast) and slow (emaSlow) averages. When that’s the case, the cascaded if statement returns color.red. Otherwise we go to the next condition.

That one sees if the bar closed above the fast average. If that’s so, the if code returns the color.green colour. Otherwise Pine Script continues to the next and final if condition.

That last test looks if the bar’s close is below the fast average. In those situations the cascaded if statement returns the color.maroon colour.

After testing these conditions, there’s a chance that none of them is true. That happens when the moving average and close price are the same. When no condition of a cascaded if statement is true and there’s no else branch, then Pine Script implicitly returns the na value. When used with a bar colour, that value turns off the coloured bar.

So after Pine Script processed the cascaded if statement, the emaBarColour variable has one of these values: color.lime, color.red, color.green, color.maroon, or na.

Now we use that variable to colour price bars:

This code calls the barcolor() function. With the emaBarColour variable we specify which colour to give the chart’s price bars.

That gives bars a different colour based on how they relate to the moving averages. Here’s how that looks on the chart:

Another way to pick from several colours is with the switch statement. The script below uses two Weighted Moving Averages (WMAs). The switch statement then checks the bar’s close against the averages. Based on the matching condition the chart’s bars get one of 4 potential colours.

The indicator’s code is:

First we set the indicator’s properties with the indicator() function. That names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then the ta.wma() function calculates two Weighted Moving Averages (WMAs). Both run on close prices (close). The first uses a length of 10 bars; the other has a period of 30 bars. We put their values in the wmaFast and wmaSlow variables for later.

Next the plot() function shows both averages on the chart. The fast one shows in blue (color.blue), the slow average uses fuchsia (color.fuchsia). Since we didn’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes them into a regular line plot.

Then we figure out what bar colour to use:

This code creates the wmaBarColour variable. Its value is set with a switch statement, which tests up to four different cases. Let’s take a look.

The first condition sees if the bar’s close is above (>) the fast WMA (wmaFast) and slow WMA (wmaSlow). With the bar above those averages, this switch statement case returns the color.lime colour. Pine Script then stores that value in the wmaBarColour variable.

The second condition looks if the bar’s close is below (<) the fast and slow averages. When that price is indeed less than wmaFast and wmaSlow, this part of the switch statement returns the color.red colour.

The third condition tests if the bar closed above the fast WMA. In that situation, the switch statement returns the color.green colour.

The fourth and final condition looks if the bar closed under the fast average. If that’s so, the switch statement returns the color.maroon colour to the wmaBarColour variable.

There’s one possibility not explicitly tested here. And that’s when the close and average are the same. For conditions that aren’t included, the switch statement returns na as the implicit default. That value will turn off the bar colour.

Now that we have a variable with the bar colour, we can apply it to the chart:

Here the barcolor() function colours with the wmaBarColour variable. That makes the chart’s price bars use one of the four colours defined above.

On the chart, the different colours based on the moving averages look like this:

The third way to pick a bar colour is with the conditional operator (?:). The indicator below computes two Simple Moving Averages (SMAs). A series of conditional operators compares the current bar’s close with the averages. Based on the outcome of those evaluations we give the bar one of four possible colours.

The indicator has this code:

First the indicator() function defines the indicator’s settings. That’s what names the script. And shows it in the same chart panel as the chart’s instrument.

Then the ta.sma() function calculates two moving averages. The first uses a length of 15 bars, the other has a period of 15. Both run on close prices (close). We put their values in the smaFast and smaSlow variables for later.

Next the plot() function shows both averages on the chart. Because we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes a regular line plot by default. The fast average shows in teal (color.teal); the slow one uses orange (color.orange).

Then we determine the bar colour to use:

Here we make the smaBarColour variable. Its value is determined by a series of conditional operators. Let’s step through them.

The first conditional operator tests if the current close (close) is above (>) the fast average (smaFast) and above the slow average (smaSlow). When that’s the case, this conditional operator returns the color.lime colour (which Pine Script then stores in the smaBarColour variable).

If the current bar isn’t above the two averages, the next conditional operator runs. This one looks if the close is below (<) the fast (smaFast) and slow (smaSlow) averages. In that situation the conditional operator returns the color.red colour.

When the bar neither closed below both averages, Pine Script evaluates the next conditional operator. That third one looks if the close is above the fast average. For that situation the operator returns the color.green colour.

Else the fourth and last conditional operator runs. This one tests if the bar closed below the fast average. In that case the operator returns the color.maroon colour.

When that last condition isn’t true, then all conditions tested false. For that outcome the final conditional operator returns the na value. That value turns off the bar colour.

After Pine Script processed this series of conditional operators, the smaBarColour variable holds one of these values: color.lime, color.red, color.green, color.maroon, or na.

Now let’s apply those values to the chart:

This code calls the barcolor() function. With the smaBarColour variable we colour the chart’s price bars based on the two moving averages.

Here’s how those four possible bar colours look on the chart:

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Multiple bar colours (with if/else)", overlay=true) // Calculate a fast and slow moving average emaFast = ta.ema(close, 15) emaSlow = ta.ema(close, 45) // Plot both exponential moving averages on the chart plot(series=emaFast, color=color.orange, title="Fast EMA") plot(series=emaSlow, color=color.blue, title="Slow EMA") // Compare close price against averages to find bar colour emaBarColour = if close > emaFast and close > emaSlow color.lime else if close < emaFast and close < emaSlow color.red else if close > emaFast color.green else if close < emaFast color.maroon // Apply the bar colour if/else chose to the chart barcolor(color=emaBarColour)
```

```pine_script
// Compare close price against averages to find bar colour emaBarColour = if close > emaFast and close > emaSlow color.lime else if close < emaFast and close < emaSlow color.red else if close > emaFast color.green else if close < emaFast color.maroon
```

```pine_script
// Apply the bar colour if/else chose to the chart barcolor(color=emaBarColour)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Multiple bar colours (with switch)", overlay=true) // Calculate a fast and slow moving average wmaFast = ta.wma(close, 10) wmaSlow = ta.wma(close, 30) // Show both averages on the chart as solid lines plot(series=wmaFast, color=color.blue, title="Fast WMA") plot(series=wmaSlow, color=color.fuchsia, title="Slow WMA") // Figure out the colour to use for the current bar wmaBarColour = switch close > wmaFast and close > wmaSlow => color.lime close < wmaFast and close < wmaSlow => color.red close > wmaFast => color.green close < wmaFast => color.maroon // Apply the chosen colour to the price bars barcolor(color=wmaBarColour)
```

```pine_script
// Figure out the colour to use for the current bar wmaBarColour = switch close > wmaFast and close > wmaSlow => color.lime close < wmaFast and close < wmaSlow => color.red close > wmaFast => color.green close < wmaFast => color.maroon
```

```pine_script
// Apply the chosen colour to the price bars barcolor(color=wmaBarColour)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Multiple bar colours (with ?:)", overlay=true) // Calculate a fast and slow moving average smaFast = ta.sma(close, 15) smaSlow = ta.sma(close, 45) // Plot both averages as lines on the chart plot(series=smaFast, color=color.teal, title="Fast SMA") plot(series=smaSlow, color=color.orange, title="Slow SMA") // Determine bar colour by comparing close against the averages smaBarColour = close > smaFast and close > smaSlow ? color.lime : close < smaFast and close < smaSlow ? color.red : close > smaFast ? color.green : close < smaFast ? color.maroon : na // Apply the chosen bar colour to the chart barcolor(color=smaBarColour)
```

```pine_script
// Determine bar colour by comparing close against the averages smaBarColour = close > smaFast and close > smaSlow ? color.lime : close < smaFast and close < smaSlow ? color.red : close > smaFast ? color.green : close < smaFast ? color.maroon : na
```

```pine_script
// Apply the chosen bar colour to the chart barcolor(color=smaBarColour)
```

- A true/false condition that defines when the situation happened.
- And which colour to give bars in that situation.

- Cascaded if statement.
- Switch statement.
- Conditional operators (?:).

- A single price bar can only have one colour. But we can use different colours for different bars.
- There are three ways to pick one from several colours: the cascaded if statement, the switch statement, and conditional operators.
- We can also choose to use the na value for particular bars. That turns off the bar colour.

1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Bar coloring. Retrieved on October 28, 2021, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Bar_coloring.html

lessons.title: How to open trades with limit orders in TradingView’s Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to open trades with limit orders in TradingView’s Pine Script?

## Long entry

### Generate limit order

### Cancel order

### Code combined

## Short entry

### Generate limit order

### Cancel order

### Code combined

## Example strategy

## Summary

### References

A TradingView strategy uses the strategy.entry() function to open trades with a limit order [1] .

To enter with a limit order, we do three things in our Pine Script strategy:

Let’s see how this works for long and short trades.

To enter a long trade with a limit order, we give the strategy.entry() function three pieces of data: the order identifier, strategy.long to make a long entry, and the limit price with the limit argument.

Here strategy.entry() generates an order named "Enter Long". The strategy.long value tells the function to open a long trade. With the limit argument we say at which price to enter. That limit price is the bar’s low (low).

While this code generates a correct limit order, when our strategy uses strategy.entry() like this the limit order executes all the time. That isn’t very helpful. What’s missing is a condition that says when to send the order.

We code such a condition with an if statement. With strategy.entry() placed inside the if statement, the function only executes when the condition tests true.

This if statement’s condition tests the ta.crossover() function. That function looks if the close price crossed above an Exponential Moving Average. That returns true when it did (and false otherwise).

When such a cross indeed happened, the if statement executes the strategy.entry() function. That makes the limit order only generate in that particular situation (as opposed to all the time).

Note that the limit price, which we set at the bar’s low, is the value of when the if statement’s condition was last true. The limit order doesn’t automatically update to each bar’s low. Instead it takes the then-current low price when it generates, and goes on to remember that value for next bars.

What’s left to do is decide when to cancel the pending limit order. Likely we don’t want to keep the order active till the end of time. At some point, the original long setup isn’t there anymore.

So we make another if statement. This one cancels the long limit order in a particular situation:

This if statement’s condition has the ta.barssince() function look if 5 bars elapsed since the last time prices crossed above an Exponential Moving Average. To test that cross we put the ta.crossover() function inside ta.barssince().

When indeed 5 bars passed since that last crossover, the if statement’s condition is true. That makes the strategy.cancel() function cancel the "Enter Long" order. And so when the order we send earlier didn’t fill within 5 bars, strategy.cancel() deletes it.

(If the long limit order already filled within 5 bars, there’s no pending order anymore and strategy.cancel() has nothing to cancel. That’s okay since the function does nothing in that situation.)

To recap, the two steps – generate a limit order and cancel when it shouldn’t fill anymore – look like so:

To enter a short trade with a limit order, we tell the strategy.entry() function three things: the order identifier, strategy.short to make a short entry, and the limit price with the limit argument.

Here’s how that looks:

Here strategy.entry() generates the "Enter Short" order. The strategy.short value tells the function to open a short trade. And the limit argument specifies the limit price. That entry price is the bar’s high.

Though this code generates a proper short limit order, if the strategy calls strategy.entry() like this, the limit order generates on each bar. That’s almost never what we want. What’s lacking is a condition that says when to send the order.

We implement such a condition with an if statement. With strategy.entry() inside an if statement, it only executes when that if statement’s condition evaluates to true.

This if statement evaluates the ta.crossunder() function. That function sees if the close price crossed below an Exponential Moving Average. That returns true when such a cross happened (and gives false otherwise).

When that cross indeed occurred, the if statement executes the strategy.entry() function. That makes the limit order only generate in that particular situation (rather than all the time).

Notice that the limit price, which we set at the bar’s high, is the price of when the if statement’s condition was last true. The limit order doesn’t automatically update to each bar’s high. Instead it takes the then-current high price when it generates, and continues to remember that value for bars to come.

The remaining task is figure out when to cancel the limit order. We probably don’t want to keep the order active for ever. Sooner or later, the short setup isn’t present anymore.

So we code another if statement. This one cancels the short limit order in a particular scenario:

The if statement’s condition makes the ta.barssince() function check if 7 bars happened since the last time prices crossed below the Exponential Average. To evaluate that cross, we place the ta.crossunder() function inside ta.barssince().

When indeed 7 bars occurred since that last crossunder, the if statement’s condition tests true. That makes the strategy.cancel() function cancel the "Enter Short" order. So when the pending order doesn’t fill within 7 bars, we aren’t interested anymore and cancel it.

If the short order did fill within those 7 bars, then the pending order is gone and strategy.cancel() has nothing to cancel. That’s no problem; the function does nothing in that situation.

To recap, we generate a short entry order and cancel when it shouldn’t fill anymore like so:

Let’s have a full strategy open long and short trades with limit orders. The script below trades the Stochastic %K and %D lines.

When %K crosses above %D, the strategy.entry() function submits a long limit entry order. If there’s a cross below, that same order function generates a short entry limit order.

The strategy’s full code is:

We begin with the strategy() function. Doing so names the script.

Then we calculate the Stochastic oscillator. To get the %K value we call the ta.stoch() function and average that function’s value with the ta.sma() function. When we average that Simple Moving Average again, we get the %D value.

Next the plot() function shows both lines on the chart. We don’t set the plot type, so Pine Script makes two regular line plots. These show in blue (color.blue) and orange (color.orange).

The hline() function then makes two horizontal lines. These show at 20 and 80. These are the Stochastics’ overbought and oversold levels.

Then we generate a limit order to open a long trade:

This if statement checks two things. First the ta.crossover() function looks if the %K line crossed above the %D line. The other requirement is that %K is below (<) 20. When both (and) things are so, the if statement’s code runs.

That code calls the strategy.entry() function. This makes a long order (strategy.long) named "Enter Long". It’s a limit order that fills at the bar’s low or better.

The long order shouldn’t remain active forever, though. The strategy cancels the order with this code:

This if statement has ta.crossover() see if the %K line crossed above 40. When that happened, the long order’s window of opportunity is closed. And so the strategy.cancel() function cancels that order if it hasn’t filled yet.

The other part of the strategy opens short trades with a limit order:

This if statement evaluates two things. First the ta.crossunder() function verifies if the %K line crossed below the %D line. We also require %K to be above (>) 80. When both (and) things are so, the if’s code block runs.

That code calls the strategy.entry() function to open a short (strategy.short) trade. This order named "Enter Short" shouldn’t execute at any price, but rather use the current bar’s high as its limit price.

The final bit of strategy code cancels that order when it doesn’t fill:

This if statement lets the ta.crossunder() function verify if %K dropped below 60. When that happened, the if statement executes strategy.cancel() to cancel (delete) the "Enter Short" order. This way the order doesn’t remain active till the end of time.

When we run the strategy on the chart, it trades crosses of the Stochastic %K and %D lines as follows:

```pine_script
strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long, limit=low)
```

```pine_script
// When prices cross above the moving average, generate a buy // (long) order placed at the bar's low if ta.crossover(close, ta.ema(close, 30)) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long, limit=low)
```

```pine_script
// When the long order didn't fill in the 5 bars since // the cross over, cancel the pending long entry order if ta.barssince(ta.crossover(close, ta.ema(close, 30))) == 5 strategy.cancel("Enter Long")
```

```pine_script
// When prices cross above the moving average, generate a buy // (long) order placed at the bar's low if ta.crossover(close, ta.ema(close, 30)) strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long, limit=low) // When the long order didn't fill in the 5 bars since // the cross over, cancel the pending long entry order if ta.barssince(ta.crossover(close, ta.ema(close, 30))) == 5 strategy.cancel("Enter Long")
```

```pine_script
strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short, limit=high)
```

```pine_script
// When prices cross under the moving average, create a sell short // order with a limit of the bar's high if ta.crossunder(close, ta.ema(close, 20)) strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short, limit=high)
```

```pine_script
// When the short order didn't fill in 7 bars since the cross under, // cancel the pending short order if ta.barssince(ta.crossunder(close, ta.ema(close, 20))) == 7 strategy.cancel("Enter Short")
```

```pine_script
// When prices cross above the moving average, create a sell short // order with a limit of the bar's high if ta.crossunder(close, ta.ema(close, 20)) strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short, limit=high) // When the short order didn't fill in 7 bars since the cross under, // cancel the pending short order if ta.barssince(ta.crossunder(close, ta.ema(close, 20))) == 7 strategy.cancel("Enter Short")
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Limit order entries") // Calculate the Stochastic %K and %D values kValue = ta.sma(ta.stoch(close, high, low, 9), 3) dValue = ta.sma(kValue, 3) // Plot the %K and %D lines, as well as overbought and oversold plot(kValue, color=color.blue, title="%K") plot(dValue, color=color.orange, title="%D") hline(20, title="Oversold") hline(80, title="Overbought") // Long entry // When %K crosses above %D, and %K is below 20, submit buy at low if ta.crossover(kValue, dValue) and kValue < 20 strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long, limit=low) // Cancel long entry when %K goes above 40 if ta.crossover(kValue, 40) strategy.cancel("Enter Long") // Short entry // When %K crosses below %D, and %K is above 80, submit short at high if ta.crossunder(kValue, dValue) and kValue > 80 strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short, limit=high) // Cancel short entry when %K drops below 60 if ta.crossunder(kValue, 60) strategy.cancel("Enter Short")
```

```pine_script
// When %K crosses above %D, and %K is below 20, submit buy at low if ta.crossover(kValue, dValue) and kValue < 20 strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long, limit=low)
```

```pine_script
// Cancel long entry when %K goes above 40 if ta.crossover(kValue, 40) strategy.cancel("Enter Long")
```

```pine_script
// When %K crosses below %D, and %K is above 80, submit short at high if ta.crossunder(kValue, dValue) and kValue > 80 strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short, limit=high)
```

```pine_script
// Cancel short entry when %K drops below 60 if ta.crossunder(kValue, 60) strategy.cancel("Enter Short")
```

1. Configure the strategy.entry() function to send a limit order. To do that the function needs three things from us:An order identifier. This is the required name for our limit order.The direction in which to trade. This is strategy.long for a long entry and strategy.short for a short entry.And the limit price to use. The function’s limit argument tells strategy.entry() where to enter.
1. An order identifier. This is the required name for our limit order.
1. The direction in which to trade. This is strategy.long for a long entry and strategy.short for a short entry.
1. And the limit price to use. The function’s limit argument tells strategy.entry() where to enter.
1. Place the strategy.entry() function inside an if statement. The condition of this if statement controls when the order generates. This way the limit order only generates in particular situations (and not all the time).
1. Cancel the limit order when it shouldn’t execute anymore. Else the pending order can fill a long time after its original setup. To delete the order we use the strategy.cancel() function [1] [2] .

- An order identifier. This is the required name for our limit order.
- The direction in which to trade. This is strategy.long for a long entry and strategy.short for a short entry.
- And the limit price to use. The function’s limit argument tells strategy.entry() where to enter.

- TradingView strategies use the strategy.entry() function to open trades with a limit order.
- That function requires three things: an order identifier (that is, order name), the direction, and the limit price.
- Long entry orders use the strategy.long value for their direction; short entries require strategy.short.
- The limit argument of strategy.entry() defines the limit price. Each time code calls the function with that argument, the then-current limit price is set.
- With strategy.entry() inside an if statement, the limit order only generates in a particular situation.
- When the order shouldn’t fill anymore, we cancel it with the strategy.cancel() function.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on April 14, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Strategies. Retrieved on April 14, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Strategies.html

lessons.title: Colouring the background of price bands and price bars in TradingView
lessons.clean_text:
# Colouring the background of price bands and price bars in TradingView

## Colouring chart areas and the full background in TradingView

## Colouring a TradingView background or chart area programmatically

## Colouring the chart and its different chart areas programmatically

## Summary

### References

In TradingView we can colour the chart’s background from top to bottom and colour a background area as well. How do these features compare and how do we combine them?

A TradingView background can be coloured with a basic TradingView colour or a hexadecimal colour value. Those backgrounds are displayed in the area of the chart’s main instrument or in the script’s subchart, depending on whether the overlay argument of the indicator() or strategy() function is set to true or false, respectively (TradingView, n.d.).

There are two types of coloured backgrounds in TradingView. The first is created by the bgcolor() function, and gives the bar’s full background from top to bottom a certain colour (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). We take a closer look at this function in colouring a chart’s background in TradingView.

The fill() function also colours the background. But instead of the full background, it colours the background area that’s between two plots or two horizontal lines (Pine Script Language Tutorial, n.d.). To know between which plots or horizontal lines the background area should be coloured, the function either requires two plot objects (that are returned by the plot() function) or two horizontal line objects, which the hline() function returns (TradingView, n.d.). While objects sound abstract, they represent a plot or line just like a numerical variable represents a certain number. For more on the fill() function and its arguments, see colouring part of the chart’s background.

The bgcolor() and fill() functions have similarities and differences. Their sameness includes a color argument to set the background colour (TradingView, n.d.). A remarkable difference is that bgcolor() can colour the background conditionally while fill() cannot. This means that the background made by fill() is the same on every bar, while bgcolor() can use different colours and even ’turn off’ the coloured background.

For a better understanding of the differences and similarities between bgcolor() and fill(), let’s look at an example indicator that combines both.

In the example below we plot an EMA (Exponential Moving Average) together with four price bands. Those price bands are computed with a certain number of standard deviations, and two of them are placed above the EMA while the other two are below this moving average.

That is like the Bollinger Bands, an indicator that generates trend following signals whenever the price exceeds its bands (Pring, 2002). When prices move outside the bands in our example indicator, we’ll colour the bar’s full background while we use fill() to colour the area between the bands. Afterwards we’ll look at charts with the indicator on it, but let’s first discuss the code:

We start the indicator with the indicator() function. With this function’s overlay argument set to true the script displays in the main price chart area and not in a separate subchart. This also means our coloured backgrounds will be applied to the chart’s instrument.

Next we add several inputs to the script:

Inputs make it easy to manually change a value that’s used in the script’s code. When we store an input function’s result in a variable, we can access that variable in the code to retrieve the input’s current value.

We make several inputs with the above code. The first is a so-called ‘source’ input, which Pine Script’s input.source() function makes. This input has a pull-down menu for selecting different kinds of price data from the instrument that the script is added to, such as its high, low, or close prices. We set its default to hl2, a value that resembles the bar’s midpoint (that is, [high + low] / 2). The name of this input is “Price data”. And we store the input’s value to the priceData variable, and we’ll use this to compute the EMA and standard deviation on.

We then add two integer inputs with the input.int() function. The first of these is named “MA length” with a default value of 20, and has its current setting stored in the maLength variable. The other integer input is named “StDev length” and has a default value of 12. This input, whose value is stored in the sdLength variable, is used later on to set the standard deviation length.

The next two inputs are floating-point inputs. These decimal value inputs are made by the input.float() function. We name them “1st StDev offset” and “2nd StDev offset” and use these later on when multiplying the standard deviation values. With the minval argument, which sets a numerical input’s minimum value, we ensure that the minimum standard deviation offset is one-tenth. Their current values are stored in the offset1 and offset2 variables.

Then we make a true/false checkbox input with the input.bool() function. We store this input’s current value in the colourBg variable that we’ll use later on to colour the background conditionally. This input is titled “Colour background?” and is enabled by default (true).

The last input option that we make is again a integer input with input.int(). We name this one “High/low lookback” and give it a default value of 20 with its current value put into the highLowLen variable. That variable is used to set the period of the highest high and lowest low when we check if there’s a strong breakout.

After creating the input options we calculate several values:

The value that we assign to the maValue variable here is returned by ta.ema(), a function that calculates the EMA based on a series of values alongside an integer that sets the amount of bars to compute the moving average on (TradingView, n.d.). We base the EMA on priceData, our input variable that we set to the bar’s midpoint (hl2) value earlier while the moving average length is set with the maLength input variable.

We calculate the standard deviation with ta.stdev(). That function, just like ta.ema(), works on a series of values and an integer that sets the standard deviation length (TradingView, n.d.). We set the first argument to the priceData input here, and set the number of bars to calculate the standard deviation on with sdLength. The value returned by ta.stdev() is stored in the sdValue variable for use later on in the script.

After computing the EMA and the standard deviation we determine the price bands. The two price bands above the EMA are stored in the maPlus2 and maPlus1 variables, while the maMin1 and maMin2 variables hold the price bands values below the moving average. We get these price bands by taking the EMA value (maValue) and adding or subtracting an offset value times the standard deviation (sdValue). That multiplication is performed with the offset1 and offset2 variables that have standard values of 1.25 and 2.25.

Next those values are plotted on the chart:

We plot the values on the chart with plot(), a function that displays whichever data its first argument is set to (TradingView, n.d.). Other plot() arguments that we use here are color (which sets the plot colour) and linewidth. That latter argument sets the plot size with values ranging from 1 (default) to 4 for the thickest plot possible (TradingView, n.d.).

The plot() function always returns a plot object, but we often have no use for it. But that’s not the case in our current example: since we’re going to fill the background area between two plots, we store each plot object in a variable for use later.

The first plot() function call displays the maPlus2 variable on the chart, which is the top price band that’s furthest away from the EMA. We set this line to the green (color.green) basic TradingView colour and give it a linewidth that’s one step above the default size. The second plot() function call is highly similar, except that now we plot the maPlus1 values that are one standard deviation offset above the EMA.

The plot object that’s stored in the maPlot variable are the EMA values (maValue) plotted in grey (color.gray), with a plot slightly greater than typically (linewidth=2). This plot has its style argument set to plot.style_circles to have it plot small, non-connecting dots instead of the standard line plot (TradingView, n.d.).

The last two plot() function calls display the maMin1 and maMin2 variables on the chart, which are the price bands placed below the EMA based on some multiple of the standard deviation value. These lines plotted in red (color.red) have their plot objects stored in the plotMin1 and plotMin2 variables with the assignment operator (=).

Now, let’s use those plot objects to colour several chart areas:

With the fill() function we colour the chart’s background that’s between two plots or two horizontal lines (TradingView, n.d.). Here we use that function four times, each time to colour a background area between two plots. With this function’s plot1 and plot2 arguments we specify between which plots the background should be filled (TradingView, n.d.).

The first fill() function call fills the area between the plotPlus2 and plotPlus1 plots, which are the price bands plotted above the EMA value. We set this background’s colour to green (color.green), made 87% transparent with the color.new() function.

The next fill() statement colours the background section between the plotPlus1 plot (the first price band above the EMA) and the EMA value itself (maPlot). This filled background is also green, but only very lightly due to the 92% transparency color.new() uses.

The third fill() function call fills the area between the maPlot and plotMin1 plots, which is the chart background between the EMA and the first price band plotted below this moving average. This area is coloured red (color.red) with 92% transparency set by the color.new() function.

The fourth and last fill() statement fills the chart area between the two price bands that are below the EMA (the plotMin1 and plotMin2 plots), and also makes this background section red but a bit more pronounced due to a lower transparency of 87%.

After colouring the area between the plots, we highlight price bars that moved outside the price bands with a coloured background:

We assign the backgroundColour variable a value here with the help of a cascaded if statement.

The first if condition tests two expressions with the and operator. This operator only returns true when the value on its left and the one on its right are both true, and returns false when one or both of them are false.

We test the colourBg variable, which is our true/false input variable of the “Colour background?” setting. This variable holds true when that input is enabled and false when disabled. The other part of our condition is whether the value of priceData (which holds the bar’s midpoint by default) is greater than (>) the topmost price band (maPlus2).

When both expressions test true, Pine Script returns green (color.green) made 90% transparent with the color.new() function. This colour then gets stored in the backgroundColour variable for use later.

Should those expressions test false, the second if condition runs. This one also checks the colourBg input variable besides verifying if the price is less than the lowest price band (priceData < maMin2). When the bar’s price indeed moved below the lowest price band and we’ve enabled the “Colour background?” input option, then we return red (color.red) with 90% transparency set by the color.new() function. This colour then becomes the value of the backgroundColour variable.

In case both if conditions are false, Pine Script implicitly returns the na value. This has the effect of turning off the coloured background.

Finally we call the bgcolor() function and pass it the backgroundColour variable. That applies one of the selected colours when the corresponding setup occurs.

So what happens here? When the price moves above the upper price band and the “Colour background?” option is enabled, then we’re making the bar’s background from top to bottom green. Should the price drop below the lowest price band and “Colour background?” is enabled, then that the background is made red. When the price hasn’t moved outside the price bands and/or the “Colour background?” option is turned off, then the background is transparent (that is, ’turned off’). This shows how we can use the “Colour background?” input option to turn the background colouring on or off.

The example ends with another code segment that conditionally colours the background:

We create two Boolean variables here: newHigh and newLow. The first is assigned true or false depending on a condition that checks if the current bar’s high equals (==) the highest high. That latter value is retrieved with the ta.highest() function with two arguments: a series of values and an integer that sets the number of bars to get the highest price of (TradingView, n.d.). With the high and highLowLen values passed into this function, we get the 20-bar high price (assuming that the “High/low lookback” input option is still set to its standard value of 20). This way our newHigh variable holds true when the bar’s high is a new 20-bar high.

The newLow variable is set similarly, this time checking if the current bar’s low equals the lowest low of the last highLowLen number of bars, including the current bar. When that’s the case, newLow holds a value of true and false otherwise.

Then we determine what additional colour to give the background. For this we make the highsLowsBackground variable and set its value with a cascaded if statement.

The first if condition looks if the coloured background is enabled (colourBg), is a new high happened (newHigh), and prices are above the topmost band (priceData > maPlus2). When those three things are the case, the color.new() function returns lime green (color.lime) with 85% transparency.

Otherwise the second if condition runs. This one looks if a coloured background is okay (colourBg), whether a new recent low happened (newLow), and if prices are below the lowest band (priceData < maMin2). When those three things are so, color.new() returns fuchsia (color.fuchsia) with 85% transparency.

Lastly, we call the bgcolor() function and pass it the highsLowsBackground variable. That makes the background transparent lime green or fuchsia, or doesn’t colour the background when neither condition occurred.

When we add the above example indicator to a EuroStoxx 50 CFD chart, it looks this:

And applied to a S&P 500 Index CFD chart, our example indicator displays as:

The input options that the script makes are the following:

When we change the ‘Price Data’ input to ‘close’, set the moving average and standard deviation length to 35 and 20, and disable the ‘Colour Background?’ option; then the previous S&P 500 Index CFD chart changes to:

The background of a TradingView chart can be coloured with the bgcolor() and fill() functions. The first colours the bar’s background from top to bottom, while the second fills the background that’s between two plots or two horizontal lines. For this the fill() function requires two plot objects (which are returned by plot()) or two horizontal line objects that hline() returns. The bgcolor() function can colour the background conditionally; something that isn’t possible with fill().

Pring, M.J. (2002). Technical Analysis Explained (4th edition). New York, NY: McGraw-Hill.

Pine Script Language Tutorial (n.d.). Retrieved on October 23, 2015, from https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sCfC873xJEMV7MGzt1L70JTStTE9kcG2q-LDuWWkBeY/

TradingView (n.d.). Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on December 29, 2015, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Overlaying coloured background areas", overlay=true) // Input options priceData = input.source(hl2, title="Price data") maLength = input.int(20, title="MA length") sdLength = input.int(12, title="StDev length") offset1 = input.float(1.25, title="1st StDev offset", minval=0.1) offset2 = input.float(2.25, title="2nd StDev offset", minval=0.1) colourBg = input.bool(true, title="Colour background?") highLowLen = input.int(20, title="High/low lookback") // Compute values maValue = ta.ema(priceData, maLength) sdValue = ta.stdev(priceData, sdLength) maPlus2 = maValue + (offset2 * sdValue) maPlus1 = maValue + (offset1 * sdValue) maMin1 = maValue - (offset1 * sdValue) maMin2 = maValue - (offset2 * sdValue) // Plot values plotPlus2 = plot(maPlus2, color=color.green, linewidth=2) plotPlus1 = plot(maPlus1, color=color.green, linewidth=2) maPlot = plot(maValue, color=color.gray, style=plot.style_circles, linewidth=2) plotMin1 = plot(maMin1, color=color.red, linewidth=2) plotMin2 = plot(maMin2, color=color.red, linewidth=2) // Colour background sections between plots fill(plot1=plotPlus2, plot2=plotPlus1, color=color.new(color.green, 87)) fill(plot1=plotPlus1, plot2=maPlot, color=color.new(color.green, 92)) fill(plot1=maPlot, plot2=plotMin1, color=color.new(color.red, 92)) fill(plot1=plotMin1, plot2=plotMin2, color=color.new(color.red, 87)) // Colour full background from top to bottom backgroundColour = if colourBg and priceData > maPlus2 color.new(color.green, 90) else if colourBg and priceData < maMin2 color.new(color.red, 90) bgcolor(backgroundColour) // Create coloured background for new highs/lows newHigh = high == ta.highest(high, highLowLen) newLow = low == ta.lowest(low, highLowLen) highsLowsBackground = if colourBg and newHigh and priceData > maPlus2 color.new(color.lime, 85) else if colourBg and newLow and priceData < maMin2 color.new(color.fuchsia, 85) bgcolor(highsLowsBackground)
```

```pine_script
// Input options priceData = input.source(hl2, title="Price data") maLength = input.int(20, title="MA length") sdLength = input.int(12, title="StDev length") offset1 = input.float(1.25, title="1st StDev offset", minval=0.1) offset2 = input.float(2.25, title="2nd StDev offset", minval=0.1) colourBg = input.bool(true, title="Colour background?") highLowLen = input.int(20, title="High/low lookback")
```

```pine_script
maValue = ta.ema(priceData, maLength) sdValue = ta.stdev(priceData, sdLength) maPlus2 = maValue + (offset2 * sdValue) maPlus1 = maValue + (offset1 * sdValue) maMin1 = maValue - (offset1 * sdValue) maMin2 = maValue - (offset2 * sdValue)
```

```pine_script
plotPlus2 = plot(maPlus2, color=color.green, linewidth=2) plotPlus1 = plot(maPlus1, color=color.green, linewidth=2) maPlot = plot(maValue, color=color.gray, style=plot.style_circles, linewidth=2) plotMin1 = plot(maMin1, color=color.red, linewidth=2) plotMin2 = plot(maMin2, color=color.red, linewidth=2)
```

```pine_script
fill(plot1=plotPlus2, plot2=plotPlus1, color=color.new(color.green, 87)) fill(plot1=plotPlus1, plot2=maPlot, color=color.new(color.green, 92)) fill(plot1=maPlot, plot2=plotMin1, color=color.new(color.red, 92)) fill(plot1=plotMin1, plot2=plotMin2, color=color.new(color.red, 87))
```

```pine_script
// Colour full background from top to bottom backgroundColour = if colourBg and priceData > maPlus2 color.new(color.green, 90) else if colourBg and priceData < maMin2 color.new(color.red, 90) bgcolor(backgroundColour)
```

```pine_script
// Create coloured background for new highs/lows newHigh = high == ta.highest(high, highLowLen) newLow = low == ta.lowest(low, highLowLen) highsLowsBackground = if colourBg and newHigh and priceData > maPlus2 color.new(color.lime, 85) else if colourBg and newLow and priceData < maMin2 color.new(color.fuchsia, 85) bgcolor(highsLowsBackground)
```

lessons.title: How to stop a TradingView strategy based on weekly loss?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to stop a TradingView strategy based on weekly loss?

## Stop trading based on weekly strategy loss in TradingView

## Overview: steps to stop trading based on weekly loss

### Step 1. Make an input option (optional)

### Step 2. Get strategy equity of last week

### Step 3. Determine the strategy’s weekly loss

### Step 4. Compare weekly performance against maximum loss

### Step 5. Check weekly loss before sending entry orders

### Step 6. Flatten the strategy when weekly loss is hit

## Example strategy: stop trading based on weekly loss

### References

A strategy that loses a significant amount of money also has to show exceptional performance to recover back. Often it’s easier to prevent large losses than make them back. Let’s see how a TradingView strategy can stop automatically based on weekly loss.

A serious strategy risk are of course losses. Once those rack up, a strategy has show exceptional performance just to break-even. One approach that prevents runaway losses is a weekly maximum. Then when that limit is reached, the strategy stops for the week.

That way our script doesn’t continue trading when opportunities are simply not there. Let’s see how we implement that in TradingView Pine.

To have our strategy script stop based on weekly performance we’ll implement the following steps:

Let’s go through these steps and see what code we have to write.

To not have to edit the code whenever the weekly loss should change, we make an input option:

We use TradingView’s input.int() function to make an integer input here. We give it a standard value of 1,250 and a minimum (minval) of 1. (The other code becomes easier when we know for sure that the input has a positive value.)

We track the input’s value with the maxLoss variable. To store a negative value in that variable we multiply the value returned by input.int() with -1. This makes other comparisons later on easier.

(If you don’t make the optional input option, do note that maxLoss in the other steps refers to the weekly maximum loss expressed as a negative value.)

One way to track weekly performance is to get the strategy equity from last week. Then during the week we compare current equity against that previous value. That equity is the sum of the strategy’s initial capital, closed trade profits, and open position profit/loss.

Here’s how we fetch that value from last week:

Here we first make the equityLastWeek variable. Then TradingView’s conditional operator (?:) gives the variable its actual value.

For that the operator evaluates a condition with two parts. The first part looks if the current bar’s day of week (dayofweek) equals (==) Monday (dayofweek.monday). The other part looks if the current bar is the first of the day. That happens when dayofweek is different than (!=) its previous bar value (dayofweek[1]).

When those two things occur at the same time, the strategy processes a price bar that’s the first of the week. And when that happens, the strategy equity from last week is the previous bar value of strategy.equity.

Should the current bar is not be the first on Monday morning, then the conditional operator repeats the previous bar value of equityLastWeek. This way that variable of ours only updates once a week, when a new week starts. On the other bars the equity from last week remains static (which is precisely how it should behave).

(If your trading week starts on Sunday, then replace the dayofweek.monday variable in the above code with the dayofweek.sunday variable.)

With the last week strategy equity we can calculate the strategy’s current performance:

To figure out the strategy’s weekly loss we compare its current equity (strategy.equity) with the strategy equity at end of last week (equityLastWeek). We store that outcome in the weeklyLoss variable for use later.

Now we can compare the strategy’s weekly performance against its maximum weekly loss. Since we also need that outcome in the next steps, it’s easiest to store it in a true/false variable.

This checks if the weeklyLoss variable is greater than (>) maxLoss, the input variable from step 1 that has a default of -1,250. As long as the strategy’s weekly performance is above that value, the okToTrade variable we make here is true (and false otherwise).

Now that we have a true/false variable that tells if our strategy should trade (true) or not (false), we can use that variable with our long and short entry conditions.

The first if statement submits an enter long order with the strategy.entry() function. But only when okToTrade and enterLong are true. When one or both are false, then our buy order isn’t submitted.

The second if statement is similar. This one checks both okToTrade and enterShort before submitting an enter short order with strategy.entry().

When the strategy hits its maximum weekly loss, okToTrade becomes false. That in turn prevents that strategy.entry() sends new long and short orders, which stops the strategy from trading.

The enterLong and enterShort variables in the above code are just a substitute for your strategy’s actual long and short conditions. Your code likely looks different. But the same idea still holds: check okToTrade before sending a new entry order.

Since TradingView has two order functions that can open trades, look in your code where you use the strategy.entry() function and the strategy.order() function.

In the last step we flatten the strategy when the weekly loss is reached. That way the losses cannot worsen further because of an open market position.

And so we exit the strategy’s open market position:

This if statement has the not logical operator see if okToTrade is false. When that variable has that value, we know that the strategy’s weekly loss is hit.

In that case we execute TradingView’s strategy.close_all() function. If our strategy has an open position, then that function sends a market order to close that position.

Two noteworthy things are:

The code below implements the above steps in a complete script. The example strategy trades two Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs). When the fast one crosses over the slower one, the script goes long. And if the short average falls under the longer one, the strategy opens a short trade.

That goes on until the weekly loss is reached. When that happens trading stops and the strategy has to wait till next week. The script also plots its weekly loss and maximum loss on the chart. That makes it easy to track how the strategy performs during the week.

The complete strategy code is:

Let’s see how this strategy behaves on the chart. On the FTSE 100 CFD chart below we see the script open a short trade on the 10th. That trade didn’t perform well, and actually made the strategy reach its maximum weekly loss.

When that happened the strategy went flat and stopped trading for the week:

The crude oil CFD chart below shows a longer-term view. Here the strategy first hits its weekly loss. Then a new week started and the strategy could try again. Unfortunately it didn’t go much better: the script again reached its maximum loss and stopped trading again.

To limit a strategy’s intra-day loss you don’t have to write custom code like we did in this article. That’s because TradingView already has a risk management function for that.

With the strategy.risk.max_intraday_loss() function we make our strategy stop for the day once a certain intra-day loss is reached (TradingView, n.d.).

Browse the risk management category for other ways to limit and manage TradingView strategy risks.

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on May 3, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/study-script-reference/

```pine_script
// Configure maximum weekly loss with input maxLoss = input.int(1250, title="Weekly Max Loss", minval=1) * -1
```

