```pine_script
// Figure out strategy equity at close of trading last week equityLastWeek = 0.0 equityLastWeek := dayofweek == dayofweek.monday and dayofweek != dayofweek[1] ? strategy.equity[1] : equityLastWeek[1]
```

```pine_script
// Calculate weekly strategy loss weeklyLoss = strategy.equity - equityLastWeek
```

```pine_script
// See if the strategy is still allowed to trade okToTrade = weeklyLoss > maxLoss
```

```pine_script
// Submit orders if okToTrade and enterLong strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long) if okToTrade and enterShort strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short)
```

```pine_script
// Close the open position when the weekly loss is hit if not okToTrade strategy.close_all()
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Stop trading after weekly loss", overlay=false, default_qty_type=strategy.fixed, default_qty_value=10) // STEP 1: // Configure maximum weekly loss with input maxLoss = input.int(1250, title="Weekly Max Loss", minval=1) * -1 // Calculate moving averages fastMA = ta.ema(close, 5) slowMA = ta.ema(close, 25) // Determine trading conditions enterLong = ta.crossover(fastMA, slowMA) enterShort = ta.crossunder(fastMA, slowMA) // STEP 2: // Figure out strategy equity at close of trading last week equityLastWeek = 0.0 equityLastWeek := dayofweek == dayofweek.monday and dayofweek != dayofweek[1] ? strategy.equity[1] : equityLastWeek[1] // STEP 3: // Calculate strategy loss this week weeklyLoss = strategy.equity - equityLastWeek // Show weekly loss and trigger level on the chart plot(math.min(weeklyLoss, 0), style=plot.style_area, color=color.new(color.red, 85)) hline(0, color=color.orange, linestyle=hline.style_solid) hline(maxLoss, color=color.red, linestyle=hline.style_solid, linewidth=3) // STEP 4: // Determine if the strategy is still allowed to trade okToTrade = weeklyLoss > maxLoss // STEP 5: // Submit orders if okToTrade and enterLong strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long) if okToTrade and enterShort strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short) // STEP 6: // Close the open position when the weekly loss is hit if not okToTrade strategy.close_all()
```

1. Optional: Make an input that configures the maximum weekly loss.
1. Fetch the strategy equity as it was at the close of trading last week.
1. Compare that last week equity with the current equity to figure out the strategy’s weekly loss.
1. Then compare that weekly loss with the maximum.
1. Include that comparison with the strategy’s entry logic. That way no entry order is send when the weekly loss is reached.
1. And finally exit the strategy’s open market position when the weekly loss is reached. That way trading stops completely.

- Since we already checked okToTrade before we send any entry order, there’s nothing else to do after we flatten the open position. In other words, our strategy now stops trading completely because of the false value that okToTrade has.
- Because we exit the open position with a market order, losses can be a bit worse than the ‘maximum’ loss due to slippage and commissions.

lessons.title: Get the number of closed trades from a Pine Script strategy in TradingView
lessons.clean_text:
# Get the number of closed trades from a Pine Script strategy in TradingView

## Quick examples of strategy.closedtrades

## Features of strategy.closedtrades

## Example strategy

## Summary

### References

In a Pine Script strategy, we get the number of completed trades with the strategy.closedtrades variable [1] . That variable returns an integer with how many trades the strategy closed. It counts those transactions from the chart’s first bar until the current bar.

When the strategy finished its historical test at the end of the chart, strategy.closedtrades returns the strategy’s total number of closed trades. We can also use this variable on any other bar. That tells how many trades the strategy closed until that bar.

There are a couple different things we can use the strategy.closedtrades variable for.

The first option is to fetch the strategy’s current number of closed trades. For that we use strategy.closedtrades where the code should use that information. To see if the strategy did more than a certain number of closed trades, we code:

In the same way we can compare strategy.closedtrades with other strategy variables. Say we want to know if the strategy’s win percentage is 50% or more. A quick test of that looks if the number of winning trades (strategy.wintrades) is more than half the total number of trades:

These strategy.closedtrades comparisons are what the strategy can use with its decision making. We could, for instance, only generate new short entries when there are less than 200 trades:

The second way to use strategy.closedtrades is with the history operator ([]). That gets us how many closed trades a particular previous bar had. When we compare that historical value against the current bar, we can tell how many trades happened since then:

When we compare the variable’s current value with its previous bar value, we find out if the strategy just closed a trade. The Pine Script code for that looks like:

The third strategy.closedtrades feature is that this variable has a value on every bar. That makes it possible for functions to calculate with the variable’s values.

We can, for example, get the Exponential Moving Average of the closed trades:

Let’s explore how a full strategy can use the strategy.closedtrades variable. The script below trades 20-bar breakouts. When prices move above the highest high, we go long. If they drop below the lowest low, we go short.

We plot the strategy’s total number of closed trades with strategy.closedtrades. We also show that variable’s Weighted Moving Average. And highlight the background for each new closed trade.

The strategy’s entire code is:

We start with the strategy() function. That’s how we name the script.

Next the ta.highest() function calculate the 20-bar highest high. The ta.lowest() function gets us the 20-bar lowest low.

Two if statements then look for trade entries. The first checks if the current bar’s high is above the highest high. When it is, the strategy.entry() function opens a long trade (strategy.long).

The other if statement looks if the low went under the lowest low. That has strategy.entry() open a short trade (strategy.short).

Then we plot the trade count on the chart:

Here the plot() function shows the current value of the strategy.closedtrades variable. Since we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes a regular line by default. That line shows in black (color.black).

Next we compute the closed trade count’s moving average:

This code first calls the ta.wma() function to calculate the 15-bar Weighted Moving Average of strategy.closedtrades. Then plot() shows that average on the chart as a blue (color.blue), solid line.

Lastly we colour the background:

This code first tests if the value of strategy.closedtrades is bigger than (>) its previous bar value. When it is, we get a true value (and get false otherwise). The outcome we store in the newClosedTrade variable.

Then the bgcolor() function colours the chart’s background. But not every bar. Instead the conditional operator (?:) evaluates the newClosedTrade variable. When it’s true, the operator returns a transparent orange (color.orange). bgcolor() then uses that colour for the background. Else, when there’s no closed trade, the conditional operator returns the na value. That turns off the coloured background.

On the chart, the strategy plots the current and average number of closed trades. The coloured background makes it easy to spot when the strategy closes a trade:

```pine_script
// See if the strategy performed more than 100 trades if strategy.closedtrades > 100 label.new(bar_index, high, text="Did more than hundred trades!")
```

```pine_script
// See if 50% or more of closed trades were profitable if strategy.wintrades >= (strategy.closedtrades * 0.5) label.new(bar_index, high, color=color.lime, text="More than half of trades are profitable!")
```

```pine_script
// When the bar closed below the 20-bar SMA, and there are // less than 200 trades in total, go short if ta.crossunder(close, ta.sma(close, 20)) and strategy.closedtrades < 200 strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short)
```

```pine_script
// Test if the strategy did more than 7 trades in the past 25 bars if strategy.closedtrades - strategy.closedtrades[25] > 7 label.new(bar_index, high, text="Did more than 7 trades recently!")
```

```pine_script
// Colour the background teal when the strategy closed a trade bgcolor(strategy.closedtrades > strategy.closedtrades[1] ? color.new(color.teal, 80) : na)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate and plot the EMA of the strategy's trade count plot(ta.ema(strategy.closedtrades, 40), color=color.blue, title="Trade Count EMA")
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Closed trades example") // Calculate the recent highest high and lowest low highestHigh = ta.highest(high, 20)[1] lowestLow = ta.lowest(low, 20)[1] // Generate the breakout trades if high > highestHigh strategy.entry("Enter Long", strategy.long) if low < lowestLow strategy.entry("Enter Short", strategy.short) // Display the strategy's total trade count plot(strategy.closedtrades, color=color.black, linewidth=2) // Calculate and plot the moving average of the trade amount tradeCountWMA = ta.wma(strategy.closedtrades, 15) plot(tradeCountWMA, color=color.blue, title="Trade Count WMA") // When there's a new closed trade, make the background orange newClosedTrade = strategy.closedtrades > strategy.closedtrades[1] bgcolor(newClosedTrade ? color.new(color.orange, 80) : na)
```

```pine_script
// Display the strategy's total trade count plot(strategy.closedtrades, color=color.black, linewidth=2)
```

```pine_script
// Calculate and plot the moving average of the trade amount tradeCountWMA = ta.wma(strategy.closedtrades, 15) plot(tradeCountWMA, color=color.blue, title="Trade Count WMA")
```

```pine_script
// When there's a new closed trade, make the background orange newClosedTrade = strategy.closedtrades > strategy.closedtrades[1] bgcolor(newClosedTrade ? color.new(color.orange, 80) : na)
```

- The value of strategy.closedtrades changes as the strategy processes the chart’s bars. On the 50th bar this variable has a different value than on bar 9,950 (assuming the strategy traded in the meantime).
- strategy.closedtrades isn’t the same as the strategy’s total trade count, as this variable excludes the strategy’s open trades (strategy.opentrades).
- Also, the strategy’s closed trades is something else than its closed positions. There’s a subtle difference between these two. A single position can have several trades. If the strategy scales into a position with 6 trades and then exits the market, there are 6 closed trades but only 1 closed position.
- On a bar-by-bar basis, the value of strategy.closedtrades doesn’t always increase sequentially. If a strategy did 44 trades and then closes two entry orders on the same bar, the value of strategy.closedtrades jumps to 46 on that bar.
- The value of strategy.closedtrades is the sum of profitable trades (strategy.wintrades), losing trades (strategy.losstrades), and break-even trades (strategy.eventrades).
- We cannot use strategy.closedtrades in indicators. If we try, TradingView generates the ‘cannot use strategy functions in indicator script’ compilation error.

- The strategy.closedtrades variable returns how many trades the strategy closed.
- That count includes the winning, losing, and break-even trades. It excludes the number of open trades.
- The closed trade count can be different than the number of closed positions: 1 position can have 5 separate scale-in trades.
- strategy.closedtrades has a value on each bar. That makes bar-to-bar comparisons possible. And allows functions to calculate with this variable.

1. TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on March 4, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v5/

lessons.title: How to make a tick-based input option in Pine Script?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to make a tick-based input option in Pine Script?

## Step 1: Make a tick input

## Step 2: Use the input’s value with code

## Example script

## Summary

The tick size tells what the smallest possible price change of an instrument is. When our script has to work with a number of ticks, we can specify that amount in the code or with an input.

An input option that configures a tick amount is much easier to work with. Our script can use such an input in several ways. For instance as a breakout filter (only trade when prices break out a certain number of ticks). Or as a stop-loss offset or profit target distance.

There are two steps to create a tick-based input:

Let’s take a look at the code!

First we make an input that sets the number of ticks. Then we translate its value into how much price change those ticks are.

The code for this step is:

Here the input.int() function makes an integer input option. It has a default value of 1. The title argument names the input ‘Ticks Offset’. With minval the input gets a minimum value of zero (0).

We now have an input that says how much ticks to use. But that amount isn’t something we can use in the code yet. For that we translate the tick amount into the instrument’s price scale equivalent.

To do so we multiply the value of input.int() with the syminfo.mintick variable. That variable returns the instrument’s smallest possible price change. When multiplied with the number of ticks, we know the price for the tick amount. We store that value in the tickOffsetInput variable for use later.

Take the E-mini S&P 500 (ES) future for instance. When we set the input to 4, our code should calculate with price value of four ES ticks. One tick is worth 0.25, so the corresponding price amount is 1.00.

The input we make in this step looks like:

Now let’s use the input in the script’s code.

Now we use the tick-based input somewhere in our script. Where that is depends on what code should use the tick amount.

Say we want to find highest high and lowest low breakouts, but only if prices move a certain number of ticks past the highs and lows. One way to use the input option for that is:

First we calculate the highest high with the ta.highest() function. That function processes high prices (high) for a length of 20 bars. We get the lowest low with the ta.lowest() function. That one handles low prices for the same length.

Then we look for breakouts. An upward breakout happens when the bar’s high is above the highest high increased with the tick-based input’s value (tickOffsetInput). When that’s so the highBreakout variable is true (and false otherwise).

For the low breakout we want the bar’s low under the lowest low minus the ‘Ticks Offset’ input. That makes the lowBreakout variable true (and makes it false otherwise).

Other code can now take action based on the highBreakout and lowBreakout variables. For instance, we can colour the background of breakouts, generate alerts, or simulate trades.

Let’s see how a full script can use a tick-based input. The indicator below colours the chart’s background for highest high and lowest low breakouts. The input configures how many ticks prices have to move for valid signals.

The indicator’s full code is:

We first configure the indicator with the indicator() function. That names the script and overlays it on the chart’s instrument.

Then we make the tick-based input as we discussed above.

Next we calculate the highest high with the ta.highest() function. And use the ta.lowest() function to get the lowest low.

We have the plot() function show both on the chart in green (color.green) and red (color.red), respectively. Since we don’t set the plot type, Pine Script makes regular line plots.

Then we determine the breakout signals. This uses the same approach as the earlier example above. So an upward breakout has prices have to move a number of ticks past the highest high. And a low breakout requires that prices drop a number of ticks below the lowest low.

Next a cascaded if statement determines the background colour. For a high breakout we use green set to 85% transparency with the color.new() function. And if a low breakout happens we use a 85% transparent red.

The final line of code calls the bgcolor() function. That applies the background to the chart.

On the chart, the indicator starts with its tick-based input set to 1. That requires prices to move one additional tick past the highest high and lowest low. The signals that generates look like so:

When we change the ‘Ticks Offset’ input option from 1 to 6, prices need to move an extra 5 ticks beyond the highest high and lowest low. That reduces the number of signals considerably:

```pine_script
// STEP 1. Make an input that specifies the number of ticks. // Then multiply its value with syminfo.mintick to turn // the ticks into the instrument's price scale. tickOffsetInput = input.int(1, title="Ticks Offset", minval=0) * syminfo.mintick
```

```pine_script
// STEP 2. Use the price value of the tick amount in the code. // Here we check if the high moved the number of ticks beyond the // highest high, and if the low went the tick amount under the lowest low. highBreakout = high > ta.highest(high, 20)[1] + tickOffsetInput lowBreakout = low < ta.lowest(low, 20)[1] - tickOffsetInput
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 indicator(title="Tick-based input example", overlay=true) // STEP 1. Make an input that specifies the number of ticks. // Then multiply its value with syminfo.mintick to turn // the ticks into the instrument's price scale. tickOffsetInput = input.int(1, title="Ticks Offset", minval=0) * syminfo.mintick // Calculate and plot the highest high and lowest low highestHigh = ta.highest(high, 20)[1] lowestLow = ta.lowest(low, 20)[1] plot(highestHigh, color=color.green, title="Highest High") plot(lowestLow, color=color.red, title="Lowest Low") // STEP 2. Use the price value of the tick amount in the code. // Here we check if the high moved the number of ticks beyond the // highest high, and if the low went the tick amount under the lowest low. highBreakout = high > highestHigh + tickOffsetInput lowBreakout = low < lowestLow - tickOffsetInput // Determine and apply background colour for high and low breakouts backgroundColour = if highBreakout color.new(color.green, 85) else if lowBreakout color.new(color.red, 85) bgcolor(backgroundColour, title="Breakout Signals")
```

1. Make and prepare the input:Create an integer input that sets the number of ticks.Multiply the input with the syminfo.mintick variable. That turns the tick amount into the instrument’s price scale equivalent.
1. Create an integer input that sets the number of ticks.
1. Multiply the input with the syminfo.mintick variable. That turns the tick amount into the instrument’s price scale equivalent.
1. Use the input’s value in the script, where the code has to work with the tick amount.

- Create an integer input that sets the number of ticks.
- Multiply the input with the syminfo.mintick variable. That turns the tick amount into the instrument’s price scale equivalent.

- To make a tick-based input we first make an integer input option with the input.int() function.
- Next we multiply the input’s value with the syminfo.mintick variable. That turns the ticks into a price change value.
- And then we use that price amount somewhere in our script.

lessons.title: How does margin affect Pine Script strategies in TradingView?
lessons.clean_text:
# How does margin affect Pine Script strategies in TradingView?

## 1) Order skipped

## 2) Order closed with margin call

## 3) No order executed (anymore)

## 4) Nothing happens

## Summary

### References

TradingView strategies can use long margin and short margin. When a strategy uses margin, its behaviour changes in a couple of ways. Those changes all relate to how the strategy equity compares against an order’s required margin.

The outcome of that comparison decides how the strategy’s orders behave. Four things can happen when a strategy uses margin:

Let’s take a closer look at each possible consequence of margin.

When the strategy generates an order and margin is enabled for that order’s direction, TradingView calculates that order’s required margin [see 1] .

If the strategy has more equity than the order’s required margin, TradingView approves the order and simulates its fill on the chart [2] .

But when the strategy equity isn’t more than the required margin, TradingView skips the order [2] . The order won’t execute and the strategy has a missed trading opportunity.

So the way that margin affects the order fill behaviour is:

What TradingView doesn’t do is adjust the order size to make its required margin less than the strategy equity. That’s a job for the strategy code itself; TradingView prefers to cancel the trade altogether.

If a strategy has much more equity available than the order’s required margin, then the long margin and short margin settings don’t impact order fill behaviour. This for instance happens when the strategy trades modest size.

When the strategy does open an order, margin can still influence that order. When the open trade starts losing money, the strategy’s equity declines. If that equity falls below the position’s required margin, a margin call happens.

TradingView issues a margin call as an exit order. That order often closes part of the position (but can also close the entire position). The goal of that margin call is to reduce the position so that its required margin is once again below the strategy equity.

Note that a margin call only happens when a strategy trades big size (relative to its strategy equity). When it doesn’t trade large positions, losing trades won’t trigger margin calls. In that case this feature of a strategy’s margin doesn’t influence the strategy.

The third way that margin affects the strategy is when it prevents trading altogether. This can happen in two ways. Either the strategy never starts trading. Or, after a series of losses, the strategy doesn’t resume trading.

In both cases, the cause is the same: the strategy’s equity is insufficient to meet each order’s required margin. That lack of equity has TradingView cancel order after order, which in effect stops the strategy’s trading.

There are a couple of reasons why this happens. The strategy may have lost a lot of equity and simply cannot afford new trades. Or its order size is too big (and has too much required margin). Its initial capital may be set too low. Or we configured its long margin and short margin settings incorrectly.

The fourth possibility is that margin doesn’t impact the strategy’s behaviour. This is the best scenario we could hope for. Why? Because it shows that the strategy didn’t take a large amount of risk.

The margin consequences we discussed above indicate that the strategy could manage risk better. For instance, when TradingView cancels orders, it’s because their size is too big compared with the strategy equity. And when a strategy doesn’t trade at all with margin active, it’s because it cannot afford to open orders of that size.

In contrast, when we enable long margin and short margin on a strategy that trades modest size, we don’t see a difference in how the strategy behaves and performs. And that’s actually a good sign.

1. An entry order is skipped (that is, not executed).
1. An open order is closed with a margin call.
1. No entry order is taken anymore (meaning, the strategy stops trading).
1. Nothing happens.

1. The strategy generates an entry order based on its coded conditions.That order can generate with the strategy.entry() function or with the strategy.order() function. These are the only two functions that can open strategy orders, and both are affected by the strategy’s margin.
1. That order can generate with the strategy.entry() function or with the strategy.order() function. These are the only two functions that can open strategy orders, and both are affected by the strategy’s margin.
1. TradingView calculates the order’s required margin. There are two outcomes now:When the strategy equity is higher than the required margin, TradingView executes the order.When the strategy equity is the same or lower than the required margin, the strategy cannot meet the order’s margin and TradingView cancels the order.
1. When the strategy equity is higher than the required margin, TradingView executes the order.
1. When the strategy equity is the same or lower than the required margin, the strategy cannot meet the order’s margin and TradingView cancels the order.

- That order can generate with the strategy.entry() function or with the strategy.order() function. These are the only two functions that can open strategy orders, and both are affected by the strategy’s margin.

- When the strategy equity is higher than the required margin, TradingView executes the order.
- When the strategy equity is the same or lower than the required margin, the strategy cannot meet the order’s margin and TradingView cancels the order.

- The long margin and short margin settings can change a strategy’s behaviour in several ways.
- Without enough equity to meet the required margin, the strategy skips orders (and sometimes doesn’t trade at all).
- When a trade loses money and equity declines below the required margin, TradingView forcibly closes (part of) the position with a margin call.
- Not every strategy suffers from negative consequences of margin. Without a large position size, margin doesn’t change the strategy’s behaviour.

1. TradingView Blog (2021, May 26). Strategy Leverage. Retrieved on January 11, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/blog/en/strategy-leverage-24638/
1. TradingView Docs (n.d.). Strategies. Retrieved on January 11, 2022, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v5/concepts/Strategies.html

lessons.title: How to get the price coordinates from a TradingView trend line?
lessons.clean_text:
# How to get the price coordinates from a TradingView trend line?

## Fetch price coordinates from TradingView trend lines

### Get a line’s price coordinates: line.get_y1() and line.get_y2()

## Quick example: get line prices with line.get_y1() and line.get_y2()

## Example script: draw lines at a strategy’s entry price

## Features of line.get_y1() and line.get_y2()

## Summary

### References

After our code makes a TradingView trend line, there are still several ways to work with the line. One option is to fetch the line’s current price coordinates. Let’s see how we do that.

TradingView indicators and strategies make trend lines with the line.new() function. When we execute that function, TradingView draws a line between two points on the chart. Each point is a combination of a time and price value.

Often when we relocate a line, we want to know what the line’s current coordinates are. There are two functions that return the line’s prices. Let’s take a look.

In Pine Script we get a line’s price coordinates with two functions (TradingView, n.d.):

To do its job, each function requires a single argument. That makes their default pattern look like this (TradingView, n.d.):

Besides getting a line’s prices, we can also get its time coordinates. See fetch time coordinates from TradingView lines for the specifics.

Here’s a quick example of how line.get_y1() and line.get_y2() retrieve a line’s prices:

This code first makes a trend line with line.new(). We put the line reference that the function returns in the myLine variable. That way we have a way to access the trend line at a later point.

Then we retrieve the line’s prices. To do so we call the line.get_y1() and line.get_y2() functions. With each function we use the myLine variable to identify which line’s prices we want. We put the values that those functions return in the startPrice and endPrice variables.

Once we have the line’s prices, there are several things we can do. One option is to update the line’s prices to a new location based on the current y-axis (price) coordinates.

By the way, TradingView doesn’t always require keyword arguments. I just use them to make examples a bit clearer. But this is also totally valid code:

Now let’s see how we might use line.get_y1() and line.get_y2() in a complete script. The example strategy below trades whenever prices cross a 20-bar Exponential Moving Average (EMA). When there’s an open position, we draw a trend line at the strategy’s entry price.

During the trade we compare the bar’s close against that line’s price value. We use the line.get_y2() function for that. When the bar drops below the entry price, we change the line’s style.

The strategy’s entire code is:

First we set the strategy’s options with the strategy() function. Then we compute the 20-bar EMA with the ta.ema() function.

With the ta.crossover() and ta.crossunder() functions we see if prices crossed above or below that moving average. We put the true/false outcome of those functions in the enterLong and enterShort variables.

Then we check each of those variables with an if statement. When found true, the strategy.entry() function opens a long (strategy.long) or short (strategy.short) position.

After that we get to the trend line code. First we make a trend line variable:

This entryLine variable has a default value of na. That ’empty’ value is just a temporary placeholder. So why bother making it in the first place?

Later we’ll make our trend line in an if statement. If we’d make the variable there, its life is limited to the if statement. So outside the if code we cannot use the variable. That’s why we make the variable in advance, in an unindented line of code. That makes it accessible anywhere in the script.

Next we see if the strategy went long or short:

When the strategy opened a long or short position, we draw a trend line that highlights the entry price. Here’s how:

This if statement checks if the strategy went long (wentLong) or short (wentShort). When one of these occurred (or), we make a new trend line with the line.new() function.

The code draws that line between the previous (bar_index[1]) and current bar (bar_index). The line’s price is set to the current position’s average entry price (strategy.position_avg_price). Since each line point has the same price, we get a horizontal line. The line reference that line.new() returns is what we store in the entryLine variable for use later.

Next we set the line’s colour with a nested if/else statement. When the strategy opened a long position (wentLong), the line.set_color() function sets the line to navy blue (color.navy). For short positions that function makes the line fuchsia (color.fuchsia).

However, drawing a trend line when there’s a new long or short position isn’t enough. We also have to manage the line for as long as there’s an open position. That’s what we do next:

This if statement looks whether the strategy’s position size (strategy.position_size) is different than (!=) zero (0). That happens whenever the strategy is long or short.

In that scenario the nested if/else statement compares the bar’s close (close) against the price coordinate of the line’s second point. We get that latter value through the line.get_y2() function. (Since we made a horizontal line, any price value of the trend line tells us what price the entire line uses.)

When the bar is above the line, we make the line solid. For that we call the line.set_style() function. With the entryLine variable we identify the line. Then we use line.style_solid to make that line solid. The else code runs when the bar closes at or below the line. In that case the same function makes the line dotted (line.style_dotted).

The last thing we do is call the line.set_x2() function. That function updates the time coordinate of the line’s second point. Here we use it to update that coordinate to the current bar number (bar_index). That extends the line one bar at a time. But only when there’s an open position (strategy.position_size != 0).

Here’s how the strategy looks on the chart:

The strategy makes blue lines for longs and fuchsia lines for shorts. Whenever prices move above the entry price, the line becomes solid. So longs are profitable with a solid line while shorts are losing money then. When prices move below the entry price, the line turns into a dotted one.

The line.get_y1() and line.get_y2() functions have a few noteworthy characteristics:

TradingView scripts make trend lines with the line.new() function. Once a line is on the chart there are several ways to retrieve its data. One option are the line.get_y1() and line.get_y2() functions. These return the line’s y-axis values.

The line.get_y1() function tells us the price coordinate of the line’s point. The line.get_y2() function does the same for the line’s second point.

Both functions need one argument: a line reference that specifies from which line we want prices. Without such a reference we cannot retrieve price values from a trend line.

TradingView Docs (n.d.). Drawings. Retrieved on December 5, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/en/v4/essential/Drawings.html

TradingView (n.d.). Pine Script Language Reference Manual. Retrieved on December 5, 2019, from https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-reference/v4/

```pine_script
line.get_y1(id) line.get_y2(id)
```

```pine_script
// Make a new trend line myLine = line.new(x1=bar_index[10], y1=close[10], x2=bar_index, y2=close) // ... // Get the line's price coordinates startPrice = line.get_y1(id=myLine) endPrice = line.get_y2(id=myLine)
```

```pine_script
// Make a new trend line myLine = line.new(bar_index[10], close[10], bar_index, close) // Get the line's price coordinates startPrice = line.get_y1(myLine) endPrice = line.get_y2(myLine)
```

```pine_script
//@version=5 strategy(title="Track entry price with trend line", overlay=true) // Calculate strategy values maValue = ta.ema(close, 20) // Set trade entry conditions enterLong = ta.crossover(close, maValue) enterShort = ta.crossunder(close, maValue) // Submit entry orders if enterLong strategy.entry("EL", strategy.long) if enterShort strategy.entry("ES", strategy.short) var line entryLine = na // Figure out change in strategy position wentLong = strategy.position_size > 0 and strategy.position_size[1] <= 0 wentShort = strategy.position_size < 0 and strategy.position_size[1] >= 0 // Make a new line when strategy opened a position if wentLong or wentShort entryLine := line.new(x1=bar_index[1], y1=strategy.position_avg_price, x2=bar_index, y2=strategy.position_avg_price, width=5) if wentLong line.set_color(entryLine, color=color.navy) else line.set_color(entryLine, color=color.fuchsia) // For as long as the strategy has an open trade, manage // the line's style and length if strategy.position_size != 0 if close > line.get_y2(entryLine) line.set_style(entryLine, style=line.style_solid) else line.set_style(entryLine, style=line.style_dotted) line.set_x2(entryLine, x=bar_index)
```

```pine_script
var line entryLine = na
```

```pine_script
// Figure out change in strategy position wentLong = strategy.position_size > 0 and strategy.position_size[1] <= 0 wentShort = strategy.position_size < 0 and strategy.position_size[1] >= 0
```

```pine_script
// Make a new line when strategy opened a position if wentLong or wentShort entryLine := line.new(x1=bar_index[1], y1=strategy.position_avg_price, x2=bar_index, y2=strategy.position_avg_price, width=5) if wentLong line.set_color(entryLine, color=color.navy) else line.set_color(entryLine, color=color.fuchsia)
```

```pine_script
// For as long as the strategy has an open trade, manage // the line's style and length if strategy.position_size != 0 if close > line.get_y2(entryLine) line.set_style(entryLine, style=line.style_solid) else line.set_style(entryLine, style=line.style_dotted) line.set_x2(entryLine, x=bar_index)
```

- line.get_y1() returns the price value of the line’s first point.
- line.get_y2() returns the price value of the line’s second point.

- id specifies the line to get the price coordinate from. (The value we use here is a line reference, which is something that the line.new() function returns when we make the line.)

- To know from which line to return prices, both functions need a line reference. There’s only one way to get such a thing: put the value that the line.new() function returns in a variable. Without such a reference there’s no way to access a trend line’s prices.
- When we use a na value with the line.get_y1() or line.get_y2() function, there is no TradingView error. But neither will the function succeed in returning the line’s price. So if your code doesn’t error but neither works, check if the line reference has a valid value.
- line.get_y1() and line.get_y2() only return a line’s prices. Use the line.get_x1() and line.get_x2() functions to know the line’s time coordinates. And with the line.set_y1() and line.set_y2() functions you can update a line’s prices.
- We cannot manually interact with the trend lines that the line.new() function makes (TradingView Docs, n.d.). That makes the line.get_y1() and line.get_y2() functions the only way to ‘read’ a line’s prices.
- line.get_y1() and line.get_y2() return the line’s price coordinates as a series of floating-point values (TradingView, n.d.). That even applies when our line is positioned with integer price coordinates.
